\text{Error}_5 &= \vert 150 - 135.00 \vert = 15.00 \\
\text{Error}_6 &= \vert 145 - 140.00 \vert = 5.00 \\
\text{Error}_7 &\text{ — forecast only, no actual — excluded from MAD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute MAD over the 3 comparable periods:**

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{14.33 + 15.00 + 5.00}{3} = \frac{34.33}{3} = 11.44 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Final Answer:** $F_7 = 145.67$ lakhs, $F_8 = 146.89$ lakhs, MAD = ₹11.44 lakhs.

*(Step-wise allocation: 1 mark setup + 4 marks for forecasts + 2 marks for MAD computation.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

Holt's Double Exponential Smoothing is a refinement of Single ES designed for time-series data that exhibit a **linear trend** (either upward or downward) but no seasonality. The method maintains **two separate equations** — one for the level ($L_t$) and one for the trend ($T_t$) — and combines them to produce the forecast.

**Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level Equation:} \quad L_t &= \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) \\
\text{Trend Equation:} \quad T_t &= \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1} \\
\text{Forecast Equation:} \quad F_{t+m} &= L_t + m \cdot T_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $\alpha$ = smoothing constant for level $(0 \leq \alpha \leq 1)$
- $\beta$ = smoothing constant for trend $(0 \leq \beta \leq 1)$
- $L_t$ = estimated level at time $t$
- $T_t$ = estimated trend (slope) at time $t$
- $m$ = number of periods into the future to forecast

**Why Holt's method is preferred over Single ES when:**

1. The time-series exhibits a **persistent upward or downward trend** (Single ES lags behind trends systematically).
2. **Long-range forecasts** are required, where trend extrapolation is critical.
3. The data does **not** have a seasonal pattern (in that case, Holt-Winters is used instead).

*(2 marks for each equation × 3 = ~3 marks; 1 mark for explaining parameters; 3 marks for explaining preference conditions.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** The monthly demand for a product over the last 8 months is: 200, 220, 210, 230, 225, 240, 250, 245. Using **Single Exponential Smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = 200$, generate forecasts for months 2 to 9 and compute the **MAPE**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Simple Moving Average**, **Weighted Moving Average**, and **Exponential Smoothing** in terms of storage requirements, weight assignment, responsiveness, and best-use scenarios.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Formula:** $F_{t+1} = 0.4 \cdot D_t + 0.6 \cdot F_t$

**Step-by-step computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= 0.4(200) + 0.6(200) = 200.00 \\
F_3 &= 0.4(220) + 0.6(200) = 88 + 120 = 208.00 \\
F_4 &= 0.4(210) + 0.6(208) = 84 + 124.8 = 208.80 \\
F_5 &= 0.4(230) + 0.6(208.8) = 92 + 125.28 = 217.28 \\
F_6 &= 0.4(225) + 0.6(217.28) = 90 + 130.37 = 220.37 \\
F_7 &= 0.4(240) + 0.6(220.37) = 96 + 132.22 = 228.22 \\
F_8 &= 0.4(250) + 0.6(228.22) = 100 + 136.93 = 236.93 \\
F_9 &= 0.4(245) + 0.6(236.93) = 98 + 142.16 = 240.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute percentage errors (using $\vert D_t - F_t \vert / D_t \times 100$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Month 2:} \quad \frac{\vert 220 - 200 \vert}{220} \times 100 &= 9.09\% \\
\text{Month 3:} \quad \frac{\vert 210 - 208 \vert}{210} \times 100 &= 0.95\% \\
\text{Month 4:} \quad \frac{\vert 230 - 208.8 \vert}{230} \times 100 &= 9.22\% \\
\text{Month 5:} \quad \frac{\vert 225 - 217.28 \vert}{225} \times 100 &= 3.43\% \\
\text{Month 6:} \quad \frac{\vert 240 - 220.37 \vert}{240} \times 100 &= 8.18\% \\
\text{Month 7:} \quad \frac{\vert 250 - 228.22 \vert}{250} \times 100 &= 8.71\% \\
\text{Month 8:} \quad \frac{\vert 245 - 236.93 \vert}{245} \times 100 &= 3.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum of percentage errors:** $9.09 + 0.95 + 9.22 + 3.43 + 8.18 + 8.71 + 3.29 = 42.87\%$

**MAPE:**

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{42.87}{7} = 6.12\%$$

**Final Answer:** $F_9 = 240.16$, MAPE = 6.12%.

*(1 mark for table + 4 marks for forecasts + 2 marks for MAPE.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Criterion | Simple Moving Average | Weighted Moving Average | Exponential Smoothing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Required** | All `n` past actuals | All `n` past actuals + weights | Only the *previous forecast* and α |
| **Weight Assignment** | All weights equal ($1/n$) | User-defined, recent weight highest | Decay automatically: $\alpha(1-\alpha)^k$ |
| **Responsiveness** | Slow — only updates when window slides | Moderate — weights can amplify recency | Tunable via α (high α = fast, low α = slow) |
| **Best-Use Scenario** | Stable demand, very small datasets | When domain knowledge suggests specific weighting | Continuous data, dynamic demand, any length |
| **Forecast for Multi-Period Ahead** | Same value repeats | Same value repeats | Grows/shrinks with trend (Holt's) |
| **Computational Complexity** | Lowest | Low | Low (Single) / Medium (Holt's / Holt-Winters) |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per major criterion × 6 + 1 mark for concluding summary.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Window Index Error (–2 marks):** In Moving Average problems, students often misalign the forecast period. Remember: a 3-period MA produces its *first* forecast at **period 4**, not period 3. If the question asks for $F_5$ and you use $D_3, D_4, D_5$, you have made an off-by-one error.
>
> 2. **Initial Forecast Assumption (–1 mark):** For Single Exponential Smoothing, *always* explicitly state $F_1 = D_1$ (or whatever initial condition the question provides). Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous initialization.
>
> 3. **α Misplacement (–2 marks):** In the formula $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$, a common error is to write $\alpha F_t$ instead of $(1-\alpha) F_t$. Recheck the formula before submitting.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to compute MAD/MSE (–2 marks):** Even if the question does not explicitly ask for it, mentioning the error metric *adds depth* and signals conceptual mastery. Always include MAD.
>
> 5. **Conflating WMA with ES (–1 mark):** WMA has *fixed* weights for the last `n` periods; ES has weights that mathematically extend *infinitely back*. Do not interchange them.
>
> 6. **Choosing Holt's ES for seasonal data (–1 mark):** Holt's handles *trend only*. If seasonality is present, you *must* use Holt-Winters.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Moving Average & Exponential Smoothing**

- **Forecasting** is the foundation of operations planning; quantitative methods use historical data only.
- **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** = arithmetic mean of the last `n` periods; requires `n ≥ 2`.
- **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** = assigns user-defined weights that must sum to 1; most recent period gets the highest weight.
- **Exponential Smoothing (ES)** = recursive weighted average with a smoothing constant α $(0 \leq \alpha \leq 1)$.
- **Why "Exponential"?** Because each older observation's weight is multiplied by a successive power of $(1-\alpha)$, producing exponential decay.
- **Single ES** is for stable data (no trend, no seasonality).
- **Holt's Double ES** adds a *trend* component using a second smoothing constant β.
- **Holt-Winters Triple ES** adds a *seasonal* component using a third constant γ.
- **High α** → forecast reacts aggressively to recent changes; **Low α** → forecast is smooth and stable.
- **MAD** = average of absolute errors; **MSE** = average of squared errors; **MAPE** = average of percentage errors.
- **Lower MAD/MSE/MAPE** indicates a better-fitting forecast model.
- **Limitation of Moving Average** = equal weighting, requires storage of all `n` values, lags during trends.
- **Advantage of Exponential Smoothing** = minimal data storage (only previous forecast), mathematically elegant, easily updatable.
- **KTU Hot Tip:** Always show the *sliding window* or *recursive step* explicitly in your answer script — methodology clarity is rewarded.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Moving Average and Exponential Smoothing Methods**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trend, Cycle, and Seasonality Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Trend, Cycle, and Seasonality Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Operations Management** and **Demand Forecasting**, a time series is a sequence of observations of a quantitative variable recorded at successive, equally spaced time intervals (hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, or yearly). The **Classical Time Series Decomposition Model** states that any time series $Y_t$ observed in an operational environment can be decomposed into four systematic components:

$$Y_t = f(T_t, C_t, S_t, I_t)$$

The four canonical components are:

1. **Trend Component ($T_t$)** — The long-term, smooth, underlying direction of movement in the series over an extended horizon. It reflects the sustained growth, decline, or stagnation driven by structural factors such as population growth, technological progress, capital investment, or market maturity.

2. **Cyclical Component ($C_t$)** — The medium-to-long-term oscillatory fluctuation around the trend, typically with a duration exceeding one year, caused by macroeconomic forces, business cycles, political shifts, and large-scale industrial dynamics.

3. **Seasonal Component ($S_t$)** — The regular, predictable, and recurring intra-year (or sub-annual) fluctuation tied to the calendar — weather, festivals, academic cycles, holidays, or operational shifts.

4. **Irregular / Random Component ($I_t$)** — The residual stochastic noise attributable to short-duration, non-recurring, unanticipated shocks (strikes, natural disasters, pandemics, sudden policy changes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome mapping for this topic generally falls under **CO1 / CO2 (Apply forecasting principles to demand analysis)**. Students are expected to *identify*, *distinguish*, and *mathematically separate* the four components in a classical decomposition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heartbeat of a Business"

Imagine you place a stethoscope on a company and listen to its sales over **10 years**. The heartbeat you hear is a single, complex wave. If you apply a *spectral filter* (the statistical decomposition), four distinct waves emerge:

| Wave | What you "hear" | Real-world analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Trend ($T_t$)** | A deep, slow ocean current pulling steadily in one direction | The general rise of e-commerce over the last decade |
| **Cycle ($C_t$)** | A slow, deep swell of the sea rising and falling every 3–7 years | Recession and boom periods in the car industry |
| **Seasonality ($S_t$)** | A daily tide — predictable, repeating each year | December surge in toy sales, July surge in ice-cream |
| **Irregular ($I_t$)** | Sudden splashes, a fish jumping, a storm hitting the shore | COVID-19 lockdown sales shock, a sudden port strike |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Idea:** Operations managers forecast demand to decide *how much* raw material to order, *how many* workers to schedule, and *how much* inventory to hold. Knowing **what fraction of tomorrow's demand is trend, cycle, season, or noise** lets the manager forecast more accurately.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Period of seasonality ($L$):** the number of observations in one full seasonal cycle (e.g., $L=12$ for monthly data with yearly seasonality, $L=4$ for quarterly data).
- **Length of cyclical wave:** typically $\geq 1$ year (not fixed in length, unlike seasonality).
- **Variance contribution of each component:** often reported in percentage terms in KTU numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Decomposition Models

There are two mathematically valid ways to combine the four components:

### (a) Additive Model

$$Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$$

- Used when the **magnitude of seasonal/cyclical swings is roughly constant** over time.
- Typical of: macroeconomic indicators expressed in absolute units (e.g., GDP, currency).
- All components are expressed in the **same units as the original series**.

### (b) Multiplicative Model

$$Y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t$$

- Used when the **magnitude of seasonal/cyclical swings grows proportionally with the trend**.
- Typical of: retail sales, airline passengers, e-commerce orders, electricity consumption.
- The components $C_t$, $S_t$, $I_t$ are **dimensionless indices** centered around $1.0$ (or $100$ when expressed in percentage form).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logarithmic Linearization:** The multiplicative model can be transformed into an additive one by taking logarithms:
> $$\ln(Y_t) = \ln(T_t) + \ln(C_t) + \ln(S_t) + \ln(I_t)$$
> This is a frequent KTU examination trick — recognizing which model is in use from a given problem.

## 2.2 Properties of Each Component (Distinguishing Checklist)

| Property | Trend ($T_t$) | Cycle ($C_t$) | Seasonality ($S_t$) | Irregular ($I_t$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Duration | Long-term (years/decades) | Medium-term (1–10 yrs) | Short, fixed period ($L$) | Short, unpredictable |
| Pattern | Smooth monotonic or gentle curve | Wave-like but **non-periodic** | **Strictly periodic** | Random noise |
| Cause | Structural / technology | Macro-economy | Calendar / climate | Random shocks |
| Predictable? | Yes, structurally | Loosely | **Highly** | No |
| Frequency in KTU | Common | Occasional | **Very common** | Rarely isolated |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Additive model | $Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$ | All in original units |
| 2 | Multiplicative model | $Y_t = T_t \cdot C_t \cdot S_t \cdot I_t$ | $C_t, S_t, I_t$ are unit-less indices |
| 3 | Seasonal index (additive) | $S_t = \bar{x}_{\text{season}} - \bar{x}_{\text{grand}}$ | Expressed in original units, $\sum S_t = 0$ |
| 4 | Seasonal index (multiplicative) | $S_t = \dfrac{\bar{x}_{\text{season}}}{\bar{x}_{\text{grand}}}$ | Dimensionless, $\sum S_t = L$ (so mean $= 1$) |
| 5 | Centered moving average (even $L$) | $\text{CMA}_t = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot x_{t-L/2} + \sum_{j} x_{t+j} + 0.5 \cdot x_{t+L/2}}{L}$ | Used to isolate $T_t + C_t$ |
| 6 | Centered moving average (odd $L$) | $\text{CMA}_t = \dfrac{\sum_{j=-(L-1)/2}^{(L-1)/2} x_{t+j}}{L}$ | Symmetric window |
| 7 | Deseasonalized series | $Y_t^{\text{des}} = Y_t - S_t$ (additive) or $Y_t / S_t$ (multiplicative) | Removes the seasonal component |
| 8 | Trend extraction (linear) | $T_t = a + b \cdot t$ | $b$ is the trend slope |
| 9 | Trend slope estimator | $\hat{b} = \dfrac{\sum (t - \bar{t})(Y_t - \bar{Y})}{\sum (t - \bar{t})^2}$ | Units of $Y$ per time period |
| 10 | Variance decomposition | $\text{Var}(Y) = \text{Var}(T) + \text{Var}(C) + \text{Var}(S) + \text{Var}(I)$ (additive) | Additivity applies only to additive model |
| 11 | Relative contribution of $S$ | $\dfrac{\text{Var}(S)}{\text{Var}(Y)} \times 100\%$ | Reported as a percentage |
| 12 | Normalized seasonal index | $S_t^{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{S_t \cdot L}{\sum S_t}$ | Forces $\sum S_t^{\text{norm}} = L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering / Industrial Utility:** Decomposition is used in:
> - **Inventory & supply-chain planning** (Kerala coir, tea, spice industries),
> - **Electricity load forecasting** (KSEB demand peaks in summer),
> - **Tourism planning** (Kerala backwater season, Onam, monsoon),
> - **Workforce scheduling** (manufacturing shifts, retail staffing),
> - **Capacity planning** in manufacturing facilities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Seasonal Index Computation (Multiplicative Model, $L=4$)

> A departmental store records quarterly sales (in ₹ lakhs) over 4 years. Compute the **seasonal index** for each quarter using the **multiplicative classical decomposition method**.

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2021 | 8 | 10 | 14 | 18 |
| 2022 | 10 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| 2023 | 12 | 14 | 18 | 22 |
| 2024 | 14 | 16 | 20 | 24 |

### Step 1 — Compute the 4-period **Centered Moving Average (CMA)** to estimate $T_t \cdot C_t$.

For quarterly data, $L=4$. The 4-period moving average at position $t$ is:

$$MA_t = \frac{Y_{t-2} + Y_{t-1} + Y_t + Y_{t+1}}{4}$$

For Q1 of 2021 (no leading data is available, so we start from Q3 2021):
- For **Q3, 2021**: $\dfrac{8 + 10 + 14 + 18}{4} = \dfrac{50}{4} = 12.50$
- For **Q4, 2021**: $\dfrac{10 + 14 + 18 + 10}{4} = \dfrac{52}{4} = 13.00$
- For **Q1, 2022**: $\dfrac{14 + 18 + 10 + 12}{4} = \dfrac{54}{4} = 13.50$
- For **Q2, 2022**: $\dfrac{18 + 10 + 12 + 16}{4} = \dfrac{56}{4} = 14.00$
- For **Q3, 2022**: $\dfrac{10 + 12 + 16 + 20}{4} = \dfrac{58}{4} = 14.50$
- For **Q4, 2022**: $\dfrac{12 + 16 + 20 + 12}{4} = \dfrac{60}{4} = 15.00$
- For **Q1, 2023**: $\dfrac{16 + 20 + 12 + 14}{4} = \dfrac{62}{4} = 15.50$
- For **Q2, 2023**: $\dfrac{20 + 12 + 14 + 18}{4} = \dfrac{64}{4} = 16.00$
- For **Q3, 2023**: $\dfrac{12 + 14 + 18 + 22}{4} = \dfrac{66}{4} = 16.50$
- For **Q4, 2023**: $\dfrac{14 + 18 + 22 + 14}{4} = \dfrac{68}{4} = 17.00$
- For **Q1, 2024**: $\dfrac{18 + 22 + 14 + 16}{4} = \dfrac{70}{4} = 17.50$
- For **Q2, 2024**: $\dfrac{22 + 14 + 16 + 20}{4} = \dfrac{72}{4} = 18.00$

Since $L$ is even, the 4-MA lands **between** two quarters. The **Centered MA** is the average of two consecutive 4-MAs:

- **CMA at Q3 2021** (between 4-MA landing at Q2.5 and Q3.5 of 2021) = $\dfrac{12.50 + 13.00}{2} = 12.75$
- **CMA at Q4 2021** = $\dfrac{13.00 + 13.50}{2} = 13.25$
- **CMA at Q1 2022** = $\dfrac{13.50 + 14.00}{2} = 13.75$
- **CMA at Q2 2022** = $\dfrac{14.00 + 14.50}{2} = 14.25$
- **CMA at Q3 2022** = $\dfrac{14.50 + 15.00}{2} = 14.75$
- **CMA at Q4 2022** = $\dfrac{15.00 + 15.50}{2} = 15.25$
- **CMA at Q1 2023** = $\dfrac{15.50 + 16.00}{2} = 15.75$
- **CMA at Q2 2023** = $\dfrac{16.00 + 16.50}{2} = 16.25$
- **CMA at Q3 2023** = $\dfrac{16.50 + 17.00}{2} = 16.75$
- **CMA at Q4 2023** = $\dfrac{17.00 + 17.50}{2} = 17.25$

The CMA estimates $T_t \cdot C_t$ (with the seasonal component already removed by averaging across all four seasons).

### Step 2 — Compute the **Ratio-to-Moving-Average (RMA)**.

For multiplicative model: $RMA_t = \dfrac{Y_t}{CMA_t}$.

- **Q3 2021:** $14 / 12.75 = 1.0980$
- **Q4 2021:** $18 / 13.25 = 1.3585$
- **Q1 2022:** $10 / 13.75 = 0.7273$
- **Q2 2022:** $12 / 14.25 = 0.8421$
- **Q3 2022:** $16 / 14.75 = 1.0847$
- **Q4 2022:** $20 / 15.25 = 1.3115$
- **Q1 2023:** $12 / 15.75 = 0.7619$
- **Q2 2023:** $14 / 16.25 = 0.8615$
- **Q3 2023:** $18 / 16.75 = 1.0746$
- **Q4 2023:** $22 / 17.25 = 1.2754$

### Step 3 — Average the RMAs **by quarter** to get raw seasonal indices.

$$\bar{RMA}_{Q1} = \frac{0.7273 + 0.7619}{2} = 0.7446$$

$$\bar{RMA}_{Q2} = \frac{0.8421 + 0.8615}{2} = 0.8518$$

$$\bar{RMA}_{Q3} = \frac{1.0980 + 1.0847 + 1.0746}{3} = 1.0858$$

$$\bar{RMA}_{Q4} = \frac{1.3585 + 1.3115 + 1.2754}{3} = 1.3151$$

### Step 4 — **Normalize** the indices so that they sum to $L=4$.

Sum of raw indices = $0.7446 + 0.8518 + 1.0858 + 1.3151 = 3.9973$.

Adjustment factor = $4 / 3.9973 = 1.0007$.

Normalized seasonal indices:

$$S_{Q1} = 0.7446 \times 1.0007 = 0.7451$$

$$S_{Q2} = 0.8518 \times 1.0007 = 0.8524$$

$$S_{Q3} = 1.0858 \times 1.0007 = 1.0866$$

$$S_{Q4} = 1.3151 \times 1.0007 = 1.3160$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $0.7451 + 0.8524 + 1.0866 + 1.3160 = 4.0001 \approx 4.00$. The small discrepancy is due to rounding. The mean of the seasonal indices is exactly $1.000$, confirming the multiplicative normalization.

**Interpretation:** Q4 sales are ~31.6% above the trend-cycle average, while Q1 sales are ~25.5% below it. This is the typical **festive-season surge** pattern in retail.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Additive Trend Estimation

> A manufacturer records annual demand for a component over 7 years: $Y = \{120, 135, 142, 158, 170, 181, 195\}$ units. Estimate the **linear trend** $T_t = a + b \cdot t$.

Let $t = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$ and $\bar{t} = 4$.

| $t$ | $Y$ | $t - \bar{t}$ | $Y - \bar{Y}$ | $(t - \bar{t})(Y - \bar{Y})$ | $(t - \bar{t})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | -3 | -30.43 | 91.29 | 9 |
| 2 | 135 | -2 | -15.43 | 30.86 | 4 |
| 3 | 142 | -1 | -8.43 | 8.43 | 1 |
| 4 | 158 | 0 | 7.57 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 170 | 1 | 19.57 | 19.57 | 1 |
| 6 | 181 | 2 | 30.57 | 61.14 | 4 |
| 7 | 195 | 3 | 44.57 | 133.71 | 9 |
| **Sum** | 1101 | 0 | 0 | **345.00** | **28** |

$\bar{Y} = 1101/7 = 157.29$.

$$\hat{b} = \frac{\sum (t-\bar{t})(Y-\bar{Y})}{\sum (t-\bar{t})^2} = \frac{345}{28} = 12.32 \text{ units/year}$$

$$\hat{a} = \bar{Y} - \hat{b}\bar{t} = 157.29 - 12.32 \times 4 = 157.29 - 49.28 = 108.01$$

Hence, the linear trend is:

$$T_t = 108.01 + 12.32 \cdot t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Forecasting tip:** The trend is rising at **12.32 units per year**. The manufacturer's demand-planning team can use this to project the 8th year's expected baseline demand: $T_8 = 108.01 + 12.32 \times 8 = 206.57 \approx 207$ units.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How KTU Asks Questions on This Topic

| Question Type | Mark Weight | Cognitive Level (Bloom) | Key Skill Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define $T_t, C_t, S_t, I_t$ | 3 | Remember | Terminology |
| Differentiate additive vs multiplicative | 3–7 | Understand | Model selection |
| Identify components from a graph | 3 | Analyze | Visual literacy |
| Compute centered moving average | 7 | Apply | Numerical computation |
| Compute seasonal indices | 14 | Apply / Analyze | Multi-step derivation |
| Compute trend via least squares | 7 | Apply | Linear regression |
| Forecast $Y_{t+1}$ | 7 | Apply / Evaluate | Real-world decision |
| Compare two models' forecasts | 14 | Evaluate | Critical reasoning |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decomposition Logic — Block Architecture

The following block diagram traces how a raw time series is dismantled into its four components. This is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* representing the classical decomposition pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Time Series Y subscript t]:::raw --> B{Model Selection}:::decision
    B -- Additive --> C[Y equals T plus C plus S plus I]:::add
    B -- Multiplicative --> D[Y equals T cross C cross S cross I]:::mul
    C --> E[Step 1: Estimate T and C using Centered Moving Average]:::step
    D --> E
    E --> F[Step 2: Detrend the Series: Y minus CMA or Y divided by CMA]:::step
    F --> G[Step 3: Average by Season to get Raw Seasonal Index]:::step
    G --> H[Step 4: Normalize Index so Sum equals L]:::step
    H --> I[Step 5: Isolate Residual as Irregular I]:::step
    I --> J[Final Outputs: T, C, S, I]:::final

    classDef raw fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1f3a,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#c08a00,stroke:#5c4a00,color:#ffffff
    classDef add fill:#1d6f42,stroke:#0c3d24,color:#ffffff
    classDef mul fill:#1d6f42,stroke:#0c3d24,color:#ffffff
    classDef step fill:#4a4a8a,stroke:#1f1f4a,color:#ffffff
    classDef final fill:#7a1f1f,stroke:#3a0b0b,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Component Identification Decision Tree

When a KTU question gives you a graph and asks you to label each component, use this decision logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Observe Pattern]:::start --> q1{Duration exceeds one year and not strictly periodic?}:::q
    q1 -- Yes --> trend1[TREND T subscript t]:::tnode
    q1 -- No --> q2{Pattern repeats with fixed period L within each year?}:::q
    q2 -- Yes --> seas[SEASONALITY S subscript t]:::snode
    q2 -- No --> q3{Wave like but irregular in length?}:::q
    q3 -- Yes --> cyc[CYCLE C subscript t]:::cnode
    q3 -- No --> irr[IRREGULAR I subscript t]:::inode

    classDef start fill:#0f4c5c,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef q fill:#9b5de5,stroke:#3a0ca3,color:#ffffff
    classDef tnode fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#0f4c5c,color:#ffffff
    classDef snode fill:#e76f51,stroke:#7a2d1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef cnode fill:#f4a261,stroke:#7a4a14,color:#ffffff
    classDef inode fill:#6c757d,stroke:#343a40,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Additive vs Multiplicative — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p[Plot Y subscript t vs t]:::plot --> c{Does seasonal amplitude grow with trend?}:::cond
    c -- Yes --> mul[Use MULTIPLICATIVE: Y equals T cross C cross S cross I]:::mul2
    c -- No --> add[Use ADDITIVE: Y equals T plus C plus S plus I]:::add2
    mul --> m1[Indices dimensionless, sum equals L]:::note
    add --> a1[All terms in original units, sum equals zero]:::note

    classDef plot fill:#264653,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef cond fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#7a651a,color:#000000
    classDef mul2 fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#0f4c5c,color:#ffffff
    classDef add2 fill:#e76f51,stroke:#7a2d1c,color:#ffffff
    classDef note fill:#f4a261,stroke:#7a4a14,color:#000000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the trend component and the cyclical component of a time series. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Trend ($T_t$) | Cycle ($C_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | Long-term (years/decades) | Medium-term (1–10 years) |
| Nature | Smooth, gradual, monotonic | Wave-like, oscillatory |
| Periodicity | **Not periodic** | **Non-fixed periodicity** (variable length) |
| Cause | Structural / demographic / technological | Macroeconomic, business cycle |
| Predictability | High (extrapolable) | Moderate (linked to macro factors) |

> *Marking note: 1 mark for the basic definition of each + 1 mark for the differentiating criterion.*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the classical multiplicative model of time series decomposition. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The classical multiplicative model expresses the observed series as the **product** of four components:
$$Y_t = T_t \times C_t \times S_t \times I_t$$
where $T_t$ is the trend, $C_t$ is the cyclical, $S_t$ is the seasonal, and $I_t$ is the irregular component. In this model, the seasonal index $S_t$ is a unit-less ratio, with $\sum S_t = L$ (so that the average seasonal index equals 1). This model is preferred when the seasonal amplitude grows proportionally with the trend level (typical of sales, passenger traffic, and revenue data).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (as per KTU ESE 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

The annual sales of a brand over 7 years are given below:

| Year | 2018 | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sales (₹ lakh) | 40 | 50 | 52 | 56 | 60 | 65 | 70 |

**(a)** Fit a **linear trend** equation to the data using the least-squares method. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Forecast the sales for the year **2025** and **2026**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Linear Trend Fit**

Let $t = 1, 2, \ldots, 7$ for years 2018 to 2024. Then $\bar{t} = 4$ and $n = 7$.

Compute $\bar{Y}$:
$$\bar{Y} = \frac{40 + 50 + 52 + 56 + 60 + 65 + 70}{7} = \frac{393}{7} = 56.14$$

Compute the deviation table:

| $t$ | $Y$ | $t - \bar{t}$ | $Y - \bar{Y}$ | $(t - \bar{t})(Y - \bar{Y})$ | $(t - \bar{t})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40 | -3 | -16.14 | 48.42 | 9 |
| 2 | 50 | -2 | -6.14 | 12.28 | 4 |
| 3 | 52 | -1 | -4.14 | 4.14 | 1 |
| 4 | 56 | 0 | -0.14 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 60 | 1 | 3.86 | 3.86 | 1 |
| 6 | 65 | 2 | 8.86 | 17.72 | 4 |
| 7 | 70 | 3 | 13.86 | 41.58 | 9 |
| **Σ** | 393 | 0 | 0 | **128.00** | **28** |

Slope:
$$\hat{b} = \frac{128}{28} = 4.571 \text{ lakh/year}$$

Intercept:
$$\hat{a} = \bar{Y} - \hat{b}\bar{t} = 56.14 - 4.571 \times 4 = 56.14 - 18.28 = 37.86$$

**Linear trend equation:**
$$T_t = 37.86 + 4.571 \cdot t$$

**Part (b) — Forecast**

For 2025, $t = 8$:
$$T_8 = 37.86 + 4.571 \times 8 = 37.86 + 36.57 = 74.43 \approx \mathbf{74.43 \text{ lakh}}$$

For 2026, $t = 9$:
$$T_9 = 37.86 + 4.571 \times 9 = 37.86 + 41.14 = 79.00 \approx \mathbf{79.00 \text{ lakh}}$$

> *The business should plan for a sales baseline of approximately ₹74.4 lakh in 2025 and ₹79.0 lakh in 2026, ignoring seasonal and cyclical deviations.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **classical additive model** of time series decomposition. Discuss when it is preferred over the multiplicative model. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The quarterly sales (in ₹ lakh) of a textile shop for 3 years are:

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2022 | 20 | 24 | 28 | 32 |
| 2023 | 22 | 26 | 30 | 34 |
| 2024 | 24 | 28 | 32 | 36 |

Compute the **4-quarter seasonal indices** using the **ratio-to-moving-average method** for the additive model. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Additive Model**

The classical additive model expresses the observed time series as the **sum** of four independent components:
$$Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$$
where $T_t$ = trend, $C_t$ = cyclical, $S_t$ = seasonal, $I_t$ = irregular.

The additive model is preferred when:
- The **magnitude of seasonal/cyclical variation remains approximately constant** as the trend changes.
- The data are expressed in **absolute units** (e.g., temperature, currency volumes).
- Statistical properties (mean, variance) are **stationary** over time.

**When not to use:** If seasonal swings grow in proportion to trend (e.g., sales growth, internet traffic), the multiplicative model is more appropriate because the additive model would produce increasing residuals.

**Part (b) — Additive Seasonal Indices via Ratio-to-MA Method**

For $L=4$ (quarterly), compute the **4-quarter moving average**:

- 4-MA at Q3 2022 = $\dfrac{20+24+28+32}{4} = 26.00$
- 4-MA at Q4 2022 = $\dfrac{24+28+32+22}{4} = 26.50$
- 4-MA at Q1 2023 = $\dfrac{28+32+22+26}{4} = 27.00$
- 4-MA at Q2 2023 = $\dfrac{32+22+26+30}{4} = 27.50$
- 4-MA at Q3 2023 = $\dfrac{22+26+30+34}{4} = 28.00$
- 4-MA at Q4 2023 = $\dfrac{26+30+34+24}{4} = 28.50$
- 4-MA at Q1 2024 = $\dfrac{30+34+24+28}{4} = 29.00$
- 4-MA at Q2 2024 = $\dfrac{34+24+28+32}{4} = 29.50$

Centered MA (average of two consecutive 4-MAs):

- CMA at Q3 2022 = $\dfrac{26.00+26.50}{2} = 26.25$
- CMA at Q4 2022 = $\dfrac{26.50+27.00}{2} = 26.75$
- CMA at Q1 2023 = $\dfrac{27.00+27.50}{2} = 27.25$
- CMA at Q2 2023 = $\dfrac{27.50+28.00}{2} = 27.75$
- CMA at Q3 2023 = $\dfrac{28.00+28.50}{2} = 28.25$
- CMA at Q4 2023 = $\dfrac{28.50+29.00}{2} = 28.75$
- CMA at Q1 2024 = $\dfrac{29.00+29.50}{2} = 29.25$
- CMA at Q2 2024 = $\dfrac{29.50+30.00}{2} = 29.75$ (where 30.00 would be 4-MA at Q3 2024: $\dfrac{24+28+32+36}{4} = 30.00$)

**Deviation (Additive RMA):** $D_t = Y_t - \text{CMA}_t$

- Q3 2022: $28 - 26.25 = +1.75$
- Q4 2022: $32 - 26.75 = +5.25$
- Q1 2023: $22 - 27.25 = -5.25$
- Q2 2023: $26 - 27.75 = -1.75$
- Q3 2023: $30 - 28.25 = +1.75$
- Q4 2023: $34 - 28.75 = +5.25$
- Q1 2024: $24 - 29.25 = -5.25$
- Q2 2024: $28 - 29.75 = -1.75$

Average by quarter (raw seasonal deviation):

$$\bar{D}_{Q1} = \frac{-5.25 + (-5.25)}{2} = -5.25$$

$$\bar{D}_{Q2} = \frac{-1.75 + (-1.75)}{2} = -1.75$$

$$\bar{D}_{Q3} = \frac{1.75 + 1.75}{2} = 1.75$$

$$\bar{D}_{Q4} = \frac{5.25 + 5.25}{2} = 5.25$$

**Normalization check:** Sum = $-5.25 - 1.75 + 1.75 + 5.25 = 0.00$. ✓ Already normalized (sum equals zero, as required for additive seasonal indices).

**Final seasonal indices (additive):**
- $S_{Q1} = -5.25$
- $S_{Q2} = -1.75$
- $S_{Q3} = +1.75$
- $S_{Q4} = +5.25$

> *Interpretation: Q4 sales are 5.25 lakh above the trend-cycle average, while Q1 sales are 5.25 lakh below it. Q2 and Q3 deviate symmetrically by ±1.75 lakh.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this topic (verified from KTU evaluator feedback):**
> 1. **Confusing trend with cycle** — Trend is the *long-run* direction; cycle is *wave-like but non-periodic*. Do not call a wave a "trend".
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize seasonal indices** — In the multiplicative model, the raw averages must be adjusted so that they sum to $L$. Forgetting this loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Using the wrong denominator in CMA** — When $L$ is even (4, 12), the 4-MA is between two periods; students forget to take a *second* average to center the MA on a period. This is a classic 3-mark deduction.
> 4. **Adding units to multiplicative seasonal indices** — Multiplicative indices are dimensionless numbers (e.g., 1.25), not absolute values. Writing "$S_{Q4} = 1.25$ lakh" is technically wrong.
> 5. **Mixing up additive and multiplicative formulas** — Students frequently apply $Y_t - S_t$ when the problem says multiplicative (correct is $Y_t / S_t$). Always read the question stem carefully.
> 6. **Forecast without mentioning the component** — A KTU 14-mark forecast problem expects the *baseline* $T_t$ to be combined with the *seasonal index* explicitly. A bare number without the equation loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Trend, Cycle, and Seasonality Components**
> - A time series $Y_t$ decomposes into **four canonical components**: $T$ (trend), $C$ (cycle), $S$ (seasonal), $I$ (irregular).
> - **Additive model** $Y_t = T_t + C_t + S_t + I_t$ — used when seasonal amplitude is **constant**; seasonal indices sum to **zero**.
> - **Multiplicative model** $Y_t = T_t \cdot C_t \cdot S_t \cdot I_t$ — used when seasonal amplitude **grows with trend**; seasonal indices sum to **$L$** (mean equals 1).
> - **Trend** is long-term, smooth, structural (years/decades); **Cycle** is medium-term, wave-like, non-periodic; **Seasonality** is short, fixed-period, calendar-driven; **Irregular** is unpredictable random noise.
> - The **Centered Moving Average (CMA)** with window length $L$ removes the seasonal component and yields $T_t + C_t$ (additive) or $T_t \cdot C_t$ (multiplicative).
> - For **even** $L$: take the average of two consecutive $L$-MAs to center on a period.
> - For **odd** $L$: a single $L$-MA is already centered.
> - The **linear trend** is fit by least squares: $T_t = a + bt$ where $\hat{b} = \sum (t-\bar t)(Y-\bar Y) / \sum (t-\bar t)^2$.
> - **Multiplicative seasonal index** for season $j$ = average of $(Y_t / CMA_t)$ for all $t$ in season $j$, then **normalize** so that $\sum S_j = L$.
> - **Additive seasonal index** for season $j$ = average of $(Y_t - CMA_t)$ for all $t$ in season $j$, then verify $\sum S_j = 0$.
> - **Forecasting rule:** Multiplicative: $\hat{Y}_{t+1} = T_{t+1} \times S_j$. Additive: $\hat{Y}_{t+1} = T_{t+1} + S_j$.
> - KTU loves asking: (i) graph identification, (ii) computation of seasonal indices, (iii) least-squares trend, (iv) one-year-ahead forecast.
> - **Mnemonic for order of components:** "**T**ruly **C**onsistent **S**easons **I**nfluence **Y**ield" → $Y = f(T, C, S, I)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Winter's Complete Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Winter's Complete Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Winter's Complete Model** is a **triple exponential smoothing** technique used in time series forecasting that simultaneously captures three components of a series: **Level**, **Trend**, and **Seasonality**. It was developed by **Peter Winters (1960)** as an extension of Holt's linear trend model, and is formally known in literature as the **Multiplicative Triple Exponential Smoothing** method.

The model is mathematically expressed using **three smoothing equations** and **one forecast equation**, governed by three smoothing parameters: $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ — all bounded in the interval $[0, 1]$.

**Core Components of the Model:**

| Symbol | Component | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $L_t$ | **Level** | The baseline average value of the series at time $t$ |
| $T_t$ | **Trend** | The direction and rate of change (slope) at time $t$ |
| $S_t$ | **Seasonality** | The repeating cyclical factor at time $t$ |
| $\alpha$ | **Level Smoothing Constant** | Weight given to the most recent level observation |
| $\beta$ | **Trend Smoothing Constant** | Weight given to the most recent trend observation |
| $\gamma$ | **Seasonal Smoothing Constant** | Weight given to the most recent seasonal index |
| $s$ | **Seasonal Period Length** | Number of periods in one complete cycle (e.g., $s = 4$ for quarterly, $s = 12$ for monthly) |
| $m$ | **Forecast Horizon** | Number of periods into the future to forecast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBIET304 - Module 1):** Winter's Complete Model is the **most heavily tested forecasting model** in the Operations Management module. Students must memorize the **three smoothing equations** and the **forecast equation** verbatim, as they are frequently asked in Part B (14-mark) derivations and numerical problems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are the **manager of an ice cream parlor in Kerala**. You notice a clear pattern in your daily sales:

- Sales are generally **rising year over year** (people are discovering your parlor).
- Sales **spike during summer** (March–May) and **dip in monsoon** (June–August).
- Within any given year, the pattern of ups and downs is **remarkably consistent**.

Now, suppose you want to predict **next month's ice cream sales** so that you can plan your inventory, staff, and raw material purchases.

A simple average would be wrong because it ignores both the **growth** and the **seasonal cycle**. A trend-only model (like Holt's) would be wrong because it ignores the **summer spike**. This is precisely where **Winter's Complete Model** shines — it captures all three patterns **simultaneously**, like a skilled manager who remembers that "last March we sold 1200 units, and sales grow about 5% per year, and March always has a seasonal boost of 1.3× the average."

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Application:** In production planning, Winter's model is widely used for forecasting **electricity demand** (Kerala State Electricity Board uses similar logic for monsoon vs. summer demand), **airline passenger traffic**, **retail inventory for seasonal goods (Onam, Vishu sales in Kerala)**, and **aggregate production planning** in manufacturing units.

---

## 1.3 When to Use Winter's Complete Model

Use this model when the time series exhibits:
1. **A clear trend** (upward or downward, linear in nature).
2. **A multiplicative seasonal pattern** (where the seasonal swing grows with the level of the series).
3. **At least 2–3 full seasonal cycles** of historical data (e.g., at least 24 months of monthly data, or 8+ quarters of quarterly data).

**Do NOT use it when:**
- The series has no seasonality (use Holt's or simple exponential smoothing instead).
- The series has purely additive seasonality (use additive triple smoothing variant).
- Data length is too short to estimate a full seasonal cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Foundational Equations of Winter's Complete Model

The model consists of **three recursive smoothing equations** (used to update Level, Trend, and Seasonality as new data arrives) and **one forecast equation** (used to project into the future).

> [!NOTE]
> All three smoothing equations must be evaluated for **every period $t$ in the historical dataset** before any future forecast $F_{t+m}$ can be computed.

### 2.1.1 Level Smoothing Equation

$$L_t = \alpha \cdot \frac{Y_t}{S_{t-s}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
- The new level $L_t$ is a **weighted blend** of two pieces of information:
  1. The **deseasonalized** version of the current observation: $\frac{Y_t}{S_{t-s}}$ (we remove the seasonal effect from the latest data point so we can isolate the underlying level).
  2. The **previous level plus trend projection**: $L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}$ (where the model "expected" this period to land, based on past patterns).
- The parameter $\alpha$ decides how much trust to place in the **latest observation** vs. the **model's prior expectation**.
- If $\alpha = 1$, the model trusts **only the latest data** (highly reactive, volatile).
- If $\alpha = 0$, the model trusts **only the past** (ignores new information).

### 2.1.2 Trend Smoothing Equation

$$T_t = \beta \cdot (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \cdot T_{t-1}$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
- The new trend $T_t$ is a **weighted blend** of:
  1. The **change in level** between this period and the last: $L_t - L_{t-1}$ (the most recently observed "slope").
  2. The **previous trend estimate**: $T_{t-1}$ (the historical slope).
- The parameter $\beta$ controls how rapidly the model **adapts to trend reversals**.

### 2.1.3 Seasonality Smoothing Equation

$$S_t = \gamma \cdot \frac{Y_t}{L_t} + (1 - \gamma) \cdot S_{t-s}$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
- The new seasonal index $S_t$ is a **weighted blend** of:
  1. The **current seasonal ratio**: $\frac{Y_t}{L_t}$ (how much this period's actual value deviated from its deseasonalized level — i.e., the freshly observed seasonal effect).
  2. The **seasonal index from one full cycle ago**: $S_{t-s}$ (the historical seasonal effect for this same position in the cycle).
- The parameter $\gamma$ controls how quickly the model **updates its perception of the seasonal pattern**.

### 2.1.4 Forecast Equation (m periods ahead)

$$F_{t+m} = (L_t + m \cdot T_t) \cdot S_{t-s+m}$$

**Logical Interpretation:**
- To forecast $m$ periods into the future, we:
  1. Project the **trend-adjusted level** forward: $L_t + m \cdot T_t$.
  2. Multiply by the **appropriate seasonal index** from the corresponding position in the future cycle: $S_{t-s+m}$.

---

## 2.2 Initialization of the Model

Before the recursive equations can be applied, the **first $s$ periods** of data must be used to compute **initial estimates** of Level, Trend, and Seasonality.

| Initial Component | Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Initial Level** $L_s$ | Average of the first season: $L_s = \frac{1}{s} \sum_{i=1}^{s} Y_i$ |
| **Initial Trend** $T_s$ | Average difference between corresponding seasons: $T_s = \frac{1}{s} \left( \frac{Y_{s+1} - Y_1}{s} + \frac{Y_{s+2} - Y_2}{s} + \cdots + \frac{Y_{2s} - Y_s}{s} \right)$ |
| **Initial Seasonal Index** $S_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, s$ | Ratio of actual to average level of that season: $S_i = \frac{Y_i}{L_s}$ (then optionally normalized so that all seasonal indices in one cycle sum to $s$) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXACT set of equations students must memorize for KTU ESE.** Board examiners expect you to write these without derivation prompts.

| Equation # | Equation | Purpose | Key Parameters |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $L_t = \alpha \cdot \frac{Y_t}{S_{t-s}} + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$ | Update Level | $\alpha$ (level) |
| 2 | $T_t = \beta \cdot (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \cdot T_{t-1}$ | Update Trend | $\beta$ (trend) |
| 3 | $S_t = \gamma \cdot \frac{Y_t}{L_t} + (1 - \gamma) \cdot S_{t-s}$ | Update Seasonality | $\gamma$ (seasonal) |
| 4 | $F_{t+m} = (L_t + m \cdot T_t) \cdot S_{t-s+m}$ | Forecast m periods ahead | Uses all $L_t, T_t, S_t$ |
| Init | $L_s = \frac{1}{s} \sum_{i=1}^{s} Y_i$ | Initial Level | Sum of first season |
| Init | $T_s = \frac{1}{s} \sum_{j=1}^{s} \frac{Y_{s+j} - Y_j}{s}$ | Initial Trend | Cross-seasonal slopes |
| Init | $S_i = Y_i / L_s$ | Initial Seasonal Index | For $i = 1, \ldots, s$ |
| Constraint | $0 \leq \alpha, \beta, \gamma \leq 1$ | Valid range of smoothing constants | All three bounded |
| Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{s} S_i = s$ | Normalization of seasonal indices | Multiplicative case |

---

## 2.4 Utility in Real-World Engineering & Operations

- **Manufacturing Aggregate Planning:** Forecast quarterly demand to plan production rates, workforce levels, and inventory.
- **Utility Load Forecasting:** Kerala State Electricity Board forecasts hourly/daily demand which has strong daily and seasonal cycles plus annual growth.
- **Retail Supply Chain:** Predicting Onam/Vishu/Christmas demand spikes helps in procurement and logistics planning.
- **Hospitality & Tourism:** Resort bookings in Munnar, Kumarakom, and Kovalam have clear trend + seasonality.
- **ERP & SAP Modules:** MM (Materials Management) and PP (Production Planning) modules embed similar exponential smoothing logic in their demand forecasting functions.

---

## 2.5 Selection of Smoothing Parameters — A Note

There is **no closed-form analytical solution** to find optimal $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$. In practice:

- Use **grid search** (try combinations like $\alpha = 0.2, 0.3, \ldots, 0.9$).
- For each combination, compute the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** or **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** over historical data.
- Choose the combination that **minimizes the error metric**.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (often tested in KTU viva):** If the series has a strong stable trend, use **moderate $\beta$ (0.2–0.4)**. If seasonality is pronounced, use **moderate $\gamma$ (0.2–0.5)**. Generally, $\alpha$ is the most reactive parameter and is often kept between **0.1 and 0.4**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forecast Equation (Conceptual Proof)

The forecast $F_{t+m}$ is the expected value of $Y_{t+m}$. The model assumes:

$$\text{Expected Series at time } (t+m) = (\text{Level} + m \cdot \text{Trend}) \times \text{Seasonal Factor}$$

The level at time $t$ is $L_t$, and since the trend $T_t$ is assumed to be **locally linear**, the expected **deseasonalized** level at time $(t+m)$ is:

$$\text{Deseasonalized Expectation} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$$

Multiplying by the seasonal index for the position $(t+m)$ in the cycle, which is the index from one cycle ago at position $s$, plus $m$:

$$F_{t+m} = (L_t + m \cdot T_t) \cdot S_{t-s+m}$$

> This is precisely Equation 4 in the formula sheet. No further algebraic manipulation is required — it is the **definitional form** of the model.

---

## 3.2 Complete Numerical Example (12-Period Quarterly Data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This numerical problem is the **exact style** asked in KTU ESE Part B. Students should reproduce this calculation step-by-step in the exam.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A company records the following **quarterly demand (in units)** for 3 years (12 quarters). The seasonal period is $s = 4$. Use **Winter's Complete Model** with $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.4$ to forecast **demand for Quarter 1 of Year 4** ($F_{13}$).

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Y1 | 100 | 120 | 140 | 110 |
| Y2 | 130 | 150 | 170 | 140 |
| Y3 | 160 | 180 | 200 | 170 |

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Initial Estimates (for $t = 4$, i.e., end of Year 1)

**Initial Level** $L_4$ (average of first 4 quarters):

$$L_4 = \frac{100 + 120 + 140 + 110}{4} = \frac{470}{4} = 117.5$$

**Initial Trend** $T_4$ (average of slopes between Year 1 and Year 2 quarters):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 &= \frac{1}{4} \left( \frac{Y_5 - Y_1}{4} + \frac{Y_6 - Y_2}{4} + \frac{Y_7 - Y_3}{4} + \frac{Y_8 - Y_4}{4} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \left( \frac{130-100}{4} + \frac{150-120}{4} + \frac{170-140}{4} + \frac{140-110}{4} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \left( 7.5 + 7.5 + 7.5 + 7.5 \right) \\
&= \frac{30}{4} = 7.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial Seasonal Indices** ($S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \frac{100}{117.5} = 0.8511 \\
S_2 &= \frac{120}{117.5} = 1.0213 \\
S_3 &= \frac{140}{117.5} = 1.1915 \\
S_4 &= \frac{110}{117.5} = 0.9362
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Normalization:** $S_1 + S_2 + S_3 + S_4 = 0.8511 + 1.0213 + 1.1915 + 0.9362 = 4.0001 \approx 4$ ✓ (Constraint satisfied)

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Recursive Update for $t = 5$ (Q1 of Year 2, $Y_5 = 130$)

Use $L_4 = 117.5$, $T_4 = 7.5$, $S_1 = 0.8511$, and $\alpha = 0.3, \beta = 0.2, \gamma = 0.4$.

**Level:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_5 &= \alpha \cdot \frac{Y_5}{S_1} + (1 - \alpha)(L_4 + T_4) \\
&= 0.3 \cdot \frac{130}{0.8511} + 0.7 \cdot (117.5 + 7.5) \\
&= 0.3 \cdot 152.74 + 0.7 \cdot 125 \\
&= 45.82 + 87.50 \\
&= 133.32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trend:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_5 &= \beta \cdot (L_5 - L_4) + (1 - \beta) \cdot T_4 \\
&= 0.2 \cdot (133.32 - 117.5) + 0.8 \cdot 7.5 \\
&= 0.2 \cdot 15.82 + 6.0 \\
&= 3.16 + 6.0 \\
&= 9.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seasonality:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_5 &= \gamma \cdot \frac{Y_5}{L_5} + (1 - \gamma) \cdot S_1 \\
&= 0.4 \cdot \frac{130}{133.32} + 0.6 \cdot 0.8511 \\
&= 0.4 \cdot 0.9751 + 0.5107 \\
&= 0.3900 + 0.5107 \\
&= 0.9007
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Recursive Update for $t = 6$ (Q2 of Year 2, $Y_6 = 150$)

Using $S_2 = 1.0213$, $L_5 = 133.32$, $T_5 = 9.16$:

**Level:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_6 &= 0.3 \cdot \frac{150}{1.0213} + 0.7 \cdot (133.32 + 9.16) \\
&= 0.3 \cdot 146.87 + 0.7 \cdot 142.48 \\
&= 44.06 + 99.74 \\
&= 143.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trend:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_6 &= 0.2 \cdot (143.80 - 133.32) + 0.8 \cdot 9.16 \\
&= 0.2 \cdot 10.48 + 7.33 \\
&= 2.10 + 7.33 \\
&= 9.43
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seasonality:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_6 &= 0.4 \cdot \frac{150}{143.80} + 0.6 \cdot 1.0213 \\
&= 0.4 \cdot 1.0431 + 0.6128 \\
&= 0.4172 + 0.6128 \\
&= 1.0300
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Continue Recursively for $t = 7$ to $t = 12$

We present the **compiled results table** to save space (this is what KTU examiners expect after you show 2-3 steps):

| $t$ | Period | $Y_t$ | $L_t$ | $T_t$ | $S_t$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 5 | Y2-Q1 | 130 | 133.32 | 9.16 | 0.9007 |
| 6 | Y2-Q2 | 150 | 143.80 | 9.43 | 1.0300 |
| 7 | Y2-Q3 | 170 | 154.22 | 9.62 | 1.1738 |
| 8 | Y2-Q4 | 140 | 164.85 | 9.85 | 0.8982 |
| 9 | Y3-Q1 | 160 | 175.43 | 10.06 | 0.9723 |
| 10 | Y3-Q2 | 180 | 185.91 | 10.25 | 1.0229 |
| 11 | Y3-Q3 | 200 | 196.55 | 10.46 | 1.0758 |
| 12 | Y3-Q4 | 170 | 207.15 | 10.66 | 0.8724 |

> [!NOTE]
> Students are **not expected to reproduce all 8 rows** in the exam. Show **2-3 rows in full detail**, then state "and continuing similarly..." and present the final values at $t = 12$.

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Compute Forecast for $t = 13$ (Q1 of Year 4)

We need to forecast $m = 1$ period ahead from $t = 12$. The seasonal index for Q1 of the next cycle is $S_{13-4+1} = S_{10}$, but $S_{10}$ belongs to Y3-Q2. **Wait — careful!** For Q1, the corresponding historical seasonal index is $S_1$ from the *previous* season. Let us clarify using the formula:

$$F_{13} = (L_{12} + 1 \cdot T_{12}) \cdot S_{12 - 4 + 1} = (L_{12} + T_{12}) \cdot S_9$$

$S_9 = 0.9723$ (the most recently updated Q1 seasonal index, from $t = 9$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{13} &= (207.15 + 10.66) \cdot 0.9723 \\
&= 217.81 \cdot 0.9723 \\
&= 211.79 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.7 Final Answer

> **Forecasted demand for Quarter 1 of Year 4 = 211.79 units (≈ 212 units).**

---

## 3.3 Accuracy Metrics Used to Validate the Model

Once the model has generated in-sample forecasts $\hat{Y}_t$ for $t = 5, 6, \ldots, 12$, the **forecast error** is $e_t = Y_t - \hat{Y}_t$. Common KTU-tested metrics:

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MAD** (Mean Absolute Deviation) | $\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n} \sum \vert e_t \vert$ | Average magnitude of error in original units |
| **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum e_t^2$ | Penalizes large errors heavily |
| **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{Y_t} \times 100$ | Scale-independent percentage error |
| **Tracking Signal** | $\text{TS} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$ | Detects systematic bias in forecasts |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always compute and report **at least one error metric** when applying Winter's model in a numerical problem. MAPE is the most examiner-friendly because it is dimensionless.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of Winter's Complete Model

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **complete lifecycle** of the model — from data ingestion, through initialization, recursive update, and final forecast generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Historical Data Y1 to Yn"] --> B["Identify Seasonal Period s"]
    B --> C{"Sufficient Data Check n greater than or equal to 2s"}
    C -- No --> D["Abort Model Insufficient Data"]
    C -- Yes --> E["Initialize Level Ls Equal to Mean of First Season"]
    E --> F["Initialize Trend Ts from Cross Seasonal Slopes"]
    F --> G["Initialize Seasonal Indices Si Equal to Yi by Ls"]
    G --> H["Normalize Seasonal Indices Sum Equal to s"]
    H --> I["Set Smoothing Constants Alpha Beta Gamma"]
    I --> J["Loop Start t from s+1 to n"]
    J --> K["Compute Lt using Alpha Yt and St minus s"]
    K --> L["Compute Tt using Beta Lt minus L t minus 1"]
    L --> M["Compute St using Gamma Yt by Lt"]
    M --> N{"t equals n Last Period"}
    N -- No --> J
    N -- Yes --> O["Final Values Ln Tn Sn Available"]
    O --> P["User Specifies Forecast Horizon m"]
    P --> Q["Compute Forecast Ft plus m using Ln Tn Sn minus s plus m"]
    Q --> R["Output Forecasted Demand"]
    R --> S["Compute Accuracy Metrics MAD MAPE MSE"]
    S --> T{"Forecast Acceptable Error within Tolerance"}
    T -- No --> U["Re tune Alpha Beta Gamma Grid Search"]
    U --> I
    T -- Yes --> V["Deploy Model for Production Planning"]
```

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram of the Three Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        A1["Raw Time Series Yt"]
    end
    subgraph DECOMP["Decomposition Engine"]
        B1["Level Component Lt"]
        B2["Trend Component Tt"]
        B3["Seasonal Component St"]
    end
    subgraph SMOOTHING["Smoothing Constants"]
        C1["Alpha Weight for Level"]
        C2["Beta Weight for Trend"]
        C3["Gamma Weight for Seasonality"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Forecast Engine"]
        D1["Future Forecast Ft plus m"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    C1 --> B1
    C2 --> B2
    C3 --> B3
    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> D1
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Forecasting Model to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Time Series Data Available"] --> B{"Trend Present"}
    B -- No --> C{"Seasonality Present"]
    B -- Yes --> D{"Seasonality Present"}
    C -- No --> E["Simple Exponential Smoothing"]
    C -- Yes --> F["Holt Winters No Trend Model"]
    D -- No --> G["Holt Linear Trend Model"]
    D -- Yes --> H{"Seasonality Type"}
    H -- Multiplicative --> I["Winter Complete Model"]
    H -- Additive --> J["Additive Triple Exponential Smoothing"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The above decision tree is **directly asked in viva questions**. Students should be able to identify the correct model given the characteristics of a time series in under 30 seconds.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: List the three smoothing parameters used in Winter's Complete Model and state the range of values they can take. Why is the range restricted to this interval?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three smoothing parameters are:

1. **$\alpha$ (Alpha)** — Level smoothing constant
2. **$\beta$ (Beta)** — Trend smoothing constant
3. **$\gamma$ (Gamma)** — Seasonal smoothing constant

All three parameters take values in the **closed interval $[0, 1]$**, i.e., $0 \leq \alpha, \beta, \gamma \leq 1$.

**Reason for the range restriction:**
- The model is a **convex weighted average** of past observations. A convex combination requires all weights to be non-negative and sum to 1. Hence each parameter is restricted to $[0, 1]$ to ensure that the resulting update equations produce stable, non-divergent, and physically meaningful level/trend/seasonal estimates.
- If any parameter exceeds 1, the model would assign **negative weight** to past observations, leading to **explosive (divergent) forecasts**.
- If any parameter equals 0, the corresponding component (level, trend, or seasonality) is **frozen** at its initial value and never updated.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between the three components — Level, Trend, and Seasonality — in Winter's Complete Model. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Component | Definition | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Level ($L_t$)** | The baseline or average value of the series after removing trend and seasonal effects | The average daily electricity consumption of a Kerala household is around 8 kWh |
| **Trend ($T_t$)** | The long-term increase or decrease in the series (its slope) | Year-on-year growth of 5% in mobile phone sales in India |
| **Seasonality ($S_t$)** | A regular, predictable, and repeating pattern within each year/cycle | Ice cream sales peak every summer (April–May) and dip during monsoon (July–August) |

**Key Distinction:** The level captures the **current magnitude**, the trend captures the **direction of change**, and seasonality captures the **recurring cyclical fluctuation** within each period.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In numerical problems, students frequently:
> 1. Forget to **deseasonalize** the input $Y_t$ in the level equation (must use $Y_t / S_{t-s}$, NOT $Y_t$ alone).
> 2. Use the **wrong seasonal index** in the forecast equation (must use $S_{t-s+m}$, NOT $S_{t-1}$).
> 3. Skip the **initialization step** and directly apply the recursive equations from $t = 1$, which is mathematically invalid.
> 4. Forget to verify that the seasonal indices sum to $s$ (no normalization check).
> 5. Round off too aggressively (keep at least 4 decimal places during intermediate steps).
> 
> **Marks lost due to these errors: typically 4–7 out of 14.**

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** State and explain the four equations of **Winter's Complete Model** for time series forecasting. Clearly define all the terms used. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A manufacturing firm has recorded the following quarterly sales (in ₹ lakhs) for 2 years:

| Quarter | Year 1 | Year 2 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Q1 | 100 | 120 |
| Q2 | 140 | 160 |
| Q3 | 160 | 180 |
| Q4 | 120 | 140 |

Using Winter's Complete Model with $\alpha = 0.2$, $\beta = 0.3$, $\gamma = 0.4$, compute the forecast for **Q1 of Year 3**. Take $s = 4$. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The four equations of Winter's Complete Model are:

**Equation 1 — Level Update:**
$$L_t = \alpha \cdot \frac{Y_t}{S_{t-s}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$

Where:
- $L_t$ = Smoothed level at time $t$
- $Y_t$ = Actual observation at time $t$
- $S_{t-s}$ = Seasonal index from one cycle ago
- $L_{t-1}, T_{t-1}$ = Previous level and trend
- $\alpha$ = Level smoothing constant

**Equation 2 — Trend Update:**
$$T_t = \beta \cdot (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \cdot T_{t-1}$$

Where $\beta$ = Trend smoothing constant.

**Equation 3 — Seasonality Update:**
$$S_t = \gamma \cdot \frac{Y_t}{L_t} + (1 - \gamma) \cdot S_{t-s}$$

Where $\gamma$ = Seasonal smoothing constant.

**Equation 4 — Forecast (m periods ahead):**
$$F_{t+m} = (L_t + m \cdot T_t) \cdot S_{t-s+m}$$

**Explanation:** The level equation deseasonalizes the current data using the seasonal index from one cycle ago and blends it with the model's prior expectation. The trend equation updates the slope based on the change in level. The seasonal equation captures the cyclical effect by comparing the current observation to the current level. Finally, the forecast equation projects the trend-adjusted level forward and reapplies the appropriate seasonal factor.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Initialize at $t = 4$ (end of Year 1)**

$$L_4 = \frac{100 + 140 + 160 + 120}{4} = \frac{520}{4} = 130$$

$$T_4 = \frac{1}{4} \left( \frac{120-100}{4} + \frac{160-140}{4} + \frac{180-160}{4} + \frac{140-120}{4} \right)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 &= \frac{1}{4} (5 + 5 + 5 + 5) = \frac{20}{4} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Seasonal Indices:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \frac{100}{130} = 0.7692 \\
S_2 &= \frac{140}{130} = 1.0769 \\
S_3 &= \frac{160}{130} = 1.2308 \\
S_4 &= \frac{120}{130} = 0.9231
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $0.7692 + 1.0769 + 1.2308 + 0.9231 = 4.0000$ ✓

**Step 2: Update for $t = 5$ ($Y_5 = 120$, Q1 of Year 2)**

$$L_5 = 0.2 \cdot \frac{120}{0.7692} + 0.8 \cdot (130 + 5) = 0.2 \cdot 156.00 + 108.00 = 31.20 + 108.00 = 139.20$$

$$T_5 = 0.3 \cdot (139.20 - 130) + 0.7 \cdot 5 = 0.3 \cdot 9.20 + 3.50 = 2.76 + 3.50 = 6.26$$

$$S_5 = 0.4 \cdot \frac{120}{139.20} + 0.6 \cdot 0.7692 = 0.4 \cdot 0.8621 + 0.4615 = 0.3448 + 0.4615 = 0.8063$$

**Step 3: Update for $t = 6, 7, 8$ (continue similarly — present compiled table):**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $L_t$ | $T_t$ | $S_t$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 5 | 120 | 139.20 | 6.26 | 0.8063 |
| 6 | 160 | 147.40 | 6.49 | 1.0566 |
| 7 | 180 | 156.10 | 6.74 | 1.2153 |
| 8 | 140 | 164.50 | 6.94 | 0.9218 |

**Step 4: Forecast for $t = 9$ ($m = 1$, Q1 of Year 3)**

$$F_9 = (L_8 + 1 \cdot T_8) \cdot S_{8-4+1} = (164.50 + 6.94) \cdot S_5$$

$$F_9 = 171.44 \cdot 0.8063 = 138.24 \text{ lakhs}$$

> **Final Answer:** Forecasted sales for Q1 of Year 3 = **₹ 138.24 lakhs (≈ ₹ 138 lakhs)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the significance of the three smoothing constants $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ in Winter's Complete Model. Discuss what happens when each constant is set to 0 or 1. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The annual sales of a company for the past 4 years (in ₹ crores) are: 50, 55, 65, 80. An analyst applies Winter's Model with seasonal period $s = 1$. Comment on the validity of this application. What alternative model would you recommend? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Significance of the Three Smoothing Constants:**

| Constant | Component Controlled | High Value Effect | Low Value Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha$ | Level | Reacts quickly to new data; smoothes level estimate using mostly the latest observation | Reacts slowly; relies heavily on past level estimates |
| $\beta$ | Trend | Quickly adapts to trend reversals (e.g., sudden growth or decline) | Assumes trend is stable; slow to detect turning points |
| $\gamma$ | Seasonality | Quickly incorporates new seasonal observations (responsive to pattern changes) | Maintains historical seasonal pattern; slow to detect shifts |

**Boundary Behavior Analysis:**

- **$\alpha = 0$:** The level is **never updated** based on new data. $L_t = L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}$ always. The model becomes purely trend-driven.
- **$\alpha = 1$:** The level is set to $\frac{Y_t}{S_{t-s}}$ exactly. The model **completely forgets** past level estimates after every period.
- **$\beta = 0$:** The trend is **frozen** at $T_0$ forever. No learning of trend changes.
- **$\beta = 1$:** The trend becomes $(L_t - L_{t-1})$ — **pure random walk in trend**, ignoring history.
- **$\gamma = 0$:** The seasonal indices are **frozen** at initial values. The model never adapts to evolving seasonality.
- **$\gamma = 1$:** Seasonal indices are set to $\frac{Y_t}{L_t}$ each period; **forgets historical seasonality**.

> A balanced choice (e.g., $\alpha = 0.2, \beta = 0.3, \gamma = 0.4$) provides a **stable yet responsive** model.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Validity Analysis:**

The data is annual: 50, 55, 65, 80 (₹ crores). The seasonal period is given as $s = 1$.

- A seasonal period of $s = 1$ means the model assumes **one period per seasonal cycle** — i.e., **no seasonality at all** in the data.
- When $s = 1$, the seasonal index $S_{t-1} = S_t$ is always 1 (after normalization), and the seasonal smoothing equation becomes a tautology.
- Effectively, **Winter's Complete Model collapses to Holt's Linear Trend Model** in this case.

**Verdict:** The application is **mathematically valid but conceptually unnecessary**. Since annual data typically does not have intra-year seasonality (unless sub-annual data is aggregated), using the full Winter's Model adds computational complexity without any benefit.

**Recommended Alternative Model:**

- Use **Holt's Linear Exponential Smoothing Model** (double exponential smoothing), which captures level and trend but not seasonality.
- Equations for Holt's Model:

$$L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$

$$T_t = \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1}$$

$$F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$$

**Forecasting the next 2 years using Holt's Model** (with $\alpha = 0.5, \beta = 0.3$ for example):
- Initialization: $L_1 = 50$, $T_1 = 5$ (using the average annual increment).
- Continue recursively and produce $F_5, F_6$.

> **Conclusion:** Since $s = 1$ implies no seasonality, the correct model choice is **Holt's Linear Trend Model**, not Winter's Complete Model. The latter is reserved for data with $s \geq 2$ and a clearly observable seasonal pattern.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE**

- ☐ Winter's Complete Model = **Triple Exponential Smoothing** (Level + Trend + Seasonality).
- ☐ **Three smoothing constants**: $\alpha$ (level), $\beta$ (trend), $\gamma$ (seasonal) — all in $[0, 1]$.
- ☐ **Level equation** uses **deseasonalized** current observation: $Y_t / S_{t-s}$.
- ☐ **Forecast equation** uses **future seasonal index**: $S_{t-s+m}$ (NOT $S_{t-1}$ or $S_t$).
- ☐ Seasonal indices must **sum to $s$** (multiplicative model normalization).
- ☐ The model is **multiplicative** in its seasonal component — appropriate when seasonal swing grows with the level.
- ☐ Requires **at least 2 full seasonal cycles** of historical data for reliable initialization.
- ☐ Initialization formulas: $L_s$ = mean of first season; $T_s$ = average of cross-seasonal slopes; $S_i = Y_i / L_s$.
- ☐ Optimal $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are found by **grid search minimizing MSE/MAD/MAPE** — no closed-form solution exists.
- ☐ If $s = 1$, the model **degenerates** into Holt's Linear Trend Model.
- ☐ Common KTU pitfalls: forgetting to deseasonalize $Y_t$, using the wrong seasonal index in forecasting, and skipping initialization.
- ☐ Related variants: **Holt's Model** (no seasonality, double smoothing), **Simple Exponential Smoothing** (no trend, no seasonality), **Damped Trend Model** (trend decays over forecast horizon).
- ☐ Real-world applications: **KSEB electricity demand**, **Kerala tourism bookings**, **Onam/Vishu retail demand**, **airline ticket sales**, **aggregate production planning**.

---

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Winter's Complete Model**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Forecast Error and Comparison of Forecasting Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Forecast Error and Comparison of Forecasting Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Forecast Error — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecast Error ($e_t$)** is defined as the algebraic difference between the **Actual Demand** observed in a given period and the **Forecasted Demand** generated for that period by the chosen forecasting model.
>
> $$e_t = A_t - F_t$$
>
> Where $A_t$ = Actual demand in period $t$, and $F_t$ = Forecasted demand for period $t$.

A *positive error* indicates **under-forecasting** (actual demand exceeded the forecast), while a *negative error* indicates **over-forecasting**. The systematic study of these errors is termed **Forecast Error Analysis**, and it is the cornerstone of validating, monitoring, and refining any forecasting system used in operations management.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Weatherman's Track Record:** Imagine a weather forecaster predicts 30°C every day for a week, but the actual temperatures swing between 25°C and 35°C. The *forecast errors* are the differences each day. If we average the *absolute* differences, we get the **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** — a single number telling us "on average, the forecaster is off by X degrees." Squaring those differences (MSE) punishes big misses more harshly, just like a teacher deducts more marks for a wildly wrong answer than for a near-miss. The **Tracking Signal** acts like a referee: if errors keep piling up in the *same direction* (always too high or always too low), it blows the whistle and tells the manager "your forecaster is biased — recalibrate!"

This is the same logic used in production planning: if a forecasting model keeps under-predicting demand, the factory will perpetually run out of stock, inflate backorders, and lose customer goodwill.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for High Recall)

- **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** — average magnitude of errors
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — penalizes large errors quadratically
- **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** — scale-independent % error
- **Mean Error (ME) / Bias** — detects systematic directional drift
- **Cumulative Forecast Error (CFE)** — running sum of errors
- **Tracking Signal (TS)** — CFE ÷ MAD, used as a control statistic

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Highlight:** MAPE is the *most frequently asked* scale-free metric in KTU ESE papers, because it allows comparison across products with different units (litres, kgs, units). Always express it as a **percentage**.

---

### 1.4 Comparison of Forecasting Methods — Definition

Comparison of forecasting methods is the **systematic, multi-criteria evaluation** of qualitative and quantitative forecasting techniques against a set of operational, statistical, and economic benchmarks (accuracy, cost, data requirements, time horizon, and complexity) to select the most appropriate model for a given decision context.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Forecast Error Analysis

The theoretical flow of forecast error analysis follows a logical cascade:

- **Step 1 — Compute Per-Period Error:** Calculate $e_t = A_t - F_t$ for every historical period where both actual and forecast values exist.
- **Step 2 — Compute Aggregate Magnitude Metrics:** Convert raw errors into MAD, MSE, RMSE, or MAPE to get a single "score" of model accuracy.
- **Step 3 — Compute Bias Metrics:** Compute Mean Error (ME) and Cumulative Forecast Error (CFE) to detect *systematic* (non-random) drift.
- **Step 4 — Compute Control Statistics:** Tracking Signal $\text{TS} = \text{CFE} / \text{MAD}$. A TS outside $\pm 4$ (or $\pm 3$ in stricter setups) signals that the model is out of control.
- **Step 5 — Diagnostic Decision:** If TS is within bounds, continue using the model. If not, retune $\alpha$ (smoothing constant), switch the model, or investigate the demand environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A low MAD alone is *insufficient*. A model can have a low MAD but a high CFE (errors cancelling out). The **Tracking Signal** is what catches this hidden bias.

### 2.2 Why Each Error Metric Exists — The "Why" Behind the Math

| Metric | Purpose | Why Managers Use It |
|---|---|---|
| **MAD** | Average absolute miss | Easy to communicate in same units as demand |
| **MSE / RMSE** | Penalize large errors | Inventory models (e.g., safety stock $z \cdot \sigma$) often use RMSE |
| **MAPE** | Relative % error | Compare accuracy across SKUs of different scales |
| **ME (Bias)** | Detects over/under-forecasting | If ME $\neq 0$, model is systematically wrong |
| **CFE** | Tracks accumulated bias | Useful for control charts |
| **Tracking Signal** | Combines CFE + MAD | A single decision threshold for model health |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula the KTU examiner expects you to reproduce. Note: Vertical bars for absolute value are written as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Metric | Formula | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forecast Error | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | Same as demand | Per-period analysis |
| 2 | MAD | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert}{n}$ | Same as demand | Magnitude of typical miss |
| 3 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2}{n}$ | Squared units | Penalizes large errors |
| 4 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same as demand | Comparable to MAD |
| 5 | MAPE | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t}\times 100$ | **Percentage** | Cross-product comparison |
| 6 | Mean Error (Bias) | $\text{ME} = \dfrac{\sum e_t}{n}$ | Same as demand | Detects directional bias |
| 7 | CFE | $\text{CFE} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t$ | Same as demand | Cumulative drift |
| 8 | Tracking Signal | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{\text{CFE}}{\text{MAD}}$ | Dimensionless | Control limit $\pm 3$ to $\pm 4$ |
| 9 | Simple Exp. Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ | Same as demand | Stable demand, no trend |
| 10 | Trend-Adjusted ES (Holt's) | $L_t = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})$ ; $T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$ | Same as demand | Linear trend present |

**Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
- $\alpha \in (0, 1)$. $\alpha \to 1 \Rightarrow$ forecast reacts strongly to recent demand. $\alpha \to 0 \Rightarrow$ forecast is highly stable.
- A valid MAPE requires $A_t \neq 0$ for all $t$.
- Tracking Signal control limits (KTU standard): $\pm 4$ MAD units (4-sigma equivalent for low-volume data); $\pm 3$ for tighter control.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In manufacturing and supply chains, **forecast error metrics feed directly into safety stock formulas**:

$$\text{Safety Stock} = z \cdot \sigma_{\text{forecast error}}$$

Here $\sigma$ is often approximated by **MAD** (using $\sigma \approx 1.25 \cdot \text{MAD}$ for a normal distribution) or by **RMSE**. Thus, *halving your MAD can directly halve the safety inventory*, freeing working capital — a direct P&L impact.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive Error Analysis

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU ESE Style):**

> A manufacturing firm has recorded the following actual sales and corresponding forecasts generated by a moving-average model for the last 6 months. Compute **MAD, MSE, MAPE, ME, CFE, and Tracking Signal**. Comment on the model's health.

| Period ($t$) | Actual ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 100 |
| 2 | 95  | 100 |
| 3 | 105 | 98  |
| 4 | 110 | 100 |
| 5 | 100 | 104 |
| 6 | 115 | 102 |

---

#### Step 1 — Per-Period Error Computation

Using $e_t = A_t - F_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= 100 - 100 = 0 \\
e_2 &= 95 - 100 = -5 \\
e_3 &= 105 - 98 = +7 \\
e_4 &= 110 - 100 = +10 \\
e_5 &= 100 - 104 = -4 \\
e_6 &= 115 - 102 = +13
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Subtracting forecast from actual; sign indicates over/under-forecasting.

---

#### Step 2 — Sum of Errors, Sum of Absolute Errors, Sum of Squared Errors

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum e_t &= 0 + (-5) + 7 + 10 + (-4) + 13 = +21 \\
\sum \vert e_t \vert &= 0 + 5 + 7 + 10 + 4 + 13 = 39 \\
\sum e_t^2 &= 0^2 + 5^2 + 7^2 + 10^2 + 4^2 + 13^2 \\
&= 0 + 25 + 49 + 100 + 16 + 169 = 359
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $5^2 = 25$ (square the absolute magnitude), etc. The squared terms are needed for MSE.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute MAD

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{39}{6} = 6.5 \text{ units}
$$

> *Interpretation:* On average, the forecast misses actual demand by **6.5 units**.

---

#### Step 4 — Compute MSE and RMSE

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{\sum e_t^2}{n} = \frac{359}{6} \approx 59.83 \text{ (units)}^2
$$

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{59.83} \approx 7.74 \text{ units}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $\sqrt{59.83} \approx 7.7355$, rounded to two decimal places. RMSE is in the same unit as demand, allowing direct comparison with MAD.

---

#### Step 5 — Compute MAPE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAPE} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1}{6}\left(\frac{0}{100} + \frac{5}{95} + \frac{7}{105} + \frac{10}{110} + \frac{4}{100} + \frac{13}{115}\right)\times 100 \\
&= \frac{1}{6}\left(0 + 0.05263 + 0.06667 + 0.09091 + 0.04000 + 0.11304\right)\times 100 \\
&= \frac{1}{6}(0.36325)\times 100 \\
&= 0.06054 \times 100 \approx 6.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The forecast is, on average, off by **6.05%** of actual demand — generally considered a *good* forecast.

---

#### Step 6 — Compute ME (Bias) and CFE

$$
\text{ME} = \frac{\sum e_t}{n} = \frac{21}{6} = +3.5 \text{ units}
$$

> *Interpretation:* A **positive ME** indicates the model systematically *under-forecasts* — actual demand is on average 3.5 units higher than predicted.

$$
\text{CFE} = \sum e_t = +21 \text{ units}
$$

> *Interpretation:* Errors are accumulating in the positive direction → model is biased.

---

#### Step 7 — Compute Tracking Signal

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{\text{CFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{21}{6.5} \approx 3.23
$$

> *Interpretation:* TS is approaching the **+4** upper control limit. While still within bounds, the model is drifting and should be retuned (e.g., re-estimated $\alpha$ in exponential smoothing, or switch to trend-adjusted Holt's method).

---

#### Step 8 — Exponential Smoothing Forecast (for Period 7)

Using $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ with $\alpha = 0.3$ and $F_1 = 100$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= 0.3(100) + 0.7(100) = 30 + 70 = 100 \\
F_3 &= 0.3(95) + 0.7(100) = 28.5 + 70 = 98.5 \\
F_4 &= 0.3(105) + 0.7(98.5) = 31.5 + 68.95 = 100.45 \\
F_5 &= 0.3(110) + 0.7(100.45) = 33 + 70.315 = 103.315 \\
F_6 &= 0.3(100) + 0.7(103.315) = 30 + 72.3205 = 102.3205 \\
F_7 &= 0.3(115) + 0.7(102.3205) = 34.5 + 71.62435 = 106.12435 \approx 106.12
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The smoothed forecast for Period 7 is **~106 units**, which is more reasonable than the raw moving-average output.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Forecasting Methods

| Criterion | Qualitative | Naïve | Moving Average | Exp. Smoothing | Linear Regression (Trend) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Required** | Expert opinion | 1 period | $n$ periods | $\alpha$ + history | Long history |
| **Cost** | High | Negligible | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Accuracy (Stable Demand)** | Moderate | Low | Moderate | **High** | Moderate |
| **Accuracy (Trend Demand)** | Moderate | Very Low | Low | Low (unless Holt) | **High** |
| **Computational Effort** | High | Trivial | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Time Horizon** | Long / Strategic | Short | Short | Short | Medium |
