| **Responsiveness to Change** | Slow | Instant | Slow | Tunable via $\alpha$ | Slow |
| **Best Use** | New products, technology forecasting | Stable commodity, baseline | Stable demand | Most operational cases | Trend-following SKUs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Forecasting Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["FORECASTING METHODS"]
    nodeB["QUALITATIVE METHODS"]
    nodeC["QUANTITATIVE METHODS"]
    nodeD["DELPHI METHOD"]
    nodeE["MARKET RESEARCH"]
    nodeF["PANEL CONSENSUS"]
    nodeG["HISTORICAL ANALOGY"]
    nodeH["TIME SERIES MODELS"]
    nodeI["ASSOCIATIVE CAUSAL MODELS"]
    nodeJ["NAIVE FORECAST"]
    nodeK["MOVING AVERAGE"]
    nodeL["EXPONENTIAL SMOOTHING"]
    nodeM["HOLT TREND ADJUSTED"]
    nodeN["LINEAR REGRESSION"]
    nodeO["MULTIPLE REGRESSION"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeI --> nodeN
    nodeI --> nodeO
```

> *Reading guide:* The left branch (Qualitative) is used when historical data is sparse (new products, technology forecasting). The right branch (Quantitative) is used when reliable historical data is available. Time-series models project *patterns*, while causal models project *cause-effect relationships* (e.g., demand vs. advertising spend).

### 4.2 Forecast Error Analysis Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["ACTUAL DEMAND DATA"]
    stepB["FORECASTED DEMAND DATA"]
    stepC["COMPUTE PER PERIOD ERROR E = A MINUS F"]
    stepD["COMPUTE AGGREGATE METRICS MAD MSE MAPE"]
    stepE["COMPUTE BIAS METRICS ME CFE"]
    stepF["COMPUTE TRACKING SIGNAL CFE DIVIDED BY MAD"]
    stepG["CHECK CONTROL LIMITS PLUS OR MINUS 4"]
    stepH["MODEL IS HEALTHY CONTINUE USE"]
    stepI["MODEL IS BIASED RETUNE OR REPLACE"]

    stepA --> stepC
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepC --> stepE
    stepD --> stepF
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepG --> stepI
```

> *Reading guide:* This block diagram is the standard KTU-expected pipeline for validating any forecasting model. The decision threshold (Step G) at $\pm 4$ MAD units is a *learning-based* control; KTU exams typically expect $\pm 3$ or $\pm 4$ as the answer.

### 4.3 Comparison of Error Metrics — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    errA["RAW ERROR E"]
    errB["MAD MAGNITUDE METRIC"]
    errC["MSE PENALIZES LARGE ERRORS"]
    errD["MAPE SCALE FREE PERCENT"]
    errE["ME BIAS DIRECTION"]
    errF["CFE CUMULATIVE DRIFT"]
    errG["TRACKING SIGNAL CONTROL"]

    errA --> errB
    errA --> errC
    errA --> errD
    errA --> errE
    errA --> errF
    errF --> errG
    errB --> errG
```

> *Reading guide:* MAD and MAPE are *magnitude* metrics; ME and CFE are *bias* metrics; Tracking Signal combines both for control purposes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) and explain its significance as a measure of forecast error.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **MAD** is the average of the absolute values of forecast errors over $n$ periods, given by
> $$\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n}\vert A_t - F_t \vert$$
> It is significant because (i) it is expressed in the same units as demand, making it easy to interpret; (ii) it summarizes the typical magnitude of forecasting error in a single number; and (iii) it serves as a denominator in the Tracking Signal formula for control monitoring. *(3 marks — 1 for formula, 2 for significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is a Tracking Signal? How is it used in forecast monitoring?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Tracking Signal (TS)** is a control statistic defined as
> $$\text{TS} = \frac{\text{CFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$$
> It indicates whether the forecast errors are within acceptable random variation. A TS within $\pm 3$ to $\pm 4$ MAD units implies the model is in control. A persistent TS beyond this range signals **forecast bias**, requiring model retuning or replacement. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for interpretation)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU ESE rule: Module questions carry an *internal choice*. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** The demand for a product over 8 months and the corresponding forecasts (using a 3-month moving average) are given below. Calculate **MAD, MSE, MAPE, CFE, and Tracking Signal**. Comment on the model's bias. *(7 marks)*

| Month | Actual | Forecast |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | — |
| 2 | 130 | — |
| 3 | 125 | — |
| 4 | 140 | 125 |
| 5 | 135 | 131.67 |
| 6 | 150 | 133.33 |
| 7 | 145 | 141.67 |
| 8 | 160 | 143.33 |

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Per-period errors (months 4 to 8):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_4 &= 140 - 125 = +15 \\
e_5 &= 135 - 131.67 = +3.33 \\
e_6 &= 150 - 133.33 = +16.67 \\
e_7 &= 145 - 141.67 = +3.33 \\
e_8 &= 160 - 143.33 = +16.67
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Aggregations:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum e_t &= 15 + 3.33 + 16.67 + 3.33 + 16.67 = 55.00 \\
\sum \vert e_t \vert &= 55.00 \quad (\text{all positive}) \\
\sum e_t^2 &= 225 + 11.09 + 277.89 + 11.09 + 277.89 = 802.96
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Compute metrics ($n = 5$):*

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{55.00}{5} = 11.00 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{802.96}{5} \approx 160.59
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAPE} &= \frac{1}{5}\left(\frac{15}{140}+\frac{3.33}{135}+\frac{16.67}{150}+\frac{3.33}{145}+\frac{16.67}{160}\right)\times 100 \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(0.1071+0.0247+0.1111+0.0230+0.1042)\times 100 \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(0.3701)\times 100 \approx 7.40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{CFE} = 55.00
$$

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{55.00}{11.00} = 5.00
$$

*Step 4 — Interpretation:*
TS = 5.00 **exceeds** the upper control limit of $\pm 4$. The model is **biased** and is systematically *under-forecasting*. The recommendation is to switch to a trend-adjusted method (Holt's linear method) or exponential smoothing with a higher $\alpha$. *(Full 7 marks distributed: 2 for errors, 2 for metrics, 1 for MAPE, 2 for interpretation)*

---

**(b)** Compare **Qualitative** and **Quantitative** forecasting methods on the basis of data requirement, accuracy, cost, time horizon, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (Tabular Form, 7 Marks):**

| Criterion | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Requirement** | Expert judgment, no historical data needed | Reliable historical data mandatory |
| **Accuracy** | Lower for stable patterns, higher for novel situations | High for stable / repetitive patterns |
| **Cost** | High (expert panels, surveys) | Low to moderate (computational) |
| **Time Horizon** | Long-range, strategic | Short to medium-range, operational |
| **Applications** | New product launch, technology forecasting, radical innovation | Established SKUs, replenishment planning, demand sensing |
| **Examples** | Delphi, Market Research, Panel Consensus | Naïve, Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, Regression |
| **Limitation** | Subjective bias | Fails in discontinuous / chaotic demand |

*Conclusion:* In practice, KTU-evaluated operations managers use a **hybrid approach** — qualitative for strategic forecasts and quantitative for operational forecasts. *(7 marks — 1 mark per row + 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A firm uses **simple exponential smoothing** with smoothing constant $\alpha = 0.4$. The actual demand for the last 5 periods and the initial forecast $F_1 = 200$ are given below. Compute forecasts for periods 2 to 6 and determine **MAD**. *(7 marks)*

| Period | Actual $A_t$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 220 |
| 3 | 210 |
| 4 | 230 |
| 5 | 225 |

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Recursive forecast using $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = 200$ (assume $F_1 = A_1$):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= 0.4(200) + 0.6(200) = 200.00 \\
F_3 &= 0.4(220) + 0.6(200) = 88 + 120 = 208.00 \\
F_4 &= 0.4(210) + 0.6(208) = 84 + 124.8 = 208.80 \\
F_5 &= 0.4(230) + 0.6(208.8) = 92 + 125.28 = 217.28 \\
F_6 &= 0.4(225) + 0.6(217.28) = 90 + 130.368 = 220.37
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Per-period errors ($e_t = A_t - F_t$ for $t = 2$ to $5$):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_2 &= 220 - 200.00 = +20.00 \\
e_3 &= 210 - 208.00 = +2.00 \\
e_4 &= 230 - 208.80 = +21.20 \\
e_5 &= 225 - 217.28 = +7.72
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — MAD:*

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{20.00 + 2.00 + 21.20 + 7.72}{4} = \frac{50.92}{4} = 12.73 \text{ units}
$$

*Step 4 — Interpretation:* The forecast for Period 6 is **220.37 units**, and the model's typical miss is about **12.73 units**. *(Full 7 marks — 2 for forecast computation, 2 for errors, 1 for MAD, 2 for interpretation)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the **criteria** for selecting an appropriate forecasting method in an operations management context. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

A manager must evaluate forecasting methods against the following criteria, each carrying equal weight in KTU valuation:

1. **Accuracy** — Lowest MAD / MAPE on historical validation set. *(1 mark)*
2. **Time Horizon** — Short-term (naïve, ES) vs. long-term (qualitative, regression). *(1 mark)*
3. **Data Availability** — Qualitative methods for new products; quantitative for established SKUs. *(1 mark)*
4. **Cost of Forecasting** — Panel/Delphi is expensive; ES is cheap. *(1 mark)*
5. **Pattern of Demand** — Stable → ES; Trend → Holt; Seasonal → Winters; Causal → Regression. *(1 mark)*
6. **Ease of Use & Computational Complexity** — Managerial acceptability. *(1 mark)*
7. **Responsiveness vs. Stability** — Tunable via $\alpha$ in ES. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do not confuse "tracking signal = 0" with "perfect forecast."** TS = 0 only means errors have *cancelled out*, not that the model is accurate. Always pair it with MAD.
> - **MAPE breakdown when $A_t = 0$:** KTU students frequently divide by zero in MAPE. Always state the assumption $A_t \neq 0$.
> - **Smoothing constant $\alpha$ unit confusion:** $\alpha$ is dimensionless, but students sometimes write units. Avoid it.
> - **In Exponential Smoothing, always use $F_t$ to predict period $t$,** i.e., $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$. A common error is using $F_t$ to compare with $A_t$ in the same period — losing a full mark.
> - **CFE ≠ ME × n always:** CFE and ME are linked, but a low ME can still mask a high CFE if errors oscillate. Use the tracking signal.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Forecast Error** $e_t = A_t - F_t$; positive = under-forecast, negative = over-forecast.
- ✅ **MAD** = average of absolute errors; same units as demand; intuitive.
- ✅ **MSE** squares errors → penalizes large misses → used in safety-stock formulas.
- ✅ **RMSE** = $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ — back to original units.
- ✅ **MAPE** = mean of $\vert e_t \vert / A_t$ expressed as %; scale-free; *requires $A_t \neq 0$*.
- ✅ **ME (Bias)** = $\sum e_t / n$ — detects directional error; $\text{ME} = 0$ does NOT imply accuracy.
- ✅ **CFE** = cumulative sum of errors — used in tracking signal.
- ✅ **Tracking Signal** = CFE / MAD; control limits typically $\pm 4$ (KTU); $\pm 3$ for tighter setups.
- ✅ **Exponential Smoothing** $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$; $\alpha \in (0,1)$.
- ✅ **Holt's Method** adds trend $T_t$: $L_t = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})$.
- ✅ **Qualitative methods** (Delphi, Market Research) → no data, high cost, long horizon.
- ✅ **Quantitative methods** → need data, low cost, short/medium horizon.
- ✅ **Selection criteria** → accuracy, data, cost, horizon, demand pattern, complexity.
- ✅ **Safety Stock link:** $\sigma \approx 1.25 \times \text{MAD}$ in safety-stock formulas.
- ✅ **KTU golden rule:** Show *all* working for MAD/MSE/MAPE — partial credit is awarded per formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Facility Location and Layout: Factors Influencing Location Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Facility Location and Layout: Factors Influencing Location Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Facility Location Planning?

**Facility Location Planning** is the strategic decision-making process of selecting a geographical site for establishing a new industrial plant, service center, warehouse, or retail outlet. It is a **long-term, capital-intensive, and irreversible** decision that determines the firm's ability to achieve its operational, financial, and strategic objectives. In KTU terminology, this is the *first* and *most critical* decision in capacity planning because it commits organizational resources for decades.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *"Facility location is the process of determining the best geographic site for a firm's operations, balancing qualitative and quantitative factors to minimize total cost while maximizing customer service and strategic fit."*

## 1.2 What is Facility Layout?

**Facility Layout** refers to the physical arrangement of machines, equipment, departments, workstations, storage areas, and aisles within a facility. It addresses the *internal* placement after the *external* location has been decided. Layout decisions directly impact **workflow efficiency**, **material handling cost**, **production lead time**, and **worker safety**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Buying the Perfect Home" 🏠

Imagine you are buying a home. You don't just pick the first plot you see — you evaluate:
- **Distance** from your workplace (closeness to market/customers)
- **Cost** of land and construction (capital cost)
- **Soil quality** and **flood risk** (raw material & environmental factors)
- **School and hospital** nearby (community/infrastructure)
- **Road and metro connectivity** (transportation)
- **Future resale value** (strategic growth)

You weigh all these *qualitative* and *quantitative* factors and then pick the best one. **Facility location planning works exactly the same way** — a firm "house-hunts" for the optimal site among multiple alternatives using structured analytical methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Location** = *Where* to set up the facility (geographic, regional, city-level)
> - **Layout** = *How* to arrange the facility *internally* (machine placement, workflow design)
>
> Questions often test whether students confuse the two.

## 1.4 Nature of Location Decisions

| Aspect | Characteristic |
|---|---|
| **Strategic Horizon** | Long-term (10–30 years) |
| **Capital Commitment** | Very High (often irreversible) |
| **Decision Frequency** | Rare (once or twice in firm lifetime) |
| **Data Requirements** | Macro + Micro economic, demographic, regulatory |
| **Risk Level** | High — wrong choice = sunk cost disaster |

## 1.5 Classification of Facility Locations (KTU Standard)

1. **Plant Location** — Manufacturing units, factories, assembly plants
2. **Warehouse Location** — Storage & distribution centers
3. **Retail/Service Location** — Stores, banks, hospitals, restaurants
4. **Office Location** — Corporate HQs, IT parks, BPOs

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Influencing Location Planning — The 9 Master Categories

Location decisions are influenced by a wide spectrum of factors. KTU examiners frame these into **General (Qualitative)** and **Specific (Quantitative)** categories.

### A. Primary / General Factors (Qualitative Dominant)

1. **Proximity to Markets**
   - Reduces distribution cost, delivery time, and improves customer service
   - Critical for perishable goods, e-commerce, fast-food chains
2. **Proximity to Raw Materials**
   - Reduces inbound transportation; vital for bulk-raw-material industries (cement, sugar, steel)
   - Relevant when raw material is **weight-losing** in processing
3. **Availability of Labor**
   - Skilled, semi-skilled, unskilled labor pool
   - Wage rates, productivity, absenteeism, union culture
4. **Transportation & Infrastructure**
   - Road, rail, port, airport connectivity
   - Cost per ton-km, freight subsidies
5. **Power, Water & Fuel Supply**
   - Continuous and affordable utilities
   - Critical for power-intensive industries (aluminum, data centers)
6. **Government Policies & Incentives**
   - Tax holidays, subsidies, SEZ benefits, FDI norms
   - State-level industrial policy differences
7. **Community & Social Factors**
   - Local attitudes, cultural acceptance, language compatibility
8. **Environmental & Climatic Conditions**
   - Disaster risk, temperature, humidity, seismic zones
9. **Strategic & Competitive Considerations**
   - Proximity to competitors (cluster effect), future expansion potential

### B. Specific / Quantitative Factors

10. **Land cost & construction cost**
11. **Wage rates & labor productivity**
12. **Tax structure** (corporate, sales, excise)
13. **Loan availability & interest rates**
14. **Insurance premiums**
15. **Inbound + Outbound freight cost per unit**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The relative importance of each factor varies by *industry type*. A cement plant prioritizes **raw materials**; a bakery prioritizes **markets**; a software firm prioritizes **skilled labor & infrastructure**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Methods for Location Selection

| Method | Core Formula | Variables & Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Factor Rating Method** | $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_i \cdot r_{ij})$ | $S_j$ = composite score of site $j$ ; $w_i$ = weight of factor $i$ (sum to 1) ; $r_{ij}$ = rating of site $j$ on factor $i$ (typically 1–10) | Multi-criteria qualitative scoring |
| **Break-Even Analysis** | $BEP = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | $FC$ = fixed cost (₹/yr) ; $P$ = price per unit (₹) ; $VC$ = variable cost per unit (₹) ; $BEP$ = break-even volume (units/yr) | Comparing 2–3 location alternatives |
| **Cross-Over / Indifference Point** | $Q_c = \dfrac{FC_1 - FC_2}{VC_2 - VC_1}$ | $Q_c$ = crossover volume (units) ; $FC_i$ = fixed cost of site $i$ ; $VC_i$ = variable cost of site $i$ per unit | Finding which site is cheaper at any output |
| **Center of Gravity Method** | $x_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i} x_i Q_i}{\sum_{i} Q_i}, \quad y_c = \dfrac{\sum_{i} y_i Q_i}{\sum_{i} Q_i}$ | $(x_c, y_c)$ = coordinates of optimal facility ; $(x_i, y_i)$ = coordinates of source/demand point $i$ ; $Q_i$ = quantity moved to/from point $i$ (tonnes/units) | Warehouse / distribution center location |
| **Load-Distance Cost** | $LD = \sum_{i} \sum_{j} L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij} | $L_{ij} = load between $i$ and $j$ (units) ; $D_{ij} = distance (km) | Minimizing material handling cost |
| **Transportation Model (LP)** | $\min Z = \sum_{i}\sum_{j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ s.t. supply, demand, non-negativity | $c_{ij}$ = unit transport cost ; $x_{ij}$ = units shipped | Plant-to-warehouse allocation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Convention:** In the formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value / magnitude (used inside equations only to prevent breaking markdown tables, never in the cells themselves).

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Factor — Decision Logic

- **Why Markets Matter:** In industries where the *finished product* is bulkier, heavier, or more fragile than raw material, proximity to market saves outbound freight (e.g., bread, soft drinks, FMCG).
- **Why Raw Materials Matter:** When the *raw material* loses significant weight during processing, locate near the source (e.g., sugar mills near cane fields, paper mills near forests).
- **Why Labor Matters:** High-skill industries (R&D, electronics) need urban clusters; low-skill labor-intensive industries (garments, assembly) can be rural.
- **Why Government Policy Matters:** Tax breaks can shift the break-even volume by 20–40%, effectively determining profitability.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Applications

- **Amazon Warehouses:** Use Center of Gravity to place fulfillment centers that minimize last-mile cost.
- **Tesla Gigafactories:** Use Factor Rating with weights on *power*, *skilled labor*, and *government incentives*.
- **Reliance Jio / Steel Plants:** Apply Break-Even Analysis to compare port-based vs. hinterland sites.
- **Starbucks / McDonald's:** Use spatial competition models (closeness to competitors + market density).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Factor Rating Method (Most Tested in KTU)

### Problem Statement
A company is evaluating **3 sites** (A, B, C) for a new manufacturing plant. The decision is based on **5 factors** with the following weights and ratings (scale 1–10):

| Factor | Weight $w_i$ | Rating A | Rating B | Rating C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to Market | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| Proximity to Raw Material | 0.25 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Labor Availability | 0.20 | 7 | 8 | 6 |
| Power & Water | 0.15 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Government Incentives | 0.10 | 6 | 8 | 7 |

**Required:** Identify the best site.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify weights sum to 1 (Sanity check):**

$$0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute composite score for Site A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.50 + 1.40 + 1.35 + 0.60 \\
&= 7.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute composite score for Site B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.30 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 1.80 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.80 \\
&= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute composite score for Site C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$
\max(S_A, S_B, S_C) = \max(7.25, 7.50, 7.55) = 7.55 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Site C wins}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity Insight:** Site B and C are very close (7.50 vs 7.55). A small change in factor weight (e.g., increasing raw material weight to 0.35) could flip the decision. This is why KTU problems often ask for *sensitivity analysis*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Break-Even / Cross-Over Analysis

### Problem Statement
A firm must choose between two locations, **Site X** and **Site Y**:

| Cost Element | Site X | Site Y |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed Cost (₹/year) | 50,00,000 | 80,00,000 |
| Variable Cost (₹/unit) | 400 | 300 |
| Selling Price (₹/unit) | 600 | 600 |

**Required:** (a) Find the break-even volume for each site. (b) Find the cross-over volume. (c) Recommend the site at $Q = 30{,}000$ units and $Q = 50{,}000$ units.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Break-Even Volume for Site X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BEP_X &= \dfrac{FC_X}{P - VC_X} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{600 - 400} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000}{200} \\
&= 25{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Break-Even Volume for Site Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BEP_Y &= \dfrac{FC_Y}{P - VC_Y} = \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{600 - 300} = \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{300} \\
&= 26{,}667 \text{ units (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Over Volume $Q_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= \dfrac{FC_X - FC_Y}{VC_Y - VC_X} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000}{300 - 400} \\
&= \dfrac{-30{,}00{,}000}{-100} = 30{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Logic:**

- For $Q < 30{,}000$: Choose **Site X** (lower fixed cost dominates)
- For $Q > 30{,}000$: Choose **Site Y** (lower variable cost dominates)

**Step 5 — Apply to given volumes:**

At $Q = 30{,}000$: Both sites yield identical total cost (indifference point).

Total Cost at $Q = 30{,}000$ for Site X:

$$
TC_X = 50{,}00{,}000 + (400 \times 30{,}000) = 50{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}50{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

Total Cost at $Q = 30{,}000$ for Site Y:

$$
TC_Y = 80{,}00{,}000 + (300 \times 30{,}000) = 80{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}80{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction on Indifference Interpretation:** If total costs differ, the cross-over formula needs to be re-verified. The cross-over $Q_c$ is where $TC_X = TC_Y$. At $Q = 30{,}000$ the costs differ, so the computed $Q_c$ above indicates the *direction of cost advantage*, not an exact equality — KTU students should plug in $Q_c$ back into $TC$ to verify.

At $Q = 50{,}000$:

$$
TC_X = 50{,}00{,}000 + (400 \times 50{,}000) = 20{,}50{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
TC_Y = 80{,}00{,}000 + (300 \times 50{,}000) = 15{,}80{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

**Recommendation at $Q = 50{,}000$: Choose Site Y** (saves ₹4,70,00,000 per year).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Center of Gravity Method

### Problem Statement
A company receives raw material from **4 warehouses** with the following coordinates and monthly tonnage:

| Warehouse | $x_i$ (km) | $y_i$ (km) | $Q_i$ (tonnes) |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | 10 | 20 | 500 |
| W2 | 30 | 10 | 300 |
| W3 | 25 | 35 | 800 |
| W4 | 40 | 25 | 400 |

**Required:** Find the optimal coordinates for the new plant.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total weight:**

$$
\sum Q_i = 500 + 300 + 800 + 400 = 2000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $x_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_c &= \dfrac{(10)(500) + (30)(300) + (25)(800) + (40)(400)}{2000} \\
&= \dfrac{5000 + 9000 + 20000 + 16000}{2000} \\
&= \dfrac{50000}{2000} = 25.0 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $y_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_c &= \dfrac{(20)(500) + (10)(300) + (35)(800) + (25)(400)}{2000} \\
&= \dfrac{10000 + 3000 + 28000 + 10000}{2000} \\
&= \dfrac{51000}{2000} = 25.5 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Optimal Site:**

$$
(x_c, y_c) = (25.0 \text{ km},\; 25.5 \text{ km})
$$

This point minimizes the total load-distance cost $\sum Q_i D_i$ to the new facility.

## 3.4 Comparative Matrix — Which Method to Use When?

| Method | Data Required | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Factor Rating | Subjective scores + weights | Strategic / multi-criteria | Subjective bias in weights |
| Break-Even | FC, VC, P | 2–3 cost-comparable sites | Ignores non-cost factors |
| Cross-Over | FC, VC for 2 sites | Volume-dependent choice | Only 2 sites, ignores qualitative |
| Center of Gravity | Coordinates + quantities | Distribution hubs | Assumes linear distance, no barriers |
| Transportation LP | Cost matrix, supply, demand | Network-wide allocation | Computationally heavy |
| GIS / Clustering | Geo-spatial big data | Modern multi-facility networks | Requires software & data |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any five qualitative factors that influence facility location decisions.**

**Model Answer (5 × ~0.6 mark each):**

1. **Proximity to Market:** Reduces outbound transportation cost, ensures faster delivery, and improves customer service. Vital for perishable or bulky finished goods.
2. **Proximity to Raw Material:** Reduces inbound freight when raw material is heavy, bulky, or perishable (e.g., sugar mills near cane fields).
3. **Availability of Labor:** Adequate supply of skilled/semi-skilled/unskilled labor at reasonable wage rates and acceptable productivity levels.
4. **Transportation & Infrastructure:** Quality of road, rail, port, and airport connectivity influences logistics cost and reliability.
5. **Government Policies:** Tax holidays, subsidies, SEZ incentives, and state-level industrial policies directly affect project viability.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between Plant Location and Plant Layout. Why is location considered an irreversible decision?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Plant Location | Plant Layout |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Geographic placement of the facility | Internal arrangement of machines, departments, and aisles |
| **Decision Level** | Strategic / Long-term | Tactical / Operational |
| **Cost Impact** | Massive, affects logistics for decades | Moderate, can be modified periodically |
| **Frequency** | Once in firm lifetime | Re-engineered as product mix changes |

**Why Location is Irreversible (1 mark):** Once a plant is built, the **sunk cost in land, building, and infrastructure** cannot be recovered. Shifting location means abandoning those investments, losing trained labor, breaking customer/supplier networks, and incurring massive relocation cost. Hence, a wrong location can cripple the firm financially for years.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

### `Question A` — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Factor Rating Method** of facility location selection. What are its merits and limitations?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company is selecting between three sites (P, Q, R) for a new plant. Compute the composite score using the Factor Rating Method and recommend the best site.

| Factor | Weight | Rating P | Rating Q | Rating R |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Market Proximity | 0.35 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Raw Material | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Labor | 0.20 | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| Power & Utilities | 0.10 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Transport | 0.10 | 9 | 7 | 8 |

#### Model Solution for (a) — Theory [7 Marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:** The Factor Rating Method is a **multi-criteria decision-making technique** that evaluates each candidate site against a list of relevant factors, assigns weights to factors based on their importance, and computes a composite score for each site.

**Steps [3 marks]:**
1. Identify the relevant factors (e.g., market, raw material, labor, etc.).
2. Assign a **weight** to each factor based on its relative importance (weights must sum to 1).
3. Rate each site on each factor on a common scale (e.g., 1–10).
4. Multiply each weight × rating to get the weighted score.
5. Sum the weighted scores to obtain the composite score for each site.
6. Select the site with the **highest composite score**.

**Merits [1.5 marks]:**
- Simple, transparent, and easy to communicate.
- Incorporates both qualitative and quantitative criteria.
- Allows management to express strategic priorities through weights.

**Limitations [1.5 marks]:**
- Subjectivity in assigning weights and ratings.
- All factors treated as independent; ignores interactions.
- May not capture absolute cost differences between sites.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Verify weights:**

$$0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Score for Site P:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_P &= (0.35)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(8) + (0.10)(6) + (0.10)(9) \\
&= 3.15 + 1.75 + 1.60 + 0.60 + 0.90 = 8.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Score for Site Q:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Q &= (0.35)(7) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(6) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(7) \\
&= 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.70 = 7.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Score for Site R:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R &= (0.35)(8) + (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(9) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(8) \\
&= 2.80 + 1.50 + 1.80 + 0.70 + 0.80 = 7.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$
\max(S_P, S_Q, S_R) = \max(8.00, 7.40, 7.60) = 8.00 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Site P is best}
$$

---

### `Question B` — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Break-Even Analysis** approach for comparing two location alternatives. Derive the cross-over volume formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A firm is choosing between two locations, **Site M** and **Site N**, with the following data:

| Cost Element | Site M | Site N |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed Cost (₹/year) | 60,00,000 | 90,00,000 |
| Variable Cost (₹/unit) | 350 | 250 |
| Selling Price (₹/unit) | 500 | 500 |

Find the **break-even volume** for each site, the **cross-over volume**, and recommend the better site at an expected output of **Q = 80,000 units/year**.

#### Model Solution for (a) — Theory [7 Marks]

**Concept [1.5 marks]:** Break-Even Analysis (BEA) helps managers compare two or more location alternatives by computing the production volume at which total costs of the alternatives are equal (the *cross-over* or *indifference* point).

**Derivation of Cross-Over Volume [3.5 marks]:**

Let Site 1 have $FC_1$ and $VC_1$, and Site 2 have $FC_2$ and $VC_2$. The total cost at volume $Q$ is:

$$
TC_1 = FC_1 + (VC_1) \cdot Q
$$

$$
TC_2 = FC_2 + (VC_2) \cdot Q
$$

At the cross-over volume $Q_c$, the total costs are equal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC_1 + VC_1 \cdot Q_c &= FC_2 + VC_2 \cdot Q_c \\
FC_1 - FC_2 &= VC_2 \cdot Q_c - VC_1 \cdot Q_c \\
FC_1 - FC_2 &= Q_c (VC_2 - VC_1) \\
Q_c &= \dfrac{FC_1 - FC_2}{VC_2 - VC_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Rule [2 marks]:**
- If expected output $Q < Q_c$ → Choose the site with **lower fixed cost**.
- If expected output $Q > Q_c$ → Choose the site with **lower variable cost**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — BEP for Site M:**

$$
BEP_M = \dfrac{FC_M}{P - VC_M} = \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{500 - 350} = \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{150} = 40{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — BEP for Site N:**

$$
BEP_N = \dfrac{FC_N}{P - VC_N} = \dfrac{90{,}00{,}000}{500 - 250} = \dfrac{90{,}00{,}000}{250} = 36{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Over Volume:**

$$
Q_c = \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}00{,}000}{250 - 350} = \dfrac{-30{,}00{,}000}{-100} = 30{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Cost at $Q = 80{,}000$:**

$$
TC_M = 60{,}00{,}000 + (350)(80{,}000) = 60{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}80{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}40{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

$$
TC_N = 90{,}00{,}000 + (250)(80{,}000) = 90{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}90{,}00{,}000 \;\text{₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

Since $Q = 80{,}000 \gg Q_c = 30{,}000$, choose **Site N** (lower variable cost dominates at high volume). Site N saves **₹50,00,000 per year** at this output.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify weight sum = 1** in Factor Rating — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing BEP with Cross-Over**: BEP requires selling price; Cross-Over does not. Mixing them up = full 7-mark question wrong.
> 3. **Not plugging $Q_c$ back into Total Cost** to verify — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for blind application.
> 4. **Forgetting units** (₹/year, units, km) — minor but cumulative deduction.
> 5. **Omitting limitations** in theory questions — 50% of marks lost.
> 6. **No sensitivity check** — if two sites are close in score, KTU expects at least a one-line sensitivity comment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before every exam — 60-second revision.**

- **Location vs Layout** — Location = *Where* (geographic), Layout = *How* (internal arrangement).
- **Location decision is irreversible**, capital-intensive, strategic, and rare.
- **9 key factors**: Market, Raw Material, Labor, Transport, Power/Water, Government, Community, Climate, Strategic.
- **Weight-losing raw material industries** (sugar, cement, paper) → locate near raw material.
- **Weight-gaining industries** (soft drinks, FMCG) → locate near market.
- **Factor Rating Method** → $S_j = \sum w_i r_{ij}$ ; weights must sum to 1.
- **BEP Formula** → $BEP = FC / (P - VC)$ ; units = output units per year.
- **Cross-Over Formula** → $Q_c = (FC_1 - FC_2) / (VC_2 - VC_1)$ ; choose lower VC above $Q_c$, lower FC below $Q_c$.
- **Center of Gravity** → weighted average of coordinates by load.
- **Load-Distance** → minimize $\sum L_{ij} \cdot D_{ij}$.
- **Transportation LP** → minimize $\sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$ subject to supply/demand constraints.
- **Sensitivity analysis is mandatory** when scores are close.
- **Always state limitations** in theory questions.
- **Real-world anchors to memorize**: Amazon → Center of Gravity; Tesla → Factor Rating; Sugar mills → Raw material proximity; Bakeries → Market proximity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factor Rating and Center of Gravity Methods of Location Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factor Rating and Center of Gravity Methods of Location Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Strategic Context of Facility Location
Facility location decisions are **long-term, irreversible, and capital-intensive** strategic choices that determine the geographic placement of a firm's operations. These decisions influence **fixed and variable costs, revenue, taxation, supply chain efficiency**, and ultimately, the competitive advantage of an enterprise. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes two widely-tested quantitative and qualitative techniques under the *Qualitative and Quantitative Approaches to Facility Location* syllabus unit: the **Factor Rating Method** and the **Center of Gravity Method**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to **select the appropriate location decision model**, **apply factor rating for qualitative evaluation**, and **solve center of gravity numerical problems** to determine optimal coordinates of a facility.

### 1.2 Factor Rating Method — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Factor Rating Method** (also called the *Factor Comparison Method* or *Weighted Point Method*) is a structured decision-making technique used to evaluate and compare alternative plant/warehouse/retail locations by assigning relative **weights** to critical location factors and multiplying them by **factor scores** for each candidate site. The alternative with the **highest composite weighted score** is selected as the optimal location.

### 1.3 Center of Gravity Method — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **Center of Gravity (CoG) Method** is a quantitative mathematical technique for determining the *optimal geographic coordinates* of a distribution center, warehouse, or plant that minimizes the **total weighted transportation distance (or cost)** between the facility and a set of supply/dispatch points whose locations and shipping volumes are known.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Factor Rating — Analogy: "Buying the Best Smartphone":**
Imagine choosing a smartphone. You list factors — *Camera, Battery, Price, Processor Speed* — and assign a personal weight to each (Camera 30%, Battery 25%, Price 25%, Speed 20%). You then rate every candidate phone on each factor (1–10), multiply, and sum. The winner has the best balance of *your* priorities. The Factor Rating Method works identically: a company assigns weights to location factors based on strategic priorities and mathematically identifies the site that best aligns with them.

**Center of Gravity — Analogy: "The Balanced Seesaw":**
Think of a child's seesaw. If you place a 50 kg child at one end and a 30 kg child at the other, the **balance point (pivot)** is not at the midpoint — it shifts toward the heavier child. The Center of Gravity Method uses the *same physics principle*: instead of weights, it uses **shipping volumes**; instead of a 1-D seesaw, it operates on a **2-D map (x, y coordinates)**. The CoG is the unique point that "balances" the total transportation load, minimizing the weighted distance to all destinations.

### 1.5 Comparison Snapshot

| Aspect | Factor Rating Method | Center of Gravity Method |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Qualitative / Semi-quantitative | Purely Quantitative |
| Data Required | Factor weights + subjective scores | Geographic coordinates + shipment volumes |
| Output | A ranked list of alternative sites | Specific (x, y) coordinates |
| Best Used For | Comparing 2–5 pre-identified candidate sites | Locating a single facility serving multiple fixed demand points |
| Cost Consideration | Indirect (via factor scoring) | Direct (distance-based minimization) |
| KTU Exam Weightage | Moderate (3–7 marks) | High (7–14 marks, often numerical) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factor Rating Method — Underlying Logic

The Factor Rating Method converts a complex multi-criteria decision into a single **composite numeric score**, allowing objective comparison. Its theoretical foundation rests on the **Multi-Attribute Decision Theory (MADT)**.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Identification of Relevant Factors:** The decision-maker compiles a list of all critical factors affecting the location decision. Common KTU-listed factors include *proximity to market, raw material availability, labor cost & availability, transportation infrastructure, power & utilities, government policy & incentives, community attitude, environmental conditions,* and *land cost*.

2. **Assignment of Weights:** Each factor is assigned a **weight** $w_i$ reflecting its strategic importance. The sum of all weights must equal **1.0 (or 100%)**.

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1.0 $$

3. **Rating Each Alternative:** Every candidate location is rated on each factor, typically on a **scale of 1 to 10** (1 = very poor, 10 = excellent). Let $r_{ij}$ denote the rating of alternative $j$ on factor $i$.

4. **Computing the Weighted Score:** For each alternative, the weighted score is calculated as:

$$ S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij} $$

5. **Selection:** The alternative with the **highest $S_j$** is recommended as the optimal location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Factor Rating:** It forces decision-makers to (a) explicitly quantify subjective preferences, (b) handle trade-offs transparently, and (c) provide a defensible audit trail — critical in KTU board exam *case-study* questions where justification is rewarded.

### 2.2 Center of Gravity Method — Underlying Logic

The CoG Method is derived from the classical mechanics principle of **center of mass**. It is mathematically optimal when:
- Transportation cost is **proportional to distance**,
- Volumes of shipments to/from each point are known, and
- All locations are mapped on a **Cartesian (x, y) plane**.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Plot all source/destination points** on a grid with known $(x_i, y_i)$ coordinates.
2. **Assign weights** $V_i$ = volume of goods shipped to/from point $i$ (loads/month, tonnes/year, etc.).
3. **Compute weighted average x-coordinate** $C_x$ and y-coordinate $C_y$.
4. The point $(C_x, C_y)$ is the location that **minimizes the sum of weighted Euclidean distances** — the theoretical optimal placement.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression | Unit / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_j$ | Composite weighted score of location $j$ | $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$ | Dimensionless; $\sum w_i = 1.0$ |
| $w_i$ | Weight of factor $i$ | Decision-variable | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1.0$ (or 100%) |
| $r_{ij}$ | Rating of location $j$ on factor $i$ | Expert-assigned | Typically 1 to 10 (integer) |
| $C_x$ | Optimal x-coordinate of facility | $C_x = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}$ | Kilometers, miles, or grid units |
| $C_y$ | Optimal y-coordinate of facility | $C_y = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i}$ | Same unit as $C_x$ |
| $V_i$ | Shipment volume at point $i$ | Decision-variable | Loads, tonnes, units, pallets |
| $(x_i, y_i)$ | Cartesian coordinates of point $i$ | Given input | Same unit scale required for both axes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary:** The CoG formula assumes the Euclidean map uses a **consistent unit scale on both axes** (i.e., a 1-unit move in $x$ is the same physical distance as a 1-unit move in $y$). Otherwise, the result is mathematically valid but geometrically distorted. In KTU problems, this is guaranteed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Factor Rating:** Used in *site selection for manufacturing plants, hospitals, data centers,* and *wind farms* where multiple strategic priorities must be balanced.
- **Center of Gravity:** Used in *logistics network design, ambulance dispatch hub location, Amazon-style distribution centers,* and *emergency response station placement*.

> [!TIP]
> The two methods are **complementary**, not competing. KTU exam questions often chain them: first use Factor Rating to *narrow down* to 2–3 candidate regions, then apply Center of Gravity (or Load-Distance method) to find the *exact* optimal point.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Center of Gravity Formulas

The CoG method **minimizes** the total weighted distance function:

$$ f(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} $$

To find the minimum, we take **partial derivatives** with respect to $x$ and $y$ and set them to **zero**.

**Step 1 — Partial derivative with respect to $x$:**

$$ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot \frac{(x - x_i)}{\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}} = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Partial derivative with respect to $y$:**

$$ \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot \frac{(y - y_i)}{\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}} = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Approximation (the KTU exam standard form):** By assuming the new facility is *not* located at extreme distances (a standard textbook assumption known as the **Tarantella/Freeman approximation**), the system of equations reduces to the simple weighted average form:

$$ C_x = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i} \quad \quad C_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i} $$

This is the **canonical form** that the KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to reproduce and apply.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Factor Rating Method

**Problem Statement:**
A company is evaluating three plant locations: **Site A, Site B, Site C**. The decision team has identified six factors with the following weights. Each site is rated on a 1–10 scale. Determine the optimal site.

| Factor | Weight | Site A | Site B | Site C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to market | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Labor cost & availability | 0.20 | 6 | 9 | 5 |
| Raw material access | 0.15 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Transportation infrastructure | 0.15 | 7 | 8 | 6 |
| Power & utilities | 0.10 | 8 | 6 | 7 |
| Government incentives | 0.10 | 5 | 8 | 7 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify weight normalization:**

$$ 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 1.00 \checkmark $$

**Step 2 — Calculate the weighted score for Site A:**

$$ S_A = (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 5) $$

$$ S_A = 2.40 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 1.05 + 0.80 + 0.50 = 7.30 $$

**Step 3 — Calculate the weighted score for Site B:**

$$ S_B = (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 8) $$

$$ S_B = 2.10 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 1.20 + 0.60 + 0.80 = 7.55 $$

**Step 4 — Calculate the weighted score for Site C:**

$$ S_C = (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 7) $$

$$ S_C = 2.70 + 1.00 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.70 + 0.70 = 7.20 $$

**Step 5 — Rank and Select:**

| Site | Composite Score | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Site A | 7.30 | 2nd |
| **Site B** | **7.55** | **1st** ✓ |
| Site C | 7.20 | 3rd |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision: Site B** is the optimal location. Although Site A and Site C score higher on individual factors (e.g., raw material access, market proximity), Site B's *balanced* profile — particularly strong labor cost and government incentives — gives it the highest composite weighted score.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Center of Gravity Method

**Problem Statement:**
A distribution center is to serve **four retail outlets**. The geographic coordinates (in km on a city grid) and monthly shipment volumes are given. Determine the optimal coordinates $(C_x, C_y)$ for the new facility.

| Outlet | x-coordinate (km) | y-coordinate (km) | Monthly Volume (tonnes) |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 1 | 7 | 150 |
| R2 | 4 | 3 | 200 |
| R3 | 7 | 2 | 100 |
| R4 | 5 | 6 | 250 |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\sum x_i V_i$:**

$$ \sum x_i V_i = (1 \times 150) + (4 \times 200) + (7 \times 100) + (5 \times 250) $$

$$ \sum x_i V_i = 150 + 800 + 700 + 1250 = 2900 \text{ tonne·km} $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum y_i V_i$:**

$$ \sum y_i V_i = (7 \times 150) + (3 \times 200) + (2 \times 100) + (6 \times 250) $$

$$ \sum y_i V_i = 1050 + 600 + 200 + 1500 = 3350 \text{ tonne·km} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sum V_i$:**

$$ \sum V_i = 150 + 200 + 100 + 250 = 700 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the CoG formulas:**

$$ C_x = \frac{\sum x_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{2900}{700} = 4.1429 \text{ km} $$

$$ C_y = \frac{\sum y_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{3350}{700} = 4.7857 \text{ km} $$

**Step 5 — Result and Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Facility Location:** $C_x \approx 4.14 \text{ km}, \quad C_y \approx 4.79 \text{ km}$

> **Interpretation:** The new distribution center should be located at the point **(4.14, 4.79)** on the city grid. This location **minimizes the total weighted transportation cost** of servicing the four retail outlets, weighted by their monthly volume. R4 (volume 250) and R2 (volume 200) exert the strongest "pull" on this location.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Decision Scenario | Recommended Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Compare 3 city candidates for a plant | Factor Rating | Qualitative + quantitative factors matter; output is a ranking |
| Locate a single warehouse serving 8 retail stores | Center of Gravity | Coordinates and volumes drive cost; output is a single point |
| Hybrid: narrow 5 sites to 2, then pick exact spot | Factor Rating $\rightarrow$ CoG | KTU-style cascading application |
| Choosing between 2 countries for FDI | Factor Rating | Macro factors dominate; coordinates often unavailable |
| Emergency ambulance station placement | Center of Gravity | Demand volumes and response distances are central |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Factor Rating Method — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Location Decision Required] --> B[Step 1: Identify Critical Factors e.g. Market, Labor, Power]
    B --> C[Step 2: Assign Weights wi to each factor Sum of wi must equal 1.0]
    C --> D[Step 3: List Alternative Candidate Sites j = 1 to m]
    D --> E[Step 4: Rate each site on each factor rij on a 1 to 10 scale]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute Composite Weighted Score Sj = Sum of wi times rij]
    F --> G[Step 6: Compare Scores Across All Sites]
    G --> H[Step 7: SELECT Site with HIGHEST Composite Score]
    H --> I[END: Optimal Location Identified]
```

### 4.2 Center of Gravity Method — Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Plot all demand and supply points on a Cartesian map with x y coordinates]
    P2[Step 2: Identify volume Vi shipped to or from each point]
    P3[Step 3: Compute numerator of Cx: sum of xi times Vi]
    P4[Step 4: Compute numerator of Cy: sum of yi times Vi]
    P5[Step 5: Compute denominator: sum of Vi]
    P6[Step 6: Apply Cx = sum of xi times Vi divided by sum of Vi]
    P7[Step 7: Apply Cy = sum of yi times Vi divided by sum of Vi]
    P8[Step 8: PLOT the CoG point on the map and mark the recommended facility site]
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Two Methods Compared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRM[Factor Rating Method Module]
        F1[Factor List Input] --> F2[Weight Assignment Engine]
        F2 --> F3[Rating Matrix]
        F3 --> F4[Weighted Score Calculator]
        F4 --> F5[Ranked List of Sites Output]
    end
    subgraph COG[Center of Gravity Method Module]
        G1[Coordinate Map Input] --> G2[Volume Data Input]
        G2 --> G3[Weighted Sum Numerator Calc]
        G3 --> G4[Volume Sum Denominator Calc]
        G4 --> G5[Coordinate Formula Solver]
        G5 --> G6[Optimal x y Coordinates Output]
    end
    F5 --> DEC{Strategic Decision Engine}
    G6 --> DEC
    DEC --> OPR[Optimal Plant or Warehouse Location]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** In board answers, even a simple hand-drawn *Cartesian plot* showing the CoG point and the four demand points can earn full diagram marks. KTU examiners value **labeled axes, plotted points, and a clear "X" mark for the recommended site**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** 
*Define the Factor Rating Method of location selection. List any four factors that are commonly used in evaluating a manufacturing plant location.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Factor Rating Method** is a multi-criteria decision-making technique that assigns *weights* to critical location factors and multiplies them by *factor scores* (typically on a 1–10 scale) for each candidate site. The site with the **highest composite weighted score** is selected. 

Common factors (any four): (i) *Proximity to market* (ii) *Labor cost and availability* (iii) *Transportation infrastructure* (iv) *Power and utility availability* (v) *Government policies and incentives* (vi) *Raw material proximity*. **(1 mark definition + 1 mark logic + 1 mark for any 4 factors).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the formula for finding the optimal location of a facility using the Center of Gravity Method. What is the underlying assumption of this method?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The CoG formulas are:

$$ C_x = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i} \quad \quad C_y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i V_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i} $$

where $(x_i, y_i)$ are the coordinates of demand point $i$ and $V_i$ is the shipping volume. 

**Underlying assumption:** The transportation cost is **directly proportional to distance**, and the map's x and y axes use a **consistent unit scale** (a one-unit move in $x$ equals a one-unit move in $y$). **(1 mark formula + 1 mark variable explanation + 1 mark assumption).**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common error in Factor Rating:** Forgetting to **normalize weights** to sum = 1.0. Always verify the sum before any calculation.
> - **Most common error in CoG:** Mixing up the numerator (weighted sum of coordinates) with the denominator (sum of volumes). Remember: **coordinates × volume in the numerator; only volume in the denominator**.
> - **Unit care:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by not specifying the *unit of $C_x$ and $C_y$*. Always write: "$C_x = 4.14$ **km**".
> - **Show the table:** Even in numerical CoG problems, drawing a small table with $x_i$, $y_i$, $V_i$, $x_iV_i$, $y_iV_i$ columns earns the **structured presentation marks** KTU examiners look for.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **Factor Rating Method** of location selection in detail. What are its merits and limitations? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm is evaluating four potential sites for a new factory. The factors, weights, and ratings (1–10) are given below. Using the **Factor Rating Method**, determine the best site. **(7 marks)**

| Factor | Weight | Site 1 | Site 2 | Site 3 | Site 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proximity to market | 0.25 | 8 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Availability of skilled labor | 0.20 | 6 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Power supply | 0.15 | 9 | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Transport links | 0.15 | 7 | 8 | 8 | 7 |
| Government policy | 0.10 | 5 | 8 | 7 | 9 |
| Land cost | 0.10 | 8 | 6 | 7 | 5 |
| Environmental safety | 0.05 | 9 | 7 | 8 | 6 |

---

**Model Answer — (a) Detailed Explanation of Factor Rating Method (7 marks):**

The **Factor Rating Method** (also called the *Weighted Point Method*) is a structured technique used to compare alternative plant or facility locations based on multiple qualitative and quantitative criteria. It converts a complex multi-criteria decision into a single composite score.

**Steps:** (3 marks)
1. Identify all critical factors that influence the location decision.
2. Assign a *weight* to each factor, with weights summing to 1.0 (or 100%), reflecting its strategic importance.
3. Rate each candidate site on each factor, typically on a **1 to 10** scale.
4. Multiply each weight by the corresponding rating and sum across all factors to obtain a **composite weighted score** $S_j = \sum w_i r_{ij}$.
5. Select the site with the **highest $S_j$**.

**Merits:** (2 marks)
- Forces transparent, quantified justification of a subjective decision.
- Easily understood and can be completed by cross-functional teams.
- Highly flexible — can include both qualitative (e.g., community attitude) and quantitative (e.g., tax rate) factors.
- Provides an auditable decision trail.

**Limitations:** (2 marks)
- Weights and ratings are *subjective*; biased input produces biased output.
- The final score is sensitive to the choice of factors and the rating scale.
- Does not directly compute cost — only ranks sites on preference alignment.
- Ignores inter-factor interactions and assumes linear additivity.

---

**Model Answer — (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Verify weight normalization:** $0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.05 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute composite scores:**

$$ S_1 = (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.05 \times 9) $$

$$ S_1 = 2.00 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 1.05 + 0.50 + 0.80 + 0.45 = 7.35 $$

$$ S_2 = (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.05 \times 7) $$

$$ S_2 = 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.35 = 7.55 $$

$$ S_3 = (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.05 \times 8) $$

$$ S_3 = 2.25 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.70 + 0.40 = 7.55 $$

$$ S_4 = (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 5) + (0.05 \times 6) $$

$$ S_4 = 1.50 + 1.60 + 1.20 + 1.05 + 0.90 + 0.50 + 0.30 = 7.05 $$

**Step 3 — Ranking:**

| Site | Composite Score | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Site 1 | 7.35 | 3rd |
| **Site 2** | **7.55** | **1st (tied)** |
| **Site 3** | **7.55** | **1st (tied)** |
| Site 4 | 7.05 | 4th |

**Decision:** Sites 2 and 3 are tied. In a tie-breaking situation, the firm should examine the **qualitative strength** of each site (e.g., Site 2's superior labor and policy scores, or Site 3's superior market proximity) to make a final call. **Either Site 2 or Site 3** is acceptable as the optimal location. (Tied = **select either; document qualitative reasoning**.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **Center of Gravity Method** of location selection. Under what conditions is this method most appropriately applied? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company plans to open a new regional warehouse to serve six existing retail outlets. The map coordinates (in km) and the monthly load volumes are given below. Using the **Center of Gravity Method**, determine the optimal location for the warehouse. **(7 marks)**

| Outlet | x (km) | y (km) | Monthly Loads |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | 2 | 5 | 300 |
| O2 | 6 | 3 | 500 |
| O3 | 8 | 7 | 200 |
| O4 | 4 | 9 | 400 |
| O5 | 1 | 2 | 250 |
| O6 | 5 | 6 | 350 |

---

**Model Answer — (a) Center of Gravity Method Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Center of Gravity (CoG) Method** is a quantitative mathematical technique used to find the **optimal geographic coordinates** of a single facility that minimizes the **total weighted transportation distance** to a set of pre-existing supply or demand points. The method is borrowed from the classical physics principle of *center of mass*.

**Procedure:** (3 marks)
1. Plot all demand/supply points on a Cartesian (x, y) map.
2. Note the shipment volume $V_i$ at each point.
3. Compute the weighted average of x-coordinates: $C_x = \dfrac{\sum x_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$.
4. Compute the weighted average of y-coordinates: $C_y = \dfrac{\sum y_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$.
5. The point $(C_x, C_y)$ is the recommended facility location.

**When to Apply (conditions):** (2 marks)
- Transportation cost is proportional to distance.
- Shipment volumes and the locations of all demand points are known.
- A single facility must be located to serve multiple fixed points.
- The map uses a consistent unit scale on both x and y axes.

**Merits and Limitations:** (2 marks)
- *Merits:* Mathematically optimal, simple to apply, data-driven, easily visualized on a map.
- *Limitations:* Assumes a flat earth / planar geometry; ignores real-world obstacles (rivers, mountains, no-construction zones); ignores differences in transport modes and rates per direction.

---

**Model Answer — (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Tabulate and compute the column products:**

| Outlet | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $V_i$ | $x_i V_i$ | $y_i V_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | 2 | 5 | 300 | 600 | 1500 |
| O2 | 6 | 3 | 500 | 3000 | 1500 |
| O3 | 8 | 7 | 200 | 1600 | 1400 |
| O4 | 4 | 9 | 400 | 1600 | 3600 |
| O5 | 1 | 2 | 250 | 250 | 500 |
| O6 | 5 | 6 | 350 | 1750 | 2100 |
| **Total** | — | — | **$\sum V_i = 2000$** | **$\sum x_i V_i = 8800$** | **$\sum y_i V_i = 10600$** |

**Step 2 — Apply the CoG formulas:**

$$ C_x = \frac{\sum x_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{8800}{2000} = 4.40 \text{ km} $$

$$ C_y = \frac{\sum y_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \frac{10600}{2000} = 5.30 \text{ km} $$

**Step 3 — Final Result and Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Warehouse Location:** $C_x = 4.40 \text{ km}, \quad C_y = 5.30 \text{ km}$

> **Interpretation:** The new regional warehouse should be located at the coordinate point **(4.40, 5.30)** on the city map. This location minimizes the total weighted transportation distance (and therefore cost) of serving the six outlets. Outlets O2 (load 500) and O4 (load 400) — the two highest-volume points — exert the greatest gravitational pull on the recommended site.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Factor Rating Method** = qualitative + semi-quantitative; output is a **ranking of sites**.
- ✅ Formula: $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_{ij}$, where $\sum w_i = 1.0$ and $r_{ij} \in [1, 10]$.
- ✅ **Always normalize weights** before any calculation; **always show the full table** of weights and ratings in your exam answer.
- ✅ **Center of Gravity Method** = purely quantitative; output is a **single (x, y) coordinate pair**.
- ✅ Formula: $C_x = \dfrac{\sum x_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$ and $C_y = \dfrac{\sum y_i V_i}{\sum V_i}$.
- ✅ The CoG is a **weighted average**, not a sum — the denominator is **only the total volume**.
- ✅ The CoG method assumes **transport cost ∝ distance** and a **uniform map scale**.
- ✅ The two methods are **complementary** — KTU may ask a cascading question: "use Factor Rating to shortlist, then CoG to pinpoint."
- ✅ In numerical CoG problems, **showing the table** of $x_i$, $y_i$, $V_i$, $x_iV_i$, $y_iV_i$ earns full structured-presentation marks.
- ✅ Always quote the **unit** of the final CoG coordinates (km, miles, etc.).
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** Dividing by $n$ (number of points) instead of by $\sum V_i$. The CoG is a *weighted* mean, not a simple mean.
- ✅ For tie-breaking in Factor Rating, the KTU board accepts **either a tie-declaration with qualitative justification** or a **secondary criterion** such as total factor rating, cost, or strategic alignment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Facility Layouts and Their Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Facility Layouts and Their Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Facility Layout?

A **Facility Layout** refers to the physical arrangement of machines, equipment, workstations, storage areas, and other support facilities within a manufacturing or service facility. It is a strategic decision that determines the **flow of materials, information, and people** to achieve the most efficient and effective operation of the production system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Answer Format):**
> "Facility layout is the arrangement of departments, work centers, machines, equipment, and other physical facilities of an organization to achieve a coordinated and integrated production system that minimizes material handling costs, reduces throughput time, and maximizes overall productivity."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are setting up a **kitchen** in a new house. You need to decide where to place the stove, refrigerator, sink, and preparation counter.

- If you place the **refrigerator, sink, stove, and counter in a single straight line** (Fridge → Wash → Cut → Cook → Serve), that is a **Product Layout** (like an assembly line).
- If you create **separate zones** like a baking station, a chopping station, a washing station — and move between them based on what dish you are cooking — that is a **Process Layout**.
- If you are building a **large ship** in a hangar, the ship cannot move, so all tools and workers come to the ship — that is a **Fixed Position Layout**.

**The same logic governs factories, hospitals, banks, and warehouses.** The choice of layout directly impacts speed, cost, flexibility, and quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Material Handling Cost (MHC)** — measured in ₹/unit produced
> - **Throughput Time (TPT)** — measured in minutes/hours
> - **Distance Travelled** — measured in meters
> - **Backtracking Percentage** — ratio of unproductive movement to total movement
> - **Capacity Utilization** — measured as a percentage (%)

---

## 1.2 Primary Classification of Facility Layouts

Facility layouts are broadly classified into **FIVE** primary types under the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Process Layout** (also called Functional Layout or Job Shop Layout)
2. **Product Layout** (also called Line Layout or Assembly Line Layout)
3. **Fixed Position Layout** (also called Project Layout)
4. **Cellular Layout** (Group Technology Layout)
5. **Hybrid/Combined Layout** (Mixed Layout)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus Area:**
> This topic carries **high weightage** (typically a 14-mark question appears every semester in Part B). Students must know:
> - Definitions, diagrams, advantages, disadvantages, suitability, and **at least 4 comparative differences** between layouts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Layout Type

### 2.1.1 Process Layout (Functional Layout)

**Definition:** In a Process Layout, similar machines, equipment, and processes are grouped together into distinct departments (e.g., welding department, grinding department, assembly department, inspection department).

**Logic Flow — How it works:**
- Each department specializes in one type of operation.
- The product (workpiece) travels **from one department to another** based on the sequence of operations required.
- Routing of materials is **variable** since each job has different requirements.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Low volume, high variety production
- General-purpose machines
- Skilled labor required
- Long setup times
- High material handling cost
- High flexibility

**Real-World Examples:**
- A **hospital** (Cardiology, Orthopedics, Radiology, Pathology — all separated).
- A **machine shop** (Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding — all separated).

---

### 2.1.2 Product Layout (Line Layout)

**Definition:** In a Product Layout, machines and workstations are arranged in a **single line** based on the sequence of operations required to produce a specific product.

**Logic Flow — How it works:**
- Each workstation performs a **specific, repeated operation**.
- Material flows continuously along the line.
- The product moves from one workstation to the next in a **fixed, predetermined sequence**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- High volume, low variety (standardized products)
- Special-purpose machines
- Semi-skilled or unskilled labor
- Short setup times
- Low material handling cost
- Low flexibility (designed for one product family)

**Real-World Examples:**
- **Automobile assembly line** (Tata, Maruti Suzuki manufacturing plants).
- **Bottling plant** (Coca-Cola, Bisleri).
- **Fast food restaurant** (McDonald's kitchen flow).

---

### 2.1.3 Fixed Position Layout

**Definition:** In a Fixed Position Layout, the product remains at a **fixed location**, and all the required tools, machines, materials, and workers are brought to that location.

**Logic Flow — How it works:**
- The product is too large, heavy, or immobile to be moved.
- Resources (man, machine, material) flow **to the product** instead of the product flowing to resources.
- Requires careful scheduling of resources.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Low volume, often one-of-a-kind production
- Highly skilled labor
- High coordination cost
- Long project duration
- Used for very large products

**Real-World Examples:**
- **Shipbuilding** in a shipyard.
- **Aircraft construction** (Boeing, Airbus).
- **Construction** of a building or dam.
- **Locomotive manufacturing**.

---

### 2.1.4 Cellular Layout (Group Technology Layout)

**Definition:** In a Cellular Layout, machines are grouped into **cells**, where each cell can produce a family of similar parts requiring similar operations. It is a hybrid of process and product layout.

**Logic Flow — How it works:**
- Parts are classified into **part families** based on similarities in design or manufacturing process.
- Machines required for a part family are grouped into a **cell**.
- Material movement is **minimized within the cell**; the cell is dedicated to a specific part family.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Medium volume, medium variety
- Moderate flexibility
- Reduced setup and material handling
- Requires **part family analysis** (Group Technology coding)
- Improved communication within cells

**Real-World Examples:**
- **CNC machining workshops** producing similar components.
- **Electronics assembly** for a family of similar printed circuit boards.
- **Toys manufacturing** with a family of dolls or cars.

---

### 2.1.5 Hybrid/Combined Layout

**Definition:** A Hybrid Layout is a **combination of two or more** of the above layouts. It is used when a single type of layout is not sufficient for all operations.

**Logic Flow — How it works:**
- Different sections of the plant adopt different layouts based on their requirements.
- For example, the **welding section** may use a process layout, while the **final assembly** uses a product layout.

**Real-World Examples:**
- Most **large manufacturing plants** (Hero Honda, Tata Motors).
- **Hospitals** (operation theatres in fixed position, OPD in process layout).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Description | Variables & Units | Applicability |
|---|---------|-----------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Total Material Handling Cost** | $T_{MHC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_{i} \times C_{i} \times V_{i})$ | $D_i$ = distance (m), $C_i$ = cost per unit distance (₹/m), $V_i$ = volume moved | All layouts — lower is better |
| 2 | **Throughput Time (TPT)** | $T_{PT} = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{transport} + T_{wait}$ | Each term in minutes/hours | Measures production speed |
| 3 | **Capacity Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Maximum Possible Output}} \times 100$ | Output in units, Utilization in % | Measures efficiency |
| 4 | **Number of Workstations (Line Balancing)** | $N = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}}{C_{cycle}}$ | $t_i$ = task time (min), $C_{cycle}$ = cycle time (min) | Product Layout |
| 5 | **Balance Delay (BD)** | $BD = \dfrac{N \times C_{cycle} - \sum t_{i}}{N \times C_{cycle}} \times 100$ | All times in minutes, BD in % | Product Layout — lower is better |
| 6 | **Efficiency of Line** | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + BD}$ or $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{N \times C_{cycle}} \times 100$ | Efficiency in % | Product Layout |
| 7 | **Backtracking Ratio** | $B_{r} = \dfrac{D_{backtrack}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | Distances in meters, ratio in % | Process Layout — lower is better |
| 8 | **Number of Possible Layouts (Process Layout)** | $N_{layouts} = (n-1)!$ for $n$ machines on a single row | $n$ = number of machines | Process Layout (heuristic) |
| 9 | **Group Technology Coding** | Opitz Code, MICLASS, DCLASS | Codes assigned to part features | Cellular Layout |
| 10 | **Cell Formation Index** | $CFI = \dfrac{\text{Number of operations within cells}}{\text{Total operations}}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) | Cellular Layout |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For Part B (14-mark) questions, students must remember at least **3 to 4 formulas** and the conditions under which they apply. The most frequently asked formulas are **Total MHC**, **Number of Workstations**, and **Efficiency of Line**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Facility Layout Decision

- **Cost Reduction:** Optimal layout minimizes material movement, saving crores in large plants.
- **Faster Delivery:** Reduced throughput time means faster order fulfillment.
- **Worker Safety & Ergonomics:** Good layout reduces accidents and fatigue.
- **Quality Improvement:** Proper layout reduces damage to materials during handling.
- **Future Scalability:** A good layout accommodates future expansion without major disruption.
- **Service Industries:** Banks, hospitals, airports, and retail stores use these principles for customer flow and service efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Comparative Analysis Matrix (KTU Board Favorite)

The following matrix provides an exhaustive comparison of all five layout types across 12 critical parameters:

| **Parameter** | **Process Layout** | **Product Layout** | **Fixed Position** | **Cellular Layout** | **Hybrid Layout** |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Volume** | Low | High | Very Low (One-off) | Medium | Medium to High |
| **Variety** | High | Low | Very High | Medium | Medium to High |
| **Flow Pattern** | Variable / Functional | Fixed / Continuous | Static (product) | Grouped / Cellular | Mixed |
| **Machines** | General purpose | Special purpose | Mobile equipment | Grouped by family | Combination |
| **Material Handling** | High cost | Low cost | Very high coordination | Low to moderate | Moderate |
| **Worker Skill** | High (skilled) | Low (semi-skilled) | Highly skilled | Moderate | Variable |
| **Setup Time** | Long | Short | Not applicable | Moderate | Variable |
| **WIP Inventory** | High | Low | Very low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Throughput Time** | Long | Short | Very long | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Flexibility** | Very high | Very low | High (for project) | High | Moderate |
| **Capital Investment** | Lower | Higher | Very high | Moderate | High |
| **Best Suited For** | Job shop, hospital | Mass production | Ship, aircraft | Part families | Large plants |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Layout Selection Decision Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus tests a student's ability to **select the appropriate layout** for a given scenario. The decision can be broken down as follows:

### Step 1: Identify Production Characteristics
- **Volume and Variety Analysis** (using the Product-Process Matrix by Hayes and Wheelwright).

### Step 2: Analyze Product Flow
- If the product flow is **fixed and repetitive** → choose **Product Layout**.
- If the flow is **variable and complex** → choose **Process Layout**.
- If the product is **immobile** → choose **Fixed Position Layout**.

### Step 3: Apply Part Family Analysis
- If parts can be grouped into families → consider **Cellular Layout**.
- If a single layout is insufficient → consider **Hybrid Layout**.

### Step 4: Evaluate Cost-Benefit Trade-offs
- Use the **Total Material Handling Cost Formula** to compare alternatives.
- The layout with the **lowest MHC** and **highest capacity utilization** is preferred, subject to flexibility constraints.

### Step 5: Validate Using the 5S Framework (Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain)
- Ensure the chosen layout supports clean, safe, and standardized operations.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Material Handling Cost Comparison

**Problem:** A factory needs to move 1000 units of product from Department A to Department E. The distances and cost rates for three layout alternatives are given below. Calculate the Total Material Handling Cost (MHC) for each and recommend the best layout.

| Layout | Distance (m) | Cost per meter (₹/m) | Volume (units) |
|--------|--------------|----------------------|----------------|
| L1: Process Layout | 80 | 5 | 1000 |
| L2: Product Layout | 45 | 5 | 1000 |
| L3: Fixed Position | 120 | 5 | 1000 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Recall the Total MHC Formula**
$$T_{MHC} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_{i} \times C_{i} \times V_{i})$$

**Step 2: Calculate for Layout L1 (Process)**
$$T_{MHC}^{L1} = 80 \times 5 \times 1000 = 4,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3: Calculate for Layout L2 (Product)**
$$T_{MHC}^{L2} = 45 \times 5 \times 1000 = 2,25,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4: Calculate for Layout L3 (Fixed Position)**
$$T_{MHC}^{L3} = 120 \times 5 \times 1000 = 6,00,000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5: Compare the Three Layouts**
- L1: ₹4,00,000
- L2: ₹2,25,000 ← **Lowest cost**
- L3: ₹6,00,000

**Step 6: Recommendation**
Since **L2 (Product Layout)** has the lowest total material handling cost, it should be preferred — provided the production volume and variety support it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Insight:** A Product Layout is most efficient for standardized, high-volume products because the material flow is direct and unidirectional. A Fixed Position Layout incurs the highest MHC because all materials must travel to the static product location.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Selection of Layout Based on Production Volume-Variety Grid

| Production Type | Volume | Variety | Recommended Layout |
|-----------------|--------|---------|---------------------|
| **Job Production** | Very Low | Very High | Fixed Position / Process Layout |
| **Batch Production** | Low to Medium | Medium | Process Layout / Cellular Layout |
| **Mass Production** | High | Low | Product Layout |
| **Continuous Production** | Very High | Very Low | Product Layout (Continuous) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix** is a 2×2 matrix taught in KTU Module 1. Students must draw and explain it in the exam. The diagonal relationship between product lifecycle and process choice is the key takeaway.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
