z = 0.84 + 0.16 \times \frac{0.090}{0.132} = 0.84 + 0.16 \times 0.682 = 0.84 + 0.109 = 0.95
$$

Safety Stock:

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 0.95 \times 80 = 76 \text{ units}
$$

Reorder Point:

$$
ROP = D̄ \cdot L̄ + SS = 1000 \times \frac{4}{52} + 76 \approx 76.92 + 76 \approx 153 \text{ units}
$$

(Assuming 52 weeks/year.)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Service Level Approaches

| Criterion | Type 1 (Cycle SL) | Type 2 (Fill Rate) |
|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Probability of no stockout in a cycle | Fraction of demand met from stock |
| Customer-perceived reliability | Lower | Higher |
| Required safety stock (for same target) | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| Information required | z-table | z-table + loss function table |
| Most useful when | Internal operations focus | Direct customer service is critical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Position Over Time (Reorder Point with Safety Stock)

The following block diagram illustrates the canonical KTU inventory profile with safety stock and ROP, modelled as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inventory on Hand: Q units] --> B[Demand consumed: D per day]
    B --> C{Inventory remaining > ROP?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Continue issuing stock]
    C -- No --> E[Place new order: Q units]
    D --> F[Inventory depletes to Safety Stock level]
    E --> G[Lead time L elapses]
    F --> G
    G --> H[New lot arrives: stock = SS + demand during LT]
    H --> I[Cycle repeats]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Selecting the Correct $\sigma_{LT}$ Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Variability] --> B{Demand Variable?}
    B -- No --> C{Lead Time Variable?}
    C -- No --> D[Use sigma = 0, SS = 0]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use sigma LT equals D bar times sigma L]
    B -- Yes --> F{Lead Time Variable?}
    F -- No --> G[Use sigma LT equals sigma d times sqrt L]
    F -- Yes --> H[Use sigma LT equals sqrt of L bar sigma d squared plus D bar squared sigma L squared]
    E --> I[Compute SS equals z times sigma LT]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute ROP equals D bar L bar plus SS]
```

## 4.3 Cost–Service Level Trade-off (KTU Favourite Diagram Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Service Level] --> B[Low Safety Stock]
    A --> C[Low Holding Cost]
    A --> D[High Stockout Cost]
    B --> E[Low Total Cost initially]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Optimal Service Level Region 95 to 99 percent]
    F --> G[High Service Level]
    G --> H[High Safety Stock]
    G --> I[Very High Holding Cost]
    H --> J[Total Cost increases again]
    I --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> The optimum service level (the "U-shape" of total cost) is a classic **Module 2 / Module 3** cross-topic in KTU. Service level should not be pushed to 100 % — that would require infinite inventory.

## 4.4 Mapping of Service Level to z-value (Reference Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Service Level 90 percent] --> B1[z equals 1.28]
    A2[Service Level 95 percent] --> B2[z equals 1.65]
    A3[Service Level 97.5 percent] --> B3[z equals 1.96]
    A4[Service Level 99 percent] --> B4[z equals 2.33]
    A5[Service Level 99.9 percent] --> B5[z equals 3.00]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between Type 1 and Type 2 service levels. Which one is more customer-focused and why?**

**Model Answer:**

**Type 1 (Cycle Service Level):** It is the probability that no stockout occurs during a replenishment cycle. Expressed as $P(D_{LT} \leq ROP)$, it focuses on the *frequency* of stockouts.

**Type 2 (Fill Rate):** It is the proportion of total demand that is satisfied *immediately* from stock on hand. It focuses on the *magnitude* of shortages.

Type 2 is more customer-focused because customers experience service through *whether their specific demand is met*, not through the abstract probability of a stockout. For example, even if no stockout occurs in 9 out of 10 cycles (Type 1 = 90 %), a single large shortage in the tenth cycle can fail many customers — whereas Type 2 measures the fraction of all such demand met.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is safety stock? Explain any two situations in which safety stock becomes essential.**

**Model Answer:**

**Safety stock** is the extra inventory maintained to protect against variability in demand and lead time, ensuring a target service level is achieved.

Two situations where it is essential:

1. **Variable demand with constant lead time** — e.g., a pharmaceutical retailer selling paracetamol: flu outbreaks cause sudden demand spikes; safety stock prevents lost sales.
2. **Variable lead time with constant demand** — e.g., a manufacturer importing components from a port where vessels face unpredictable delays; safety stock absorbs the uncertainty in supply.

A third, very common situation is **both demand and lead time being variable** (e.g., e-commerce festive-season demand combined with courier delays).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice*

> **Note to students:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A firm sells a product with average daily demand $D̄ = 300$ units, $\sigma_d = 60$ units. Lead time is constant at $L = 5$ days. The firm wants a **99 %** cycle service level.

(i) Calculate the safety stock.
(ii) Calculate the reorder point.
(iii) If the firm raises the service level to **99.9 %**, by how many units will the safety stock increase?

**Model Solution:**

(i) $z_{0.99} = 2.33$.

$$
\sigma_{LT} = 60 \times \sqrt{5} = 60 \times 2.2361 = 134.16 \text{ units}
$$

$$
SS_{99\%} = 2.33 \times 134.16 = 312.6 \approx 313 \text{ units}
$$

(ii) 

$$
ROP = 300 \times 5 + 313 = 1500 + 313 = 1813 \text{ units}
$$

(iii) $z_{0.999} = 3.00$.

$$
SS_{99.9\%} = 3.00 \times 134.16 = 402.5 \approx 403 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\Delta SS = 403 - 313 = 90 \text{ units}
$$

The safety stock increases by 90 units when moving from 99 % to 99.9 % service level — illustrating the *non-linear* cost of the last few decimal points of service.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Explain the standard normal loss function $L(z)$ and describe how it is used to compute safety stock when the target is a **fill rate** rather than a cycle service level.

**Model Answer:**

The standard normal loss function $L(z)$ gives the **expected number of standard units short** when the reorder point is set at $z$ standard deviations above the mean of the demand-during-lead-time distribution. Mathematically:

$$
L(z) = \int_{z}^{\infty} (x - z) \phi(x) \, dx
$$

where $\phi(x)$ is the standard normal density. $L(z)$ is always non-negative, decreasing rapidly as $z$ increases, and is tabulated in standard tables (e.g., $L(0) = 0.3989$, $L(1) = 0.0833$, $L(2) = 0.0080$).

**Procedure to find SS for a target fill rate $\beta$:**

1. Write the fill rate equation:

$$
\beta = 1 - \frac{\sigma_{LT} \cdot L(z)}{Q}
$$

2. Rearrange to solve for $L(z)$:

$$
L(z) = \frac{Q (1 - \beta)}{\sigma_{LT}}
$$

3. Use the $L(z)$ table (or interpolate) to find the corresponding $z$.
4. Compute $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$.

This approach is more **operationally meaningful** in customer-driven industries (e-commerce, FMCG, hospitals) because it directly targets the percentage of demand satisfied — which is what the customer experiences.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A component has the following demand and lead-time characteristics:

- Average daily demand: $D̄ = 80$ units
- Standard deviation of daily demand: $\sigma_d = 12$ units
- Average lead time: $L̄ = 6$ days
- Standard deviation of lead time: $\sigma_L = 1.5$ days

The desired cycle service level is **97.5 %**. Calculate:
(i) $\sigma_{LT}$ (both demand and lead time are variable)
(ii) Safety stock
(iii) Reorder point

**Model Solution:**

(i) $z_{0.975} = 1.96$.

$$
\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{L̄ \sigma_d^2 + D̄^2 \sigma_L^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{6 \times 12^2 + 80^2 \times 1.5^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{6 \times 144 + 6400 \times 2.25}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{864 + 14400} = \sqrt{15264} = 123.55 \text{ units}
$$

(ii) 

$$
SS = 1.96 \times 123.55 = 242.16 \approx 243 \text{ units}
$$

(iii) 

$$
ROP = D̄ \cdot L̄ + SS = 80 \times 6 + 243 = 480 + 243 = 723 \text{ units}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Discuss the **trade-off between service level and inventory cost**. Why is a 100 % service level impractical?

**Model Answer:**

The two principal cost components of an inventory system are:

- **Holding/carrying cost** (incurred by safety stock, capital tied up, insurance, storage)
- **Stockout cost** (lost sales, lost goodwill, production stoppage, penalty)

As the desired service level increases, safety stock rises proportionally (for Type 1 SL, linearly with $z$). Holding cost therefore increases. Conversely, stockout cost decreases. The **total cost curve** is U-shaped; the **optimum service level** is at the minimum of this curve.

**Why 100 % service level is impractical:**

1. To achieve 100 % cycle service level, $z \to \infty$, requiring **infinite safety stock** and infinite holding cost.
2. Even with infinite stock, **true 100 % fill rate** would require stocking for every conceivable demand realisation, including tail events.
3. The marginal cost of the last decimal point of service level is **enormously high** while the marginal benefit (avoiding a few more stockouts) is very small.
4. Operationally, customer satisfaction rarely requires 100 %; industry practice (Amazon, Apple) targets 95–99 %.

Hence, KTU board questions frequently test the conceptual reasoning behind choosing a "good enough" service level.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take $\sqrt{L}$** when lead time is constant and demand is variable (the single most common error).
> 2. **Confusing $D̄ \times \sigma_L$ with $\sigma_D \times L$** in the both-variable formula.
> 3. **Using the wrong z-value** — z = 1.65 corresponds to 95 %, not 90 % and not 99 %. Memorise the table.
> 4. **Not rounding up** the final safety stock to a whole unit.
> 5. **Mixing Type 1 and Type 2 calculations** in the same problem. Read the question: "probability of no stockout" = Type 1; "fraction of demand met" = Type 2.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing 235.2 instead of 236 units).
> 7. **Skipping the $L(z)$ interpolation step** in fill-rate problems — partial marks are awarded for the correct setting up of the equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Service Level** is a *measure*, not a cost. Always specify whether it is Type 1 (cycle) or Type 2 (fill rate).
- **Type 1 SL** is set via the **z-table**; **Type 2 SL** is set via the **loss function $L(z)$**.
- The **canonical safety stock equation** is $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$, regardless of variability.
- $\sigma_{LT}$ has **three forms** depending on what is variable (demand, lead time, or both) — pick the right one every time.
- The **Reorder Point** is **always** $ROP = D̄ L̄ + SS$.
- Round safety stock **up** to the next whole unit.
- The **higher the service level, the higher the safety stock** — but the relationship is *not* linear with SL %; it is linear with the z-value.
- **Both-variable formula:** $\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{L̄ \sigma_d^2 + D̄^2 \sigma_L^2}$ is the **most-tested** formula in KTU ESE.
- **100 % service level is impossible at finite cost** — examiners love this conceptual question.
- **Fill rate** = fraction of demand met from stock; it is *always* ≥ cycle service level for a given safety stock.
- For empirical SL calculations, **count cycles without stockout** in a year, not days.
- Remember the **z-value quick-reference:** 1.28, 1.65, 1.96, 2.33, 3.00 for 90, 95, 97.5, 99, 99.9 %.
- **Always state assumptions** in your answer: normally distributed demand, independent demand and lead time, single-echelon system.
- The optimum service level balances **holding cost** (↑ with SL) against **stockout cost** (↓ with SL).
- KTU-evaluated case studies (e.g., Toyota, Amazon) frequently test the *qualitative* implications of safety stock decisions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Inventory Control: ABC Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Selective Inventory Control: ABC Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ABC Analysis** is a **Selective Inventory Control** technique based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, wherein inventory items are classified into three categories — **A, B, and C** — based on their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)**, so that managerial attention, control effort, and safety stock are concentrated on the most financially significant items.

The **Annual Consumption Value** of an item is mathematically defined as:

$$
ACV_i = U_i \times C_i
$$

where:
- $U_i$ = **Annual Usage (units consumed per year)** of item $i$
- $C_i$ = **Unit Cost (₹ per unit)** of item $i$

Items are then ranked in **descending order** of $ACV$, and cumulative percentages of item count and consumption value are computed to determine class boundaries.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Study Table" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are preparing for the KTU University Exam with 10 different subjects. You notice that just 2 subjects (Mathematics and Electronics) account for nearly 70% of your expected marks, while 5 subjects together contribute only 10%. Logically, you allocate your maximum study hours to those 2 high-weightage subjects, moderate time to 3 medium ones, and minimal revision to the remaining low-weightage ones. **You are applying ABC Analysis to your study plan!**

In a warehouse with **thousands of SKUs** (Stock Keeping Units):
- A small percentage of items tie up a **disproportionately large amount of capital** → these need **tight control** (Class A).
- A large percentage of items consume only a **small fraction of capital** → these can be managed with **loose control** (Class C).

### The Pareto (80/20) Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vilfredo Pareto's Principle (1897):** Roughly **80% of the effects come from 20% of the causes.** Applied to inventory: **~80% of the annual consumption value** is contributed by **~20% of the inventory items.**

| Class | % of Total Items (SKU Count) | % of Annual Consumption Value | Control Intensity |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | 10% – 20% | 70% – 80% | **Tight / Rigorous** |
| **B** | 20% – 30% | 15% – 25% | **Moderate** |
| **C** | 50% – 70% | 5% – 10% | **Loose / Simple** |

### Objectives of ABC Analysis

- **Focus managerial effort** on high-value, critical items.
- **Reduce carrying costs** by optimizing safety stock levels per class.
- **Improve stockout prevention** for high-impact items.
- **Simplify record-keeping** for low-value items.
- **Optimize working capital** locked in inventory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Foundation & Control Logic

The core philosophy of ABC Analysis rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **Effort-Yield Asymmetry:** Managerial time is a scarce resource. Spending equal time on every item is economically irrational.
2. **Value Concentration:** Money in inventory is not uniformly distributed — it is heavily skewed.
3. **Differential Control:** Different items warrant different policies on ordering, review, and physical security.

### Step-by-Step Classification Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Students who simply memorize "A = 10-20%" without showing the cumulative calculation pipeline lose marks. Always demonstrate the **ranking → cumulative % → classification** workflow.

**Step 1 — Inventory Listing:** Enumerate every distinct inventory item (SKU) with its annual usage and unit cost.

**Step 2 — ACV Computation:** For each item $i$, compute $ACV_i = U_i \times C_i$.

**Step 3 — Descending Sort:** Rank all items from highest $ACV$ to lowest $ACV$.

**Step 4 — Cumulative Summation:**
$$
\text{Cumulative \% of Items}_k = \frac{k}{N} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Cumulative \% of Value}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} ACV_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} ACV_i} \times 100
$$

where $N$ = total number of items, and $k$ = rank position.

**Step 5 — Class Assignment:** Apply the classification cutoffs (e.g., items contributing to the first **~75-80%** of cumulative value → **Class A**; next **~15%** → **Class B**; remaining → **Class C**).

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Purpose / Application |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Annual Consumption Value** | $ACV_i = U_i \times C_i$ | Compute monetary worth of item $i$ per year |
| 2 | **% of Total Value** | $\%V_i = \dfrac{ACV_i}{\sum ACV_i} \times 100$ | Share of item in total inventory investment |
| 3 | **Cumulative % of Value** | $\sum \%V_1 \text{ to } \%V_k$ | Determine cut-off boundary for A and B |
| 4 | **% of Total Items** | $\%N_i = \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100$ | Share of item count (each item = 1/N %) |
| 5 | **Cumulative % of Items** | $\dfrac{k}{N} \times 100$ | Track item-count progression |
| 6 | **Pareto Ratio Check** | $\dfrac{\text{Cum \% Value}}{\text{Cum \% Items}}$ | Verify whether items follow 80/20 skew |

### Control Policies by Class

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently test the *control measures* associated with each class. Memorize the following differential control matrix:

| Control Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class C |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Ordering Policy** | Frequent small orders | Moderate ordering | Bulk/large orders |
| **Inventory Records** | Strict, perpetual | Periodic review | Simple, two-bin system |
| **Safety Stock** | Low (high turnover) | Moderate | High (buffer) |
| **Forecasting** | Detailed, scientific | Reasonable | Simple rules of thumb |
| **Physical Verification** | Continuous | Periodic (monthly/quarterly) | Annual / spot check |
| **Management Attention** | Top management | Middle management | Clerical / routine |
| **Lead Time Sensitivity** | Critical | Moderate | Insensitive |

### Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Spare parts in a power plant — a few critical turbine blades justify tight control, while thousands of bolts/nuts fall under Class C.
- **Pharmaceutical Warehouses:** A small number of high-cost active ingredients (Class A) vs. large volumes of excipients (Class C).
- **E-Commerce Fulfillment:** Amazon-style warehouses use ABC logic to determine pick-face placement of SKUs.
- **Aerospace MRO:** Mission-critical components (Class A) cannot tolerate stockouts, while consumables (Class C) follow simple replenishment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### Illustrative Problem (KTU University Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A manufacturing firm maintains **10 inventory items** with the following data. Perform **ABC Analysis** and classify the items.

| Item Code | Annual Usage (Units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P1 | 500 | 200 |
| P2 | 4000 | 20 |
| P3 | 100 | 800 |
| P4 | 2000 | 50 |
| P5 | 50 | 1500 |
| P6 | 800 | 100 |
| P7 | 6000 | 10 |
| P8 | 300 | 250 |
| P9 | 1500 | 60 |
| P10 | 1000 | 40 |

---

### Step 1 — Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV) for Each Item

Using $ACV_i = U_i \times C_i$:

$$
ACV_{P1} = 500 \times 200 = 100000
$$

$$
ACV_{P2} = 4000 \times 20 = 80000
$$

$$
ACV_{P3} = 100 \times 800 = 80000
$$

$$
ACV_{P4} = 2000 \times 50 = 100000
$$

$$
ACV_{P5} = 50 \times 1500 = 75000
$$

$$
ACV_{P6} = 800 \times 100 = 80000
$$

$$
ACV_{P7} = 6000 \times 10 = 60000
$$

$$
ACV_{P8} = 300 \times 250 = 75000
$$

$$
ACV_{P9} = 1500 \times 60 = 90000
$$

$$
ACV_{P10} = 1000 \times 40 = 40000
$$

### Step 2 — Sum Total Annual Consumption Value

$$
\sum ACV = 100000 + 80000 + 80000 + 100000 + 75000 + 80000 + 60000 + 75000 + 90000 + 40000
$$

$$
\sum ACV = 780000 \text{ ₹}
$$

### Step 3 — Rank Items in Descending Order of ACV

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | % of Total Value | Cumulative % Value | % of Items (1/N × 100) | Cumulative % Items |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | P1 | 100000 | 12.82% | 12.82% | 10% | 10% |
| 2 | P4 | 100000 | 12.82% | 25.64% | 10% | 20% |
| 3 | P9 | 90000 | 11.54% | 37.18% | 10% | 30% |
| 4 | P2 | 80000 | 10.26% | 47.44% | 10% | 40% |
| 5 | P3 | 80000 | 10.26% | 57.69% | 10% | 50% |
| 6 | P6 | 80000 | 10.26% | 67.95% | 10% | 60% |
| 7 | P5 | 75000 | 9.62% | 77.56% | 10% | 70% |
| 8 | P8 | 75000 | 9.62% | 87.18% | 10% | 80% |
| 9 | P7 | 60000 | 7.69% | 94.87% | 10% | 90% |
| 10 | P10 | 40000 | 5.13% | 100.00% | 10% | 100% |

### Step 4 — Detailed Derivation of Cumulative Percentages

For the **first ranked item (P1)**:

$$
\%V_1 = \frac{100000}{780000} \times 100 = 12.82\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \% Value}_1 = 12.82\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \% Items}_1 = \frac{1}{10} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

For the **second item (P4)**:

$$
\%V_2 = \frac{100000}{780000} \times 100 = 12.82\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \% Value}_2 = 12.82 + 12.82 = 25.64\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \% Items}_2 = \frac{2}{10} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

For the **seventh item (P5)** — the **critical cutoff**:

$$
\%V_7 = \frac{75000}{780000} \times 100 = 9.62\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \% Value}_7 = 12.82 + 12.82 + 11.54 + 10.26 + 10.26 + 10.26 + 9.62 = 77.56\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \% Items}_7 = \frac{7}{10} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Classification Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Cutoff Convention (Industry Standard):**
> - **Class A:** Items contributing to the **first ~70–80%** of cumulative value.
> - **Class B:** Items contributing to the **next ~15–20%** of cumulative value.
> - **Class C:** Remaining items contributing the **last ~5–10%** of cumulative value.

| Item | Cum % Value | Class | Justification |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| P1 | 12.82% | **A** | In top 70–80% value band |
| P4 | 25.64% | **A** | In top 70–80% value band |
| P9 | 37.18% | **A** | In top 70–80% value band |
| P2 | 47.44% | **A** | In top 70–80% value band |
| P3 | 57.69% | **A** | In top 70–80% value band |
| P6 | 67.95% | **A** | In top 70–80% value band |
| P5 | 77.56% | **A / B Border** | ~78% — fits Class A strict band |
| P8 | 87.18% | **B** | Next value band |
| P7 | 94.87% | **B** | Next value band |
| P10 | 100.00% | **C** | Final low-value residual |

### Step 6 — Final Classification Summary (Industry-Standard Cutoff)

For the **typical KTU examiner-approved split** (A: first 70% value, B: next 20%, C: last 10%):

| Class | Items | Item Count | % of Total Items | % of Total Value |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | P1, P4, P9, P2, P3, P6, P5 | 7 | 70% | 77.56% |
| **B** | P8, P7 | 2 | 20% | 17.31% |
| **C** | P10 | 1 | 10% | 5.13% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Different KTU papers may apply **slightly different cutoffs** (75/20/5 or 80/15/5). The **methodology remains identical** — only the boundary threshold changes. Always state your assumed cutoff in the answer script.

### Step 7 — Interpretive Insight (Pareto Verification)

The **Pareto Ratio** for the top 5 items (50% of items) is:

$$
\text{Pareto Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cum \% Value of Top 5}}{\text{Cum \% Items of Top 5}} = \frac{57.69}{50} = 1.15
$$

This indicates **moderate value concentration** — consistent with Pareto's principle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    H0 -->|80% < Cum Value <= 95%| J0[Class B: Moderate Control]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define ABC Analysis. State the Pareto Principle as applied to inventory management.*

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **ABC Analysis (1.5 Marks):** ABC Analysis is a Selective Inventory Control technique that classifies inventory items into three categories — **A, B, and C** — based on their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV = Annual Usage × Unit Cost)**, in descending order of financial importance. Class A items are tightly controlled, Class B moderately controlled, and Class C loosely controlled.
>
> **Pareto Principle (1.5 Marks):** Also called the **80/20 Rule**, it states that approximately **80% of the total consumption value** of inventory is contributed by only about **20% of the items**, while the remaining 80% of items account for merely 20% of the value. This asymmetric distribution justifies **differential managerial attention**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any **six differences** between the control policies of Class A and Class C items.*

> **Model Answer (3 Marks — Any 6 differences, 0.5 mark each):**

| # | Class A | Class C |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Tight, rigorous control | Loose, simple control |
| 2 | Frequent ordering in small lots | Bulk ordering, infrequent |
| 3 | Perpetual inventory records maintained | Two-bin system / minimal records |
| 4 | Low safety stock (high turnover) | High safety stock buffer |
| 5 | Detailed forecasting methods | Rough estimation / rules of thumb |
| 6 | Continuous physical verification | Annual or spot-check verification |
| 7 | Top-management attention | Clerical / routine handling |
| 8 | Critical for stockout prevention | Stockouts tolerable |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> *(a)* Explain the **steps involved in conducting ABC Analysis** with a suitable flowchart. *(7 Marks)*
> *(b)* A company has **8 items** with the following data. Perform **ABC Analysis** and classify the items. State the assumptions used. *(7 Marks)*

| Item | Annual Usage (Units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| X1 | 1000 | 10 |
| X2 | 200 | 100 |
| X3 | 5000 | 5 |
| X4 | 100 | 200 |
| X5 | 3000 | 20 |
| X6 | 500 | 50 |
| X7 | 50 | 500 |
| X8 | 8000 | 2 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Steps in ABC Analysis (7 Marks):**

1. **List all inventory items** along with their annual usage quantity and per-unit cost. *(1 Mark)*
2. **Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV)** for each item: $ACV_i = U_i \times C_i$. *(1 Mark)*
3. **Rank items in descending order** of ACV — from highest value item to the lowest. *(1 Mark)*
4. **Compute the percentage of total value** for each item: $\%V_i = (ACV_i / \sum ACV) \times 100$. *(1 Mark)*
5. **Compute cumulative percentage of value** and **cumulative percentage of item count**. *(1 Mark)*
6. **Plot a Pareto curve** (cumulative % value vs. cumulative % items). *(1 Mark)*
7. **Apply classification cutoffs** — typically A: top 70-80% value, B: next 15-20%, C: remaining 5-10%. Assign appropriate control policies. *(1 Mark)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute ACV for each item:**

$$
ACV_{X1} = 1000 \times 10 = 10000
$$

$$
ACV_{X2} = 200 \times 100 = 20000
$$

$$
ACV_{X3} = 5000 \times 5 = 25000
$$

$$
ACV_{X4} = 100 \times 200 = 20000
$$

$$
ACV_{X5} = 3000 \times 20 = 60000
$$

$$
ACV_{X6} = 500 \times 50 = 25000
$$

$$
ACV_{X7} = 50 \times 500 = 25000
$$

$$
ACV_{X8} = 8000 \times 2 = 16000
$$

**Step 2: Total ACV:**

$$
\sum ACV = 10000 + 20000 + 25000 + 20000 + 60000 + 25000 + 25000 + 16000 = 201000
$$

**Step 3: Ranked Table with Cumulative Percentages:**

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | % Value | Cum % Value | Cum % Items |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | X5 | 60000 | 29.85% | 29.85% | 12.5% |
| 2 | X3 | 25000 | 12.44% | 42.29% | 25.0% |
| 3 | X6 | 25000 | 12.44% | 54.73% | 37.5% |
| 4 | X7 | 25000 | 12.44% | 67.16% | 50.0% |
| 5 | X2 | 20000 | 9.95% | 77.11% | 62.5% |
| 6 | X4 | 20000 | 9.95% | 87.06% | 75.0% |
| 7 | X8 | 16000 | 7.96% | 95.02% | 87.5% |
| 8 | X1 | 10000 | 4.98% | 100.00% | 100.0% |

**Step 4: Classification (assuming 75/20/5 cutoff):**

| Class | Items | Item Count | % of Items | % of Value |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | X5, X3, X6, X7, X2 | 5 | 62.5% | 77.11% |
| **B** | X4 | 1 | 12.5% | 9.95% |
| **C** | X8, X1 | 2 | 25.0% | 12.94% |

**Assumption:** Class boundary adopted as A ≤ 80% value, B = 80–95%, C > 95%.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> *(a)* What are the **advantages and limitations of ABC Analysis**? *(7 Marks)*
> *(b)* Differentiate between **ABC Analysis and VED Analysis** of inventory. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Advantages & Limitations (7 Marks):**

**Advantages (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Concentrates managerial effort** on high-value items, improving cost-efficiency. *(1 M)*
2. **Reduces carrying costs** by optimizing stock levels per class. *(1 M)*
3. **Improves stockout control** for critical high-value items. *(0.5 M)*
4. **Simplifies record-keeping** for low-value items, saving clerical time. *(0.5 M)*
5. **Foundation for advanced techniques** like EOQ, safety stock, and Just-in-Time. *(0.5 M)*

**Limitations (3.5 Marks):**
1. **Criterion is purely monetary** — ignores criticality, lead time, and obsolescence risk. *(1 M)*
2. **Annual usage may fluctuate** — classification becomes outdated quickly. *(1 M)*
3. **Unit cost variation** (raw material price changes) can distort rankings. *(0.5 M)*
4. **Subjective cutoff boundaries** — different firms use different thresholds. *(0.5 M)*
5. **Does not account for storage constraints** or perishability. *(0.5 M)*

**Part (b) — ABC vs VED Analysis (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | ABC Analysis | VED Analysis |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Basis of Classification** | Annual Consumption Value (₹) | Criticality / Vitality of item |
| **Categories** | A, B, C | V (Vital), E (Essential), D (Desirable) |
| **Focus** | Monetary worth | Production / operational impact |
| **Decision Variable** | Unit cost × annual usage | Effect of stockout on production |
| **Application** | Working capital optimization | Spare parts management / downtime prevention |
| **Control Logic** | Tight on high-value | Tight on Vital items |
| **Used Independently?** | Yes | Yes, but more powerful when combined (ABC-VED Matrix) |
| **Industry Use** | General manufacturing, retail | Maintenance, MRO, heavy engineering |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort items in descending order** before computing cumulative percentages — instant loss of 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Not stating the classification cutoff assumption** (70/20/10 vs 80/15/5) — deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Annual Usage Volume** with **Annual Consumption Value** — read the question carefully!
> 4. **Skipping the justification column** in classification tables — examiners demand logic, not just numbers.
> 5. **Not converting units consistently** (e.g., annual usage in units, value in ₹) — dimensional inconsistency is penalized.
> 6. In theory questions, **failing to mention control policies** for each class — these are 2-mark easy points students miss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Card — Read Before Exam Entry**

- **ABC = Always Better Control**, based on the **Pareto (80/20) Principle**.
- **Core formula:** $ACV = \text{Annual Usage} \times \text{Unit Cost}$.
- **Three classes:** **A** (10–20% items, 70–80% value) | **B** (20–30% items, 15–25% value) | **C** (50–70% items, 5–10% value).
- **Class A** → **tight control**, perpetual records, low safety stock, frequent orders, top management attention.
- **Class B** → **moderate control**, periodic review, min-max system, middle management.
- **Class C** → **loose control**, two-bin system, bulk orders, high safety stock, clerical handling.
- **Always rank in DESCENDING order of ACV** before cumulative computation.
- **Cumulative %** must be calculated for **BOTH** value and item count.
- **Pareto ratio** = Cum % Value ÷ Cum % Items — high values indicate strong concentration.
- **Limitations:** monetary-only criterion, ignores criticality (use VED for that), ignores lead time, ignores obsolescence.
- **ABC + VED** combined analysis gives a **2D matrix** (9 cells) — a high-yield KTU question.
- **Cutoff values are FIRM-DEPENDENT** — always **state the assumption** in your answer script.
- **Diagrams (Pareto curve, ABC pyramid)** fetch 1–2 extra marks in long answers.
- ABC Analysis is a **static snapshot** — must be **recomputed periodically** (typically annually).
- **Real-world use cases:** Pharma, FMCG, e-commerce, aerospace MRO, power plant spares.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: XYZ Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** XYZ Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**XYZ Analysis** is a quantitative inventory classification technique that categorizes inventory items based on the **variability (fluctuation) of their demand** over a specific time horizon. It complements the **ABC Analysis** (which classifies based on annual consumption value) by adding a second dimension that focuses on the **predictability and stability** of demand patterns.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, XYZ Analysis is positioned as a **demand-pattern classification tool** used in Materials Management, Supply Chain Management, and Inventory Control modules. Items are grouped into three categories:

- **X Items** — Demand is highly **stable** with very little variation. Forecasting is easy and reliable.
- **Y Items** — Demand shows **moderate variation** with some unpredictability. Forecasting is reasonably possible but requires attention.
- **Z Items** — Demand is **highly erratic and unpredictable**. Forecasting is very difficult and often unreliable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** XYZ Analysis is a critical sub-topic under *Inventory Control Techniques* in Module 2. It is almost always examined in conjunction with ABC Analysis, making the **ABC-XYZ Matrix** a high-yield concept.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are managing a **Kirana (grocery) store** in Kerala:

| Item Type | Demand Behaviour | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **X Items** | You sell almost the same quantity every day. Easy to predict. | Salt, Rice (basmati), Sugar |
| **Y Items** | Demand fluctuates a bit with the season or local events. | Cold drinks, Batteries, Umbrellas |
| **Z Items** | Demand is wild and unpredictable. Sometimes zero, sometimes a sudden spike. | Bridal sarees, Christmas cakes, Onam special items |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** ABC tells you **"What is the money?"** (Value-based), while XYZ tells you **"How predictable is the demand?"** (Variability-based). Together, they form a **2D Inventory Strategy Grid** for the operations manager.

The standard threshold metric used for classification is the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**, which is the ratio of standard deviation to the mean demand.

> [!TIP]
> **Why CV and not just Standard Deviation?** Because CV is a *dimensionless normalized measure*. It allows fair comparison between items with very different demand volumes (e.g., 10,000 units/month vs. 50 units/month) by removing the effect of the mean.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Classification Logic

The XYZ Analysis procedure follows a structured, repeatable workflow:

1. **Data Collection:** Gather the periodic demand (usually monthly or quarterly) of each inventory item for a defined historical period (typically 12 months).
2. **Compute the Arithmetic Mean ($\bar{x}$):** Calculate the average demand for each item across the observation period.
3. **Compute the Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** Measure the dispersion of demand around the mean.
4. **Compute the Coefficient of Variation (CV):** Normalize the dispersion by dividing by the mean.
5. **Classify into X, Y, or Z:** Apply the standard CV thresholds to assign each item to its category.
6. **Cross-Tabulate with ABC:** Build the 3×3 ABC-XYZ Matrix for strategic inventory decisions.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{x}_i$ | Arithmetic Mean demand of item $i$ | $\bar{x}_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij}}{n}$ | Units per period |
| $\sigma_i$ | Standard Deviation of demand | $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_i)^2}{n}}$ | Same as demand units (population formula) |
| $CV_i$ | Coefficient of Variation | $CV_i = \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\bar{x}_i} \times 100\%$ | Dimensionless percentage |
| $MAD_i$ | Mean Absolute Deviation (alternative) | $MAD_i = \dfrac{\sum \vert x_{ij} - \bar{x}_i \vert}{n}$ | Used in some textbooks instead of $\sigma$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Thresholds (KTU Standard Convention):**
> - **X-Items:** $CV \leq 0.50$ (≤ 50%) — Highly stable demand
> - **Y-Items:** $0.50 < CV \leq 1.00$ (50% to 100%) — Moderate variation
> - **Z-Items:** $CV > 1.00$ (> 100%) — Erratic demand
>
> *Note: Some texts use $\sigma \leq 0.5 \bar{x}$ as the X-boundary. Always check the specific KTU question's given limits.*

### 2.3 The ABC-XYZ Combined Matrix (Strategic Value)

When XYZ results are cross-tabulated with ABC categories, a powerful **9-cell matrix** emerges, which directly dictates inventory policy:

| | **AX** (High Value, Stable) | **AY** (High Value, Variable) | **AZ** (High Value, Erratic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| | **BX** (Med Value, Stable) | **BY** (Med Value, Variable) | **BZ** (Med Value, Erratic) |
| | **CX** (Low Value, Stable) | **CY** (Low Value, Variable) | **CZ** (Low Value, Erratic) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** A **CX item** (cheap and stable) needs almost no managerial attention — keep large buffer stocks. An **AZ item** (expensive and erratic) demands the *highest* managerial effort — use vendor-managed inventory, safety stock analysis, and frequent review.

### 2.4 Strategic Inventory Policy Per Cell

| **Category** | **Inventory Policy** | **Forecasting Effort** | **Review Frequency** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **X-Items** | Lean / Just-in-Time, minimal safety stock | Simple moving average | Periodic (monthly) |
| **Y-Items** | Moderate safety stock, EOQ-based ordering | Exponential smoothing | Frequent (weekly) |
| **Z-Items** | High safety stock, vendor-managed, or make-to-order | Advanced (ARIMA, ML models) | Continuous review |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Coefficient of Variation

The Coefficient of Variation is derived by **normalizing the standard deviation** with respect to the mean to make it scale-invariant.

**Step 1 — Mean (Arithmetic Average):**
For an item $i$ with $n$ historical demand observations $x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{in}$, the arithmetic mean is the sum of all observations divided by $n$:

$$\bar{x}_i = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Variance (Average Squared Deviation):**
The variance is the mean of the squared deviations of each observation from the mean:

$$\sigma_i^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( x_{ij} - \bar{x}_i \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation:**
Taking the non-negative square root of the variance:

$$\sigma_i = \sqrt{\sigma_i^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( x_{ij} - \bar{x}_i \right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why square the deviations?** Squaring eliminates the cancellation of positive and negative deviations (since $\sum (x_{ij} - \bar{x}_i) = 0$ identically). It also penalizes large deviations more heavily, which is desirable when measuring demand risk.

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Variation:**
Divide the standard deviation by the mean to obtain a dimensionless relative measure:

$$CV_i = \frac{\sigma_i}{\bar{x}_i} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The percentage scaling is conventional for easy threshold comparison. The thresholds (0.5, 1.0) refer to this percentage scale, i.e., 50% and 100% respectively.

---

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (5-Item XYZ Classification)

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing firm recorded the following quarterly demand (in units) for 5 items over 5 quarters. Classify them into X, Y, and Z categories.

| Quarter | Item P | Item Q | Item R | Item S | Item T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 | 100 | 50 | 10 | 100 | 50 |
| Q2 | 105 | 80 | 50 | 200 | 200 |
| Q3 | 98 | 30 | 5 | 50 | 10 |
| Q4 | 102 | 70 | 80 | 300 | 300 |
| Q5 | 100 | 60 | 20 | 150 | 5 |

**Threshold:** $X: CV \leq 50\%$, $Y: 50\% < CV \leq 100\%$, $Z: CV > 100\%$

#### Step 1 — Calculate the Mean for Each Item

$$\bar{x}_P = \frac{100 + 105 + 98 + 102 + 100}{5} = \frac{505}{5} = 101 \text{ units}$$

$$\bar{x}_Q = \frac{50 + 80 + 30 + 70 + 60}{5} = \frac{290}{5} = 58 \text{ units}$$

$$\bar{x}_R = \frac{10 + 50 + 5 + 80 + 20}{5} = \frac{165}{5} = 33 \text{ units}$$

$$\bar{x}_S = \frac{100 + 200 + 50 + 300 + 150}{5} = \frac{800}{5} = 160 \text{ units}$$

$$\bar{x}_T = \frac{50 + 200 + 10 + 300 + 5}{5} = \frac{565}{5} = 113 \text{ units}$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate the Squared Deviations

**For Item P ($\bar{x} = 101$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(100-101)^2 &= (-1)^2 = 1 \\
(105-101)^2 &= (4)^2 = 16 \\
(98-101)^2 &= (-3)^2 = 9 \\
(102-101)^2 &= (1)^2 = 1 \\
(100-101)^2 &= (-1)^2 = 1 \\
\text{Sum} &= 1 + 16 + 9 + 1 + 1 = 28
\end{aligned}$$

**For Item Q ($\bar{x} = 58$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(50-58)^2 &= (-8)^2 = 64 \\
(80-58)^2 &= (22)^2 = 484 \\
(30-58)^2 &= (-28)^2 = 784 \\
(70-58)^2 &= (12)^2 = 144 \\
(60-58)^2 &= (2)^2 = 4 \\
\text{Sum} &= 64 + 484 + 784 + 144 + 4 = 1480
\end{aligned}$$

**For Item R ($\bar{x} = 33$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(10-33)^2 &= (-23)^2 = 529 \\
(50-33)^2 &= (17)^2 = 289 \\
(5-33)^2 &= (-28)^2 = 784 \\
(80-33)^2 &= (47)^2 = 2209 \\
(20-33)^2 &= (-13)^2 = 169 \\
\text{Sum} &= 529 + 289 + 784 + 2209 + 169 = 3980
\end{aligned}$$

**For Item S ($\bar{x} = 160$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(100-160)^2 &= (-60)^2 = 3600 \\
(200-160)^2 &= (40)^2 = 1600 \\
(50-160)^2 &= (-110)^2 = 12100 \\
(300-160)^2 &= (140)^2 = 19600 \\
(150-160)^2 &= (-10)^2 = 100 \\
\text{Sum} &= 3600 + 1600 + 12100 + 19600 + 100 = 37000
\end{aligned}$$

**For Item T ($\bar{x} = 113$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(50-113)^2 &= (-63)^2 = 3969 \\
(200-113)^2 &= (87)^2 = 7569 \\
(10-113)^2 &= (-103)^2 = 10609 \\
(300-113)^2 &= (187)^2 = 34969 \\
(5-113)^2 &= (-108)^2 = 11664 \\
\text{Sum} &= 3969 + 7569 + 10609 + 34969 + 11664 = 68780
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate the Standard Deviation

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_P &= \sqrt{\frac{28}{5}} = \sqrt{5.6} \approx 2.37 \\
\sigma_Q &= \sqrt{\frac{1480}{5}} = \sqrt{296} \approx 17.20 \\
\sigma_R &= \sqrt{\frac{3980}{5}} = \sqrt{796} \approx 28.21 \\
\sigma_S &= \sqrt{\frac{37000}{5}} = \sqrt{7400} \approx 86.02 \\
\sigma_T &= \sqrt{\frac{68780}{5}} = \sqrt{13756} \approx 117.29
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate the Coefficient of Variation

$$\begin{aligned}
CV_P &= \frac{2.37}{101} \times 100\% \approx 2.35\% \\
CV_Q &= \frac{17.20}{58} \times 100\% \approx 29.66\% \\
CV_R &= \frac{28.21}{33} \times 100\% \approx 85.49\% \\
CV_S &= \frac{86.02}{160} \times 100\% \approx 53.76\% \\
CV_T &= \frac{117.29}{113} \times 100\% \approx 103.80\%
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 5 — Final Classification Summary

| Item | Mean | Std. Dev. | CV (%) | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | 101 | 2.37 | 2.35% | **X** |
| Q | 58 | 17.20 | 29.66% | **X** |
| S | 160 | 86.02 | 53.76% | **Y** |
| R | 33 | 28.21 | 85.49% | **Y** |
| T | 113 | 117.29 | 103.80% | **Z** |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Even though Item S has a *larger* standard deviation than Item T in absolute terms (86.02 vs. 117.29), Item S has a *smaller* relative variation relative to its mean demand. This is precisely why **CV is the correct metric** — it captures relative risk, not absolute fluctuation.

---

### 3.3 ABC-XYZ Strategic Mapping (Continued from Example)

Assume an additional ABC analysis identified the following value ranks:

| Item | ABC Rank | XYZ Rank | Cell | Strategic Policy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P | A (High Value) | X (Stable) | **AX** | Tight control, JIT, low safety stock |
| Q | B (Medium Value) | X (Stable) | **BX** | EOQ, periodic review |
| S | A (High Value) | Y (Variable) | **AY** | Moderate safety stock, close monitoring |
| R | C (Low Value) | Y (Variable) | **CY** | Two-bin system, basic re-ordering |
| T | A (High Value) | Z (Erratic) | **AZ** | **Critical control:** high safety stock, frequent review, vendor partnership |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 XYZ Analysis Classification Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the algorithmic decision sequence an operations manager follows when performing XYZ classification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Inventory Item i]) --> step1[Step 1: Collect n-period demand data]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Compute arithmetic mean x-bar]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Compute standard deviation sigma]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Compute CV = sigma / x-bar times 100 percent]
    step4 --> dec1{CV less than or equal to 0.50}
    dec1 -- Yes --> catX[Assign Category X: Stable Demand]
    dec1 -- No --> dec2{CV less than or equal to 1.00}
    dec2 -- Yes --> catY[Assign Category Y: Moderate Variation]
    dec2 -- No --> catZ[Assign Category Z: Erratic Demand]
    catX --> cross[Step 5: Cross-tabulate with ABC rank]
    catY --> cross
    catZ --> cross
    cross --> policy[Step 6: Apply inventory policy per cell]
    policy --> endA([End: Item classified and policy assigned])
```

### 4.2 ABC-XYZ 9-Cell Strategic Matrix

This block diagram maps each of the 9 cells to its recommended inventory management approach.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph matrix["ABC-XYZ Strategic Matrix"]
        direction TB
        cellAX["AX: JIT, low safety stock"]
        cellAY["AY: Moderate stock, close monitoring"]
        cellAZ["AZ: High control, vendor partnership"]
        cellBX["BX: EOQ, periodic review"]
        cellBY["BY: Safety stock, weekly review"]
        cellBZ["BZ: Tight control, frequent reorder"]
        cellCX["CX: Large buffer, minimal effort"]
        cellCY["CY: Two-bin system, basic reorder"]
        cellCZ["CZ: Order on demand or drop"]
    end
    inputA[ABC Rank] --> matrix
    inputX[XYZ Rank] --> matrix
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Decision Tree for Inventory Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is demand stable?}
    q1 -- Yes X --> q2{Is value high?}
    q1 -- No Y or Z --> q3{Is value high?}
    q2 -- Yes A --> polAX[JIT with low safety stock]
    q2 -- No B or C --> polLowX[EOQ with simple reorder]
    q3 -- Yes A --> polHighVar[Close monitoring, safety stock]
    q3 -- No C --> polLowVar[Two-bin or make-to-order]
    Yhigh[High value Y] --> polHighVar
    Zany[Erratic demand] --> polHighVar
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
> **Define XYZ Analysis. How is it different from ABC Analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**XYZ Analysis** is an inventory classification technique that groups items based on the **variability of their demand**, measured by the **Coefficient of Variation (CV = σ / mean)**. Items are classified into:

- **X:** Stable demand (CV ≤ 0.5) — highly predictable
- **Y:** Moderate variation (0.5 < CV ≤ 1.0) — somewhat predictable
- **Z:** Erratic demand (CV > 1.0) — highly unpredictable

**Difference from ABC Analysis:**

| Aspect | ABC Analysis | XYZ Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Classification criterion | Annual consumption value (₹) | Variability of demand |
| Metric used | Pareto's 80/20 rule | Coefficient of Variation (CV) |
| Focus | Monetary significance | Forecasting difficulty |
| Purpose | What to control tightly | How to control (review frequency, stock policy) |

*(Award full 3 marks for clear definitions + 1 distinguishing point + the table.)*

---

> **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
> **What is the Coefficient of Variation? Why is it preferred over standard deviation as a classification metric in XYZ Analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** is a dimensionless relative measure of dispersion defined as:

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$$

**Why preferred over $\sigma$:**

1. **Scale Invariance:** It is a *dimensionless* (unitless) ratio, allowing fair comparison between items with vastly different demand volumes (e.g., 10,000 units/month vs. 50 units/month).
2. **Normalized Risk Measure:** It expresses variability *relative to the mean*, which reflects the true demand risk. An item with $\sigma = 100$ on a mean of 200 (CV = 50%) is riskier than one with $\sigma = 100$ on a mean of 1000 (CV = 10%), but plain $\sigma$ cannot capture this.
3. **Universal Thresholds:** Standard CV thresholds (0.5, 1.0) can be applied uniformly across the inventory, whereas $\sigma$ thresholds would have to be item-specific.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified Module 2]`
>
> **(a)** A firm has recorded the following monthly demand (in units) for 6 items over 6 months. Classify each item into X, Y, or Z using the Coefficient of Variation method. Use thresholds: $X: CV \leq 0.5$, $Y: 0.5 < CV \leq 1.0$, $Z: CV > 1.0$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the inventory management policies you would recommend for items falling in the AZ, BX, and CZ categories of the ABC-XYZ matrix. **(7 marks)**

| Month | I1 | I2 | I3 | I4 | I5 | I6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 100 | 20 | 80 | 200 | 10 |
| 2 | 52 | 110 | 80 | 75 | 50 | 200 |
| 3 | 48 | 90 | 10 | 85 | 300 | 5 |
| 4 | 51 | 95 | 100 | 70 | 100 | 300 |
| 5 | 50 | 105 | 5 | 90 | 250 | 50 |
| 6 | 49 | 100 | 60 | 78 | 150 | 100 |

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute the mean for each item (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{I1} &= \frac{50+52+48+51+50+49}{6} = \frac{300}{6} = 50 \\
\bar{x}_{I2} &= \frac{100+110+90+95+105+100}{6} = \frac{600}{6} = 100 \\
\bar{x}_{I3} &= \frac{20+80+10+100+5+60}{6} = \frac{275}{6} \approx 45.83 \\
\bar{x}_{I4} &= \frac{80+75+85+70+90+78}{6} = \frac{478}{6} \approx 79.67 \\
\bar{x}_{I5} &= \frac{200+50+300+100+250+150}{6} = \frac{1050}{6} = 175 \\
\bar{x}_{I6} &= \frac{10+200+5+300+50+100}{6} = \frac{665}{6} \approx 110.83
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Sum of Squared Deviations (SSD) (2 marks):**

For I1: $(50-50)^2 + (52-50)^2 + (48-50)^2 + (51-50)^2 + (50-50)^2 + (49-50)^2 = 0+4+4+1+0+1 = 10$

For I2: $(100-100)^2 + (110-100)^2 + (90-100)^2 + (95-100)^2 + (105-100)^2 + (100-100)^2 = 0+100+100+25+25+0 = 250$

For I3: $(20-45.83)^2 + (80-45.83)^2 + (10-45.83)^2 + (100-45.83)^2 + (5-45.83)^2 + (60-45.83)^2$
$= 667.39 + 1166.59 + 1284.69 + 2933.99 + 1666.59 + 200.69 \approx 7919.94$

For I4: $(80-79.67)^2 + (75-79.67)^2 + (85-79.67)^2 + (70-79.67)^2 + (90-79.67)^2 + (78-79.67)^2$
$\approx 0.11 + 21.81 + 28.41 + 93.51 + 106.71 + 2.79 \approx 253.34$

For I5: $(200-175)^2 + (50-175)^2 + (300-175)^2 + (100-175)^2 + (250-175)^2 + (150-175)^2$
$= 625 + 15625 + 15625 + 5625 + 5625 + 625 = 43750$

For I6: $(10-110.83)^2 + (200-110.83)^2 + (5-110.83)^2 + (300-110.83)^2 + (50-110.83)^2 + (100-110.83)^2$
$\approx 10164.39 + 7953.61 + 11190.69 + 35753.19 + 3698.69 + 117.39 \approx 68877.96$

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviation, CV, and Classify (4 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{I1} &= \sqrt{\frac{10}{6}} = \sqrt{1.667} \approx 1.29 \\
CV_{I1} &= \frac{1.29}{50} \times 100\% \approx 2.58\% \rightarrow \mathbf{X} \\[6pt]
\sigma_{I2} &= \sqrt{\frac{250}{6}} = \sqrt{41.67} \approx 6.45 \\
CV_{I2} &= \frac{6.45}{100} \times 100\% \approx 6.45\% \rightarrow \mathbf{X} \\[6pt]
\sigma_{I3} &= \sqrt{\frac{7919.94}{6}} = \sqrt{1319.99} \approx 36.33 \\
CV_{I3} &= \frac{36.33}{45.83} \times 100\% \approx 79.27\% \rightarrow \mathbf{Y} \\[6pt]
\sigma_{I4} &= \sqrt{\frac{253.34}{6}} = \sqrt{42.22} \approx 6.50 \\
CV_{I4} &= \frac{6.50}{79.67} \times 100\% \approx 8.16\% \rightarrow \mathbf{X} \\[6pt]
\sigma_{I5} &= \sqrt{\frac{43750}{6}} = \sqrt{7291.67} \approx 85.39 \\
CV_{I5} &= \frac{85.39}{175} \times 100\% \approx 48.79\% \rightarrow \mathbf{X} \\[6pt]
\sigma_{I6} &= \sqrt{\frac{68877.96}{6}} = \sqrt{11479.66} \approx 107.14 \\
CV_{I6} &= \frac{107.14}{110.83} \times 100\% \approx 96.67\% \rightarrow \mathbf{Y}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Classification Table:**

| Item | Mean | $\sigma$ | CV (%) | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I1 | 50.00 | 1.29 | 2.58% | **X** |
| I2 | 100.00 | 6.45 | 6.45% | **X** |
| I3 | 45.83 | 36.33 | 79.27% | **Y** |
| I4 | 79.67 | 6.50 | 8.16% | **X** |
| I5 | 175.00 | 85.39 | 48.79% | **X** |
| I6 | 110.83 | 107.14 | 96.67% | **Y** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on boundary values:** Some textbooks use $\sigma$ thresholds (e.g., X: $\sigma \leq 0.5 \bar{x}$). When the question explicitly states "CV method," always compute and quote the CV in percentage form.

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**(i) AZ Items (High Value + Erratic Demand) — 2.5 marks:**

These are the **most critical items** for the operations manager because they consume a large share of inventory investment but their demand is unpredictable. Recommended policies:

- Maintain **high safety stock** (using service-level-based formulas, e.g., $SS = Z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L}$).
- Use **continuous review** (Q-system) rather than periodic review.
- Employ **advanced forecasting techniques** (e.g., exponential smoothing with trend/seasonality, ARIMA, or machine learning models).
- Consider **vendor-managed inventory (VMI)** or **blanket purchase agreements** with reliable suppliers.
- Negotiate **flexible lead times** and explore **dual sourcing** to mitigate supply risk.

**(ii) BX Items (Medium Value + Stable Demand) — 2 marks:**

These are **workhorse items** that consume moderate capital but are highly predictable. They represent the "easy-to-manage" middle ground.

- Apply the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** model for replenishment.
- Use a **periodic review (P-system)** with fixed reorder intervals.
- Maintain **moderate safety stock** based on average demand variability.
- Use **simple forecasting methods** like moving averages.
- Bulk discounts may be considered since the demand is predictable.

**(iii) CZ Items (Low Value + Erratic Demand) — 2.5 marks:**

These items have **low individual impact** but their unpredictability makes them operationally tricky. They often have the **highest total carrying cost** if overstocked.

- Adopt a **"make-to-order" or "order-on-demand"** strategy to avoid inventory buildup.
- Use **standardized components** that can be shared across multiple products.
- Consider **drop-shipping** or direct-from-supplier fulfillment.
- **Eliminate** the item from active inventory if possible (outsourcing the part).
- Use **rough-cut forecasting**; do not invest in sophisticated demand models.
- Maintain a **minimal safety stock** (often zero) and accept occasional stockouts.

---

> **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the ABC-XYZ matrix. Construct the matrix and label each of the 9 cells with an appropriate inventory management policy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The quarterly demand (in hundreds) of a product for the last 8 quarters is: 20, 25, 18, 22, 30, 15, 28, 24. Calculate the Coefficient of Variation and classify the item as X, Y, or Z. Comment on the inventory control implications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

The **ABC-XYZ Matrix** is a 3×3 cross-classification of inventory items that combines:
- **ABC classification** based on **annual consumption value** (₹), using Pareto's principle (A: top 70–80% of value, B: next 15–25%, C: bottom 5–10%).
- **XYZ classification** based on **demand variability** using the Coefficient of Variation (X: CV ≤ 0.5, Y: 0.5 < CV ≤ 1.0, Z: CV > 1.0).

**The 9-Cell Matrix with Inventory Policies:**

| | **X (Stable)** | **Y (Moderate)** | **Z (Erratic)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A (High Value)** | **AX** — JIT, tight control, low safety stock, periodic review | **AY** — Moderate safety stock, close monitoring, EOQ + safety stock | **AZ** — High control, continuous review, VMI, advanced forecasting |
| **B (Medium Value)** | **BX** — EOQ, periodic review, moderate safety stock | **BY** — Weekly review, safety stock based on MAD | **BZ** — Tight reorder points, frequent review, possible drop-ship |
| **C (Low Value)** | **CX** — Two-bin system, large buffer, minimal effort | **CY** — Make-to-order, basic reorder | **CZ** — Order on demand / drop, minimal stock, standardize |

**Strategic Logic:**

- Items along the **diagonal (AX, BY, CZ)** are "balanced" — value matches variability, requiring standard policies.
- Items **above the diagonal (AY, AZ, BZ)** have *higher variability than their value warrants* — these need **tighter managerial attention**.
- Items **below the diagonal (BX, CX, CY)** have *lower variability than their value* — they can be managed with **leaner systems**.

*(Award 3 marks for the matrix structure, 2 marks for policy mapping, 2 marks for strategic interpretation.)*

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Given data:** 20, 25, 18, 22, 30, 15, 28, 24 (in hundreds), $n = 8$

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean (1 mark):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{20+25+18+22+30+15+28+24}{8} = \frac{182}{8} = 22.75$$

**Step 2 — Compute Squared Deviations (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(20-22.75)^2 &= (-2.75)^2 = 7.5625 \\
(25-22.75)^2 &= (2.25)^2 = 5.0625 \\
(18-22.75)^2 &= (-4.75)^2 = 22.5625 \\
(22-22.75)^2 &= (-0.75)^2 = 0.5625 \\
(30-22.75)^2 &= (7.25)^2 = 52.5625 \\
(15-22.75)^2 &= (-7.75)^2 = 60.0625 \\
(28-22.75)^2 &= (5.25)^2 = 27.5625 \\
(24-22.75)^2 &= (1.25)^2 = 1.5625 \\
\text{Sum of Squared Deviations} &= 177.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Standard Deviation (1 mark):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{177.50}{8}} = \sqrt{22.1875} \approx 4.71$$

**Step 4 — Compute CV (1 mark):**

$$CV = \frac{4.71}{22.75} \times 100\% \approx 20.70\%$$

**Step 5 — Classification and Comments (1 mark):**

Since $CV \approx 20.70\% \leq 50\%$, the item is classified as **X (Stable Demand)**.

**Inventory Control Implications:**

- The demand is **highly predictable** — a simple **moving-average forecast** will suffice.
- The firm can adopt a **Just-in-Time (JIT)** approach with **low safety stock**.
- A **periodic review (P-system)** with fixed reorder intervals is appropriate.
- The **EOQ model** can be applied confidently to minimize total inventory costs.
- The firm can negotiate **long-term contracts** with suppliers to lock in favourable prices.
- Low forecasting effort is required, freeing managerial time for Z-class items.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ABC with XYZ (2-mark penalty):** ABC is value-based (₹ consumption × unit cost), XYZ is variability-based (CV). Many students mix them up.
> 2. **Wrong Standard Deviation Formula (1–2 marks lost):** The KTU convention for XYZ uses the **population formula** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum(x-\bar{x})^2 / n}$, NOT the sample formula with $n-1$. Using $n-1$ will give a slightly different answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the ×100% Scaling:** Always express CV in percentage form, then compare against 50% and 100% thresholds. A CV of 0.51 (as a fraction) is **Y**, not X.
> 4. **Boundary Inclusivity:** The threshold $CV \leq 0.5$ means **X includes 0.5**. A common error is to write 0.5 as the boundary of Y.
> 5. **Using $\sigma$ instead of CV:** Some students skip the CV calculation and classify directly on $\sigma$. This is acceptable only if the question explicitly says so. Otherwise, always compute CV.
> 6. **Neglecting the ABC-XYZ linkage:** When asked about inventory policy, students often describe generic policies without mapping to the 9 cells. Examiners reward specific cell-by-cell policy mapping.
> 7. **Misinterpreting the Question Type:** A 14-mark question has 2 sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Skipping one sub-part forfeits 50% of the marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for XYZ Analysis (Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> - **Core Idea:** XYZ classifies inventory by **demand variability**, not by value.
> - **Three Categories:** X (stable, $CV \leq 0.5$), Y (moderate, $0.5 < CV \leq 1.0$), Z (erratic, $CV > 1.0$).
> - **Key Metric:** **Coefficient of Variation (CV = $\sigma / \bar{x} \times 100\%$)** — dimensionless, scale-invariant.
> - **Why CV and not $\sigma$:** Allows fair comparison across items with different demand volumes.
> - **Standard Deviation Formula:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 / n}$ (population form, $n$ in the denominator).
> - **Step Sequence:** Collect data → Mean → Squared deviations → $\sigma$ → CV → Classify.
> - **ABC-XYZ Matrix:** 3×3 grid combining value (A/B/C) and variability (X/Y/Z) classifications.
> - **Strategic Asymmetry:** Items above the diagonal (e.g., AZ, AY) need **tightest** control; items below (e.g., CX) need the **least**.
> - **X-Items Policy:** JIT, low safety stock, simple forecasting, periodic review.
> - **Y-Items Policy:** Moderate safety stock, exponential smoothing, weekly review.
> - **Z-Items Policy:** High safety stock, advanced forecasting (ARIMA/ML), continuous review, VMI, or make-to-order.
> - **Boundary Value Trap:** A CV of exactly **0.5** is X (inclusive upper bound).
> - **Always scale CV by 100%** when comparing to the 50% and 100% thresholds.
> - **Most common exam format:** Numerical classification of 5–6 items + policy discussion for 2–3 ABC-XYZ cells.
> - **High-Yield Integration:** XYZ Analysis is almost always paired with ABC Analysis in KTU exam questions; mastering the **9-cell matrix** is essential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VED Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** VED Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable Analysis)** is a *selective inventory control technique* used predominantly in **spare parts management and maintenance operations**. It classifies inventory items (typically spare parts, components, or consumables) based on the **criticality of their unavailability** to the production system — i.e., *how severely the absence of an item would impact the continuity, safety, and efficiency of operations*.

The three categories are formally defined as follows:

- **Vital (V):** Items whose non-availability will **completely halt production**, cause irreversible damage to machinery, or create serious safety hazards. These items demand **mandatory, uninterrupted stocking** with high safety stock and strict reorder-level discipline.
- **Essential (E):** Items whose non-availability will **significantly slow down or reduce production efficiency** but will not bring the plant to a complete standstill. They require **reasonable buffer stocks** and are managed through moderate control.
- **Desirable (D):** Items whose non-availability will have **negligible or no significant effect** on production continuity. They can be procured on an *as-and-when-needed* basis with **minimal or zero safety stock**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Pharmacy Model**
>
> Imagine a hospital's pharmacy cabinet:
>
> - **Vital (V)** items are like **emergency life-saving drugs** — insulin for a diabetic patient, adrenaline for an allergic reaction, or oxygen for a critical patient. If these are missing *even for a minute*, life is at risk. They are **always stocked** in abundance, monitored constantly, and replenished aggressively.
>
> - **Essential (E)** items are like **strong antibiotics or surgical sutures** — needed for most major procedures. If they are missing, surgeries may be delayed, infections may worsen, and recovery slows down, but the hospital does not immediately shut down. These are stocked with **controlled buffer levels**.
>
> - **Desirable (D)** items are like **vitamins, cosmetic bandages, or tonics** — they improve comfort or quality of life but are not critical to survival. If they run out, patients can wait or be referred to a nearby pharmacy. These are ordered **on demand** with minimal inventory investment.

**VED Analysis applies the same logic to a factory's spare parts cupboard:** some spare parts are *mission-critical* (Vital), some are *operationally important* (Essential), and some are *nice-to-have* (Desirable). This classification enables the operations manager to allocate the firm's scarce capital, storage space, and managerial attention **proportionally to criticality**, not equally across all items.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conceptual Boundaries

> [!TIP]
> **Key Boundaries that Distinguish VED from other Techniques:**
>
> - **ABC Analysis** classifies items based on **Annual Consumption Value (₹)** — a *monetary* axis.
> - **VED Analysis** classifies items based on **Criticality of Shortage** — a *functional/operational* axis.
> - VED is therefore called a **criticality-based** technique, while ABC is a **value-based** technique.
> - VED is most commonly applied to **spares, MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating) items, and lubricants** in production environments.

---

### 1.4 Why VED Matters in KTU Context

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, VED Analysis is taught as a **Module 2 core concept** under *Inventory Control Techniques*. It carries significant weightage in both **Continuous Assessment (CA)** and the **End Semester Examination (ESE)**, particularly in:

- 3-mark short-answer questions (definitions, classification logic)
- 14-mark analytical questions (worked numerical problems, comparison tables)
- Case-study-based applications in **factory maintenance management**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of VED Classification

The classification is performed by asking **three foundational questions** for every inventory item:

1. **What happens to production output if this item runs out for 24 hours?**
2. **What happens to machine condition / safety if this item runs out for 24 hours?**
3. **What is the cost of downtime — both direct (lost production) and indirect (contractual penalties, safety incidents)?**

The answers map directly to V, E, or D.

---

### 2.2 Classification Decision Matrix (Conceptual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The decision rules below are the **most exam-relevant** content in this section. Memorize the threshold conditions.

| Decision Criterion | Vital (V) | Essential (E) | Desirable (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Production Halts Completely? | **Yes** | No | No |
| Production Slows / Degrades? | Yes | **Yes** | No |
| Machine Damage Risk? | **Severe / Irreversible** | Moderate | Negligible |
| Safety Hazard? | **Imminent** | Possible | None |
| Inventory Policy | Always in stock, high safety stock, strict re-order | Moderate buffer, periodic review | Order on demand, minimal stock |
| Control Attention | **Maximum (Class A monitoring)** | Medium | Low |

---

### 2.3 Quantitative Framework — Criticality Scoring Model

While the qualitative classification is most common, **KTU examiners frequently ask for a numerical scoring approach** in 14-mark questions. The **Criticality Score (CS)** for each item is computed as:

$$
CS_i = w_1 \cdot PL_i + w_2 \cdot DT_i + w_3 \cdot SA_i + w_4 \cdot LT_i
$$

Where:

- $CS_i$ = Criticality Score of item $i$ (range 0 to 1)
- $PL_i$ = Normalized **Production Loss** impact score
- $DT_i$ = Normalized **Downtime Cost** impact score
- $SA_i$ = Normalized **Safety Hazard** score
- $LT_i$ = Normalized **Lead-time Risk** score
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Importance weights, such that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$

**Standard Classification Thresholds (industry-typical, accepted by KTU):**

$$
CS \geq 0.70 \implies \text{Vital (V)}
$$

$$
0.40 \leq CS < 0.70 \implies \text{Essential (E)}
$$

$$
CS < 0.40 \implies \text{Desirable (D)}
$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **complete high-yield formula bank** for VED Analysis. Revise from this section before every KTU exam.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Criticality Score | $CS = w_1 PL + w_2 DT + w_3 SA + w_4 LT$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq CS \leq 1$ |
| Weight Constraint | $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ | Sum of weights equals **1** |
| Vital Threshold | $CS \geq 0.70$ | High control, high stock |
| Essential Threshold | $0.40 \leq CS < 0.70$ | Moderate control |
| Desirable Threshold | $CS < 0.40$ | Low / zero stock |
| Reorder Level (V) | $ROL_V = (D_{max} \times L_{max}) + SS_V$ | $SS_V$ is **high** (20–30% of avg. demand) |
| Reorder Level (E) | $ROL_E = (D_{max} \times L_{max}) + SS_E$ | $SS_E$ is **moderate** (10–15%) |
| Reorder Level (D) | $ROL_D = (D_{max} \times L_{max}) + SS_D$ | $SS_D$ is **low** (0–5%) |
| EOQ (Economic Order Quantity) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 A D}{h}}$ | Universal formula |
| % of Items in V | $n_V \div N \times 100$ | $n_V$ = # of Vital items, $N$ = Total items |
| % of Items in E | $n_E \div N \times 100$ | Essential share |
| % of Items in D | $n_D \div N \times 100$ | Desirable share |

> [!TIP]
> The notation $D_{max}$ refers to **maximum daily consumption** and $L_{max}$ refers to **maximum lead time (in days)**. $SS$ stands for **Safety Stock**, and $A$ is ordering cost per order, $D$ is annual demand, and $h$ is holding cost per unit per year.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why every production / maintenance engineer must understand VED:**
>
> 1. **Capital Efficiency:** A manufacturing plant may have 10,000+ spare parts. VED ensures the firm's working capital is **not trapped** in non-critical stocks.
> 2. **Downtime Minimization:** Vital items, by definition, halt production. VED ensures they are *never* out of stock.
> 3. **Storage Optimization:** Warehouse space is expensive. VED directs space to where it yields the highest operational return.
> 4. **Procurement Strategy:** Vital items warrant **long-term vendor contracts**; Desirable items can use **spot purchase**.
> 5. **Combined Frameworks:** In practice, VED is cross-tabulated with **ABC** to produce an integrated **ABC-VED matrix** for surgical precision in inventory control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Complete VED Classification Procedure (Algorithmic Flow)

The KTU examiner expects a student to **explicitly enumerate these steps** in a 7-mark sub-question. Below is the **exhaustive, copy-ready** procedure.

**Step 1 — Inventory Enumeration:**
Prepare a master list of all spare parts / inventory items to be classified. Let this set be denoted as $S = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_N\}$, where $N$ is the total number of items.

**Step 2 — Define the Criticality Dimensions:**
Identify the 4 standard impact factors for each item $i$:
- $PL_i$ (Production Loss)
- $DT_i$ (Downtime Cost)
- $SA_i$ (Safety Hazard)
- $LT_i$ (Lead-time Risk)

**Step 3 — Score Each Dimension (0 to 1 scale):**
For each item, assign normalized scores. A common 5-point Likert scale is converted as:
- 0 = No impact
- 0.25 = Low impact
- 0.50 = Moderate impact
- 0.75 = High impact
- 1.00 = Critical / Severe impact

**Step 4 — Assign Weights:**
Management (or the KTU problem statement) assigns weights to each dimension. For example, in a heavy-engineering plant:

$$
w_1 = 0.40, \quad w_2 = 0.30, \quad w_3 = 0.20, \quad w_4 = 0.10
$$

**Verification of weight constraint:**

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Criticality Score:**

$$
CS_i = 0.40 \cdot PL_i + 0.30 \cdot DT_i + 0.20 \cdot SA_i + 0.10 \cdot LT_i
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Thresholds:**

$$
\text{If } CS_i \geq 0.70 \Rightarrow V, \quad \text{If } 0.40 \leq CS_i < 0.70 \Rightarrow E, \quad \text{If } CS_i < 0.40 \Rightarrow D
$$

**Step 7 — Group and Tally:**
Count the number of items in each category, compute percentage share:

$$
\%V = \frac{n_V}{N} \times 100, \quad \%E = \frac{n_E}{N} \times 100, \quad \%D = \frac{n_D}{N} \times 100
$$

**Step 8 — Apply Inventory Policies:**
Formulate distinct **inventory control policies** (reorder level, safety stock, EOQ, review period) for each V, E, D category.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A manufacturing firm maintains 10 spare parts. A panel of experts has scored each part on four criticality dimensions (0 to 1 scale) as given below. Weights are: $w_1 = 0.40$, $w_2 = 0.30$, $w_3 = 0.20$, $w_4 = 0.10$. Classify each part as V, E, or D using the threshold scheme $V: CS \geq 0.70$, $E: 0.40 \leq CS < 0.70$, $D: CS < 0.40$. Also compute the category-wise share of items.

| Item Code | $PL_i$ | $DT_i$ | $SA_i$ | $LT_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SP-01 | 0.95 | 0.90 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
| SP-02 | 0.80 | 0.75 | 0.50 | 0.60 |
| SP-03 | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.40 |
| SP-04 | 0.85 | 0.90 | 0.70 | 0.75 |
| SP-05 | 0.50 | 0.55 | 0.40 | 0.30 |
| SP-06 | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.05 | 0.25 |
| SP-07 | 0.90 | 0.85 | 0.60 | 0.70 |
| SP-08 | 0.45 | 0.50 | 0.20 | 0.35 |
| SP-09 | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.00 | 0.20 |
| SP-10 | 0.75 | 0.80 | 0.55 | 0.65 |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

**Item SP-01:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{01} &= (0.40 \times 0.95) + (0.30 \times 0.90) + (0.20 \times 1.00) + (0.10 \times 0.80) \\
&= 0.380 + 0.270 + 0.200 + 0.080 \\
&= 0.930
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.930 \geq 0.70$, **SP-01 = Vital (V)**.

**Item SP-02:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{02} &= (0.40 \times 0.80) + (0.30 \times 0.75) + (0.20 \times 0.50) + (0.10 \times 0.60) \\
&= 0.320 + 0.225 + 0.100 + 0.060 \\
&= 0.705
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.705 \geq 0.70$, **SP-02 = Vital (V)**.

**Item SP-03:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{03} &= (0.40 \times 0.30) + (0.30 \times 0.20) + (0.20 \times 0.10) + (0.10 \times 0.40) \\
&= 0.120 + 0.060 + 0.020 + 0.040 \\
&= 0.240
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.240 < 0.40$, **SP-03 = Desirable (D)**.

**Item SP-04:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{04} &= (0.40 \times 0.85) + (0.30 \times 0.90) + (0.20 \times 0.70) + (0.10 \times 0.75) \\
&= 0.340 + 0.270 + 0.140 + 0.075 \\
&= 0.825
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.825 \geq 0.70$, **SP-04 = Vital (V)**.

**Item SP-05:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{05} &= (0.40 \times 0.50) + (0.30 \times 0.55) + (0.20 \times 0.40) + (0.10 \times 0.30) \\
&= 0.200 + 0.165 + 0.080 + 0.030 \\
&= 0.475
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.40 \leq 0.475 < 0.70$, **SP-05 = Essential (E)**.

**Item SP-06:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{06} &= (0.40 \times 0.20) + (0.30 \times 0.15) + (0.20 \times 0.05) + (0.10 \times 0.25) \\
&= 0.080 + 0.045 + 0.010 + 0.025 \\
&= 0.160
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.160 < 0.40$, **SP-06 = Desirable (D)**.

**Item SP-07:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{07} &= (0.40 \times 0.90) + (0.30 \times 0.85) + (0.20 \times 0.60) + (0.10 \times 0.70) \\
&= 0.360 + 0.255 + 0.120 + 0.070 \\
&= 0.805
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.805 \geq 0.70$, **SP-07 = Vital (V)**.

**Item SP-08:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{08} &= (0.40 \times 0.45) + (0.30 \times 0.50) + (0.20 \times 0.20) + (0.10 \times 0.35) \\
&= 0.180 + 0.150 + 0.040 + 0.035 \\
&= 0.405
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.40 \leq 0.405 < 0.70$, **SP-08 = Essential (E)**.

**Item SP-09:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{09} &= (0.40 \times 0.10) + (0.30 \times 0.05) + (0.20 \times 0.00) + (0.10 \times 0.20) \\
&= 0.040 + 0.015 + 0.000 + 0.020 \\
&= 0.075
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.075 < 0.40$, **SP-09 = Desirable (D)**.

**Item SP-10:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{10} &= (0.40 \times 0.75) + (0.30 \times 0.80) + (0.20 \times 0.55) + (0.10 \times 0.65) \\
&= 0.300 + 0.240 + 0.110 + 0.065 \\
&= 0.715
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.715 \geq 0.70$, **SP-10 = Vital (V)**.

---

### 3.4 Consolidated Classification Result

| Item Code | $CS_i$ | Category |
|---|---|---|
| SP-01 | 0.930 | Vital |
| SP-02 | 0.705 | Vital |
| SP-03 | 0.240 | Desirable |
| SP-04 | 0.825 | Vital |
| SP-05 | 0.475 | Essential |
| SP-06 | 0.160 | Desirable |
| SP-07 | 0.805 | Vital |
| SP-08 | 0.405 | Essential |
| SP-09 | 0.075 | Desirable |
| SP-10 | 0.715 | Vital |

**Tally and Category Share:**

$$
n_V = 5 \quad (\text{SP-01, SP-02, SP-04, SP-07, SP-10})
$$

$$
n_E = 2 \quad (\text{SP-05, SP-08})
$$

$$
n_D = 3 \quad (\text{SP-03, SP-06, SP-09})
$$

$$
\%V = \frac{5}{10} \times 100 = 50\%, \quad \%E = \frac{2}{10} \times 100 = 20\%, \quad \%D = \frac{3}{10} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Inventory Policy Recommendation Table:**

| Category | % of Items | Inventory Control Policy |
|---|---|---|
| Vital (V) | **50%** | Always in stock, $\geq 25\%$ safety stock, monthly review, dual sourcing, vendor-managed inventory |
| Essential (E) | **20%** | Buffer stock of 10–15%, bi-monthly review, single-source preferred |
| Desirable (D) | **30%** | Order on demand, $\leq 5\%$ safety stock, quarterly review, spot purchase allowed |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that the **Vital category has the highest % of items (50%)** — this is realistic for a spare parts inventory where machine reliability depends on a few critical spares. The KTU answer should explicitly state this insight.

```mermaid
    H --> K[Inventory Policy: Minimal Stock, Order on Demand]

```
