# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inputs to the Master Production Schedule

The MPS does not exist in isolation. It is computed from four primary inputs:

1. **End-item Forecasts** — the demand projection for each finished good.
2. **Actual Customer Orders (Booked Orders)** — confirmed purchase orders received.
3. **Inventory Status / Beginning Inventory** — on-hand stock for each end item.
4. **Production Capacity Constraints** — derived from the Aggregate Plan.

## 2.2 The Two Demand Sources — Forecast vs. Orders

| Aspect | Forecast (Predicted Demand) | Actual Customer Orders |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Nature | Estimated, uncommitted | Firm, committed |
| Variability | High | Low |
| Used in MPS for | Future periods (beyond order horizon) | Current & near-term periods |
| Frozen? | No | Yes |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Logic Rule:** In the **near-term frozen zone**, the MPS is driven by **actual orders**, not forecasts. In the **far zone**, it is driven by **forecasts**. This dual logic is the essence of the MPS.

## 2.3 Available-to-Promise (ATP) — A Critical MPS Concept

When a customer places an order, the salesperson must know how much **uncommitted inventory** exists in the next planned MPS receipt. This is the **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** quantity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ATP Definition:** *The quantity of a product that is currently uncommitted and available to be promised to a new customer based on the next scheduled MPS receipt.*

### ATP Calculation Rule
$$\text{ATP} = \text{MPS Quantity in next receipt period} - \text{Committed Customer Orders before the next MPS receipt}$$

If the customer order exceeds ATP, the order is either **back-ordered** or **partial-shipped**.

## 2.4 Projected Available Balance (PAB) — The MPS Balance Equation

The **Projected Available Balance (PAB)** is the projected on-hand inventory at the end of each time period, calculated as:

$$\text{PAB}_t = \text{PAB}_{t-1} + \text{MPS}_t - \text{(Forecast}_t \text{ or Actual Demand}_t\text{)}$$

Where:
- $\text{PAB}_{t-1}$ = Projected Available Balance at the end of the previous period
- $\text{MPS}_t$ = Quantity scheduled to be produced in period $t$
- $\text{Forecast}_t$ = Expected demand (or actual orders) in period $t$

### Boundary Condition
- Initial $\text{PAB}_0$ = Beginning Inventory (known).
- $\text{PAB}_t$ must never fall below **safety stock** (a non-negative lower bound under the standard MPS rule, though safety stock exceptions exist).

## 2.5 Time Fences in MPS — Demand Fence & Planning Fence

The MPS uses two critical **time fences** that control how the schedule can be changed:

| Time Fence | Duration (typical) | Rule | Rationale |
|------------|---------------------|------|-----------|
| **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** | Inside DTF (e.g., 1–3 weeks) | Only **firm customer orders** are used. Forecasts are ignored. MPS quantities are **frozen**. | Protects the shop floor and suppliers from last-minute panic changes. |
| **Planning Time Fence (PTF)** | Inside PTF, beyond DTF (e.g., 3–8 weeks) | MPS quantities **can be changed**, but only by **replanning** (offsetting within the frozen window). | Allows minor adjustments while protecting capacity. |
| **Beyond PTF** | Far future (e.g., > 8 weeks) | MPS can be **completely revised** based on new forecasts. | High uncertainty, low cost of change. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Mixing up the two fences. **DTF = Frozen (only orders).** **PTF = Adjustable but capacity-protected.** Beyond PTF = Free to change.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Projected Available Balance** | $\text{PAB}_t = \text{PAB}_{t-1} + \text{MPS}_t - \text{Demand}_t$ | Units of finished product |
| 2 | **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** | $\text{ATP} = \text{Next MPS Receipt} - \text{Committed Orders before receipt}$ | Units of finished product |
| 3 | **Cumulative ATP** (over multiple periods) | $\text{ATP}_{\text{cum}} = \sum \text{MPS}_t - \sum \text{Committed Orders}_t$ | Used for long-horizon promises |
| 4 | **Beginning Inventory** | $\text{PAB}_0$ | Given in the problem |
| 5 | **Planned Order Release** (in MRP context) | $\text{POR} = \text{MPS Receipt} - \text{Lead Time Offset}$ | Time-phased |
| 6 | **Safety Stock Constraint** | $\text{PAB}_t \geq \text{Safety Stock Level}$ | Non-negative or above SS |
| 7 | **Lot-Sizing Rule (L4L)** | $\text{MPS}_t = \text{Net Requirement}_t$ | Lot-for-Lot |
| 8 | **Lot-Sizing Rule (EOQ)** | $\text{MPS}_t = \text{EOQ Qty}$, triggered when PAB < reorder point | Economic Order Quantity |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table, we have used $\vert$ avoidance by writing all absolute value requirements as **non-negative PAB** statements, ensuring the markdown table integrity is preserved.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of MPS

- **Automotive Industry** — Toyota uses MPS to schedule the build sequence of every vehicle variant on the final assembly line.
- **Electronics Manufacturing** — Apple uses MPS to coordinate iPhone production across global assembly partners.
- **FMCG / Consumer Goods** — Procter & Gamble uses MPS to time production runs of Tide detergent to match retail seasonal demand spikes.
- **Aerospace** — Boeing uses MPS to schedule airframe production given long lead times for engines and avionics.

In every case, the MPS acts as the **single source of truth** that aligns sales, production, procurement, and finance.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Problems & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The MPS Row Format (Standard KTU Board Layout)

Every MPS problem in the KTU 2024 scheme uses this standard table layout:

| Column | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| **Period (Weeks)** | $1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$ |
| **Forecast** | Predicted demand |
| **Customer Orders (Booked)** | Confirmed orders |
| **Projected Available Balance (PAB)** | Projected inventory |
| **MPS Quantity (Production)** | Planned production |
| **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** | Uncommitted quantity |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Basic MPS PAB Computation (14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement
A company produces **Product X**. The forecast for the next 5 weeks is: **40, 50, 60, 50, 40 units** respectively. The customer orders booked for each week are: **30, 45, 25, 20, 15 units**. The beginning inventory is **30 units**. The safety stock is **0**. The company uses **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** lot-sizing. The lead time is 1 week (production orders are released 1 week in advance). Construct the MPS.

### Step 1 — Define the Net Requirement Logic

For each period, the net requirement is calculated as:

$$\text{Net Req}_t = \text{Demand}_t - \text{PAB}_{t-1}$$

If $\text{Net Req}_t \leq 0$, then $\text{MPS}_t = 0$.
If $\text{Net Req}_t > 0$, then $\text{MPS}_t = \text{Net Req}_t$ (Lot-for-Lot rule).

### Step 2 — Initialize Period 0

Given: $\text{PAB}_0 = 30$ (Beginning Inventory)

### Step 3 — Period-by-Period Walk

**Period 1:**
- Demand = Forecast or Order? Use the **higher of the two** (conservative planning) OR follow problem statement. Here, Forecast (40) > Orders (30). Per KTU convention, use **Forecast** when not in demand time fence.
- $\text{Net Req}_1 = 40 - 30 = 10$
- $\text{MPS}_1 = 10$
- $\text{PAB}_1 = 30 + 10 - 40 = 0$

**Period 2:**
- Demand = 50
- $\text{Net Req}_2 = 50 - 0 = 50$
- $\text{MPS}_2 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_2 = 0 + 50 - 50 = 0$

**Period 3:**
- Demand = 60
- $\text{Net Req}_3 = 60 - 0 = 60$
- $\text{MPS}_3 = 60$
- $\text{PAB}_3 = 0 + 60 - 60 = 0$

**Period 4:**
- Demand = 50
- $\text{Net Req}_4 = 50 - 0 = 50$
- $\text{MPS}_4 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_4 = 0 + 50 - 50 = 0$

**Period 5:**
- Demand = 40
- $\text{Net Req}_5 = 40 - 0 = 40$
- $\text{MPS}_5 = 40$
- $\text{PAB}_5 = 0 + 40 - 40 = 0$

### Step 4 — Final MPS Table

| Period | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast | — | 40 | 50 | 60 | 50 | 40 |
| Customer Orders | — | 30 | 45 | 25 | 20 | 15 |
| PAB | 30 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **MPS Quantity** | — | **10** | **50** | **60** | **50** | **40** |
| ATP (next receipt − committed) | — | 0 | 5 | 35 | 30 | 25 |

### Step 5 — ATP Calculation Detail

For Period 1, the next MPS receipt is 10 units in Period 1 itself, and the committed orders before that receipt = 0 (since receipt is at start of period 1). So ATP$_1$ = 10.

For Period 2, ATP$_2$ = MPS$_2$ − (Orders for Period 1) = 50 − 30 − 0 (PAB$_1$) = wait — using the rule: ATP is the *uncommitted portion of the next MPS receipt*. So:

$$\text{ATP}_2 = \text{MPS}_2 - \text{Orders}_1 = 50 - 30 = 20$$

(plus 0 PAB = 20). Recalibrated ATP table:

| Period | ATP Calculation | ATP |
|--------|-----------------|-----|
| 1 | $10 - 0$ | 10 |
| 2 | $50 - 30$ | 20 |
| 3 | $60 - 45 - 0$ | 15 |
| 4 | $50 - 25$ | 25 |
| 5 | $40 - 20$ | 20 |

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — MPS with Safety Stock (Full 14-Mark Solution)

### Problem Statement
A company has beginning inventory of **20 units**. The forecast for weeks 1–6 is **50, 40, 60, 70, 50, 50 units**. Customer orders are **20, 30, 40, 20, 30, 20 units**. Safety stock is **10 units**. The lead time is 1 week. Use Lot-for-Lot.

### Step 1 — Modify the PAB Boundary Condition

$$\text{PAB}_t \geq \text{Safety Stock} = 10$$

So PAB cannot drop below 10.

### Step 2 — Period-by-Period Calculation

**Period 0:** $\text{PAB}_0 = 20$

**Period 1:**
- Demand = 50
- $\text{Net Req}_1 = 50 - 20 = 30$
- $\text{MPS}_1 = 30$
- $\text{PAB}_1 = 20 + 30 - 50 = 0$ → **Below safety stock!**

### Step 3 — Correction with Safety Stock Constraint

Since PAB$_1$ = 0 < 10, we must produce extra to cover the safety stock shortfall:

$$\text{Adjusted MPS}_1 = \text{Net Req}_1 + (\text{Safety Stock} - \text{PAB}_1) = 30 + (10 - 0) = 40$$

Re-checking: $\text{PAB}_1 = 20 + 40 - 50 = 10$ ✓ (meets safety stock)

**Period 2:**
- Demand = 40
- $\text{Net Req}_2 = 40 - 10 = 30$
- $\text{MPS}_2 = 30$
- $\text{PAB}_2 = 10 + 30 - 40 = 0$ → Below safety stock again.

Adjust: $\text{Adjusted MPS}_2 = 30 + (10 - 0) = 40$
Re-check: $\text{PAB}_2 = 10 + 40 - 40 = 10$ ✓

**Period 3:**
- Demand = 60
- $\text{Net Req}_3 = 60 - 10 = 50$
- $\text{MPS}_3 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_3 = 10 + 50 - 60 = 0$ → Below SS.
- $\text{Adjusted MPS}_3 = 50 + 10 = 60$
- $\text{PAB}_3 = 10 + 60 - 60 = 10$ ✓

**Period 4:**
- Demand = 70
- $\text{Net Req}_4 = 70 - 10 = 60$
- $\text{MPS}_4 = 60$
- $\text{PAB}_4 = 10 + 60 - 70 = 0$ → Below SS.
- $\text{Adjusted MPS}_4 = 60 + 10 = 70$
- $\text{PAB}_4 = 10 + 70 - 70 = 10$ ✓

**Period 5:**
- Demand = 50
- $\text{Net Req}_5 = 50 - 10 = 40$
- $\text{MPS}_5 = 40$
- $\text{PAB}_5 = 10 + 40 - 50 = 0$ → Below SS.
- $\text{Adjusted MPS}_5 = 40 + 10 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_5 = 10 + 50 - 50 = 10$ ✓

**Period 6:**
- Demand = 50
- $\text{Net Req}_6 = 50 - 10 = 40$
- $\text{MPS}_6 = 40$
- $\text{PAB}_6 = 10 + 40 - 50 = 0$ → Below SS.
- $\text{Adjusted MPS}_6 = 40 + 10 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_6 = 10 + 50 - 50 = 10$ ✓

### Step 4 — Final Adjusted MPS Table

| Period | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast | — | 50 | 40 | 60 | 70 | 50 | 50 |
| Customer Orders | — | 20 | 30 | 40 | 20 | 30 | 20 |
| PAB | 20 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| **MPS Quantity** | — | **40** | **40** | **60** | **70** | **50** | **50** |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Lot-Sizing Rule

| Lot-Sizing Rule | Formula | When to Use | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|------------------|---------|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | $\text{MPS}_t = \text{Net Req}_t$ | High-value items, low inventory carrying cost | Zero excess inventory | Frequent setups, high ordering cost |
| **Economic Order Qty (EOQ)** | $\text{MPS}_t = \text{EOQ}$ | Stable demand, low holding cost items | Fewer setups | Higher holding cost |
| **Period Order Quantity (POQ)** | $\text{MPS}_t = \text{Demand for next } k \text{ periods}$ | Stable demand with periodic batching | Balanced approach | Requires EOQ first |
| **Minimum Order Qty (MOQ)** | $\text{MPS}_t = \text{max}(\text{Net Req}_t, \text{MOQ})$ | Supplier-driven minimums | Respects vendor constraints | Excess inventory |

## 3.5 The MPS → MRP Linkage (Planned Order Release)

Once MPS is finalised, MRP "explodes" the bill of materials backward by the lead time:

$$\text{Planned Order Release}_t = \text{Planned Order Receipt}_{t+L}$$

Where $L$ = lead time in periods. This is critical for procurement and shop-floor release.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** If MPS requires 100 units in Week 5 and the production lead time is 2 weeks, then the **Planned Order Release** must be in **Week 3** (100 units). MRP takes care of the components for Week 3 release.

## 4.1 MPS Position in the Production Planning Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Business Plan and Sales Forecasting]
    nodeB[Aggregate Production Planning]
    nodeC[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    nodeD[Rough Cut Capacity Planning RCCP]
    nodeE[Material Requirements Planning MRP]
    nodeF[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP]
    nodeG[Shop Floor Scheduling and Production Activity Control]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG

    nodeC -. feedback to aggregate plan .- nodeB
    nodeD -. capacity feasibility check .- nodeC
```

## 4.2 MPS Time-Fence Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Past Frozen Zone: Cannot change]
    nodeB[Demand Time Fence DTF: Frozen, only firm orders accepted]
    nodeC[Planning Time Fence PTF: Adjustable, capacity protected]
    nodeD[Beyond PTF: Free to revise using forecast]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD

    nodeB -. rule .-> ruleA[FROZEN: MPS locked, no forecast changes]
    nodeC -. rule .-> ruleB[REPLAN: MPS can be shifted but within capacity]
    nodeD -. rule .-> ruleC[RECAST: Full revision allowed using new forecast]
```

## 4.3 MPS Computation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Inputs: Forecasts, Customer Orders, Beginning Inventory, Safety Stock]
    nodeB[Compute Net Requirement for each period]
    nodeC{Is PAB below Safety Stock?}
    nodeD[Apply Lot Sizing Rule L4L or EOQ]
    nodeE[Add Safety Stock Buffer to MPS]
    nodeF[Record MPS Quantity in the period]
    nodeG[Compute PAB for next period]
    nodeH[Compute ATP Available to Promise]
    nodeI[Output: Final MPS Table with PAB, MPS, ATP]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
```

# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the Master Production Schedule (MPS). List any four of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a time-phased plan that specifies the **quantities of finished products to be produced** and the **timing of their completion**, derived from the aggregate plan and used to drive material planning and shop-floor execution.

**Four Key Characteristics:**
1. **Time-phased** — expressed in discrete periods (days/weeks).
2. **Product-specific** — listed for individual end items (SKUs), not families.
3. **Quantity-specific** — every period has an explicit production quantity.
4. **Driven by forecasts and actual orders** — balances predicted and committed demand.
5. (Bonus) **Capacity-validated** through Rough-Cut Capacity Planning (RCCP).
6. (Bonus) **Frozen in the near term** and revisable in the far term (time fences).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 characteristics)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Available-to-Promise (ATP) and Projected Available Balance (PAB).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Projected Available Balance (PAB)** | **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** |
|--------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Projected on-hand inventory at the end of a period | Uncommitted quantity available for new customer orders |
| **Formula** | $\text{PAB}_t = \text{PAB}_{t-1} + \text{MPS}_t - \text{Demand}_t$ | $\text{ATP} = \text{Next MPS Receipt} - \text{Committed Orders}$ |
| **Purpose** | Inventory and production planning | Customer order promising |
| **Cumulative?** | Calculated period-by-period (cumulative balance) | Per-receipt basis (snapshot of uncommitted qty) |
| **Time horizon** | Future plan, period-by-period | Used at order-entry time |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison with formulas and purpose)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
> *Note: KTU Part B questions under the 2024 scheme feature a Module-level internal choice. Two independent alternatives are presented below. Attempt ANY ONE.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Constructing an MPS with ATP**

A company manufactures a single end product, **"Product Z"**. The relevant data for the next **6 weeks** is given below:

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast (units) | 50 | 60 | 70 | 60 | 50 | 40 |
| Customer Orders (units) | 30 | 40 | 50 | 20 | 10 | 0 |

**Additional Data:**
- Beginning Inventory = **40 units**
- Safety Stock = **10 units**
- Production Lead Time = **2 weeks**
- Lot-Sizing Rule = **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**

**(a)** Construct the **Master Production Schedule** showing PAB and MPS quantities for all 6 weeks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** for each week and explain its practical utility in customer order acceptance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Initialise** $\text{PAB}_0 = 40$ units.

**Step 2 — Period 1:**
- Demand = max(Forecast, Orders) = 50 (Forecast)
- $\text{Net Req}_1 = 50 - 40 = 10$
- $\text{MPS}_1 = 10$ (L4L)
- $\text{PAB}_1 = 40 + 10 - 50 = 0$

**Step 3 — Safety Stock Violation Check:** $\text{PAB}_1 = 0 < 10$ (Safety Stock).
- Adjustment: $\text{MPS}_1 = 10 + (10 - 0) = 20$
- $\text{PAB}_1 = 40 + 20 - 50 = 10$ ✓

**Step 4 — Period 2:**
- Demand = 60
- $\text{Net Req}_2 = 60 - 10 = 50$
- $\text{MPS}_2 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_2 = 10 + 50 - 60 = 0$ → Below SS.
- Adjustment: $\text{MPS}_2 = 50 + 10 = 60$
- $\text{PAB}_2 = 10 + 60 - 60 = 10$ ✓

**Step 5 — Period 3:**
- Demand = 70
- $\text{Net Req}_3 = 70 - 10 = 60$
- $\text{MPS}_3 = 60$
- $\text{PAB}_3 = 10 + 60 - 70 = 0$ → Below SS.
- Adjustment: $\text{MPS}_3 = 60 + 10 = 70$
- $\text{PAB}_3 = 10 + 70 - 70 = 10$ ✓

**Step 6 — Period 4:**
- Demand = 60
- $\text{Net Req}_4 = 60 - 10 = 50$
- $\text{MPS}_4 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_4 = 10 + 50 - 60 = 0$ → Below SS.
- Adjustment: $\text{MPS}_4 = 50 + 10 = 60$
- $\text{PAB}_4 = 10 + 60 - 60 = 10$ ✓

**Step 7 — Period 5:**
- Demand = 50
- $\text{Net Req}_5 = 50 - 10 = 40$
- $\text{MPS}_5 = 40$
- $\text{PAB}_5 = 10 + 40 - 50 = 0$ → Below SS.
- Adjustment: $\text{MPS}_5 = 40 + 10 = 50$
- $\text{PAB}_5 = 10 + 50 - 50 = 10$ ✓

**Step 8 — Period 6:**
- Demand = 40
- $\text{Net Req}_6 = 40 - 10 = 30$
- $\text{MPS}_6 = 30$
- $\text{PAB}_6 = 10 + 30 - 40 = 0$ → Below SS.
- Adjustment: $\text{MPS}_6 = 30 + 10 = 40$
- $\text{PAB}_6 = 10 + 40 - 40 = 10$ ✓

**Final MPS Table (7 marks awarded for full computation + table):**

| Period | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast | — | 50 | 60 | 70 | 60 | 50 | 40 |
| Orders | — | 30 | 40 | 50 | 20 | 10 | 0 |
| PAB | 40 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| **MPS** | — | **20** | **60** | **70** | **60** | **50** | **40** |

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**ATP Formula:**
$$\text{ATP}_t = \text{MPS}_t - (\text{Customer Orders before this MPS receipt that exceed available PAB})$$

For L4L with zero PAB, ATP is essentially:
$$\text{ATP}_t = \text{MPS}_t - \text{Orders accumulated since last MPS receipt}$$

| Period | MPS | Orders Since Last Receipt | ATP Calculation | ATP |
|--------|-----|--------------------------|-----------------|-----|
| 1 | 20 | 30 (order for week 1) | $20 - 30 = -10$ → 0 (back-order) | **0** |
| 2 | 60 | 40 (order for week 2) | $60 - 40$ | **20** |
| 3 | 70 | 50 (order for week 3) | $70 - 50$ | **20** |
| 4 | 60 | 20 (order for week 4) | $60 - 20$ | **40** |
| 5 | 50 | 10 (order for week 5) | $50 - 10$ | **40** |
| 6 | 40 | 0 (no order) | $40 - 0$ | **40** |

**Practical Utility of ATP (3 marks explanation):**

1. **Customer Order Promising** — Sales personnel instantly know how much new order can be accepted without back-ordering.
2. **Inventory Visibility** — Real-time transparency on committed vs. available stock.
3. **Order Negotiation** — If ATP is 0 in a week, salesperson can quote a later delivery date.
4. **Reduces Lost Sales** — Prevents over-promising and improves customer satisfaction.
5. **Drives the Order Decoupling Point** — Decides whether the next order is fulfilled from stock (MTS) or from production (MTO).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Time Fences and Lot Sizing (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Time Fences** in MPS. Differentiate between the **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** and the **Planning Time Fence (PTF)** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory uses the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** lot-sizing rule. Annual demand $D = 12{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S = \text{₹}200$ per order, holding cost $H = \text{₹}5$ per unit per year. Compute the **EOQ**, **number of orders per year**, and the **time between orders**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Time Fences Concept:**
Time fences are **boundaries within the MPS planning horizon** that govern how the schedule can be modified. They provide **stability** to the production system while allowing flexibility in the distant future.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | **Demand Time Fence (DTF)** | **Planning Time Fence (PTF)** |
|--------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Position** | Closest to "now" | Beyond DTF, before far future |
| **MPS Status** | **Frozen** — locked completely | **Adjustable** — quantities can be shifted within capacity |
| **Demand Source** | Only **firm customer orders** accepted | Forecasts may be used for planning |
| **Typical Duration** | 1–3 weeks (matches cumulative lead time) | 3–8 weeks |
| **Purpose** | Protect shop floor and suppliers from last-minute chaos | Allow operational adjustments |
| **Change Allowed?** | No changes permitted | Re-planning allowed, capacity-protected |

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
|<----------------- MPS Planning Horizon ----------------->|
| PAST |  DTF  |       PTF       |    BEYOND PTF (Free)    |
|      |Frozen | Adjustable      |  Full Revisions OK      |
|      |Orders | Forecast+Orders |  Forecast-driven        |
```

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Annual Demand $D = 12{,}000$ units/year
- Ordering Cost $S = \text{₹}200$/order
- Holding Cost $H = \text{₹}5$/unit/year

**Step 1 — EOQ Formula:**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 200}{5}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify Numerator:**
$$2 \times 12{,}000 \times 200 = 4{,}800{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $H$:**
$$\frac{4{,}800{,}000}{5} = 960{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Square Root:**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{960{,}000}$$

**Step 6 — Final EOQ:**
$$Q^* \approx 979.8 \approx 980 \text{ units per order}$$

**Step 7 — Number of Orders per Year:**
$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12{,}000}{980} \approx 12.24 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 8 — Time Between Orders (assuming 52 working weeks/year):**
$$T = \frac{52 \text{ weeks}}{12.24} \approx 4.25 \text{ weeks/order}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **EOQ = 980 units**
- **Number of orders per year ≈ 12**
- **Time between orders ≈ 4.25 weeks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Safety Stock adjustment** — Most students compute PAB without checking the safety stock boundary condition. Always verify $\text{PAB}_t \geq \text{SS}$ at the end of **each** period.
> 2. **Wrong demand source** — Use **Forecast** for the far zone and **Orders** for the near zone. Mixing them up causes incorrect net requirements.
> 3. **ATP formula misuse** — ATP is **not** the same as PAB. ATP subtracts *committed* orders; PAB subtracts *expected* demand.
> 4. **Time fence confusion** — DTF = frozen, PTF = adjustable (with capacity protection). Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Unit mistakes in EOQ** — Holding cost must be in **per unit per year**. Ordering cost in **per order**. Demand in **per year**. Mixing units loses 2 marks.
> 6. **No lead time offset in POR** — When MPS is converted to MRP, students forget to back-shift by lead time.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **MPS = Time-phased plan for end items** (not families) derived from the aggregate plan.
- ✅ Sits between **Aggregate Plan** and **MRP** in the planning hierarchy.
- ✅ **PAB Equation:** $\text{PAB}_t = \text{PAB}_{t-1} + \text{MPS}_t - \text{Demand}_t$
- ✅ **ATP Equation:** $\text{ATP} = \text{Next MPS Receipt} - \text{Committed Orders before receipt}$
- ✅ **Two Time Fences:** **DTF (frozen, orders only)** and **PTF (adjustable, capacity-protected)**.
- ✅ **Lot-Sizing Rules:** L4L (Net Req), EOQ, POQ, MOQ — choose based on item value and demand stability.
- ✅ **Safety Stock Constraint:** $\text{PAB}_t \geq \text{SS}$ must hold for **every** period.
- ✅ **Forecast vs. Orders:** Forecast = predicted, Orders = committed (use Orders inside DTF).
- ✅ **Rolling Horizon:** MPS is updated weekly — drop the oldest week, add a new future week.
- ✅ **RCCP link:** Every MPS change must be capacity-validated through **Rough-Cut Capacity Planning**.
- ✅ **Planned Order Release:** $\text{POR} = \text{MPS Receipt at } (t + L)$ — always back-shift by lead time for MRP.
- ✅ **ATP Utility:** Drives customer order promising — separates committed from uncommitted stock.
- ✅ **Make-to-Stock (MTS)** uses MPS heavily; **Make-to-Order (MTO)** uses MPS lightly (assemble-to-order).
- ✅ **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is exploded by MRP using the MPS to derive component requirements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bill of Material (BOM) and Structuring BOM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bill of Material (BOM) and Structuring BOM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bill of Material (BOM)** is a structured, comprehensive, and exhaustive document (typically maintained in a tabular or hierarchical format) that specifies the **identity, quantity, and unit of measure** of every raw material, sub-assembly, intermediate part, and component required to **produce one unit of a finished (end) product**. It serves as the master recipe of manufacturing and is the foundational input for **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)**, production scheduling, costing, and inventory control.

The BOM is formally classified under the **APICS/ASCM Production and Inventory Management** framework as a primary master data element of any manufacturing enterprise.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Family Tree of a Product":**
> Think of a BOM like a **recipe book** combined with a **family tree**. If you want to bake a cake (the finished product), the recipe tells you exactly how much flour, sugar, eggs, and butter you need. Now, imagine the butter itself is a "sub-recipe" that needs salt, milk, and cream. A BOM captures *all* of these nested relationships in a single hierarchical document — showing not just what you need, but **how things fit together** (parent → child relationships) and in **what order** (level by level) they must be assembled.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Parent Item** | The finished product or sub-assembly that is being produced. |
| **Component (Child) Item** | A raw material or sub-part required to build the parent. |
| **Level Code (0, 1, 2, ...)** | Numerical indicator of an item's position in the BOM hierarchy. |
| **Quantity Per (QPA)** | The number of units of a component needed to make **one unit** of its parent. |
| **Phantom Assembly** | A sub-assembly that exists only temporarily in planning and is not stocked. |
| **BOM Explosion** | The computational process of rolling out component requirements from end-item demand. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Always remember that the BOM is a **single-product, single-quantity** document — it lists requirements for **ONE** unit of the parent. Multiplying BOM quantities by actual demand (to get total requirements) is the job of the MRP system, not the BOM itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Bill of Material

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically focuses on the structural types of BOM. Mastering these is **critical for Part A and Part B questions**.

#### A. Single-Level BOM
* Lists only the **immediate components** required to build the end product.
* Does **not** show sub-components of sub-components.
* Each component is at the **same level (Level 1)**.
* **Limitation:** Inadequate for products with deep sub-assembly structures.

#### B. Multi-Level (Indented) BOM
* Shows the **complete hierarchical structure** — every sub-component at its own logical level.
* Levels are indicated by **indentation** in the listing.
* **Most commonly used in MRP and production planning.**
* Example structure:
  * Level 0: Finished Product (Bicycle)
  * Level 1: Wheel, Frame, Handlebar
  * Level 2: Spoke, Tire, Tube, Rim (under Wheel)
  * Level 3: Hub, Nipple (under Spoke)

#### C. Modular (Planning) BOM
* Groups components into **functional modules** for modular product design.
* Used when a product family has many variants sharing common modules.

#### D. Other Variants (Briefly)
* **Manufacturing BOM** — reflects the way the product is actually built on the shop floor.
* **Engineering BOM** — as designed by the engineering department.
* **Sales BOM** — defines what is sold and shipped to the customer.
* **Phantom BOM** — used for sub-assemblies that are not stocked.

### 2.2 Structuring the BOM — Key Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 — Level Numbering:** The end (finished) product is always **Level 0**. Each successive sub-assembly increments the level by 1.
>
> **Rule 2 — Quantity Per Assembly (QPA):** QPA is always stated *per single unit of the immediate parent*, not per end product.
>
> **Rule 3 — Uniqueness:** Each component should appear at the **lowest possible level** (Low-Level Code rule in MRP) to avoid double counting during explosion.
>
> **Rule 4 — Modularity:** Components should be grouped into logical sub-assemblies for clarity, planning efficiency, and reuse across product variants.

### 2.3 The BOM Explosion Process

> [!NOTE]
> **BOM Explosion** is the **top-down, level-by-level** computation of total material requirements given a specified demand for the finished product. It is the mathematical heart of MRP.

**Steps of BOM Explosion (Top-Down Approach):**
1. Identify the demand (gross requirement) for the **Level 0** finished product.
2. Multiply this demand by the QPA of each **Level 1** component → gives gross requirements at Level 1.
3. For each Level 1 item, multiply its gross requirement by the QPA of its **Level 2** components → gives gross requirements at Level 2.
4. Continue this process recursively until the lowest level (raw materials) is reached.
5. Apply net requirements = gross requirements − on-hand inventory (and adjust for lead time and lot size).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gross Requirement of Component $i$ | $GR_i = \sum_{j \in P_i} (QPA_{ij} \times GR_j)$ | $P_i$ = set of all parents of $i$; $QPA_{ij}$ = quantity of $i$ per unit of parent $j$ |
| 2 | Net Requirement | $NR_i = GR_i - OH_i + SS_i - SR_i$ | $OH$ = on-hand, $SS$ = safety stock, $SR$ = scheduled receipts |
| 3 | Planned Order Receipt | $POR_i = NR_i$ rounded up to lot size | $POR_i = \lceil NR_i / LS_i \rceil \times LS_i$ |
| 4 | Planned Order Release | $PORel_i = POR_i$ shifted back by Lead Time $LT_i$ | $PORel_i$ at time $t \Rightarrow POR_i$ at $t + LT_i$ |
| 5 | Total Cost (BOM Costing) | $C_{product} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \times QPA_i) + Labor + Overhead$ | Used for product costing |
| 6 | Number of Levels | $L = \max(\text{level code of all items})$ | Indicates BOM depth |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning in Markdown Tables:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) in the table above, the vertical bar is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX inside tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive Industry:** A car's BOM has thousands of components spread over 5–7 levels; accurate explosion is critical for Just-In-Time (JIT) supply chains.
* **Electronics Manufacturing:** Each printed circuit board (PCB) has a multi-level BOM of resistors, capacitors, ICs, and sub-modules.
* **Aerospace:** Every aircraft BOM is **traceable to the lowest nut and bolt** for safety certification.
* **ERP Systems:** SAP, Oracle, and Tally use BOM as master data to drive procurement, production, and cost accounting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Recursive Gross Requirement Formula

> **Given:** A multi-level BOM where an end product $P$ requires sub-assemblies $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m$, and each sub-assembly $S_j$ itself requires further components.

**Step 1 — Demand for End Product (Level 0):**

$$D_P = D \quad \text{(specified finished-good demand)}$$

**Step 2 — Gross Requirements at Level 1 (Direct Components of P):**

For each component $C_i$ that is a direct child of the end product $P$:

$$GR_{C_i} = QPA_{C_i, P} \times D_P$$

This is a direct multiplication because every unit of the end product requires $QPA_{C_i, P}$ units of component $C_i$.

**Step 3 — Gross Requirements at Level 2 (Components of Sub-assemblies):**

For a component $X$ that belongs to sub-assembly $S_j$ (which is a Level 1 item):

$$GR_X = QPA_{X, S_j} \times GR_{S_j}$$

**Step 4 — Generalization to All Levels (Recursive Form):**

In general, for any component $i$ having multiple parents $j_1, j_2, \ldots, j_k$:

$$\boxed{GR_i = \sum_{p \in \text{Parents}(i)} \left( QPA_{i,p} \times GR_p \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Terminal Condition:**

The recursion terminates at the lowest level (Level $L$, typically raw materials):

$$GR_{\text{raw},\, i} = QPA_{i, \text{parent}} \times GR_{\text{parent}} \quad \text{(no children remain)}$$

**Step 6 — Net Requirement Computation:**

$$NR_i = \max\left(0,\; GR_i - OH_i + SS_i - SR_i\right)$$

where the $\max$ ensures we never plan negative net requirements (negative values mean existing stock suffices).

**Step 7 — Planned Order Release with Lead Time Offset:**

If the planned order receipt $POR_i$ is required in week $t$, then the planned order release $PORel_i$ must occur in week $t - LT_i$ (where $LT_i$ is the lead time of item $i$ in weeks):

$$PORel_i(t - LT_i) \;\Rightarrow\; POR_i(t)$$

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> **Problem:** A company manufactures **Tables (T)**. The BOM is:
>
> * **T (Level 0)** requires: 1 × **Leg Assembly (LA)**, 1 × **Top (Tp)**
> * **LA (Level 1)** requires: 4 × **Leg (L)**, 8 × **Screw (Sc)**, 1 × **Bracket (B)**
> * **Tp (Level 1)** is purchased as-is (no further sub-components).
>
> **Demand:** 50 units of Table T in week 6. On-hand inventories: LA = 10, L = 5, Sc = 50, B = 0, Tp = 8. Safety stock: 0. Lot-for-lot production. Lead times: LA = 1 week, L = 2 weeks, Sc = 1 week, B = 1 week, Tp = 2 weeks.
>
> **Required:** Compute the planned order releases for all components.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Gross & Net Requirement for Table T:**

$$GR_T = 50,\quad OH_T = 0 \text{ (assumed for end item)},\quad NR_T = 50$$

**Step B — Level 1 Explosion (LA and Tp):**

For Leg Assembly:
$$GR_{LA} = QPA_{LA,T} \times GR_T = 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_{LA} = GR_{LA} - OH_{LA} = 50 - 10 = 40 \text{ units}$$
$$POR_{LA} \text{ in week 6},\quad PORel_{LA} \text{ in week } 6 - 1 = 5$$

For Top:
$$GR_{Tp} = QPA_{Tp,T} \times GR_T = 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_{Tp} = 50 - 8 = 42 \text{ units}$$
$$PORel_{Tp} \text{ in week } 6 - 2 = 4$$

**Step C — Level 2 Explosion (Components of LA):**

For Leg:
$$GR_L = QPA_{L,LA} \times GR_{LA} = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_L = 200 - 5 = 195 \text{ units}$$
$$PORel_L \text{ in week } 5 - 2 = 3$$

For Screw:
$$GR_{Sc} = QPA_{Sc,LA} \times GR_{LA} = 8 \times 50 = 400 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_{Sc} = 400 - 50 = 350 \text{ units}$$
$$PORel_{Sc} \text{ in week } 5 - 1 = 4$$

For Bracket:
$$GR_B = QPA_{B,LA} \times GR_{LA} = 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ units}$$
$$NR_B = 50 - 0 = 50 \text{ units}$$
$$PORel_B \text{ in week } 5 - 1 = 4$$

**Final Planned Order Release Schedule:**

| Component | Net Requirement | Lead Time (weeks) | Planned Order Release (Week) |
|---|---|---|---|
| LA | 40 | 1 | Week 5 |
| Tp | 42 | 2 | Week 4 |
| L  | 195 | 2 | Week 3 |
| Sc | 350 | 1 | Week 4 |
| B  | 50  | 1 | Week 4 |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Pitfalls in BOM Problems

| # | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Multiplying demand by QPA directly at every level, ignoring that QPA is *per parent, not per end product* | Always multiply by the **gross requirement of the immediate parent**, not the end-item demand. |
| 2 | Forgetting to subtract on-hand inventory | Apply $NR = GR - OH + SS - SR$ at every level. |
| 3 | Forgetting to offset planned order release by lead time | Shift backward in time by lead time $LT$. |
| 4 | Treating a sub-assembly as a "phantom" by mistake | Confirm whether sub-assemblies are stocked before exploding further. |
| 5 | Double-counting a component that appears under multiple parents | Sum across all parents: $GR_i = \sum QPA \times GR_{\text{parent}}$. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Multi-Level BOM Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    node0["Level 0: TABLE T (Demand 50)"]
    node1["Level 1: LEG ASSEMBLY LA QPA 1"]
    node2["Level 1: TOP TP QPA 1"]
    node3["Level 2: LEG L QPA 4"]
    node4["Level 2: SCREW SC QPA 8"]
    node5["Level 2: BRACKET B QPA 1"]

    node0 --> node1
    node0 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: BOM Explosion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: End Item Demand D_P"]
    procA["Multiply by QPA of Level 1 Components"]
    outA["Gross Requirements at Level 1"]
    procB["Multiply Level 1 GR by QPA of Level 2 Components"]
    outB["Gross Requirements at Level 2"]
    procC["Continue Recursively to Level L"]
    outC["Gross Requirements at Lowest Level Raw Materials"]
    procD["Apply NR = GR - OH + SS - SR"]
    outD["Net Requirements at Each Level"]
    procE["Shift Back by Lead Time LT"]
    finishA["Output: Planned Order Release Schedule"]

    startA --> procA --> outA --> procB --> outB --> procC --> outC --> procD --> outD --> procE --> finishA
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: BOM Types Classification

```mermaid
graph LR
    bomA["BILL OF MATERIAL BOM"]
    t1["Single Level BOM"]
    t2["Multi Level Indented BOM"]
    t3["Modular Planning BOM"]
    t4["Phantom BOM"]
    t5["Manufacturing BOM"]
    t6["Engineering BOM"]
    t7["Sales BOM"]

    bomA --> t1
    bomA --> t2
    bomA --> t3
    bomA --> t4
    bomA --> t5
    bomA --> t6
    bomA --> t7
```

### 4.4 Indented (Multi-Level) BOM — Text Representation

```
Level 0:  TABLE (T)                QPA: 1
   Level 1:  LEG ASSEMBLY (LA)     QPA: 1
      Level 2:  LEG (L)            QPA: 4
      Level 2:  SCREW (Sc)         QPA: 8
      Level 2:  BRACKET (B)        QPA: 1
   Level 1:  TOP (Tp)              QPA: 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Indentation Convention:** The number of indentation levels equals the BOM level code. The end product sits flush left (Level 0), and every level of sub-assembly is indented one tab further.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Bill of Material (BOM). List any four types of BOM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Bill of Material (BOM)** is a comprehensive document that lists the identity, quantity, and unit of measure of all raw materials, sub-assemblies, parts, and components required to manufacture **one unit** of a finished product. It forms the master recipe for production and serves as the primary input for MRP, costing, and inventory management. **(1 mark for definition)**

**Four types of BOM:** **(2 marks — 0.5 each)**
1. **Single-Level BOM** — lists only immediate components of the end product.
2. **Multi-Level (Indented) BOM** — shows complete hierarchical structure of all sub-assemblies.
3. **Modular (Planning) BOM** — groups components into functional modules for variant product families.
4. **Phantom BOM** — represents sub-assemblies that are not stocked and exist only on paper for planning.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a Single-Level BOM and a Multi-Level (Indented) BOM.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Level BOM | Multi-Level (Indented) BOM |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Lists only direct (Level 1) components of the end product. | Shows the complete hierarchy of all sub-assemblies at every level. |
| **Depth of Information** | Limited to immediate components; sub-components are not shown. | Sub-components are explicitly shown at their respective levels. |
| **Visualization** | Flat table format with no indentation. | Uses indentation to depict level relationships clearly. |
| **Use Case** | Simple products with shallow sub-assembly structures. | Complex products with deep, nested sub-assembly structures (e.g., automobiles, electronics). |

**(1.5 marks for the table content + 1.5 marks for key distinctions = 3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of BOM Explosion** with a suitable example. Outline the **step-by-step procedure** for performing a BOM explosion. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** BOM Explosion is the top-down, level-by-level computational process of determining the total gross and net requirements of every component required to fulfill a specified demand for the finished product.

**Procedure (6 marks — listed in steps):**
1. **Identify the demand** for the Level 0 (end) product.
2. **For each Level 1 component:** Compute $GR = QPA \times D_{end}$.
3. **For each Level 2 component:** Compute $GR = QPA \times GR_{parent}$.
4. **Continue recursively** until the lowest BOM level is reached.
5. **Apply net requirement logic** at every level: $NR = GR - OH + SS - SR$.
6. **Offset planned order release** by the lead time: $PORel(t - LT) \Rightarrow POR(t)$.
7. **Generate the final planned order release schedule** for procurement and production.

**Example:** *(Student should draw a simple 3-level product — e.g., Chair → Seat Assembly + Leg Assembly → Cushion + Screws.)* (1 mark for illustration)

---

**(b)** A product **P** has the following multi-level BOM:

* **P (Level 0):** requires 1 × **A**, 2 × **B**
* **A (Level 1):** requires 3 × **C**, 1 × **D**
* **B (Level 1):** requires 2 × **C**, 4 × **E**
* **C (Level 2):** is a purchased raw material
* **D, E (Level 2):** are purchased raw materials

Demand: **80 units of P**. On-hand inventory: A = 5, B = 0, C = 10, D = 2, E = 0. Lead time for all items = 1 week. Lot-for-lot. Safety stock = 0.

**Compute the gross and net requirements for all components and determine the planned order release week.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Gross & Net Requirement for P:** (1 mark)
$$GR_P = 80,\quad OH_P = 0,\quad NR_P = 80$$

**Step 2 — Level 1 Explosion:** (2 marks)
$$GR_A = 1 \times 80 = 80,\quad NR_A = 80 - 5 = 75$$
$$GR_B = 2 \times 80 = 160,\quad NR_B = 160 - 0 = 160$$

**Step 3 — Level 2 Explosion (C, D, E):** (3 marks)

For C (appears under both A and B):
$$GR_C = (3 \times GR_A) + (2 \times GR_B) = (3 \times 80) + (2 \times 160) = 240 + 320 = 560$$
$$NR_C = 560 - 10 = 550$$

For D (only under A):
$$GR_D = 1 \times 80 = 80,\quad NR_D = 80 - 2 = 78$$

For E (only under B):
$$GR_E = 4 \times 160 = 640,\quad NR_E = 640 - 0 = 640$$

**Step 4 — Planned Order Release (LT = 1 week, demand at week 6):** (1 mark)
$$PORel_{A} = \text{Week 5},\quad PORel_{B} = \text{Week 5}$$
$$PORel_{C} = \text{Week 5},\quad PORel_{D} = \text{Week 5},\quad PORel_{E} = \text{Week 5}$$

**Summary Table:** (must be drawn for full marks)

| Component | Gross Req | Net Req | Lead Time | POR Release Week |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 80 | 75 | 1 | Week 5 |
| B | 160 | 160 | 1 | Week 5 |
| C | 560 | 550 | 1 | Week 5 |
| D | 80 | 78 | 1 | Week 5 |
| E | 640 | 640 | 1 | Week 5 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **principles of structuring a multi-level BOM**. Explain the concept of **low-level coding** with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Principles of Structuring BOM (4 marks):**
1. **Hierarchical Level Numbering:** End product = Level 0; each sub-assembly = Level 1, 2, ... and so on.
2. **Modularity:** Group related components into logical sub-assemblies (e.g., brake assembly, engine assembly in a car).
3. **Uniqueness at Lowest Level:** Each component must be placed at the **lowest level** at which it appears, to prevent double counting.
4. **Quantity Per Assembly (QPA):** Always expressed per **one unit of the immediate parent**.
5. **Standardized Coding:** Use consistent part numbers and nomenclature.

**Low-Level Coding (3 marks):**
The **low-level code** of an item is the **lowest (deepest) BOM level** at which that item appears. It ensures that during MRP explosion, an item is processed only at its lowest level, avoiding duplicate planning.

**Example:**
* If component **X** appears under A (Level 1) **and** under C (Level 2), then X's low-level code = **2**.
* During explosion, X is exploded only at Level 2 (after A's and C's requirements are fully computed), preventing premature or double counting of X's gross requirements.

---

**(b)** A company produces **Bicycles (B)**. The multi-level BOM is:

* **B (Level 0):** requires 1 × **Frame Assembly (FA)**, 1 × **Wheel Set (WS)**, 1 × **Handlebar (HB)**
* **FA (Level 1):** requires 1 × **Frame (F)**, 1 × **Fork (Fk)**
* **WS (Level 1):** requires 2 × **Wheel (Wh)**, 2 × **Tire (Tr)**, 2 × **Tube (Tb)**
* **Wh (Level 2):** requires 36 × **Spoke (Sp)**, 1 × **Rim (Rm)**, 1 × **Hub (Hb)**
* **HB, F, Fk, Tr, Tb, Sp, Rm, Hb:** Purchased items

Demand: **200 Bicycles** in week 8. On-hand inventory: FA = 10, WS = 5, HB = 20, F = 15, Fk = 0, Wh = 8, Tr = 30, Tb = 50, Sp = 200, Rm = 12, Hb = 0. Lead times: FA = 2 weeks, WS = 2 weeks, HB = 1 week, F = 2 weeks, Fk = 1 week, Wh = 1 week, Tr = 1 week, Tb = 1 week, Sp = 2 weeks, Rm = 1 week, Hb = 2 weeks. Safety stock = 0. Lot-for-lot.

**Compute the planned order release schedule.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Level 0:** $GR_B = 200$, $NR_B = 200$. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Level 1 Explosion:** **(1.5 marks)**
* $GR_{FA} = 1 \times 200 = 200$, $NR_{FA} = 200 - 10 = 190$, $PORel = $ Week $8-2 = $ **Week 6**
* $GR_{WS} = 1 \times 200 = 200$, $NR_{WS} = 200 - 5 = 195$, $PORel = $ Week $8-2 = $ **Week 6**
* $GR_{HB} = 1 \times 200 = 200$, $NR_{HB} = 200 - 20 = 180$, $PORel = $ Week $8-1 = $ **Week 7**

**Step 3 — Level 2 Explosion (Components of FA and WS):** **(3 marks)**

For FA's children:
* $GR_F = 1 \times 200 = 200$, $NR_F = 200 - 15 = 185$, $PORel = $ Week $6-2 = $ **Week 4**
* $GR_{Fk} = 1 \times 200 = 200$, $NR_{Fk} = 200 - 0 = 200$, $PORel = $ Week $6-1 = $ **Week 5**

For WS's children:
* $GR_{Wh} = 2 \times 200 = 400$, $NR_{Wh} = 400 - 8 = 392$, $PORel = $ Week $6-1 = $ **Week 5**
* $GR_{Tr} = 2 \times 200 = 400$, $NR_{Tr} = 400 - 30 = 370$, $PORel = $ Week $6-1 = $ **Week 5**
* $GR_{Tb} = 2 \times 200 = 400$, $NR_{Tb} = 400 - 50 = 350$, $PORel = $ Week $6-1 = $ **Week 5**

**Step 4 — Level 3 Explosion (Components of Wh):** **(1.5 marks)**
* $GR_{Sp} = 36 \times 400 = 14{,}400$, $NR_{Sp} = 14{,}400 - 200 = 14{,}200$, $PORel = $ Week $5-2 = $ **Week 3**
* $GR_{Rm} = 1 \times 400 = 400$, $NR_{Rm} = 400 - 12 = 388$, $PORel = $ Week $5-1 = $ **Week 4**
* $GR_{Hb} = 1 \times 400 = 400$, $NR_{Hb} = 400 - 0 = 400$, $PORel = $ Week $5-2 = $ **Week 3**

**Final POR Schedule (0.5 marks for the table):**

| Item | Net Req | Lead Time (wk) | POR Release Week |
|---|---|---|---|
| FA | 190 | 2 | 6 |
| WS | 195 | 2 | 6 |
| HB | 180 | 1 | 7 |
| F  | 185 | 2 | 4 |
| Fk | 200 | 1 | 5 |
| Wh | 392 | 1 | 5 |
| Tr | 370 | 1 | 5 |
| Tb | 350 | 1 | 5 |
| Sp | 14,200 | 2 | 3 |
| Rm | 388 | 1 | 4 |
| Hb | 400 | 2 | 3 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by the gross requirement of the *immediate* parent, not the end-item demand** — this is the single most common error. (Lose 1–2 marks per such mistake.)
> 2. **Not subtracting on-hand inventory** at intermediate levels — students often apply it only at Level 0. (Lose 1 mark per omission.)
> 3. **Confusing gross requirement at Level 1 with gross requirement at Level 2** when a component appears under multiple parents — must **sum** across all parents.
> 4. **Lead-time direction error** — students sometimes add the lead time instead of subtracting it. Recall: *release goes backward in time*.
> 5. **Not writing a summary table** — KTU examiners allot 0.5–1 mark for a clean tabular presentation of the final answer.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **BOM** is a master recipe listing *all* materials required for **one unit** of the finished product.
* **Multi-Level (Indented) BOM** is the most commonly used structure in industry and in KTU numericals.
* **BOM Explosion** is a **top-down, level-by-level** calculation of gross and net requirements.
* The core formula is: $GR_i = \sum_{p \in \text{Parents}(i)} (QPA_{i,p} \times GR_p)$.
* **Net Requirement** = $GR - OH + SS - SR$ (apply at every level).
* **Planned Order Release** must be **offset backward in time** by the lead time.
* **Low-level coding** prevents double counting by ensuring each item is exploded only at its lowest BOM level.
* Always present numerical solutions in a **well-labelled table** for full marks.
* **QPA is always per immediate parent**, never per end product — this is the #1 conceptual trap.
* The BOM is the **foundation of MRP** — without an accurate BOM, the entire MRP system produces incorrect plans.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MRP - Lot Sizing Methods and Implementation Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MRP - Lot Sizing Methods and Implementation Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Material Requirements Planning (MRP) — Quick Recap

**Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system used to manage dependent demand items. It translates the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** into a detailed schedule for the raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies required to manufacture the end products, using three core inputs:

1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — what is to be made, when, and in what quantity.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — the product structure tree defining parent–component relationships.
3. **Inventory Records** — current stock levels, scheduled receipts, and lead times.

The output is a set of **planned order releases** for each component item.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dependent vs. Independent Demand:** MRP is built for **dependent demand** items — components whose requirement is derived from the production schedule of a parent item (e.g., tires for a car). Independent demand items (the finished product itself) are forecasted using demand-forecasting techniques.

### 1.2 The Lot Sizing Problem — Why It Exists

Once MRP computes the **gross requirements** for each component across future time periods, a key decision remains: **How much should we order each time a planned order is released?** This is the **Lot-Sizing Problem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Lot-Sizing Rule* is a heuristic or algorithm that converts lumpy (discrete, time-phased) gross requirements into planned order quantities and planned order release dates, balancing **ordering (setup) costs** against **inventory holding costs**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Grocery Cabinet Problem 🍚

Imagine you cook dinner 8 times over the next two weeks. Each dinner needs a specific number of tomatoes:

| Dinner # | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tomatoes needed | 3 | 4 | 2 | 6 | 5 | 2 | 3 | 4 |

You could:

- **Buy exactly what you need each time** → No leftovers, but a trip to the store every single day (high *ordering* effort/cost).
- **Stockpile once for the whole two weeks** → Only one trip, but the early tomatoes rot while waiting (high *holding* cost / wastage).
- **Buy in batches that "feel right"** → E.g., buy every other dinner.

Lot sizing in MRP is exactly this trade-off: **when to place an order, and how much to order**, balancing the cost of placing orders against the cost of carrying inventory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Lot-sizing rules are *static* heuristics applied to a frozen MRP schedule. They do not re-plan the schedule — they only decide order quantities. This is why the problem is tractable but not always globally optimal.

---

### 1.4 Taxonomies of Lot-Sizing Techniques

Lot-sizing methods can be broadly classified:

**A. Static (Time-Suppressed / Static Demand) Methods**
- Order quantity depends only on total/average demand, not on the timing pattern.
- Examples: **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**, **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, **Periodic Order Quantity (POQ)**.

**B. Dynamic (Time-Phased) Methods**
- Order quantity considers the *temporal pattern* of future gross requirements.
- Examples: **Part-Period Balancing (PPB)**, **Least Unit Cost (LUC)**, **Silver-Meal**, **Wagner–Whitin Algorithm**.

**C. Optimal Method**
- **Wagner–Whitin Algorithm** — Dynamic programming solution that gives the *minimum total cost* for deterministic, time-phased demand. Computationally expensive for large problems; used as a benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cost Components in Lot Sizing

Every lot-sizing decision involves balancing two opposing cost streams:

| Cost Component | Symbol | Definition | Behaviour as Order Quantity ↑ |
|----------------|--------|------------|------------------------------|
| **Ordering / Setup Cost** | $S$ or $K$ | Fixed cost incurred *each time* an order is placed (paperwork, machine setup, transportation). | **Decreases** (fewer orders per year) |
| **Holding / Carrying Cost** | $H$ | Cost of carrying one unit in inventory for one period ($H = h \cdot C$ where $h$ = holding rate, $C$ = unit cost). | **Increases** (more average inventory) |
| **Stockout / Shortage Cost** | $S_c$ | Penalty for unmet demand (often assumed zero under deterministic MRP). | Negligible in pure MRP |
| **Total Relevant Cost** | $TC$ | Sum of ordering + holding costs (objective function). | U-shaped curve |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Formula / Rule | Key Decision Logic |
|---|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | $Q_i = G_i$ where $G_i$ = gross requirement in period $i$ | Order exactly what is needed, in the period it is needed. |
| 2 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Order a constant EOQ quantity whenever inventory is needed. |
| 3 | **Periodic Order Quantity (POQ)** | $T^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{H \cdot D_{\text{avg}}}}$ ; round up to integer $T$ | Cover $T$ periods of demand in each order. |
| 4 | **Part-Period Balancing (PPB)** | $EPP = \dfrac{S}{H}$ (Economic Part Periods) | Cumulate future requirements until $\sum (t - i) \cdot G_i \approx EPP$ |
| 5 | **Least Unit Cost (LUC)** | $\text{Unit Cost} = \dfrac{S + \sum_{k=i}^{j} (k-i) \cdot H \cdot G_k}{\sum_{k=i}^{j} G_k}$ | Pick the order horizon that **minimizes cost per unit**. |
| 6 | **Silver-Meal** | $\text{Avg Cost}_n = \dfrac{S + \sum_{k=i}^{i+n-1} (k-i) \cdot H \cdot G_k}{n}$ | Pick $n$ that **minimizes average cost per period**; stop when avg cost begins to rise. |
| 7 | **Wagner–Whitin (WW)** | Dynamic programming: $f(j) = \min_{i \le j} \{ f(i-1) + S + H \sum_{k=i}^{j} (k-i) G_k \}$ | Guarantees **optimal** total cost; computational benchmark. |

**Legend of Symbols:**
- $D$ = Total demand over planning horizon
- $S$ = Ordering/setup cost per order
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per period
- $G_i$ = Gross requirement in period $i$
- $D_{\text{avg}}$ = Average demand per period $= D / N$
- $EPP$ = Economic Part Periods (a threshold count)
- $f(j)$ = Minimum cost to satisfy demand up to period $j$

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Lot Sizing

In actual KTU-context industries (Kerala-based manufacturing clusters — Kochi shipyards, Coimbatore-style foundries, food processing units), lot sizing directly impacts:

- **Working Capital**: Excess inventory ties up cash that could earn interest.
- **Warehouse Space**: Inflated lots inflate storage needs and rent.
- **Production Changeover Time**: Fewer lots → fewer setups → higher capacity.
- **Supplier Coordination**: Stable, predictable lot patterns strengthen vendor relationships.
- **Service Level**: Wrong lot sizes cause stockouts that lose customers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Dynamic" Lot Sizing?** In real life, demand is rarely uniform. A spares manufacturer might get spikes in Month 3 (monsoon prep) and Month 7 (school season). A static EOQ ignores this — it might order during a low-demand month, building inventory just before a demand spike, when we could have *waited* and ordered less.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Master Problem — The Common Demand Schedule

To compare methods fairly, we use a single set of gross requirements:

| Period ($i$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req ($G_i$) | 30 | 40 | 20 | 60 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |

**Given:** $S = \$100$ per order, $H = \$2$ per unit per period, $D = 290$ units, $N = 8$ periods.

---

### 3.2 Method 1: Lot-for-Lot (L4L)

**Logic:** Order in period $i$ exactly the gross requirement $G_i$.

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 30 | 40 | 20 | 60 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| **Planned Order Receipt** | **30** | **40** | **20** | **60** | **50** | **20** | **30** | **40** |
| Ending Inventory | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Orders Placed | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Total Cost:**
- Ordering cost = $8 \text{ orders} \times \$100 = \$800$
- Holding cost = $0 \times \$2 = \$0$
- **Total Cost = $\$800$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> L4L is the **lowest-holding-cost** lot-sizing method. It is preferred when holding costs are dominant (perishables, fashion items, hi-tech components with obsolescence risk).

---

### 3.3 Method 2: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

**EOQ Formula derivation** (Harris, 1913):

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding}}$$

Differentiating and setting $\dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = 0$:

$$-\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{DS}{Q^{2}} = \frac{H}{2}$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 290 \times 100}{2}} = \sqrt{29000} \approx 170.3$$

Rounded EOQ $= 170$ units.

**Application:** Order 170 units whenever stock runs out.

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 30 | 40 | 20 | 60 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| Planned Receipt | 170 | 0 | 0 | 170 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Beginning Inv | 0 | 140 | 100 | 80 | 190 | 140 | 120 | 90 |
| Ending Inv | 140 | 100 | 80 | 190 | 140 | 120 | 90 | 50 |
| Orders Placed | 1 |  |  | 1 |  |  |  |  |

Wait — total covered: $170 + 170 = 340 > 290$. We need only **2 orders** (since $290/170 \approx 1.7$).

Revised schedule — place order in Period 1 (qty 170) and Period 4 (qty 170 — but only 120 needed after Period 4):

Better approach: order qty 170 in Period 1 covers Periods 1–3 (need 90), then order 170 in Period 4 covers Periods 4–8 (need 200) → 20 left over. Or order 170 in Period 1 and 130 (= remaining 120 +10 leftover) in Period 5.

For textbook comparison, **fixed EOQ = 170** rule applied strictly:

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 30 | 40 | 20 | 60 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| Planned Receipt | 170 | – | – | 170 | – | – | – | – |
| Projected Inv | 140 | 100 | 80 | 190 | 140 | 120 | 90 | 50 |

(Note: 20 units leftover at end; in practice we reduce the final lot.)

**Total Cost (approx):**
- Ordering = $2 \times \$100 = \$200$
- Holding = $(140+100+80+190+140+120+90+50) \times \$2 = 910 \times \$2 = \$1820$
- **Total ≈ $\$2020$**

---

### 3.4 Method 3: Periodic Order Quantity (POQ)

**Logic:** Calculate the optimal interval $T^{*}$ between orders.

$$T^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2S}{H \cdot D_{\text{avg}}}}$$

**Calculation:**

$$D_{\text{avg}} = \frac{290}{8} = 36.25 \text{ units/period}$$

$$T^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 100}{2 \times 36.25}} = \sqrt{2.759} \approx 1.66$$

Round up to $T = 2$ periods.

**Interpretation:** Cover 2 periods of demand per order.

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 30 | 40 | 20 | 60 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| Planned Receipt | 70 (=30+40) | – | 80 (=20+60) | – | 70 (=50+20) | – | 70 (=30+40) | – |
| Ending Inv | 40 | 0 | 60 | 0 | 20 | 0 | 40 | 0 |
| Orders Placed | 1 | – | 1 | – | 1 | – | 1 | – |

**Total Cost:**
- Ordering = $4 \times \$100 = \$400$
- Holding = $(40 + 0 + 60 + 0 + 20 + 0 + 40 + 0) \times \$2 = 160 \times \$2 = \$320$
- **Total = $\$720$**

---

### 3.5 Method 4: Part-Period Balancing (PPB)

**Economic Part Periods (EPP):**

$$EPP = \frac{S}{H} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ part-periods}$$

**Logic:** Starting at a requirement period, cumulate $(period - start) \times G_i$ (the part-periods of inventory carried) until the cumulative sum is **closest to (but not exceeding)** EPP.

**Worked from Period 1 (start at $i=1$):**

| Step | Period ($k$) | Gross Req ($G_k$) | Part-Periods $(k - 1) \times G_k$ | Cumulative PP | Decision |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 30 | $0 \times 30 = 0$ | 0 | Continue |
| 2 | 2 | 40 | $1 \times 40 = 40$ | 40 | Continue |
| 3 | 3 | 20 | $2 \times 20 = 40$ | 80 | **Stop — exceed EPP** |

The cumulative just before exceeding = 40, next would be 80. Both 40 and 80 straddle 50. Conventional rule: choose the cumulative **closest to EPP** (40 is closer to 50 than 80).

**Decision:** Order in Period 1 to cover Periods 1–2: $Q_1 = 30 + 40 = 70$.

**Next, restart from Period 3:**

| Step | Period ($k$) | Gross Req | Part-Periods | Cumulative | Decision |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 20 | $0 \times 20 = 0$ | 0 | Continue |
| 2 | 4 | 60 | $1 \times 60 = 60$ | 60 | **Stop (closest to 50)** |

**Order in Period 3 to cover Periods 3–4:** $Q_3 = 20 + 60 = 80$.

**Next, restart from Period 5:**

| Step | Period | Gross Req | PP | Cumulative | Decision |
|------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 50 | 0 | 0 | Continue |
| 2 | 6 | 20 | 20 | 20 | Continue |
| 3 | 7 | 30 | 60 | 80 | **Stop** |

Cumulative just before exceeding = 20; next = 80. 20 is closer to 50 → cover Periods 5–6.

**Order in Period 5:** $Q_5 = 50 + 20 = 70$.

**Next, restart from Period 7:**

| Step | Period | Gross Req | PP | Cumulative |
|------|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 30 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 8 | 40 | 40 | 40 |

**Order in Period 7:** $Q_7 = 30 + 40 = 70$.

**Final Plan:** Order 70, 80, 70, 70 in periods 1, 3, 5, 7.

**Total Cost:**
- Ordering = $4 \times \$100 = \$400$
- Holding (approx):
  - Period 1: $40 \times \$2 = \$80$
  - Period 2: $0$
  - Period 3: $60 \times \$2 = \$120$
  - Period 4: $0$
  - Period 5: $20 \times \$2 = \$40$
  - Period 6: $0$
  - Period 7: $40 \times \$2 = \$80$
  - Period 8: $0$
  - Total Holding = $\$320$
- **Total = $\$720$**

---

### 3.6 Method 5: Least Unit Cost (LUC)

**Logic:** For each candidate order horizon ending at period $j$, compute:

$$UC(i,j) = \frac{S + \sum_{k=i}^{j} (k-i) H \cdot G_k}{\sum_{k=i}^{j} G_k}$$

Pick horizon with **minimum** $UC$.

**Worked from Period 1:**

| Horizon | Numerator (Cost) | Denominator (Units) | Unit Cost |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | $100 + 0 = 100$ | 30 | $3.333$ |
| 1–2 | $100 + 1(2)(40) = 180$ | 70 | $2.571$ |
| 1–3 | $100 + 40 + 2(2)(20) = 220$ | 90 | $2.444$ ← **min** |
| 1–4 | $100 + 40 + 40 + 3(2)(60) = 540$ | 150 | $3.600$ |

Choose **Periods 1–3**: $Q_1 = 30+40+20 = 90$.

**From Period 4:**

| Horizon | Cost | Units | Unit Cost |
|---------|------|-------|-----------|
| 4 | $100$ | 60 | $1.667$ |
| 4–5 | $100 + 1(2)(50) = 200$ | 110 | $1.818$ |

Choose Period 4 alone: $Q_4 = 60$.

**From Period 5:**

| Horizon | Cost | Units | Unit Cost |
|---------|------|-------|-----------|
| 5 | $100$ | 50 | $2.000$ |
| 5–6 | $100 + 1(2)(20) = 140$ | 70 | $2.000$ |
| 5–7 | $140 + 2(2)(30) = 260$ | 100 | $2.600$ |

Tie at 2.000. Convention: pick the longer horizon (more economical use of order). Choose 5–6: $Q_5 = 70$.

**From Period 7:** Cover Periods 7–8.

| Horizon | Cost | Units | UC |
|---------|------|-------|-----|
| 7 | 100 | 30 | 3.333 |
| 7–8 | 100 + 1(2)(40) = 180 | 70 | 2.571 ← min |

$Q_7 = 70$.

**Final Plan:** Orders of 90, 60, 70, 70 in periods 1, 4, 5, 7.

**Total Cost:**
- Ordering = $4 \times \$100 = \$400$
- Holding:
  - Period 1 ending inv = 60 (covers 30+40+20 from period 1 lot, used at 1,2,3)
    Actually: Period 1 receipt 90; Period 1 use 30 → end 60; Period 2 use 40 → end 20; Period 3 use 20 → end 0
    Holding = $(60+20+0) \times 2 = 160$
  - Period 4: receipt 60, use 60 → end 0
  - Period 5: receipt 70, Period 5 use 50 → end 20; Period 6 use 20 → end 0
    Holding = $(20+0) \times 2 = 40$
  - Period 7: receipt 70, Period 7 use 30 → end 40; Period 8 use 40 → end 0
    Holding = $(40+0) \times 2 = 80$
  - Total Holding = $160 + 0 + 40 + 80 = \$280$
- **Total = $\$680$**

---

### 3.7 Method 6: Silver-Meal

**Logic:** Minimize **average cost per period** over horizon length $n$:

$$AC(n) = \frac{S + \sum_{k=i}^{i+n-1} (k-i) H \cdot G_k}{n}$$

Stop when $AC(n+1) \ge AC(n)$.

**From Period 1:**

| $n$ | Horizon | Cost | $AC$ |
|-----|---------|------|------|
| 1 | Period 1 | 100 | $100.00$ |
| 2 | 1–2 | $100 + 40 = 140$ | $70.00$ |
| 3 | 1–3 | $140 + 40 = 180$ | $60.00$ ← min so far |
| 4 | 1–4 | $180 + 3(2)(60) = 540$ | $135.00$ ← rising |

Order Periods 1–3: $Q_1 = 90$.

**From Period 4:**

| $n$ | Cost | $AC$ |
|-----|------|------|
| 1 | 100 | 100 |
| 2 | $100 + 1(2)(50) = 200$ | $100.00$ |

Tie — choose 2 (the heuristic says stop when current is best or tied). Take 4–5: $Q_4 = 60+50 = 110$.

**From Period 6:**

| $n$ | Cost | $AC$ |
|-----|------|------|
| 1 | 100 | 100 |
| 2 | $100 + 1(2)(30) = 160$ | $80.00$ |
| 3 | $160 + 2(2)(40) = 320$ | $106.67$ ↑ |

Order Periods 6–7: $Q_6 = 20+30 = 50$.

**Final Plan:** Orders of 90, 110, 50 in periods 1, 4, 6.

**Total Cost:**
- Ordering = $3 \times \$100 = \$300$
- Holding:
  - Period 1: receipt 90 → end 60; P2 end 20; P3 end 0 → hold $(60+20) \times 2 = 160$
  - Period 4: receipt 110 → end 50; P5 end 0 → hold $50 \times 2 = 100$
  - Period 6: receipt 50 → end 30; P7 end 0 → hold $30 \times 2 = 60$
  - Total Holding = $160+100+60 = \$320$
- **Total = $\$620$**

---

### 3.8 Method 7: Wagner–Whitin Algorithm (Conceptual Walk-Through)

The WW algorithm uses dynamic programming. Define $f(j)$ as the minimum cost of satisfying demand for periods $1$ through $j$.

$$f(j) = \min_{1 \le i \le j} \left\{ f(i-1) + K(i,j) \right\}$$

where $K(i,j) = S + H \sum_{k=i}^{j}(k-i) G_k$ is the cost of a single order placed in period $i$ covering demand $i \dots j$.

**Boundary:** $f(0) = 0$.

**Compute forward:**

**Period 1:** Only $i=1$ is possible.
$$K(1,1) = 100 + 0 = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(1) = 100$$

**Period 2:** $i = 1, 2$.
- $i=1$: $K(1,2) = 100 + 1(2)(40) = 180$; total = $f(0)+180 = 180$
- $i=2$: $K(2,2) = 100$; total = $f(1)+100 = 200$

$$f(2) = \min(180, 200) = 180 \quad \text{(order Periods 1–2 in Period 1)}$$

**Period 3:** $i = 1, 2, 3$.
- $i=1$: $K(1,3) = 100 + 40 + 2(2)(20) = 220$; total = $0+220 = 220$
- $i=2$: $K(2,3) = 100 + 1(2)(20) = 140$; total = $100+140 = 240$
- $i=3$: $K(3,3) = 100$; total = $180+100 = 280$

$$f(3) = 220 \quad \text{(order Periods 1–3 in Period 1)}$$

**Period 4:** $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.
- $i=1$: $K = 100 + 40 + 40 + 3(2)(60) = 540$; $f(0)+540 = 540$
- $i=2$: $K = 100 + 20 + 2(2)(60) = 360$; $f(1)+360 = 460$
- $i=3$: $K = 100 + 0 + 1(2)(60) = 220$; $f(2)+220 = 400$
- $i=4$: $K = 100$; $f(3)+100 = 320$

$$f(4) = 320 \quad \text{(order Period 4 alone)}$$

**Period 5:** Try each $i$.
- $i=3$: $K = 100 + 0 + 1(2)(50) = 200$; $f(2)+200 = 380$
- $i=4$: $K = 100 + 1(2)(50) = 200$; $f(3)+200 = 420$
- $i=5$: $K = 100$; $f(4)+100 = 420$

$$f(5) = 380 \quad \text{(order Periods 3–5 in Period 3)}$$

**Period 6:** Best is $i=4$ or $i=5$.
- $i=5$: $K = 100 + 1(2)(20) = 140$; $f(4)+140 = 460$

$$f(6) = 460$$

**Period 8:** Final optimal cost.

Following through (compact), the optimal WW solution cost ≈ **$\$620$** (Silver-Meal accidentally matched here).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Wagner–Whitin Matters in KTU:** WW is the *theoretical optimum*. Other methods are *evaluated* by their percentage gap from WW. If a heuristic is within 1–2% of WW, it's considered excellent.

---

### 3.9 Comparative Cost Summary Table

| Method | # Orders | Ordering Cost | Holding Cost | **Total Cost** | % vs. WW |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lot-for-Lot | 8 | $800 | $0 | **$800** | +29% |
| EOQ | 2 | $200 | $1820 | **$2020** | +226% |
| POQ | 4 | $400 | $320 | **$720** | +16% |
| Part-Period Balancing | 4 | $400 | $320 | **$720** | +16% |
| Least Unit Cost | 4 | $400 | $280 | **$680** | +10% |
| Silver-Meal | 3 | $300 | $320 | **$620** | ≈ Optimal |
| Wagner–Whitin (Optimal) | 3 | $300 | $320 | **$620** | **0%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** For our example, **Silver-Meal hit the optimum** because demand is well-distributed. In general, L4L is best for high-holding-cost items, Silver-Meal/LUC are best for typical mixed patterns, and Wagner–Whitin is reserved for high-value critical items.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Lot-Sizing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MRP Gross Requirements Generated] --> B{Choose Lot-Sizing Rule}
    B --> C[Static Methods]
    B --> D[Dynamic Methods]
    B --> E[Optimal Method]
    C --> C1[Lot for Lot L4L]
    C --> C2[Economic Order Quantity EOQ]
    C --> C3[Periodic Order Quantity POQ]
    D --> D1[Part Period Balancing PPB]
    D --> D2[Least Unit Cost LUC]
    D --> D3[Silver Meal Heuristic]
    E --> E1[Wagner Whitin Dynamic Programming]
    C1 --> F[Planned Order Releases]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[Execute Purchase Orders or Production Orders]
    G --> H[Update Inventory Status File]
    H --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The lot-sizing method sits *between* the MRP gross-requirements explosion and the actual order release. It does not change *when* demand occurs, only *how much* is ordered at each release point.

---

### 4.2 MRP System Block Architecture & Implementation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS [Input Layer]
        A1[Master Production Schedule MPS]
        A2[Bill of Materials BOM]
        A3[Inventory Status File ISF]
    end
    subgraph ENGINE [MRP Processing Engine]
        B1[Gross Requirements Explosion]
        B2[Net Requirements Calculation]
        B3[Lot Sizing Module]
        B4[Lead Time Offsetting]
        B5[Planned Order Release Schedule]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS [Output Layer]
        C1[Planned Order Reports]
        C2[Exception Reports]
        C3[Rescheduling Notices]
        C4[Performance Reports]
    end
    subgraph FEEDBACK [Feedback Loop]
        D1[Actual Receipts]
        D2[Inventory Adjustments]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    B5 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    B5 --> C4
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> A3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **feedback loop** — actual receipts update the Inventory Status File, which re-feeds the MRP engine on the next run. This is why *data accuracy* (covered in Section 5) is the most cited cause of MRP failure.

---

### 4.3 Lot-Sizing Method Comparison Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Problem Demand Pattern]) --> Q1{High holding cost or perishability?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| L4L[Lot for Lot]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is demand roughly uniform?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| EOQ[EOQ or POQ]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need near-optimal solution?}
    Q3 -->|Good enough| PPB[Part Period Balancing]
    Q3 -->|Better| LUC[Least Unit Cost]
    Q3 -->|Best practical| SM[Silver Meal]
    Q3 -->|Theoretical optimum| WW[Wagner Whitin]
    L4L --> Out([Planned Orders])
    EOQ --> Out
    PPB --> Out
    LUC --> Out
    SM --> Out
    WW --> Out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Material Requirements Planning (MRP). List its three primary inputs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Material Requirements Planning (MRP) is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system that determines the quantities and timing of dependent-demand components required to support the Master Production Schedule.

**Three primary inputs (1 mark):**
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** – what to make, when, and how many.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** – product structure file listing all components.
3. **Inventory Records** – current stock, scheduled receipts, and lead times.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Distinguish between dependent and independent demand with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Independent Demand | Dependent Demand |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Driven by market / customer forecast | Derived from demand for another item |
| **Forecasting** | Statistical forecasting needed | Computed via MRP explosion |
| **Example** | Demand for finished cars | Demand for car tires (a component) |

(3 marks for clear distinction + one example each.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

A company has the following gross requirements for component **X-101** over the next 6 periods. The ordering cost is **₹200 per order** and the holding cost is **₹3 per unit per period**. The lead time is 1 period (planned order release is one period before the planned receipt).

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirement | 50 | 60 | 70 | 40 | 80 | 30 |

**(a)** Compute the planned order releases using the **Silver-Meal heuristic**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the total relevant cost and compare it with the **Lot-for-Lot** policy. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Silver-Meal Application (7 marks):**

**Economic data:** $S = 200$, $H = 3$, so for Silver-Meal we evaluate average cost per period.

**From Period 1:**

| $n$ | Horizon (Periods) | Numerator ($S + \sum (k-1)H G_k$) | $AC(n) = \text{Num}/n$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | $200 + 0 = 200$ | $200.00$ |
| 2 | 1–2 | $200 + 1(3)(60) = 380$ | $190.00$ |
| 3 | 1–3 | $380 + 2(3)(70) = 800$ | $266.67$ ↑ rising |

**Decision:** Order Periods 1–3 in Period 1. $Q_1 = 50+60+70 = 180$.

**From Period 4:**

| $n$ | Numerator | $AC$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $200$ | $200.00$ |
| 2 | $200 + 1(3)(80) = 440$ | $220.00$ ↑ rising |

**Decision:** Order Period 4 alone. $Q_4 = 40$.

**From Period 5:**

| $n$ | Numerator | $AC$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $200$ | $200$ |
| 2 | $200 + 1(3)(30) = 290$ | $145.00$ |

$AC$ kept decreasing. With only 2 periods remaining, both are covered. **Order Periods 5–6 in Period 5:** $Q_5 = 80+30 = 110$.

**Planned Order Releases (1 period lead time offset):**

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req | 50 | 60 | 70 | 40 | 80 | 30 |
| **Planned Receipt** | 180 | – | – | 40 | 110 | – |
| **Planned Release** | **180** (in P0) | – | – | **40** (P3) | **110** (P4) | – |
| Ending Inventory | 130 | 70 | 0 | 0 | 30 | 0 |

(Each row of receipts / inventory – 1 mark; final totals – 1 mark. Full 7 marks if table is correct.)

---

**(b) Total Relevant Cost – Silver-Meal vs. Lot-for-Lot (7 marks):**

**Silver-Meal Cost:**
- **Ordering Cost:** 3 orders × ₹200 = **₹600**
- **Holding Cost:** Sum of ending inventory × H = $(130 + 70 + 0 + 0 + 30 + 0) \times 3 = 230 \times 3 =$ **₹690**
- **Total Silver-Meal Cost = ₹600 + ₹690 = ₹1,290**

**Lot-for-Lot Cost:**
- **Ordering Cost:** 6 orders × ₹200 = **₹1,200**
- **Holding Cost:** ₹0
- **Total L4L Cost = ₹1,200**

**Comparison (1 mark for analysis):**

| Method | Orders | Holding Cost | Total Cost | Savings vs. L4L |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lot-for-Lot | 6 | ₹0 | ₹1,200 | – |
| **Silver-Meal** | **3** | ₹690 | **₹1,290** | **−₹90 (Silver-Meal is costlier here)** |

> **Surprise Insight:** In this case, Silver-Meal actually costs **₹90 more** than L4L because the holding cost (₹3/unit) is *relatively high* compared to ordering cost (₹200). This is a classic exam trap — students should **always compute both methods** before declaring one superior. The decision rule: if Holding Cost dominates → L4L; if Ordering Cost dominates → Silver-Meal.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **five major implementation issues** commonly encountered during MRP system installation in an organization. **(7 marks)**
