**(b)** Compare **Lot-for-Lot (L4L), EOQ, and Wagner–Whitin** lot-sizing techniques across five performance dimensions. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Five Implementation Issues of MRP (7 marks; ~1.5 marks each):**

1. **Data Accuracy Problems (BOM & Inventory Errors):** MRP is "garbage-in, garbage-out." Incorrect Bills of Materials or wrong on-hand quantities cause the system to suggest wrong planned orders. Studies show BOM accuracy below 95% leads to schedule failures.

2. **Inadequate Top-Management Commitment:** MRP is a cross-functional system (production, purchasing, inventory, finance). Without CEO/CFO sponsorship, departments resist sharing data or following system suggestions.

3. **Software Selection & Integration Complexity:** Choosing between ERP modules (SAP, Oracle, Tally-with-MRP add-on) and ensuring they integrate with existing payroll, sales, and finance systems is technically and financially demanding.

4. **Resistance to Change & Training Gaps:** Planners used to intuition-based ordering may distrust the computer's recommendations. Insufficient training on using exception reports leads to manual overrides that defeat the system.

5. **Inaccurate or Unstable Master Production Schedule:** If the MPS changes frequently (engineering changes, demand volatility), the MRP explodes constantly, generating *nervous* planned orders that suppliers cannot honor.

*(Bonus point: poor lead-time estimation and supplier unreliability also disrupt MRP execution.)*

---

**(b) Comparison of L4L, EOQ, and Wagner–Whitin (7 marks; ~1.4 marks per dimension):**

| Dimension | Lot-for-Lot | EOQ | Wagner–Whitin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Not optimal (good for high-H items) | Suboptimal (ignores timing) | **Provably optimal** |
| **Holding Cost** | **Lowest possible** (zero inventory) | High (large lots) | Balanced |
| **Computation** | Trivial | Trivial formula | Complex DP; $O(N^{2})$ |
| **Data Need** | Just gross reqs. | Average demand | Full time-phased demand |
| **Use Case** | Job shops, assemblies with BOM-driven runs | Stable, uniform demand | Critical, expensive items |

(1 mark per row of the comparison; 1 mark for concluding remark recommending **Wagner–Whitin for high-value items, EOQ for stable bulk, L4L for low-H components**.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** In PPB, students often forget to *restart* the cumulative count after each order decision. If you start from Period 1 and reach EPP at Period 4, the *next* lot starts from Period 5 — **not** Period 4.
> - **Mistake 2:** In Silver-Meal, the rule is "stop when $AC(n+1) \ge AC(n)$," not when $AC$ reaches a minimum equal to a previous value. A tie at two consecutive horizons (e.g., both = ₹100) is broken by **preferring the longer horizon**.
> - **Mistake 3:** Many students apply EOQ directly to MRP without verifying that demand is approximately uniform — a guaranteed method-mismatch trap.
> - **Mistake 4:** Confusing *gross* and *net* requirements when setting up the lot-sizing schedule. Lot sizing is performed on the **net requirement schedule** (after subtracting on-hand and scheduled receipts).
> - **Mistake 5:** In Wagner–Whitin, $K(i,j) = S + H \sum_{k=i}^{j}(k-i) G_k$. Forgetting the $(k-i)$ multiplier turns this into a Part-Period Balancing answer and costs full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Core Definitions**
- MRP = dependent-demand planning system; 3 inputs = MPS, BOM, Inventory Records.
- Lot sizing = deciding *order quantity* and *order timing* given gross requirements.
- $S$ (setup cost), $H$ (holding cost/unit/period), $G_i$ (gross req in period $i$).

✅ **Method Mapping**
- **L4L** → $Q_i = G_i$; lowest holding, highest ordering; **use when $H$ is high**.
- **EOQ** → $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$; **assumes constant demand**.
- **POQ** → cover $T$ periods where $T = \sqrt{2S / (H \cdot D_{\text{avg}})}$; round up.
- **PPB** → cumulate part-periods until $\sum(t-i)G_i \approx EPP$ where $EPP = S/H$.
- **LUC** → minimize cost per **unit** over horizon.
- **Silver-Meal** → minimize cost per **period** over horizon; stop when AC rises.
- **Wagner–Whitin** → dynamic programming optimum; $f(j) = \min_i \{f(i-1) + K(i,j)\}$.

✅ **Strategic Heuristic**
- High **$H/S$** ratio → **L4L** wins.
- Low **$H/S$** ratio → Silver-Meal / Wagner–Whitin win.
- Moderate, irregular demand → **Silver-Meal** is the recommended practical compromise.

✅ **Implementation Issues — Mnemonic "DRIFT"**
- **D**ata accuracy (BOM & inventory)
- **R**esistance to change / training
- **I**ntegration with other software
- **F**ickle / unstable MPS
- **T**op-management commitment

✅ **Key Exam Traps to Avoid**
- Always restart cumulative count in PPB after each lot.
- Apply lot sizing on **net** requirements, not gross.
- Wagner–Whitin optimality is *only for deterministic, time-phased, integer demand* with no backorders.
- Don't confuse Silver-Meal's tie-break rule with LUC's.
- Lead-time offsetting = planned order release is **one period before** planned receipt (for LT = 1).

✅ **Quick Comparative Numbers (For Exam Memorization)**
| Pattern | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform demand | EOQ / POQ | Simplicity, near-optimal |
| Lumpy, high-H | L4L | Avoids inventory pile-up |
| Lumpy, low-H | Silver-Meal | Reduces setups |
| Critical, expensive | Wagner–Whitin | Optimality needed |

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — MRP Lot Sizing & Implementation Issues**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Course PBIET304 | Operations Management*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MRP-II
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🏭 MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning)

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MRP-II

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP-II)** is a comprehensive computer-based production planning and control system that integrates and coordinates all manufacturing resources — including materials, capacity, manpower, machines, and financial resources — into a single, closed-loop information system to support the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, MRP-II is treated as the direct evolution of **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)**. While MRP focuses *only* on *what* materials are needed, *when*, and *in what quantity*, MRP-II extends this by linking materials planning to **production planning, capacity planning, shop floor control, and financial planning**.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Operating Theatre"

Imagine a hospital running multiple **operating theatres** simultaneously:

- **MRP** is like the **anaesthetist** — they ensure the right drugs (materials) are in the right quantity at the right time. But they don't know the surgeon's schedule, the available beds, or the nurses' shifts.
- **MRP-II** is the **Hospital Operations Manager** — they synchronize *all* departments: doctors (labour), ICU beds (capacity), medical supplies (materials), the operating schedule (MPS), and the hospital's budget (finance).

In short, **MRP plans materials; MRP-II plans the entire manufacturing "operation"**.

### 🔑 Key Terminology (Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Expansion | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **MRP** | Material Requirements Planning | Plans dependent-demand inventory |
| **MRP-II** | Manufacturing Resource Planning | Closed-loop system integrating all resources |
| **MPS** | Master Production Schedule | What to produce, when, and in what quantity |
| **BOM** | Bill of Materials | List of components needed to make a product |
| **CRP** | Capacity Requirements Planning | Verifies capacity feasibility of the MPS |
| **SFC** | Shop Floor Control | Monitors work-in-progress in real time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Closed-Loop MRP** is the *intermediate step* between MRP and MRP-II — it adds the feedback loop from shop floor execution back to planning. MRP-II is essentially **Closed-Loop MRP + Financial Integration + Simulation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 Evolution Pathway: MRP → Closed-Loop MRP → MRP-II → ERP

The KTU syllabus expects students to know **how MRP-II evolved**. Let's break it down step-by-step:

#### **Stage 1 — MRP (1960s–1970s)**
- Function: *Dependent demand explosion* of components from a parent item's BOM.
- **Inputs:** MPS, BOM, Inventory Status File.
- **Output:** Planned Order Releases (timing & quantities).
- **Limitation:** Did not validate *whether capacity existed* to execute the plan.

#### **Stage 2 — Closed-Loop MRP (1970s)**
- Adds the **feedback loop**:
  - Capacity is checked against the plan.
  - If infeasible → plan is revised → re-run explosion.
- **New modules added:** CRP + SFC (Shop Floor Control).

#### **Stage 3 — MRP-II (1980s — Oliver Wight)**
- The term was popularised by **Oliver Wight** in his book *"MRP-II: Unlocking America's Productivity Potential"* (1981).
- Adds **Financial Planning** and **Simulation (What-If Analysis)**.
- Covers: Master Scheduling, MRP, CRP, SFC, Purchasing, Financial Integration.

#### **Stage 4 — ERP (1990s–Today)**
- Extends MRP-II beyond manufacturing to **HR, CRM, SCM, and Finance** across the enterprise.
- Examples: SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Hook:** *"Differentiate MRP, MRP-II, and ERP."* This comes in exams almost every cycle. The analytical matrix in Section 3 will give you a ready-made answer.

---

### 📐 The Six Logical Steps in an MRP-II Explosion Run

1. **Read the MPS** → Identify independent demand items.
2. **Explode the BOM** → Determine gross requirements of components.
3. **Apply Inventory Status** → Subtract on-hand, scheduled receipts, safety stock.
4. **Apply Lot-Sizing Rule** → Convert net requirements into planned order quantities.
5. **Offset by Lead Time** → Determine *Planned Order Release* date.
6. **Validate with CRP** → Confirm capacity availability; loop back if not feasible.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> MRP-II is more *conceptual* than computational, but the following equations are testable.

$$
\text{Net Requirement (NR)} = \text{Gross Requirement (GR)} - \text{On-Hand Inventory (OHI)} - \text{Scheduled Receipts (SR)} + \text{Safety Stock (SS)}
$$

$$
\text{Planned Order Release Date} = \text{Planned Order Receipt Date} - \text{Lead Time (LT)}
$$

$$
\text{Planned Order Quantity} = f(\text{Lot-Sizing Rule})
$$

**Lot-Sizing Methods (formulas):**

| Method | Formula | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Lot-for-Lot (L4L) | $Q = \text{NR}$ | Order exactly the net requirement |
| Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Balanced ordering & holding cost |
| Periodic Order Quantity (POQ) | $Q = D \times T_p$ | Cover demand for $T_p$ periods |
| Part Period Balancing | $Q$ such that $H \times \sum (t-1) \approx S$ | Order when holding ≈ setup cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** All math operators like $\sqrt{}$ and fractions must be inside `$` or `$$` blocks. Note the blank lines surrounding every display equation.

**Where:**
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Ordering / setup cost (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $T_p$ = Period of supply (days or weeks)

### 🌍 Real-World Utility

MRP-II systems are the **backbone of discrete manufacturing industries** such as automotive, aerospace, and consumer electronics. By 2024, **~75% of mid-to-large Indian manufacturing firms** (especially in Kerala's industrial clusters) use MRP-II modules within ERP suites for:
- Reducing inventory carrying costs by **15–30%**
- Improving on-time delivery rates by **20%**
- Enhancing capacity utilization by **10–25%**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Derivation: Converting Net Requirements to Planned Order Releases

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **exhaustive logical derivation** of an MRP-II explosion — the kind KTU examiners love for a 14-mark question.

**Step 1 — Identify Gross Requirement (GR) for the parent item from the MPS.**

$$
\text{GR}_{\text{week } t} = \text{MPS Quantity}_{\text{week } t}
$$

*Text logic:* The gross requirement represents the *external (independent) demand* pulled in by customer orders or forecasts.

**Step 2 — Explode the BOM to get GR for each component.**

$$
\text{GR}_{\text{component}, t} = \text{GR}_{\text{parent}, t} \times \text{Quantity per Assembly (qpa)}
$$

*Text logic:* If 1 parent unit needs 3 of component X, then producing 100 parents needs 300 X.

**Step 3 — Compute Projected On-Hand (POH).**

$$
\text{POH}_{t} = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{Scheduled Receipts}_{t} - \text{GR}_{t}
$$

*Text logic:* POH is a running balance. It decreases as we meet demand and increases when we receive planned order releases.

**Step 4 — Compute Net Requirement (NR).**

$$
\text{NR}_{t} = \text{GR}_{t} - \text{POH}_{t-1} - \text{Scheduled Receipts}_{t} + \text{SS}
$$

*Text logic:* Subtract what we already have. The Safety Stock ensures a buffer against variability.

**Step 5 — Convert NR to Planned Order Receipt (POR).**

$$
\text{POR}_{t} = \text{Lot-Sizing Rule applied to NR}_{t}
$$

*Text logic:* For L4L, POR equals NR. For EOQ, POR rounds up to the nearest multiple of $Q^*$.

**Step 6 — Compute Planned Order Release (POL).**

$$
\text{POL}_{t} = \text{POR}_{t} \text{ scheduled for period } (t - \text{LT})
$$

*Text logic:* Lead time is offset *backwards* — we must release the order earlier so that we receive it in time.

### 📊 Analytical Matrix: MRP vs. MRP-II vs. ERP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important comparison** in Module 3. Memorize this table.

| Parameter | **MRP** | **MRP-II** | **ERP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Material Requirements Planning | Manufacturing Resource Planning | Enterprise Resource Planning |
| **Year Introduced** | 1960s | 1980 (Oliver Wight) | 1990s |
| **Scope** | Materials only | All manufacturing resources | Entire enterprise |
| **Key Function** | Dependent demand explosion | Production planning + control + finance | Integration of all business functions |
| **Modules Included** | MRP logic | MPS, MRP, CRP, SFC, Purchasing, Finance | All MRP-II + HR, CRM, SCM, BI |
| **Feedback Loop** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (closed-loop) | ✅ Yes (closed-loop) |
| **Capacity Planning** | ❌ Not included | ✅ CRP module | ✅ Advanced APS |
| **Financial Integration** | ❌ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes + multi-currency |
| **Simulation / What-If** | ❌ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes + advanced analytics |
| **Geographic Scope** | Single plant | Single company (multi-plant) | Multi-company, global |
| **Example Software** | Early IBM PICS | SAP R/2, early JDE | SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud |
| **Cost-Benefit** | Low cost, quick ROI | Medium cost, structural gains | High cost, enterprise-wide gains |

### 🧩 Detailed Module Architecture of MRP-II

$$
\text{MRP-II} = \underbrace{\text{MPS}}_{\text{Top-Level Plan}} + \underbrace{\text{BOM}}_{\text{Product Structure}} + \underbrace{\text{MRP}}_{\text{Materials Plan}} + \underbrace{\text{CRP}}_{\text{Capacity Validation}} + \underbrace{\text{SFC}}_{\text{Execution}} + \underbrace{\text{Finance}}_{\text{Cost Roll-up}}
$$

This single equation captures the *essence* of MRP-II. KTU examiners often ask: *"List the modules of MRP-II and explain any three."* The above decomposition is your cheat-code.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1 — MRP-II Closed-Loop Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **closed-loop information flow** of an MRP-II system. Each node represents a functional block; arrows represent data/information transfer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forecast and Customer Orders] --> B[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    B --> C[BOM Explosion]
    C --> D[MRP Logic]
    D --> E[Capacity Requirements Planning CRP]
    E --> F{Feasible?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Planned Order Releases]
    G --> H[Shop Floor Control SFC]
    H --> I[Purchasing and Vendor Management]
    I --> J[Manufacturing Execution]
    J --> K[Inventory and WIP Updates]
    K --> L[Financial Planning Module]
    L --> M[Cost and Performance Reports]
    M --> N[Management Decision Support]
    N --> B
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** from `N (Management Decision Support)` back to `B (MPS)` represents the *closed-loop* nature of MRP-II.
- The **inner loop** between `B → C → D → E → F → (No) → B` represents the **capacity-feasibility feedback**.
- The **forward flow** from `G → H → I → J` represents the **execution chain**.

---

### 🗺️ Diagram 2 — MRP-II Module Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[MRP II System]
    Root --> L1A[Top Level Planning]
    Root --> L1B[Execution Level]
    Root --> L1C[Support Level]
    L1A --> M1[MPS Module]
    L1A --> M2[Resource Planning Module]
    L1B --> M3[MRP Module]
    L1B --> M4[CRP Module]
    L1B --> M5[Shop Floor Control Module]
    L1C --> M6[Purchasing Module]
    L1C --> M7[Inventory Module]
    L1C --> M8[Financial Module]
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5
    M5 --> M6
    M5 --> M7
    M5 --> M8
    style Root fill:#4B0082,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style L1A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L1B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L1C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M5 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M6 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M7 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style M8 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- MRP-II is **hierarchically structured** into three layers: Top-Level Planning, Execution, and Support.
- The **downward arrows** show top-down control; the **lateral arrows** show real-time coordination between execution modules.

---

### 🧱 Block Diagram 3 — Input-Process-Output Model of MRP-II

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1[MPS] --> Process[MRP II Engine]
    Input2[BOM] --> Process
    Input3[Inventory Status] --> Process
    Input4[Capacity Data] --> Process
    Input5[Shop Floor Feedback] --> Process
    Input6[Cost and Finance Data] --> Process
    Process --> Out1[Planned Order Releases]
    Process --> Out2[Capacity Load Profile]
    Process --> Out3[Purchase Requisitions]
    Process --> Out4[Production Cost Reports]
    Process --> Out5[Performance Dashboards]
    style Process fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style Input1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Input2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Input3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Input4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Input5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Input6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Out1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Out2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Out3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Out4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Out5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualisation tip for KTU:** When asked to "draw the MRP-II architecture," you can replicate Diagram 1 in your exam booklet. The yellow nodes are inputs, the orange node is the central engine, and the green nodes are outputs. Labelling arrows with the *data type* (e.g., "POs", "Capacity Hours") scores extra marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### ✍️ Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define MRP-II. Mention any two of its limitations.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning) is a closed-loop, computer-integrated system that plans and controls all manufacturing resources — materials, capacity, manpower, and finance — by extending the basic MRP logic into production planning, shop floor control, and financial modules.

**Two limitations:**
1. **High implementation cost and complexity** — requires trained staff, hardware, and consultancy; not feasible for small firms.
2. **Dependent on accurate data** — Garbage-In-Garbage-Out: incorrect BOMs, inventory counts, or lead times can produce infeasible plans.
3. (Optional) **Limited scope** — focuses on internal manufacturing; does not extend to customers/suppliers (this is addressed by ERP).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. What is the role of the Master Production Schedule (MPS) in an MRP-II system?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **MPS (Master Production Schedule)** is the *driver* of the entire MRP-II system. It specifies *what finished products* are to be produced, *in what quantities*, and *by when* — based on customer orders and demand forecasts. The MPS is then exploded via the BOM to trigger the MRP run, which determines material and capacity requirements. Without an accurate MPS, the entire MRP-II cascade produces unreliable plans.

---

### 📝 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
> *(Internal Choice Pattern — Answer ANY ONE of the following)*

---

### 🅰️ **Question A: (14 Marks)**

**a) Explain the evolution of MRP-II from MRP. Discuss its key functional modules.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Evolution Path:**

1. **MRP (1960s)** – Focused on dependent demand explosion using MPS, BOM, and inventory data. Limitation: no capacity validation.
2. **Closed-Loop MRP (1970s)** – Added the feedback loop through **Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP)** and **Shop Floor Control (SFC)**. If capacity is insufficient, the plan is revised.
3. **MRP-II (1980s)** – Popularised by **Oliver Wight** (1981). Added the **Financial Planning module** and **Simulation (what-if) capability**, integrating all manufacturing resources — materials, machines, labour, money.
4. **ERP (1990s)** – Extended MRP-II to cover HR, CRM, SCM, and analytics across the entire enterprise.

**Key Functional Modules of MRP-II:**

| # | Module | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Master Production Scheduling (MPS) | Top-level plan of finished goods |
| 2 | Bill of Materials (BOM) | Product structure definition |
| 3 | MRP Logic | Computes net requirements & planned order releases |
| 4 | Capacity Requirements Planning (CRP) | Validates capacity feasibility |
| 5 | Shop Floor Control (SFC) | Monitors & controls WIP execution |
| 6 | Purchasing Module | Manages vendor POs and receipts |
| 7 | Inventory Module | Maintains on-hand, in-transit, and safety stock |
| 8 | Financial Planning Module | Integrates cost roll-up & budget tracking |

**Conclusion:** MRP-II transformed MRP from a *materials-only* tool into a *holistic manufacturing management* philosophy, paving the way for modern ERP systems.

---

**b) Differentiate between MRP, MRP-II, and ERP. State any two advantages of MRP-II over MRP.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Tabular Comparison:**

| Parameter | **MRP** | **MRP-II** | **ERP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scope | Materials only | All manufacturing resources | Entire enterprise |
| Capacity Planning | Absent | CRP module | Advanced APS |
| Financial Integration | No | Yes | Yes (multi-currency, multi-company) |
| Feedback Loop | Open-loop | Closed-loop | Closed-loop + analytics |
| Shop Floor Execution | Not covered | SFC module | MES + IoT integration |
| Example | Inventory planner | SAP R/2, JDE | SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud |

**Two Advantages of MRP-II over MRP:**

1. **Integrated Capacity Validation:** MRP-II uses CRP to ensure the production plan is feasible with available capacity, preventing the "unrealistic plan" problem of MRP.
2. **Financial Visibility:** MRP-II links production activities to cost and budget, enabling management to see the financial impact of every production decision in real time.
3. (Optional) **Simulation (What-If Analysis):** Planners can test multiple demand scenarios before committing to an MPS.

---

### 🅱️ **Question B: (14 Marks)**

**a) Describe the closed-loop information flow of an MRP-II system with the help of a block diagram.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**The Closed-Loop MRP-II Flow** consists of the following sequential blocks connected by bidirectional arrows:

1. **Forecast & Customer Orders → MPS:** Independent demand is converted into a production plan.
2. **MPS → BOM Explosion:** The BOM is exploded to identify component-level requirements.
3. **BOM Explosion → MRP Logic:** Gross requirements are netted against inventory status, leading to planned order releases.
4. **MRP → CRP (Capacity Requirements Planning):** The plan is tested against machine & labour capacity.
5. **CRP → Feasibility Check:** If the plan is infeasible, the loop returns to the MPS for revision.
6. **Feasible Plan → Shop Floor Control (SFC):** Work orders are released to the shop floor.
7. **SFC → Purchasing & Inventory:** Raw materials are procured and issued.
8. **Execution → Feedback to Inventory & Finance:** Real-time status updates flow back to inventory records and financial accounts.
9. **Finance → Management Reports:** Cost variance and performance metrics inform management decisions.
10. **Management → MPS:** Strategic decisions (e.g., changing the product mix) flow back to the MPS, closing the loop.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Forecast/Orders] → [MPS] ⇄ [BOM] → [MRP] → [CRP] → {Feasible?} 
                                                      ↓ Yes
                                            [Planned Orders] → [SFC]
                                                      ↓
                                            [Purchasing + Inventory]
                                                      ↓
                                            [Finance & Performance Reports]
                                                      ↓
                                            [Management Decision] → back to MPS
```

**Conclusion:** The closed-loop structure ensures that planning is continuously validated against execution reality — a defining feature of MRP-II.

---

**b) A company produces a product 'X' which requires 2 units of component 'Y'. The MPS for week 4 shows a demand of 100 units of X. The on-hand inventory of Y is 30 units. Scheduled receipts in week 3 are 50 units. Lead time for Y is 2 weeks. Using Lot-for-Lot, compute the Planned Order Release for Y.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Quantity per Assembly (qpa) = 2 units of Y per unit of X
- MPS for Week 4: 100 units of X
- On-hand inventory of Y: 30 units
- Scheduled Receipts in Week 3: 50 units
- Lead Time (LT) = 2 weeks
- Lot-Sizing Rule: Lot-for-Lot (L4L)

**Step 1: Compute Gross Requirement (GR) of Y in Week 4.**

$$
\text{GR}_Y = \text{MPS}_X \times \text{qpa} = 100 \times 2 = 200 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Available Inventory in Week 4 (just before demand).**

$$
\text{Available} = \text{On-Hand} + \text{Scheduled Receipts} = 30 + 50 = 80 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Net Requirement (NR) in Week 4.**

$$
\text{NR}_Y = \text{GR}_Y - \text{Available} = 200 - 80 = 120 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Lot-for-Lot Rule.**

$$
\text{Planned Order Receipt (POR)}_{\text{Week 4}} = \text{NR}_Y = 120 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5: Offset by Lead Time to get Planned Order Release (POL).**

$$
\text{POL}_{\text{Week}} = \text{Week of POR} - \text{LT} = 4 - 2 = \text{Week 2}
$$

**Final Answer:**
**Planned Order Release for Y = 120 units in Week 2.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing POR with POL:** Many students write the *receipt* week as the *release* week. Remember: **Release = Receipt − Lead Time**.
> 2. **Forgetting to explode the BOM:** If the question says "demand for X" and you compute Y's requirement without multiplying by `qpa`, you lose 2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Not mentioning Safety Stock:** If the question does not specify SS, ignore it. If it does, **add SS to NR** — many students mistakenly subtract it.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts:** A block diagram of MRP-II's closed loop fetches **at least 1.5 marks** even with minimal labels.
> 5. **Writing "MRP-II is the same as ERP":** This is a **favourite trap**. Always clarify that ERP *evolved from* MRP-II, but covers *much more* (HR, CRM, SCM, etc.).

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **MRP-II = MRP + CRP + SFC + Finance + Simulation** — the "closed-loop" extension of MRP.
- ✅ The **MPS** is the single most critical input — garbage MPS = garbage plan.
- ✅ MRP-II was popularised by **Oliver Wight in 1981**.
- ✅ **Closed-Loop MRP** is the *intermediate* step between MRP and MRP-II.
- ✅ MRP-II does **not** include HR, CRM, or SCM — those are added in **ERP**.
- ✅ Key formulas to memorize:
  - $\text{NR} = \text{GR} - \text{OH} - \text{SR} + \text{SS}$
  - $\text{POL} = \text{POR Date} - \text{Lead Time}$
  - $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ (EOQ lot-sizing)
- ✅ Advantages: capacity validation, financial integration, simulation.
- ✅ Limitations: high cost, requires accurate data, limited to manufacturing.
- ✅ Most-asked exam topics: evolution, comparison with MRP/ERP, closed-loop flow diagram, basic MRP table computation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Hack:** Memorize the **comparison table** (MRP vs. MRP-II vs. ERP) and the **closed-loop block diagram**. Together, they can answer **40–50%** of all Module 3 questions in a KTU exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to ERP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to ERP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** is defined as a comprehensive, integrated, cross-functional software architecture that automates and unifies an organisation's critical business processes by sharing a **single, centralised relational database** across all functional departments in **real time** to support operational excellence, managerial decision-making, and strategic planning.

In the language of the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme Operations Management syllabus**, ERP is positioned as the operational backbone of modern enterprises — a tool that converts fragmented, siloed departmental data into a unified, transparent, and decision-ready information system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Central Nervous System" of a Company

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine a cricket team without a captain. The batsman does not know what the bowler is planning, the fielders do not know the batsman's weakness, and the physio has no clue about fatigue levels. Every player works in a silo. The team performs poorly.
>
> Now, install a **captain with a wireless earpiece** connected to every player, the dressing room, the analyst, the physio, and the strategy team. Suddenly, the bowler instantly knows the batsman's weakness, the fielder gets real-time positioning, and the coach changes the field the moment a wicket falls. **This wireless network + the captain is ERP.**

**Key Intuitions to Lock In:**

- **The Central Database** = The captain's single source of truth.
- **Modules (Finance, HR, Sales, Production, etc.)** = The individual players/departments, each with a defined role.
- **Real-time Integration** = The wireless earpiece — data flows instantly between departments.
- **Single User Interface** = The captain's dashboard.

### 1.3 Glossary of Mandatory KTU Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Term | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | **ERP** | Enterprise Resource Planning — integrated software for managing the entire enterprise. |
> | **MRP** | Material Requirements Planning — the *predecessor* of ERP focused on inventory and BOM. |
> | **MRP II** | Manufacturing Resource Planning — extends MRP to shop floor capacity and scheduling. |
> | **Module** | An independent functional sub-system of ERP (e.g., Finance Module). |
> | **OLAP** | Online Analytical Processing — the analytical query layer on top of ERP data. |
> | **BPR** | Business Process Re-engineering — the process redesign that often accompanies ERP. |
> | **SaaS / Cloud ERP** | ERP delivered as a subscription service over the internet. |
> | **CRM / SCM** | Customer Relationship Management / Supply Chain Management — modules often integrated with ERP. |

### 1.4 Why ERP Exists — The Problem It Solves

> [!NOTE]
> Before ERP, organisations ran **legacy, stand-alone systems** — a separate software for payroll, another for inventory, another for accounts. The result was:
> - **Data Redundancy** (same customer data stored 5 times).
> - **Data Inconsistency** (inventory says 100 units; sales says 80; finance says 120).
> - **Manual Reconciliation** (spreadsheet hell).
> - **Latency in Decision-Making** (month-end closing takes 15 days).
>
> ERP eliminates this by enforcing **one database, one source of truth, one version of the numbers**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content

### 2.1 The Evolution of ERP — A Structured Timeline

ERP did not appear in a single stroke. It evolved through four distinct generations. **This is a high-frequency KTU question topic.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 1 — Inventory Control Systems (1960s):**
> Basic computerised systems tracking inventory reorder points using **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)**. Standalone, department-specific.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 2 — MRP — Material Requirements Planning (1970s):**
> Used the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** to calculate *what*, *how many*, and *when* to procure or manufacture. Answered: *"When do I need to order raw materials?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 3 — MRP II — Manufacturing Resource Planning (1980s):**
> Closed the loop. MRP II integrated MRP with **shop floor capacity planning, machine scheduling, and costing**. It simulated *"What if we accept this order?"* before committing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 4 — ERP — Enterprise Resource Planning (1990s):**
> Extended MRP II beyond manufacturing to the **entire enterprise** — finance, HR, sales, procurement, projects. Added **client-server architecture** and a **centralised relational database**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 5 — ERP II / Extended ERP (2000s onwards):**
> Web-based, **service-oriented architecture (SOA)**, integrated with **CRM, SCM, BI, and e-commerce**. Today, this is delivered as **Cloud ERP (SaaS)**.

**Why this evolution matters (the "Why" behind the "How"):**
- Business complexity grew → standalone systems could not keep up.
- Globalisation demanded **real-time, cross-border visibility**.
- The **Internet** enabled web-based access.
- Customer expectations shifted to **24/7 self-service portals**.

### 2.2 Architecture of an ERP System — The Three Logical Tiers

> [!NOTE]
> A standard ERP system uses a **three-tier architecture**. This is a Board-favourite diagram.

1. **Presentation Layer (Front-End / GUI)** — The user interface. Web browsers, mobile apps, dashboards. It only displays data; it does not store business logic.
2. **Application Layer (Business Logic)** — The "brain." Contains the rules, workflows, validation, and processing logic. Examples: "If invoice value > ₹5 lakh → trigger approval workflow."
3. **Database Layer (Data Storage)** — A single, centralised **RDBMS** (Oracle, SQL Server, SAP HANA). All modules read/write from this *one* database.

### 2.3 Core Modules of an ERP System — The KTU High-Yield Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical ERP package (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Tally, Ramco) contains a suite of modules. **Students are expected to know the purpose of at least 6-8 core modules.**

| Module | Functional Coverage | KTU-Expected Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Finance & Accounting** | General Ledger, AP/AR, fixed assets, taxation | The financial nerve centre; every transaction ultimately posts here. |
| **Human Resource Management (HRM)** | Payroll, recruitment, appraisals, leave, training | Manages the organisation's people-data; integrates attendance with payroll. |
| **Sales & Distribution (SD)** | Quotations, sales orders, delivery, billing | Connects the customer order to inventory dispatch and invoicing. |
| **Materials Management / Procurement (MM)** | Purchase requisitions, vendor management, GRN | Manages inflow of materials and vendor master data. |
| **Production Planning (PP)** | MPS, MRP, shop floor control, capacity planning | Converts demand into executable manufacturing plans. |
| **Inventory / Warehouse Management** | Stock levels, batch tracking, serial numbers | Real-time visibility of *what is where* in the supply chain. |
| **Quality Management (QM)** | Incoming inspection, in-process checks, certifications | Ensures conformance to standards (ISO, Six Sigma). |
| **Project Management** | Project costing, milestone billing, WBS | Tracks revenue, cost, and progress of long-cycle projects. |
| **Plant Maintenance (PM)** | Equipment breakdown logs, preventive maintenance schedules | Reduces downtime via predictive maintenance. |
| **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** | Lead, opportunity, complaint, service ticket | Often a bolt-on or integrated add-on to core ERP. |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Conceptual "Formula Sheet" — Benefits vs Risks

> [!NOTE]
> Since ERP is non-quantitative, the "formula sheet" is replaced by a **memorable trade-off matrix**. KTU examiners test this directly.

| Dimension | Benefits of ERP | Risks / Limitations of ERP |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Single version of truth; eliminates redundancy | Costly data migration from legacy systems |
| **Process** | Standardised best-practice workflows | Forces rigid processes; may not suit unique workflows |
| **Visibility** | Real-time dashboards across geographies | Initial implementation may take 12–36 months |
| **Cost** | Long-term OPEX reduction; lower inventory carrying cost | High CAPEX (licence + hardware + consultants) |
| **People** | Empowers decision-making with accurate data | Resistance to change; need for extensive training |
| **Vendor** | Continuous upgrades, support | Vendor lock-in; dependence on single supplier |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industrial Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Manufacturing Plants:** Synchronise production with sales orders and raw material arrival in real time.
> - **Construction & EPC Firms:** Track project costing, subcontractor bills, and milestone revenue.
> - **Logistics & Supply Chain:** End-to-end shipment visibility (WMS + TMS integration).
> - **Healthcare & Hospitality:** Patient/billing/inventory integration.
> - **Higher Education & PSUs:** Government of India initiatives (e.g., GeM, Bharatkosh) push ERP adoption even in public sector.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is **conceptual and architectural** rather than mathematical. Hence, in place of algebraic derivations, this section provides a **rigorous, step-by-step logical derivation of the ERP Implementation Life Cycle** and an **analytical matrix** that mirrors the reasoning a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation — The ERP Implementation Life Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most-tested 14-mark framework on this topic. **Memorise the 6 stages in order.**

**Stage 1 — Pre-Evaluation / As-Is Analysis**
- The organisation audits its **current (as-is) business processes**, existing IT systems, and pain points.
- Output: **Gap Analysis Report** identifying what the current system cannot do.
- *Logic:* You cannot prescribe medicine without diagnosis.

**Stage 2 — Requirement Gathering & Business Case**
- Functional and technical requirements are documented.
- ROI calculation: *Cost of ERP* vs. *Quantified Benefits* (inventory reduction, faster order-to-cash, fewer FTEs in reconciliation).
- *Logic:* Board approves a *Business Case* with a measurable payback period.

**Stage 3 — Vendor Selection & Package Finalisation**
- Shortlist ERP packages (SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Cloud ERP, Microsoft Dynamics 365, TallyPrime, Ramco, etc.).
- Evaluation criteria: **Functionality fit, Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), implementation partner capability, scalability, support**.
- *Logic:* Choose the package whose standard processes match 70-80% of your needs (the "70% fit" rule).

**Stage 4 — Configuration & Customisation**
- The package is **configured** (using in-built settings) and only minimally **customised** (writing new code).
- Master data setup: chart of accounts, vendor master, customer master, BOM, routings.
- *Logic:* Configuration is cheap and upgrade-safe; customisation is expensive and breaks with every new version.

**Stage 5 — Training & Go-Live**
- **End-user training** is conducted (often in tiers: super-users → end-users → management).
- Data migration from legacy systems is performed and **reconciled**.
- A **Go-Live** date is fixed, often with a **Pilot / Big-Bang / Phased rollout** strategy.
- *Logic:* Without trained users, even a perfect system will fail (the "people" failure mode).

**Stage 6 — Post-Implementation Support & Continuous Improvement**
- A **help-desk** is operational.
- Bug fixes, periodic upgrades, and **KPI dashboards** track realised benefits.
- *Logic:* Go-live is *not* the end — it is the beginning of optimisation.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why ERP Forces a Paradigm Shift (Cause → Effect)

| Cause (Business Driver) | Effect (What ERP Delivers) | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Decentralised decision-making | Centralised data warehouse with role-based access | Replacing independent local sensors with a SCADA control room |
| Manual reconciliation errors | Single integrated database | Replacing a chain with a single, precision-machined gear train |
| Long order-to-cash cycle | Automated order-to-invoice workflow | Replacing manual gear-shifting with automatic transmission |
| Compliance & audit pressure | Built-in statutory reports (GST, TDS, IFRS) | Installing an always-on diagnostic scanner |
| Global multi-plant operations | Multi-currency, multi-language, multi-plant master data | Installing a universal operating system across machines |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ERP vs Standalone Functional Software

| Parameter | Standalone Software (Legacy) | ERP System |
|---|---|---|
| Database | Separate per department | **One central** database |
| Data Entry | Repeated in every module | Enter once, use everywhere |
| Reporting | Departmental, fragmented | Cross-functional, unified |
| Real-time visibility | Latency (end of day) | **Real-time** |
| Cost (long-term) | Low initial, high hidden cost | High initial, low operational cost |
| Process standardisation | Ad-hoc | Enforced best-practice workflows |
| Scalability | Poor (silos break) | Built-in (designed for scale) |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example — "How a Single Transaction Moves Through ERP"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A classic **KTU 14-mark "explain with example" question** is: *"Trace a customer order through an integrated ERP system."* The complete logical chain is:

1. **Sales Order Created** in the **SD Module** → inventory availability is checked via real-time read of the **Inventory Module**.
2. If stock is insufficient, the **MRP run** in the **PP Module** triggers a **Purchase Requisition** in the **MM Module**.
3. The **MM Module** raises a **Purchase Order** to the vendor.
4. Goods Receipt Note (GRN) is posted in **MM** → stock updates in **Inventory** in real time.
5. **Delivery** is created in **SD** → the **Inventory** stock is reduced.
6. **Billing** is auto-generated in **SD** → an **Accounting Document** is auto-posted in **Finance** (debtor entry).
7. The customer pays → **Bank Reconciliation** auto-posts in **Finance**.
8. Simultaneously, the **Costing** sub-module calculates gross margin, and the **BI/Analytics** layer refreshes the management dashboard.

**The "Why" of this chain:** A *single event* — the customer order — triggers 8 cross-functional transactions **without a single manual re-entry of data**. This is the **integrated ERP promise** in action.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Two board-ready Mermaid diagrams are provided below. The first maps the **three-tier ERP architecture**; the second traces the **evolutionary lineage** of ERP. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to avoid clashes with Mermaid reserved keywords.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Three-Tier ERP Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[User Layer - Browsers and Mobile Apps] --> A2[Presentation Tier - Web GUI and Dashboards]
    A2 --> A3[Application Tier - Business Logic and Workflow Engine]
    A3 --> A4[Database Tier - Centralised RDBMS]
    A4 --> A3
    A3 --> A2
    A2 --> A1
    A3 --> B1[Finance and Accounting Module]
    A3 --> B2[HRM Module]
    A3 --> B3[Sales and Distribution Module]
    A3 --> B4[Production Planning Module]
    A3 --> B5[Materials Management Module]
    A3 --> B6[Inventory and Warehouse Module]
    A3 --> B7[Quality Management Module]
    A3 --> B8[Project Management Module]
    A4 --> C1[Master Data Tables]
    A4 --> C2[Transaction Data Tables]
    A4 --> C3[Analytical Data Warehouse]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Evolution of ERP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 - 1960s - Inventory Control Systems] --> S2[Stage 2 - 1970s - MRP - Material Requirements Planning]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 - 1980s - MRP II - Manufacturing Resource Planning]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 - 1990s - ERP - Enterprise Resource Planning]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 - 2000s Onwards - ERP II - Web Enabled and SOA]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 - 2010s Onwards - Cloud ERP - SaaS and AI Enabled]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — End-to-End Order-to-Cash Flow Inside ERP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 - Customer Order in SD Module] --> P2[Step 2 - Real-time Inventory Check in WM Module]
    P2 --> P3{Step 3 - Stock Available?}
    P3 -- No --> P4[Step 4 - MRP Run in PP Module Generates Purchase Requisition]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 - Purchase Order to Vendor in MM Module]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 - Goods Receipt Updates Inventory in WM]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 - Delivery and Billing in SD Module]
    P3 -- Yes --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 - Auto Accounting Entry in Finance Module]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 - Customer Payment and Bank Reconciliation in Finance]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 - MIS Dashboard and Analytics Refreshed]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model 1] — Bloom Level: Remember**
> **Define Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP). List any four functional modules of an ERP system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** ERP is an integrated, cross-functional software system that uses a **single centralised relational database** to automate and unify an organisation's core business processes — including finance, HR, manufacturing, supply chain, and sales — in **real time** to support operational efficiency and strategic decision-making.

**Four Functional Modules (½ mark each):**
1. **Finance & Accounting** (GL, AP, AR, Asset Accounting).
2. **Human Resource Management** (Payroll, Attendance, Appraisal).
3. **Sales & Distribution** (Quotation, Order, Delivery, Billing).
4. **Production Planning** (MPS, MRP, Shop Floor Control).
*(Any four equivalent modules accepted.)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model 2] — Bloom Level: Understand**
> **Differentiate between MRP and MRP II. Why is MRP II considered a stepping stone to ERP?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | MRP | MRP II |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Materials & BOM | Materials + Capacity + Costing |
| **Time horizon** | "What & when to order" | "What, when, with what capacity, at what cost" |
| **Feedback loop** | Open loop (no feedback from shop floor) | Closed loop (feedback from execution) |
| **Scope** | Inventory-centric | Manufacturing-wide |

**Why a stepping stone to ERP (1 mark):** MRP II introduced **closed-loop feedback, capacity planning, and shop-floor integration**, which proved that *manufacturing-wide integration* was possible. ERP simply extended this *integrated* philosophy to the **entire enterprise** — finance, HR, sales, projects — using a **centralised database**, thereby inheriting MRP II's DNA.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — Bloom Level: Apply / Analyse**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-tier architecture of an ERP system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **centralised database** in ensuring data integrity.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six-stage ERP implementation life cycle**. What is the significance of the *"70% fit rule"* in package selection?

#### Model Solution for Q3(a) — Architecture of ERP (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The three-tier architecture separates the ERP system into three logical layers, each performing a distinct function.

**Tier 1 — Presentation Layer (2 marks):**
- The **front-end interface** — web browsers, mobile apps, GUI dashboards.
- Renders data for users; contains no business logic.
- Provides **role-based screens** (e.g., a store-keeper sees GRN; an accountant sees journal entries).

**Tier 2 — Application Layer (2 marks):**
- The **business logic engine** — workflows, validations, approval rules, calculations.
- Example rule: *"If invoice value > ₹5,00,000 → route to CFO for approval."*
- Houses the **modules** (Finance, HR, PP, MM, SD, etc.) and the **middleware** that enables them to communicate.

**Tier 3 — Database Layer (1 mark):**
- A single, **centralised RDBMS** (e.g., SAP HANA, Oracle, MS SQL Server).
- All modules **read from and write to** the same database → eliminates data silos.

**Role of Centralised Database in Data Integrity (1 mark):**
- **Eliminates redundancy** (one customer master, not five).
- **Enforces referential integrity** (foreign keys between sales order and inventory).
- **Provides real-time consistency** (sales sees what production sees).
- **Enables single-version reporting** for statutory and management use.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):** 1 mark for labelled three-tier block diagram.

#### Model Solution for Q3(b) — ERP Implementation Life Cycle (7 Marks)

**The six stages (1 mark per stage, totalling 6 marks):**

1. **Pre-Evaluation & As-Is Study** — Audit current processes; identify gaps; quantify pain.
2. **Business Case & ROI** — Quantify cost vs. benefit; secure board approval.
3. **Package / Vendor Selection** — Evaluate SAP, Oracle, MS Dynamics, Tally, Ramco against a weighted scorecard.
4. **Configuration & Customisation** — Configure the standard package; minimise custom code; set up master data.
5. **Training, Data Migration & Go-Live** — Train users; migrate legacy data; choose Big-Bang vs. Phased go-live.
6. **Post-Implementation Support & Continuous Improvement** — Help-desk, upgrades, KPI tracking, benefits realisation.

**The 70% Fit Rule (1 mark):**
- The 70% fit rule states that the organisation should select a package whose **standard (vanilla) processes match at least 70% of its business requirements** *without customisation*.
- **Rationale:** Customisation is **expensive, slow, and breaks with every upgrade**. A package that fits 70% out-of-the-box allows the organisation to **adapt its processes to the package's best practices** (lower TCO, faster go-live, upgrade-safe). The remaining 30% is either configured, lightly customised, or handled via bolt-on modules.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module Choice] — Bloom Level: Analyse / Evaluate**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **evolution of ERP** from MRP to Cloud ERP, highlighting the key capability added at each stage.
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Trace the journey of a **customer sales order** through an integrated ERP system, explaining how it touches at least five different modules in real time. What business value does this integration deliver?

#### Model Solution for Q4(a) — Evolution of ERP (7 Marks)

**Six evolutionary stages (1 mark each, brief explanation):**

1. **Inventory Control Systems (1960s)** — Computerised reorder point logic using EOQ; *capability added:* basic inventory visibility.
2. **MRP — Material Requirements Planning (1970s)** — Uses **BOM + MPS** to explode demand; *capability added:* time-phased material planning.
3. **MRP II — Manufacturing Resource Planning (1980s)** — Adds **capacity planning, shop floor control, costing**; *capability added:* closed-loop manufacturing simulation.
4. **ERP — Enterprise Resource Planning (1990s)** — Extends beyond manufacturing to **finance, HR, SCM, projects** on a **centralised relational database**; *capability added:* enterprise-wide integration.
5. **ERP II (2000s)** — **Web-based, SOA-enabled**; integrates **CRM, SCM, BI, e-commerce**; *capability added:* inter-enterprise and customer-facing collaboration.
6. **Cloud ERP / SaaS (2010s onwards)** — Delivered over the **internet on subscription**; *capability added:* low upfront CAPEX, anywhere-access, AI/ML-driven insights, automatic upgrades.

**Connecting statement (1 mark):** Each stage **absorbed the previous** and added a new capability boundary — from a single function (inventory) to the entire enterprise to the entire *ecosystem*.

#### Model Solution for Q4(b) — Tracing a Sales Order Through ERP (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step trace (1 mark per step, 6 steps covered in detail, 1 mark for the concluding business value):**

1. **Sales Order Creation (SD Module)** — The sales executive raises a sales order in the SD module against a customer master record.
2. **Real-time Inventory Availability Check (Inventory/WM Module)** — The system reads the **centralised database** in real time and confirms (or rejects) availability.
3. **Production & Procurement Trigger (PP + MM Modules)** — If stock is short, **MRP** in PP generates a **planned order**, which the planner converts into a **Purchase Requisition** in MM; a Purchase Order goes to the vendor.
4. **Goods Receipt & Quality Inspection (MM + QM Modules)** — When materials arrive, GRN updates inventory; QM performs inspection and posts a usage decision.
5. **Delivery, Billing & Accounting (SD + Finance Modules)** — A Delivery document reduces inventory; a Billing document auto-generates a **Customer Invoice**; an **accounting document** is auto-posted in Finance (Debtor + Revenue + Tax entries).
6. **Payment & Reporting (Finance + BI Module)** — On customer payment, bank reconciliation auto-posts; management dashboards refresh with real-time revenue, receivables ageing, and gross margin.

**Business Value Delivered (1 mark):**
- **Single data entry, eight downstream effects** — eliminates manual re-keying.
- **Real-time, consistent information** — the same order value is visible simultaneously to sales, production, finance, and management.
- **Faster order-to-cash cycle**, lower inventory carrying cost, fewer reconciliation errors, and a **single audit trail** for statutory compliance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in ERP Questions — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Writing only the definition, not the "Why" (Lose 1-2 marks):** A definition alone is not enough. Examiners expect the *operational purpose* (e.g., *"ERP eliminates data silos and provides real-time, cross-functional visibility"*). Always add the *why* after the *what*.
> 2. **Confusing MRP with MRP II (Lose 1-2 marks):** MRP = materials + BOM; MRP II = MRP + capacity + costing + shop-floor feedback. Mixing them up is a classic board-exam pitfall.
> 3. **Listing modules without explaining their integration (Lose 2-3 marks in 14-mark answers):** A 14-mark question expects you to *trace a transaction across modules* or *explain cross-module integration*, not just list names.
> 4. **Forgetting the centralised database in the architecture (Lose 1 mark):** The *single centralised database* is the **defining feature** of ERP. Omit it, and the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Ignoring the risks / limitations of ERP (Lose 1 mark in advantage-disadvantage questions):** KTU examiners reward balanced answers. Always mention at least 2 limitations (high cost, long implementation, resistance to change, vendor lock-in, customisation risk).
> 6. **No diagram in a 14-mark answer (Lose 1-2 marks):** A neat, **labelled block diagram** (three-tier architecture, evolution flow, or transaction trace) is mandatory. It earns easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Introduction to ERP**
>
> - **ERP** = integrated software + **single centralised database** + **real-time** cross-functional visibility.
> - The **evolutionary lineage** is: Inventory Control → **MRP** → **MRP II** → **ERP** → **ERP II** → **Cloud ERP (SaaS)**.
> - The **three-tier architecture**: Presentation → Application → Database. *Always draw the diagram.*
> - The **defining feature** of ERP is the **single shared database** — not the modules themselves.
> - **Core modules** to remember: Finance, HRM, SD, MM, PP, Inventory/WM, QM, PM, Project System. *At least 6 expected in an exam answer.*
> - **Six-stage implementation life cycle:** As-Is Study → Business Case → Package Selection → Configuration & Customisation → Training & Go-Live → Post-Implementation Support.
> - **70% fit rule** = select a package that covers ≥70% of requirements *as-is*; minimise customisation.
> - **Benefits:** single source of truth, real-time reporting, standardised processes, faster order-to-cash, better compliance.
> - **Limitations / risks:** high cost, long implementation time, resistance to change, vendor lock-in, customisation fragility, data migration risk.
> - **Classic 14-mark framing:** *"Trace a customer order / purchase order through an integrated ERP system."* Practice drawing the **8-step end-to-end flow** with module names.
> - Always include a **block diagram** in any 14-mark answer — it is a guaranteed 1-2 mark earner.
> - Connect every concept back to the **"single database + real-time + cross-functional"** mantra. This is the examiner's mental model of a "perfect ERP answer."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activity Planning, Scheduling, Shop Loading, and Sequencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Activity Planning, Scheduling, Shop Loading, and Sequencing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Activity Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Activity Planning** is the systematic process of identifying, listing, and defining all the individual tasks (activities) that must be performed to complete a project, along with their **duration**, **resource requirements**, and **precedence relationships**.

**Decomposition of the concept:**
- An **activity** is any work element that consumes **time** and **resources**.
- Activity Planning answers the question: **"What work must be done?"**
- It forms the foundation for **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** in project management.

**Real-World Analogy:** Imagine planning your sister's wedding. You don't just say "have a wedding." You break it down:
- Book the venue (1 week)
- Send invitations (2 weeks before)
- Order catering (1 month before)
- Buy dress (2 months before)

Each of these is an **activity**. Listing them, deciding how long they take, and figuring out what must come first is **Activity Planning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Activity Planning is the **first stage** in Production Planning and Control (PPC). Without it, scheduling and sequencing cannot proceed.

---

## 1.2 Scheduling

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Scheduling** is the process of determining **when** each activity should start and finish, considering precedence constraints, resource availability, and project deadlines. It converts the activity plan into a **time-bound execution roadmap**.

**Key elements of scheduling:**
- **Start time** and **Finish time** of each activity
- **Gantt Charts** (visual bar-chart representation)
- **Critical Path Method (CPM)** – identifies the longest path
- **Forward Pass** (Earliest times) and **Backward Pass** (Latest times)

**Real-World Analogy:** Once you know all the wedding tasks, scheduling tells you *exactly* which day to do each. "Send invitations on 15th November, book the pandit on 1st December." The schedule is your **calendar of execution**.

---

## 1.3 Shop Loading

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Shop Loading** is the process of **assigning work** (jobs/orders) to specific **work centers, machines, or departments** in a manufacturing facility, ensuring that the capacity of each work center is neither underutilized nor overloaded.

**Important characteristics:**
- Also called **Capacity Allocation** or **Load Assignment**.
- Performed **after scheduling** but **before sequencing**.
- Output: A **Load Chart** (Gantt-style chart showing which job is loaded on which machine, and for how long).

**Real-World Analogy:** Think of a hospital. Once surgeries are scheduled (which surgery, when), the hospital must decide **which operation theatre** each surgery goes to. Theatre-1 gets a heart surgery, Theatre-2 gets a knee replacement. That's shop loading — distributing tasks to specific resources.

---

## 1.4 Sequencing

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Sequencing** is the process of determining the **order** in which jobs should be processed on machines/work centers to optimize one or more performance measures such as **makespan**, **flow time**, **tardy jobs**, or **idle time**.

**Key Sequencing rules in KTU syllabus:**
- **Johnson's Rule** — for 2 machines and *n* jobs
- **CDS (Campbell, Dudek, Smith) Algorithm** — extension of Johnson's rule for *n* machines
- **Palmer's Heuristic (Slope Index Method)** — for *n* jobs on *m* machines

**Real-World Analogy:** In a laundromat with 3 machines (wash, dry, fold), 10 customers are waiting. Sequencing decides the **order**: Customer A goes first, then B, then C — chosen to minimize total waiting time. The order of processing is sequencing.

---

## 1.5 Interrelationship: The PPC Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aggregate Planning] --> B[Activity Planning]
    B --> C[Scheduling]
    C --> D[Shop Loading]
    D --> E[Sequencing]
    E --> F[Dispatching]
    F --> G[Execution & Control]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the order:** **"A SLaSh"** → **A**ctivity → **S**cheduling → **L**oading → **S**equencing. This is the canonical flow within KTU's Production Planning and Control chapter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Production Planning — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Activity Planning — The "What" Stage
- Decomposes a project or order into **workable activities**.
- Each activity gets:
  - A **name / ID**
  - An **estimated duration** (often using PERT: $t_e = \frac{o + 4m + p}{6}$)
  - A list of **predecessors**
  - **Resource requirements** (manpower, machines, materials)

### 2.1.2 Scheduling — The "When" Stage
Two major types:
1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — high-level production targets.
2. **Detailed Schedule** — specific operations on specific machines.

**Forward Pass Formulas (CPM):**
- $ES_i = \max_{j \in \text{pred}(i)} (EF_j)$
- $EF_i = ES_i + t_i$

**Backward Pass Formulas (CPM):**
- $LF_i = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(i)} (LS_k)$
- $LS_i = LF_i - t_i$

**Float/Slack:**
- $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$
- $FF_i = ES_{i+1} - EF_i$ (for successor $i+1$)

### 2.1.3 Shop Loading — The "Where" Stage
Two strategic approaches:
- **Infinite Loading** (push system): Load all jobs ignoring capacity.
- **Finite Loading** (pull system): Load only what capacity allows (often used in **Theory of Constraints**).

**Load Chart:** A Gantt chart on a **machine-vs-time axis** showing which job is on which machine during which time period.

### 2.1.4 Sequencing — The "In What Order" Stage
Three performance measures KTU tests most:
1. **Makespan** ($C_{max}$) — total time to complete all jobs.
2. **Flow Time** ($F_j = C_j - R_j$) — time a job spends in system.
3. **Tardiness** ($T_j = \max(0, C_j - d_j)$) — lateness beyond due date.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Johnson's Rule – 2 machine | Min of $A_i$ and $B_i$ decides placement; smaller goes first (on A) or last (on B) | $A_i$ = time on M/c 1, $B_i$ = time on M/c 2 |
| Johnson's Rule condition | Works exactly when $\min(A_i) \geq \max(B_i)$ or $\min(B_i) \geq \max(A_i)$ | Otherwise gives approximate solution |
| Makespan | $C_{max} = \max(C_j)$ | Hours/Days |
| Mean Flow Time | $\bar{F} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} F_j$ | Hours/Days |
| Elapsed Time | $C_j$ — completion time of job $j$ | Hours/Days |
| CDS Algorithm | Convert $n$ machines to $k$ fictitious 2-machine problems ($k = n-1$); solve each by Johnson; pick best $C_{max}$ | $k = n - 1$ alternative pairings |
| Palmer's Slope Index | $S_i = -1$ (last m/c) to $+1$ (first m/c); index $= 2k - (m+1)$ where $k$ is machine rank | Used for tie-breaking |
| Earliest Start | $ES_i = \max(EF_{\text{predecessors}})$ | Days |
| Latest Finish | $LF_i = \min(LS_{\text{successors}})$ | Days |
| Total Float | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | Zero for critical activities |
| Load of Work Center | $\text{Load} = \sum (\text{Standard hours of jobs assigned})$ | Hours |
| Utilization | $U = \frac{\text{Loaded hours}}{\text{Available hours}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| PERT Expected Time | $t_e = \frac{o + 4m + p}{6}$ | Days (Optimistic, Most likely, Pessimistic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** When applying **Johnson's Rule**, always list jobs in a single column, identify the minimum value in the entire row, and place that job either at the **earliest available position** (if minimum is on Machine A) or at the **latest available position** (if minimum is on Machine B). Strike off the job and **repeat** until all jobs are placed.

---

## 2.3 Why This Topic Matters in Real Engineering

- **Project managers** (construction, software, R&D) use these techniques daily.
- **Manufacturing plants** use sequencing rules to reduce WIP and meet delivery dates.
- **Service operations** (banks, hospitals) apply loading logic to distribute customers across service counters.
- **Lean and Six Sigma practitioners** rely on shop loading to eliminate bottlenecks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In Indian manufacturing (e.g., Tata Steel, Ashok Leyland, Kerala's KSFE production units), sequencing directly affects **on-time delivery performance (OTDP)** — a key KPI evaluated by KTU case studies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Johnson's Rule — 2 Machines, 5 Jobs (14-mark standard)

**Problem Statement:** Five jobs are to be processed on two machines in the order **M1 → M2**. Processing times (in hours) are given. Determine the **optimal sequence** and compute the **total elapsed time (makespan)** and **average flow time**.

| Job | M1 (hours) | M2 (hours) |
|-----|------------|------------|
| A   | 5          | 2          |
| B   | 1          | 6          |
| C   | 9          | 7          |
| D   | 3          | 8          |
| E   | 10         | 4          |

### Step-by-Step Solution using Johnson's Rule

**Step 1: Verify Johnson's applicability**
- $\min(A_i) = 1$ (Job B, on M1)
- $\max(B_i) = 8$ (Job D, on M2)
- Since $\min(A_i) = 1 < \max(B_i) = 8$, Johnson's rule gives an **approximate (heuristic)** solution — not a guaranteed optimum. KTU still expects Johnson's application.

**Step 2: Identify minimum processing time in the entire table**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Minimum of all } A_i, B_i \\
&= \min(5, 1, 9, 3, 10, 2, 6, 7, 8, 4) \\
&= 1 \quad \text{(Job B on M1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the minimum is on **M1**, place Job B in the **earliest available position**.

Sequence so far: **[ B , , , , ]**

Strike off Job B. Updated times:

| Job | M1 | M2 |
|-----|----|----|
| A   | 5  | 2  |
| C   | 9  | 7  |
| D   | 3  | 8  |
| E   | 10 | 4  |

**Step 3: Next minimum**

$$
\min(5, 9, 3, 10, 2, 7, 8, 4) = 2 \text{ (Job A on M2)}
$$

Minimum is on **M2** → place Job A in the **latest available position**.

Sequence: **[ B , , , A ]**

Strike off A. Updated:

| Job | M1 | M2 |
|-----|----|----|
| C   | 9  | 7  |
| D   | 3  | 8  |
| E   | 10 | 4  |

**Step 4: Next minimum**

$$
\min(9, 3, 10, 7, 8, 4) = 3 \text{ (Job D on M1)}
$$

Place D at the **earliest available position** (because M1).

Sequence: **[ B , D , , A ]**

Updated:

| Job | M1 | M2 |
|-----|----|----|
| C   | 9  | 7  |
| E   | 10 | 4  |

**Step 5: Next minimum**

$$
\min(9, 10, 7, 4) = 4 \text{ (Job E on M2)}
$$

Place E at the **latest available position** (because M2).

Sequence: **[ B , D , , A ]** → place E at the position next to A.

Sequence: **[ B , D , E , A ]**

**Step 6: Place remaining job C** at the only empty slot.

**Final Optimal Sequence: B → D → C → E → A**

**Step 7: Compute Elapsed Time Table**

| Job | M1 In | M1 Out | M2 In | M2 Out |
|-----|-------|--------|-------|--------|
| B   | 0     | 1      | 1     | 7      |
| D   | 1     | 4      | 7     | 15     |
| C   | 4     | 13     | 15    | 22     |
| E   | 13    | 23     | 23    | 27     |
| A   | 23    | 28     | 28    | 30     |

**Idle time on M1:** between Job B out (1) and Job D in (1) → **0 hrs**
**Idle time on M2:** before B starts (0–1) → 1 hr, and before E starts (22–23) → 1 hr, before A (27–28) → 1 hr.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Elapsed Time (Makespan)} \ C_{max} &= 30 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Total Flow Time} &= (7 - 0) + (15 - 0) + (22 - 0) + (27 - 0) + (30 - 0) \\
&= 7 + 15 + 22 + 27 + 30 = 101 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Average Flow Time} &= \frac{101}{5} = 20.2 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Trick:** The makespan = $A_{\text{first job}} + B_{\text{last job}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \max(0, \text{overlap})$. Many students verify using the sum rule: $\sum A_i + \sum B_i - \text{idle time on M2}$ (when no idle on M1). Here: $5+1+9+3+10 + 2+6+7+8+4 = 55$ hrs of total work; if both machines worked full time, makespan = 55/2 ≈ 27.5. We got 30 because of M2 idling.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: CDS Algorithm — 3 Machines, 4 Jobs (14-mark standard)

**Problem Statement:** Find the optimal sequence using **CDS algorithm** for 4 jobs on 3 machines.

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|-----|----|----|----|
| J1  | 4  | 5  | 8  |
| J2  | 9  | 3  | 7  |
| J3  | 8  | 1  | 3  |
| J4  | 6  | 4  | 5  |

### CDS Method — Step-by-Step Derivation

For $n$ machines, the CDS algorithm generates **$k = n - 1 = 2$** fictitious 2-machine problems by combining pairs of machines.

**Fictitious Problem 1 (Pair: M1+M2 vs M2+M3)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= M1 + M2 \\
H_1 &= M2 + M3
\end{aligned}
$$

| Job | $G_1 = M1 + M2$ | $H_1 = M2 + M3$ |
|-----|------------------|------------------|
| J1  | 4 + 5 = 9        | 5 + 8 = 13       |
| J2  | 9 + 3 = 12       | 3 + 7 = 10       |
| J3  | 8 + 1 = 9        | 1 + 3 = 4        |
| J4  | 6 + 4 = 10       | 4 + 5 = 9        |

Apply Johnson's Rule to this $G_1, H_1$ table:

**Iteration 1:** $\min(9, 12, 9, 10, 13, 10, 4, 9) = 4$ (J3, $H_1$ = M2+M3 side) → Place J3 at the **end**.

Sequence: **[ _ , _ , _ , J3 ]**

**Iteration 2:** Remaining jobs J1, J2, J4. Min of $G_1$: 9 (J1, J3), $H_1$: 9 (J4). J1's 9 is on $G_1$ side → place J1 at the **start**.

Sequence: **[ J1 , _ , _ , J3 ]**

**Iteration 3:** Remaining J2, J4. Min $G_1$: J2=12, J4=10 → 10 is J4 on $G_1$ → place J4 at the **earliest free position**.

Sequence: **[ J1 , J4 , _ , J3 ]**

**Iteration 4:** Place J2 in remaining slot.

**Sequence from Problem 1: J1 → J4 → J2 → J3**

Now compute **elapsed time** for this sequence on the **original 3-machine data**:

| Job | M1 In | M1 Out | M2 In | M2 Out | M3 In | M3 Out |
|-----|-------|--------|-------|--------|-------|--------|
| J1  | 0     | 4      | 4     | 9      | 9     | 17     |
| J4  | 4     | 10     | 10    | 14     | 17    | 22     |
| J2  | 10    | 19     | 19    | 22     | 22    | 29     |
| J3  | 19    | 27     | 27    | 28     | 29    | 32     |

**Elapsed Time (Problem 1) = 32 hours**

---

**Fictitious Problem 2 (Pair: M1 vs M2+M3)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_2 &= M1 \\
H_2 &= M2 + M3
\end{aligned}
$$

| Job | $G_2 = M1$ | $H_2 = M2 + M3$ |
|-----|------------|------------------|
| J1  | 4          | 13               |
| J2  | 9          | 10               |
| J3  | 8          | 4                |
| J4  | 6          | 9                |

Apply Johnson's Rule:

**Iteration 1:** $\min(4, 9, 8, 6, 13, 10, 4, 9) = 4$ → two options: J1 on $G_2$ (M1) or J3 on $H_2$ (M2+M3). Convention: pick the one found first. J1 on $G_2$ → place J1 at the **start**.

Sequence: **[ J1 , _ , _ , _ ]**

**Iteration 2:** Min = 4 (J3 on $H_2$) → place J3 at the **end**.

Sequence: **[ J1 , _ , _ , J3 ]**

**Iteration 3:** Remaining J2, J4. Min $G_2$: 6 (J4) → place J4 at the **earliest free position**.

Sequence: **[ J1 , J4 , _ , J3 ]**

**Iteration 4:** Place J2.

**Sequence from Problem 2: J1 → J4 → J2 → J3**

Compute elapsed time on original 3-machine data:

Same table as above (since the sequence coincides) → **Elapsed Time = 32 hours**.

---

### CDS Final Answer

Since both fictitious problems yield the **same sequence (J1 → J4 → J2 → J3)** with **makespan = 32 hours**, this is the **CDS-optimal sequence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two iterations?** The CDS algorithm generates $k = n - 1$ alternatives. With $n = 3$ machines, we get **2 fictitious 2-machine problems**. KTU expects you to compute both, find the makespan for each, and **select the minimum makespan sequence**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Shop Loading — Load Chart Construction

**Problem Statement:** A workshop has 3 machines (M1, M2, M3). 4 jobs with the following standard hours must be assigned:

| Job | M1 | M2 | M3 |
|-----|----|----|----|
| A   | 5  | 3  | 4  |
| B   | 2  | 6  | 3  |
| C   | 4  | 2  | 5  |
| D   | 3  | 4  | 2  |

Available capacity: **40 hours per week per machine**.

**Solution:**

**Total load on each machine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{M1} &= 5 + 2 + 4 + 3 = 14 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{M2} &= 3 + 6 + 2 + 4 = 15 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{M3} &= 4 + 3 + 5 + 2 = 14 \text{ hrs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Utilization:**

$$
U_{M1} = \frac{14}{40} \times 100\% = 35\%, \quad U_{M2} = 37.5\%, \quad U_{M3} = 35\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> All three machines are **under-loaded**, indicating excess capacity. Production planner can take additional orders to improve utilization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow: How Activity Planning Cascades into Sequencing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Customer Order or Project] --> A1[Identify Activities]
    A1 --> A2[Estimate Duration and Resources]
    A2 --> A3[Establish Precedence]
    A3 --> A4[Activity Plan Ready]

    A4 --> B1[Build Master Schedule]
    B1 --> B2[Apply CPM Forward Pass]
    B2 --> B3[Apply CPM Backward Pass]
    B3 --> B4[Compute Floats]
    B4 --> B5[Schedule Ready with Gantt Chart]

    B5 --> C1[Assign Jobs to Work Centers]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Machine Load]
    C2 --> C3[Check Capacity vs Load]
    C3 --> C4[Rebalance or Accept]
    C4 --> C5[Load Chart Ready]

    C5 --> D1[Apply Johnson or CDS Rule]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Makespan]
    D2 --> D3[Verify Against Deadline]
    D3 --> D4[Final Sequence]
```

---

## 4.2 Johnson's Rule Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Start with n jobs on 2 machines] --> N1[Build time table with A and B columns]
    N1 --> N2[Find overall minimum time]
    N2 --> N3{Is min on Machine A?}
    N3 -- Yes --> N4[Place job at earliest empty slot]
    N3 -- No --> N5[Place job at latest empty slot]
    N4 --> N6[Strike off the job]
    N5 --> N6
    N6 --> N7{All jobs placed?}
    N7 -- No --> N2
    N7 -- Yes --> N8[Final sequence ready]
    N8 --> N9[Compute makespan from elapsed time table]
```

---

## 4.3 CDS Algorithm Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Step1: n machines k equals n minus 1] --> A2[Step2: Build k fictitious 2 machine problems]
    A2 --> A3[Step3: For each pairing apply Johnson rule]
    A3 --> A4[Step4: Compute makespan for each sequence]
    A4 --> A5{Compare makespans}
    A5 --> B1[Pick minimum makespan sequence]
    A5 --> B2[Tie break by Palmer slope index]
    B1 --> A6[Final CDS sequence]
    B2 --> A6
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Gantt Chart for a 5-Job Schedule

| Time Slot | 0-1 | 1-4 | 4-7 | 7-13 | 13-22 | 22-27 | 27-28 | 28-30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Machine 1** | JOB B | JOB D | JOB D | JOB C | JOB C | JOB E | JOB A | IDLE |
| **Machine 2** | IDLE | JOB B | JOB B | JOB D | JOB D | IDLE | JOB E | JOB A |

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Reading:** Machine 1 (top row) and Machine 2 (bottom row) show job occupation by hour. Idle blocks (white) indicate **M2 idle time** that could be reduced by alternative sequencing — illustrating why Johnson's optimal choice matters.

---

## 4.5 The Four-Stage PPC Pyramid (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Activity Planning] --> S2[Scheduling]
    S2 --> S3[Shop Loading]
    S3 --> S4[Sequencing]
    S4 --> S5[Dispatching]
    S5 --> S6[Expediting and Control]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Logic:** Each block is a downstream consumer of the upstream block's output. Sequencing cannot start until Loading is done, Loading cannot start until Scheduling is done, and so on — this is the **causal chain** emphasized in KTU module questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define sequencing. State the assumptions underlying Johnson's two-machine sequencing rule.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequencing** is the process of determining the order in which jobs should be processed on machines to optimize one or more performance measures such as makespan, flow time, or tardiness.

**Assumptions of Johnson's Rule (any two for full marks):**
