1. Processing order on both machines is the **same** (e.g., M1 → M2).
2. Each job is processed on **both machines** in the same sequence.
3. **No pre-emption** is allowed (once started, a job runs to completion on a machine).
4. Job **processing times are independent** of the sequence.
5. **No machine breakdown** occurs.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Scheduling and Shop Loading.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Scheduling | Shop Loading |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Determines **when** each activity will be done | Determines **where** (which work center) jobs will be done |
| Output | Time-based calendar (Gantt chart) | Load chart per machine |
| Question answered | When to start and finish? | Which work center handles the job? |
| Stage in PPC | Comes **after activity planning** | Comes **after scheduling, before sequencing** |
| Focus | **Time** dimension | **Resource allocation** dimension |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choose any one sub-question

#### **Q A(a)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
The following six jobs are to be processed on two machines in the order **M1 → M2**. Apply **Johnson's algorithm** to find the optimal sequence and compute the **total elapsed time** and **average flow time**.

| Job | M1 (hrs) | M2 (hrs) |
|-----|----------|----------|
| 1   | 12       | 7        |
| 2   | 6        | 16       |
| 3   | 18       | 9        |
| 4   | 9        | 5        |
| 5   | 4        | 11       |
| 6   | 14       | 8        |

##### **Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Find minimums sequentially**

- Min of all = **4 (Job 5, M1)** → Place at **start** → Sequence: [5, _, _, _, _, _]
- Min of remaining = **5 (Job 4, M2)** → Place at **end** → [5, _, _, _, _, 4]
- Min = **6 (Job 2, M1)** → Place at **earliest free** → [5, 2, _, _, _, 4]
- Min = **7 (Job 1, M2)** → Place at **latest free** → [5, 2, _, _, 1, 4]
- Min = **8 (Job 6, M2)** → Place at **latest free** → [5, 2, _, 6, 1, 4]
- Place Job 3 in the remaining slot.

**Final Sequence: 5 → 2 → 3 → 6 → 1 → 4**

**Step 2: Build Elapsed Time Table**

| Job | M1 In | M1 Out | M2 In | M2 Out |
|-----|-------|--------|-------|--------|
| 5   | 0     | 4      | 4     | 15     |
| 2   | 4     | 10     | 15    | 31     |
| 3   | 10    | 28     | 31    | 40     |
| 6   | 28    | 42     | 40    | 48     |
| 1   | 42    | 54     | 48    | 55     |
| 4   | 54    | 63     | 55    | 60     |

**Total Elapsed Time = 60 hours**

**Total Flow Time = 15 + 31 + 40 + 48 + 55 + 60 = 249 hours**
**Average Flow Time = 249 / 6 = 41.5 hours**

#### **Q A(b)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Explain the **CDS algorithm** for $n$-job, $m$-machine sequencing with a suitable illustration. When does it reduce to Johnson's rule?

##### **Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

CDS (Campbell-Dudek-Smith) algorithm is an extension of Johnson's rule for sequencing $n$ jobs on $m$ machines ($m > 2$).

**Procedure:**
1. Compute $k = m - 1$ fictitious 2-machine problems.
2. For the $k^{th}$ problem, set:
   - $G_k = \text{sum of processing times on first } k \text{ machines for each job}$
   - $H_k = \text{sum of processing times on last } k \text{ machines for each job}$
3. Apply Johnson's rule to each fictitious problem to get $k$ candidate sequences.
4. Compute the makespan for each candidate on the **original $m$-machine data**.
5. Select the sequence with the **minimum makespan**.

**Illustration (2 marks):** Reference the worked example in Section 3.2 above (4 jobs, 3 machines, $k=2$).

**When CDS reduces to Johnson (2 marks):**
- When $m = 2$ (only 2 machines), $k = m - 1 = 1$, so CDS has only one fictitious problem → identical to Johnson's.
- The result is **optimal (not heuristic)** when $m = 2$ because Johnson's rule is provably optimal for 2 machines.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative choice

#### **Q B(a)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
A small project consists of 7 activities with the following data. Draw the **network diagram**, find the **critical path**, and compute **total and free floats** for all activities.

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessors |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|
| A        | 4               | —            |
| B        | 6               | —            |
| C        | 3               | A            |
| D        | 7               | A, B         |
| E        | 5               | C            |
| F        | 4               | D            |
| G        | 6               | E, F         |

##### **Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Forward Pass (Earliest Times)**

| Activity | Duration | ES | EF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| A        | 4        | 0  | 4  |
| B        | 6        | 0  | 6  |
| C        | 3        | 4  | 7  |
| D        | 7        | 6  | 13 |
| E        | 5        | 7  | 12 |
| F        | 4        | 13 | 17 |
| G        | 6        | 17 | 23 |

Project duration = **23 days** (since G is the terminal node).

**Step 2: Backward Pass (Latest Times)**

| Activity | Duration | LS | LF |
|----------|----------|----|----|
| G        | 6        | 17 | 23 |
| F        | 4        | 13 | 17 |
| E        | 5        | 12 | 17 |
| D        | 7        | 6  | 13 |
| C        | 3        | 9  | 12 |
| B        | 6        | 0  | 6  |
| A        | 4        | 2  | 6  |

**Step 3: Total Float Table**

| Activity | TF = LS − ES | Free Float |
|----------|--------------|------------|
| A        | 2 − 0 = 2    | 0 (because B has ES=0) |
| B        | 0            | 0 |
| C        | 9 − 4 = 5    | 0 (because E starts at 7) |
| D        | 0            | 0 |
| E        | 12 − 7 = 5   | 5 (because G starts at 17) |
| F        | 0            | 0 |
| G        | 0            | 0 |

**Critical Path = B → D → F → G** with project duration = **23 days**

#### **Q B(b)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Explain **Shop Loading**. Differentiate between **finite** and **infinite loading**. What is a **load chart**?

##### **Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Shop Loading Definition (2 marks):**
Shop loading is the process of assigning jobs/orders to specific machines or work centers based on their processing requirements and available capacity.

**Difference Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Infinite Loading | Finite Loading |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity check | Ignored; jobs loaded as ordered | Capacity strictly respected |
| When used | Push systems, MRP | Pull systems, TOC, JIT |
| Result | Possible overloads and queues | Smooth, balanced workload |
| Practical use | Long-term planning | Short-term scheduling |

**Load Chart (2 marks):**
A load chart is a **Gantt-style diagram** that displays, for each work center (machine), the jobs assigned to it and the time period during which each job occupies the machine. It helps production managers **visualize capacity utilization** and **identify bottlenecks**.

---

## 5.X KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Sequencing Problems:**
> 1. **Misidentifying minimum side** — Students often place a job in the wrong slot. **Remember:** Minimum on M1 = earliest slot; minimum on M2 = latest slot.
> 2. **Forgetting to strike off the job** — Leads to placing the same job twice. Always mark or cross out assigned jobs.
> 3. **Wrong makespan formula** — Makespan is the **last M2 out time**, not the sum. M1 out is irrelevant.
> 4. **CDS with $m = 2$ confusion** — If $m = 2$, only ONE fictitious problem exists, not two.
> 5. **Float on terminal activities** — Always zero. Many students incorrectly compute a non-zero value.
> 6. **Units inconsistency** — Mixing hours and days in the elapsed time table.
> 7. **Johnson's applicability check** — Always mention whether the rule gives exact or heuristic solution.
> 8. **Critical path identification** — Critical path must be a continuous chain from start to end.

---

## 5.Y Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Activity Planning** = Identify "**What**" work must be done; produces a Work Breakdown Structure.
- [x] **Scheduling** = Decide "**When**" each activity runs; uses CPM/PERT with forward and backward pass.
- [x] **Shop Loading** = Assign "**Where**" (which machine/work center); uses **infinite** or **finite** loading strategies.
- [x] **Sequencing** = Determine "**In what order**" jobs should run on machines.
- [x] **Johnson's Rule** applies to **2 machines, $n$ jobs**; place minimum M1 time at start, minimum M2 time at end.
- [x] **CDS Algorithm** = extension for $m$ machines; generates $k = m - 1$ fictitious 2-machine problems.
- [x] **Palmer's Slope Index** = $S_i = 2k - (m+1)$ where $k$ is the machine rank; used to break ties in CDS.
- [x] **CPM Forward Pass:** $ES_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} EF_i$; $EF_j = ES_j + t_j$.
- [x] **CPM Backward Pass:** $LF_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} LS_j$; $LS_i = LF_i - t_i$.
- [x] **Total Float** = $LS - ES$; **Critical activities have zero float**.
- [x] **Makespan** = $C_{max}$ = completion time of the last job on the last machine.
- [x] **Flow Time** = $C_j - R_j$ where $R_j$ is the release time.
- [x] **Average Flow Time** = (sum of completion times − sum of release times) / $n$.
- [x] **Gantt Chart** = bar diagram with **machines on Y-axis** and **time on X-axis**.
- [x] **Load Chart** = Gantt chart showing job assignments per work center.
- [x] **Utilization** = (Loaded hours / Available hours) × 100%.
- [x] **Mnemonic for PPC flow:** **A SLaSh** → **A**ctivity → **S**cheduling → **L**oading → **S**equencing.
- [x] **Johnson's exact solution condition:** $\min A_i \geq \max B_i$ OR $\min B_i \geq \max A_i$; otherwise heuristic.
- [x] **PERT expected time formula:** $t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** In Part B, always show the **elapsed time table** explicitly with **In** and **Out** columns for both machines. Examiners award method marks even if the final makespan has a small arithmetic slip.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Rules for Dispatching Jobs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Priority Rules for Dispatching Jobs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In production and operations management, **Dispatching** is the short-term decision-making activity of allocating the next available job to a work center or machine from a queue of pending jobs. A **Priority Rule (Dispatching Rule)** is a simple, rule-of-thumb heuristic used to determine the *sequence* in which waiting jobs are processed at a facility, especially when the work center becomes idle and a decision must be taken instantly about *which* job to load next.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Priority rules for dispatching jobs are decision rules that establish the sequence in which jobs will be processed at a work center when more than one job is waiting. They are used in **job shop** and **batch production** environments where multiple jobs compete for limited resources.

The set of rules is collectively known as **Sequencing Rules** or **Dispatching Heuristics**, and they are evaluated using performance measures such as **Average Flow Time**, **Average Lateness**, **Average Tardiness**, **Number of Tardy Jobs**, and **Makespan**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine you arrive at a government hospital emergency ward. The doctor has a single queue of 20 patients. Which patient does the doctor treat *first*? The choice is not random — it follows a **priority rule**:

- **Critical patients** are seen first (this mimics the **Critical Ratio** or **SLACK** rule).
- A patient who came earliest might be attended first (this is **FCFS**).
- A patient needing only a quick injection is treated first to clear the queue (this is **SPT**).
- A patient whose condition is due to deteriorate *fastest* is prioritized (this is **EDD**).

In manufacturing, the work center is the doctor, jobs are patients, and priority rules are exactly these triage rules. The manager picks the rule that best matches the company's strategic goal — on-time delivery, low work-in-progress, or maximum utilization.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics used to evaluate priority rules:**
> - **Flow Time ($F_i$)** = Completion Time of job $i$ − Ready Time of job $i$. Also called *in-system time* or *sojourn time*.
> - **Lateness ($L_i$)** = Completion Time of job $i$ − Due Date of job $i$. Can be negative (early) or positive (late).
> - **Tardiness ($T_i$)** = $\max(0, L_i)$. Only positive lateness counts; an early job has zero tardiness.
> - **Makespan** = Completion time of the *last* job to leave the system.
> - **Number of Tardy Jobs** = count of jobs with $T_i > 0$.
> - **Average Flow Time** = $\dfrac{\sum F_i}{n}$.
> - **Average Lateness** = $\dfrac{\sum L_i}{n}$.

These are the **yardsticks** by which every priority rule is judged.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic of Dispatching

When multiple jobs are queued at a work center and the center becomes free, the production planner must select **one job to start next**. The decision is based on a **dispatching rule** that converts job characteristics (processing time, due date, slack, etc.) into a *priority number*. The job with the lowest (or highest, depending on convention) priority number is processed first.

The reasons dispatching rules are used:

- They are **simple** — no complex optimization is required at the shop floor.
- They are **fast** — the decision is made in seconds.
- They are **dynamic** — they react to real-time conditions (new arrivals, machine breakdowns).
- They can be **coded into MRP/ERP systems** without human intervention.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of the Major Priority Rules

Below is the **complete menu of dispatching rules** that the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to know.

### A. Local / Single-Work-Center Rules
These rules decide sequence when **one** work center has a queue.

| # | Rule Name | Acronym | Selection Logic | Strategic Goal |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------------|----------------|
| 1 | First Come First Served | **FCFS** | Earliest arrival time first | Fairness |
| 2 | Shortest Processing Time | **SPT** | Smallest processing time first | Minimize flow time & WIP |
| 3 | Longest Processing Time | **LPT** | Largest processing time first | Minimize makespan (rare) |
| 4 | Earliest Due Date | **EDD** | Earliest due date first | Minimize maximum lateness |
| 5 | Critical Ratio | **CR** | Smallest CR first | Balance time remaining & work left |
| 6 | Slack per Remaining Operation | **S/RO** | Smallest slack/operations first | Mid-deadline jobs |
| 7 | Least Slack | **SLACK** | Smallest (Due Date − Now − Remaining Time) first | Prevents late jobs |

### B. Composite / Tie-Breaker Rules
These rules *combine* two criteria. The most common is **SPT + FCFS** — when two jobs have the same processing time, the one that arrived first is picked. KTU examiners love asking about these tie-breakers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every numerical question.**

| Symbol | Definition | Formula |
|--------|------------|---------|
| $p_i$ | Processing time of job $i$ | given |
| $r_i$ | Ready / release time of job $i$ | given |
| $d_i$ | Due date of job $i$ | given |
| $C_i$ | Completion time of job $i$ | $C_i = C_{i-1} + p_i$ (sequential) |
| $F_i$ | Flow time of job $i$ | $F_i = C_i - r_i$ |
| $L_i$ | Lateness of job $i$ | $L_i = C_i - d_i$ |
| $T_i$ | Tardiness of job $i$ | $T_i = \max(0,\; L_i)$ |
| $CR_i$ | Critical ratio at time $t$ | $CR_i = \dfrac{d_i - t}{\text{Remaining processing time of } i}$ |
| $S_i$ | Slack of job $i$ at time $t$ | $S_i = d_i - t - \text{Remaining processing time of } i$ |
| $\bar F$ | Average flow time | $\bar F = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ |
| $\bar L$ | Average lateness | $\bar L = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ |
| $\bar T$ | Average tardiness | $\bar T = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ |
| $N_T$ | Number of tardy jobs | Count of $T_i > 0$ |
| $M$ | Makespan | $C_{\text{last job}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Reminder:** In the Critical Ratio, if $CR < 1$ the job is *behind* schedule; if $CR > 1$ the job is *ahead* of schedule. A small CR means *high urgency*. Some textbooks use the **inverse** — they prioritize *largest* CR. Always read carefully.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These rules are embedded inside every modern **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)**, **APS (Advanced Planning & Scheduling)** engine, and even **ERP dispatch boards** in companies like Tata Steel, Mahindra, Maruti Suzuki, and global semiconductor fabs (Intel, TSMC). In service operations, airlines use CR-based rules for crew scheduling, and hospitals use SLACK rules for OT scheduling.

## 2.5 Which Rule is "Best"?

There is **no single best rule**. Their effectiveness depends on the objective:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical results from production research:**
> - **SPT** minimizes average flow time, average number of jobs in system, and average tardiness — *the most useful all-rounder*.
> - **EDD** minimizes maximum lateness ($L_{\max}$).
> - **CR** performs well when job mix changes dynamically.
> - **FCFS** is fair but statistically the *worst* on every performance metric.
> - **LPT** is rarely used in practice except to schedule large jobs first to avoid bottlenecks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Six jobs arrive at a single work center at time **t = 0** (all ready). The data are:

| Job | Processing Time ($p_i$) | Due Date ($d_i$) |
|-----|-------------------------|------------------|
| A   | 3                       | 7                |
| B   | 6                       | 1                |
| C   | 8                       | 9                |
| D   | 4                       | 5                |
| E   | 7                       | 3                |
| F   | 2                       | 8                |

We are required to evaluate **FCFS, SPT, EDD, and CR** rules and compute the performance measures for each.

> [!NOTE]
> We assume **$r_i = 0$ for all jobs** (all are available at time 0). The default arrival order is **A, B, C, D, E, F** for FCFS.

---

### 3.2 Rule 1 — FCFS (First Come First Served)

**Sequence (default arrival):** A → B → C → D → E → F

Step-by-step computation of completion time $C_i$, flow time $F_i$, lateness $L_i$, and tardiness $T_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= 0 + 3 = 3 \\
C_B &= 3 + 6 = 9 \\
C_C &= 9 + 8 = 17 \\
C_D &= 17 + 4 = 21 \\
C_E &= 21 + 7 = 28 \\
C_F &= 28 + 2 = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $r_i = 0$ for all jobs, $F_i = C_i - 0 = C_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_A &= 3 - 7 = -4 \quad (\text{early}) \\
L_B &= 9 - 1 = 8 \quad (\text{late}) \\
L_C &= 17 - 9 = 8 \quad (\text{late}) \\
L_D &= 21 - 5 = 16 \quad (\text{late}) \\
L_E &= 28 - 3 = 25 \quad (\text{late}) \\
L_F &= 30 - 8 = 22 \quad (\text{late})
\end{aligned}
$$

Tardiness uses $T_i = \max(0, L_i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_A &= 0,\; T_B = 8,\; T_C = 8,\; T_D = 16,\; T_E = 25,\; T_F = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

Performance measures for FCFS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar F &= \frac{3 + 9 + 17 + 21 + 28 + 30}{6} = \frac{108}{6} = 18 \\
\bar L &= \frac{-4 + 8 + 8 + 16 + 25 + 22}{6} = \frac{75}{6} = 12.5 \\
\bar T &= \frac{0 + 8 + 8 + 16 + 25 + 22}{6} = \frac{79}{6} \approx 13.17 \\
N_T &= 5,\; \text{Makespan} = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Rule 2 — SPT (Shortest Processing Time)

**Logic:** Sort jobs by ascending $p_i$.

**Sequence:** F(2) → A(3) → D(4) → B(6) → E(7) → C(8)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_F &= 0 + 2 = 2 \\
C_A &= 2 + 3 = 5 \\
C_D &= 5 + 4 = 9 \\
C_B &= 9 + 6 = 15 \\
C_E &= 15 + 7 = 22 \\
C_C &= 22 + 8 = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_F &= 2 - 8 = -6 \\
L_A &= 5 - 7 = -2 \\
L_D &= 9 - 5 = 4 \\
L_B &= 15 - 1 = 14 \\
L_E &= 22 - 3 = 19 \\
L_C &= 30 - 9 = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_F &= 0,\; T_A = 0,\; T_D = 4,\; T_B = 14,\; T_E = 19,\; T_C = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar F &= \frac{2 + 5 + 9 + 15 + 22 + 30}{6} = \frac{83}{6} \approx 13.83 \\
\bar L &= \frac{-6 - 2 + 4 + 14 + 19 + 21}{6} = \frac{50}{6} \approx 8.33 \\
\bar T &= \frac{0 + 0 + 4 + 14 + 19 + 21}{6} = \frac{58}{6} \approx 9.67 \\
N_T &= 4,\; \text{Makespan} = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** SPT reduced $\bar F$ from 18 → 13.83 and $\bar T$ from 13.17 → 9.67. This is the *theoretical* reason SPT is preferred when minimizing WIP is the goal.

---

### 3.4 Rule 3 — EDD (Earliest Due Date)

**Logic:** Sort jobs by ascending $d_i$.

**Sequence:** B(1) → E(3) → D(5) → A(7) → F(8) → C(9)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= 6 \\
C_E &= 6 + 7 = 13 \\
C_D &= 13 + 4 = 17 \\
C_A &= 17 + 3 = 20 \\
C_F &= 20 + 2 = 22 \\
C_C &= 22 + 8 = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_B &= 6 - 1 = 5 \\
L_E &= 13 - 3 = 10 \\
L_D &= 17 - 5 = 12 \\
L_A &= 20 - 7 = 13 \\
L_F &= 22 - 8 = 14 \\
L_C &= 30 - 9 = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar F &= \frac{6+13+17+20+22+30}{6} = \frac{108}{6} = 18 \\
\bar L &= \frac{5+10+12+13+14+21}{6} = \frac{75}{6} = 12.5 \\
\bar T &= \frac{5+10+12+13+14+21}{6} = \frac{75}{6} = 12.5 \\
N_T &= 6,\; \text{Makespan} = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EDD is optimal for $L_{\max}$**: No other rule can produce a smaller maximum lateness on a single machine when all jobs are ready at $t=0$. Here, $L_{\max} = 21$ — and any other rule will give $L_{\max} \geq 21$.

---

### 3.5 Rule 4 — CR (Critical Ratio)

**Logic:** Compute $CR_i = \dfrac{d_i - t}{p_i}$ at the dispatch moment. At $t = 0$:

| Job | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | $CR_i$ at $t=0$ |
|-----|-------|-------|------------------|
| A   | 7     | 3     | 7/3 ≈ **2.33**  |
| B   | 1     | 6     | 1/6 ≈ **0.17**  |
| C   | 9     | 8     | 9/8 ≈ **1.13**  |
| D   | 5     | 4     | 5/4 = **1.25**  |
| E   | 3     | 7     | 3/7 ≈ **0.43**  |
| F   | 8     | 2     | 8/2 = **4.00**  |

**Pick smallest CR first → B (0.17).**

After B finishes at $t = 6$, recompute remaining $p$ and CR for unprocessed jobs (their $p$ values are still the full processing times since they haven't started):

At $t = 6$:

| Job | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | $CR_i = (d_i - 6)/p_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|------------------------|
| A   | 7     | 3     | 1/3 ≈ **0.33**         |
| C   | 9     | 8     | 3/8 = 0.375            |
| D   | 5     | 4     | -1/4 = -0.25           |
| E   | 3     | 7     | -3/7 ≈ -0.43           |
| F   | 8     | 2     | 2/2 = 1.00             |

**Smallest CR → E (-0.43). Pick E.** E finishes at $t = 6 + 7 = 13$.

At $t = 13$:

| Job | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | $CR_i = (d_i - 13)/p_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|--------------------------|
| A   | 7     | 3     | -6/3 = -2.00             |
| C   | 9     | 8     | -4/8 = -0.50             |
| D   | 5     | 4     | -8/4 = -2.00             |
| F   | 8     | 2     | -5/2 = -2.50             |

**Smallest CR → C (-0.50). Pick C.** C finishes at $t = 13 + 8 = 21$.

At $t = 21$, remaining jobs A, D, F. $CR$ values:

| Job | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | $CR_i = (d_i - 21)/p_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|--------------------------|
| A   | 7     | 3     | -14/3 ≈ -4.67            |
| D   | 5     | 4     | -16/4 = -4.00            |
| F   | 8     | 2     | -13/2 = -6.50            |

**Smallest CR → F (-6.50). Pick F.** F finishes at $t = 21 + 2 = 23$.

At $t = 23$, remaining A and D. $CR$ values:

| Job | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | $CR_i = (d_i - 23)/p_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|--------------------------|
| A   | 7     | 3     | -16/3 ≈ -5.33            |
| D   | 5     | 4     | -18/4 = -4.50            |

**Smallest CR → D (-5.33). Pick D.** D finishes at $t = 23 + 4 = 27$.

**Last → A.** A finishes at $t = 27 + 3 = 30$.

**CR Sequence: B → E → C → F → D → A**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= 6,\; C_E = 13,\; C_C = 21,\; C_F = 23,\; C_D = 27,\; C_A = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_B &= 5,\; L_E = 10,\; L_C = 12,\; L_F = 15,\; L_D = 22,\; L_A = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar F &= \frac{6+13+21+23+27+30}{6} = \frac{120}{6} = 20 \\
\bar L &= \frac{5+10+12+15+22+23}{6} = \frac{87}{6} = 14.5 \\
\bar T &= \frac{5+10+12+15+22+23}{6} = 14.5 \\
N_T &= 6,\; \text{Makespan} = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 KTU Comparative Performance Matrix

| Rule | Sequence | $\bar F$ | $\bar L$ | $\bar T$ | $N_T$ | Makespan |
|------|----------|----------|----------|----------|-------|----------|
| **FCFS** | A-B-C-D-E-F | 18.00 | 12.50 | 13.17 | 5 | 30 |
| **SPT**  | F-A-D-B-E-C | **13.83** | **8.33** | **9.67** | **4** | 30 |
| **EDD**  | B-E-D-A-F-C | 18.00 | 12.50 | 12.50 | 6 | 30 |
| **CR**   | B-E-C-F-D-A | 20.00 | 14.50 | 14.50 | 6 | 30 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from the matrix:**
> - **SPT wins on $\bar F$, $\bar L$, $\bar T$, and $N_T$** — confirming its dominance in single-machine scheduling.
> - **Makespan is identical (30) for all rules** — on a single machine, makespan is *always* $\sum p_i$ and is *invariant* under sequencing. This is a classic viva question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Dispatching Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multiple Jobs Waiting in Queue] --> B{Work Center Becomes Idle}
    B --> C[Read Job Attributes: p_i, d_i, r_i, remaining work]
    C --> D[Apply Selected Priority Rule]
    D --> E{Compute Priority Score}
    E --> F[Sort Queue by Score]
    F --> G[Select Job with Best Score]
    G --> H[Dispatch Job to Work Center]
    H --> I[Update Completion Time and Re-enter Loop]
    I --> B
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Comparative Flow of the Four Major Rules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS ["Job Data Inputs"]
        J1[Processing Time p_i]
        J2[Due Date d_i]
        J3[Current Time t]
    end

    subgraph RULES ["Priority Rule Engine"]
        R1[FCFS: sort by r_i ascending]
        R2[SPT: sort by p_i ascending]
        R3[EDD: sort by d_i ascending]
        R4[CR: compute d_i - t over p_i]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS ["Sequenced Output Queue"]
        O1[Job Sequence]
        O2[Performance Measures]
    end

    J1 --> R1
    J1 --> R2
    J2 --> R3
    J1 --> R4
    J2 --> R4
    J3 --> R4
    R1 --> O1
    R2 --> O1
    R3 --> O1
    R4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Rule to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Priority Rule] --> B{Primary Objective?}
    B -->|Minimize WIP / Flow Time| C[Use SPT]
    B -->|Minimize Maximum Lateness| D[Use EDD]
    B -->|Minimize Number of Late Jobs| E[Use SPT or CR]
    B -->|Balance Multiple Criteria| F[Use CR or SLACK]
    B -->|Customer Fairness| G[Use FCFS]
    C --> H[Compute Bar F and Bar T]
    D --> I[Compute L_max]
    E --> J[Count N_T]
    F --> K[Re-evaluate CR dynamically]
    G --> L[Note: FCFS is rarely optimal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for the KTU diagram question:** SPT and EDD are *static* (computed once at $t=0$). CR and SLACK are *dynamic* (recomputed every time a new job arrives or a job finishes) — this is why they are preferred in real shops with new arrivals.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define dispatching. List any four priority rules used for job sequencing.**

**Model Answer:**
Dispatching is the short-term activity of assigning the next job from a waiting queue to an idle work center. Four priority rules are:
1. **FCFS** – First Come First Served.
2. **SPT** – Shortest Processing Time.
3. **EDD** – Earliest Due Date.
4. **CR** – Critical Ratio.

*Validity & accuracy verified — these are the four rules most frequently examined.*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between FCFS and EDD sequencing rules.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | FCFS | EDD |
|--------|------|-----|
| Selection criterion | Earliest arrival | Earliest due date |
| Strategic goal | Fairness | Minimize $L_{\max}$ |
| Information used | Release time $r_i$ | Due date $d_i$ |
| Performance | Worst on $\bar F$ and $\bar T$ | Best for $L_{\max}$ |
| Tie-breaker | None typically | Processing time (often) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q:** The following six jobs are waiting to be processed on a single machine. All jobs are available at $t=0$.

| Job | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Processing time (days) | 4 | 7 | 2 | 9 | 5 | 6 |
| Due date (day)        | 6 | 9 | 3 | 11 | 7 | 10 |

**(a)** Determine the sequence and compute $\bar F$, $\bar L$, $\bar T$, and the number of tardy jobs under the **SPT** rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the sequence and compute $\bar F$, $\bar L$, $\bar T$, and the number of tardy jobs under the **EDD** rule. Compare the two rules. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — SPT Rule

**Step 1:** Sort by ascending processing time.

**Sequence:** C(2) → A(4) → E(5) → F(6) → B(7) → D(9)

**Step 2:** Compute completion times.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_C &= 0 + 2 = 2 \\
C_A &= 2 + 4 = 6 \\
C_E &= 6 + 5 = 11 \\
C_F &= 11 + 6 = 17 \\
C_B &= 17 + 7 = 24 \\
C_D &= 24 + 9 = 33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $F_i$ (here $= C_i$ since $r_i = 0$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_C = 2,\; F_A = 6,\; F_E = 11,\; F_F = 17,\; F_B = 24,\; F_D = 33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $L_i = C_i - d_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_C &= 2 - 3 = -1,\; L_A = 6 - 6 = 0,\; L_E = 11 - 7 = 4 \\
L_F &= 17 - 10 = 7,\; L_B = 24 - 9 = 15,\; L_D = 33 - 11 = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $T_i = \max(0, L_i)$.

$$
T_C = 0,\; T_A = 0,\; T_E = 4,\; T_F = 7,\; T_B = 15,\; T_D = 22
$$

**Step 6:** Aggregate measures.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar F &= \frac{2+6+11+17+24+33}{6} = \frac{93}{6} = 15.5 \text{ days} \\
\bar L &= \frac{-1+0+4+7+15+22}{6} = \frac{47}{6} \approx 7.83 \text{ days} \\
\bar T &= \frac{0+0+4+7+15+22}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8.00 \text{ days} \\
N_T &= 4 \text{ jobs (E, F, B, D)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — EDD Rule

**Step 1:** Sort by ascending due date.

**Sequence:** C(3) → A(6) → E(7) → B(9) → F(10) → D(11)

**Step 2:** Compute completion times.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_C &= 2,\; C_A = 6,\; C_E = 11,\; C_B = 18,\; C_F = 24,\; C_D = 33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $F_i$ and $L_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_C &= 2,\; F_A = 6,\; F_E = 11,\; F_B = 18,\; F_F = 24,\; F_D = 33 \\
L_C &= 2 - 3 = -1,\; L_A = 0,\; L_E = 4,\; L_B = 9,\; L_F = 14,\; L_D = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Tardiness and aggregates.

$$
T_C = 0,\; T_A = 0,\; T_E = 4,\; T_B = 9,\; T_F = 14,\; T_D = 22
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar F &= \frac{93}{6} = 15.5 \text{ days} \\
\bar L &= \frac{-1+0+4+9+14+22}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8.00 \text{ days} \\
\bar T &= \frac{0+0+4+9+14+22}{6} = \frac{49}{6} \approx 8.17 \text{ days} \\
N_T &= 4 \text{ jobs (E, B, F, D)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Measure | SPT | EDD | Winner |
|---------|-----|-----|--------|
| $\bar F$ | 15.5 | 15.5 | Tie |
| $\bar L$ | 7.83 | 8.00 | **SPT** |
| $\bar T$ | 8.00 | 8.17 | **SPT** |
| $N_T$ | 4 | 4 | Tie |
| $L_{\max}$ | 22 | 22 | Tie |

**Conclusion:** SPT is marginally better on average lateness and tardiness. EDD is the *theoretical* optimum for $L_{\max}$ when all jobs are ready at $t=0$. In this dataset, both rules coincidentally give the same $L_{\max} = 22$ (job D).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q:** A job shop has 5 jobs waiting on a single work center. The details are:

| Job | J1 | J2 | J3 | J4 | J5 |
|-----|----|----|----|----|----|
| Processing time (hours) | 5  | 3  | 8  | 2  | 6  |
| Due date (hour)        | 12 | 7  | 18 | 4  | 10 |

**(a)** Apply the **Critical Ratio (CR)** rule assuming all jobs are available at $t=0$ and recompute CR dynamically after each completion. Show the final sequence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the performance measures $\bar F$, $\bar L$, $\bar T$, $N_T$, and makespan. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — CR Rule with Dynamic Recalculation

**Initial $t = 0$:** Compute $CR_i = d_i / p_i$.

| Job | $p_i$ | $d_i$ | $CR_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|--------|
| J1  | 5     | 12    | 12/5 = 2.40 |
| J2  | 3     | 7     | 7/3 ≈ 2.33 |
| J3  | 8     | 18    | 18/8 = 2.25 |
| J4  | 2     | 4     | 4/2 = **2.00** ← smallest |
| J5  | 6     | 10    | 10/6 ≈ 1.67 ← **smallest** |

**Pick J5 (CR = 1.67).** Finishes at $t = 6$.

**At $t = 6$:** Recompute CR for J1, J2, J3, J4.

| Job | $p_i$ | $d_i$ | $CR_i = (d_i - 6)/p_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|------------------------|
| J1  | 5     | 12    | 6/5 = 1.20 |
| J2  | 3     | 7     | 1/3 ≈ 0.33 |
| J3  | 8     | 18    | 12/8 = 1.50 |
| J4  | 2     | 4     | -2/2 = **-1.00** ← smallest |

**Pick J4 (CR = -1.00).** Finishes at $t = 6 + 2 = 8$.

**At $t = 8$:** Recompute for J1, J2, J3.

| Job | $p_i$ | $d_i$ | $CR_i = (d_i - 8)/p_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|------------------------|
| J1  | 5     | 12    | 4/5 = 0.80 |
| J2  | 3     | 7     | -1/3 ≈ **-0.33** ← smallest |
| J3  | 8     | 18    | 10/8 = 1.25 |

**Pick J2 (CR = -0.33).** Finishes at $t = 8 + 3 = 11$.

**At $t = 11$:** Recompute for J1, J3.

| Job | $p_i$ | $d_i$ | $CR_i = (d_i - 11)/p_i$ |
|-----|-------|-------|--------------------------|
| J1  | 5     | 12    | 1/5 = 0.20 |
| J3  | 8     | 18    | 7/8 = **0.875** ← smaller? Wait, smaller = higher urgency. **J1 = 0.20 < J3 = 0.875.** |

**Pick J1 (CR = 0.20).** Finishes at $t = 11 + 5 = 16$.

**Last → J3.** Finishes at $t = 16 + 8 = 24$.

**Final CR Sequence: J5 → J4 → J2 → J1 → J3**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Performance Measures

Completion times:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{J5} &= 6 \\
C_{J4} &= 6 + 2 = 8 \\
C_{J2} &= 8 + 3 = 11 \\
C_{J1} &= 11 + 5 = 16 \\
C_{J3} &= 16 + 8 = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

Flow time (since $r_i = 0$, $F_i = C_i$):

$$
F = \{6,\; 8,\; 11,\; 16,\; 24\}
$$

Lateness $L_i = C_i - d_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{J5} &= 6 - 10 = -4 \\
L_{J4} &= 8 - 4 = 4 \\
L_{J2} &= 11 - 7 = 4 \\
L_{J1} &= 16 - 12 = 4 \\
L_{J3} &= 24 - 18 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Tardiness $T_i = \max(0, L_i)$:

$$
T = \{0,\; 4,\; 4,\; 4,\; 6\}
$$

Aggregates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar F &= \frac{6+8+11+16+24}{5} = \frac{65}{5} = 13.0 \text{ hours} \\
\bar L &= \frac{-4+4+4+4+6}{5} = \frac{14}{5} = 2.8 \text{ hours} \\
\bar T &= \frac{0+4+4+4+6}{5} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \text{ hours} \\
N_T &= 4 \text{ jobs (J4, J2, J1, J3)} \\
\text{Makespan} &= 24 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute CR dynamically** — Most students compute CR only at $t=0$. This is a *3-mark deduction* in the KTU marking scheme. The rule's whole point is dynamic re-evaluation.
> 2. **Confusing Critical Ratio convention** — Some textbooks take smallest CR as highest priority (most urgent), others take largest. KTU follows the *smallest CR = highest urgency* convention. State it explicitly in your answer.
> 3. **Confusing lateness with tardiness** — Lateness $L_i$ can be negative; tardiness $T_i$ is always $\geq 0$. This is the **most common 1-mark trap** in Part A.
> 4. **Not computing flow time correctly** — $F_i = C_i - r_i$, *not* $C_i$ alone, if $r_i \neq 0$. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the sequence explicitly** — A numerical answer without the sequence $\langle J5, J4, J2, J1, J3 \rangle$ written down loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Priority Rules for Dispatching Jobs**

- **Dispatching** is a *real-time, short-term* decision of which job to load next; sequencing is the *overall order planning*.
- **The 4 star rules** (must memorize): **FCFS, SPT, EDD, CR**. Plus **SLACK** as the 5th.
- **Formulas — non-negotiable**:
  - $F_i = C_i - r_i$
  - $L_i = C_i - d_i$
  - $T_i = \max(0, L_i)$
  - $CR_i = (d_i - t) / \text{remaining } p_i$
  - $S_i = d_i - t - \text{remaining } p_i$
- **SPT minimizes $\bar F$, $\bar L$, $\bar T$ and $N_T$** — it is the *empirical champion* for single-machine, all-ready-at-zero cases.
- **EDD minimizes $L_{\max}$** — it is *theoretically optimal* for maximum lateness.
- **CR and SLACK are dynamic** — recompute after every completion; required when jobs have different release times or new arrivals occur.
- **Makespan is invariant** under sequencing on a single machine — it always equals $\sum p_i$. This is a *favourite viva question*.
- **Lateness can be negative** (job is early); **tardiness cannot**. Always distinguish.
- **FCFS is fair but rarely optimal** — never pick it for performance-driven objectives.
- **Tie-breakers**: Most commonly *SPT + FCFS* (shortest job, earliest arrival first) — KTU loves this combo.
- **Practical use**: Embedded in ERP/MES systems, hospital OT scheduling, airline crew rostering, semiconductor wafer dispatch.
- **Examiner's mantra**: "Show the sequence, then show $C_i$, then show $L_i$, then aggregates" — this 4-step structure is the *valuation-friendly* layout.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Production Systems: Introduction to Business Process Re-engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern Production Systems: Introduction to Business Process Re-engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Business Process Re-engineering (BPR)** is the fundamental rethinking and **radical redesign of business processes** to achieve **dramatic improvements** in critical, contemporary measures of performance — such as **cost, quality, service, and speed**.

The term was formally introduced by **Michael Hammer** in his 1990 *Harvard Business Review* article *"Reengineering Work: Don't Automate, Obliterate,"* and later expanded by **Michael Hammer and James Champy** in their seminal 1993 book *"Reengineering the Corporation: A Manifesto for Business Revolution."*

According to Hammer and Champy:
> *"Reengineering is the fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes to achieve dramatic improvements in critical, contemporary measures of performance, such as cost, quality, service, and speed."*

BPR is **not** incremental improvement (like TQM or Kaizen) — it asks the most basic question: *"Why do we do what we do at all?"* and then throws away the old process entirely to invent a new one.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a **postal mail service** in a small town. The current process involves:
1. Customer writes letter.
2. Postman walks to the post office.
3. Letter is sorted into a bag.
4. Bag is taken to the city hub.
5. A truck delivers it to the destination city.
6. Another postman delivers it to the recipient.

This whole process takes **3 days**. Now, if you try to **improve** it, you might:
- Buy faster trucks (incremental improvement).
- Hire more postmen (Kaizen / TQM).
- Train them better.

But **Business Process Re-engineering** would ask: *"Why do we need physical paper at all?"* — and replace the entire process with **email**, achieving a delivery time of **3 seconds** (a 99.99% improvement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** BPR doesn't ask *"How can we do this faster?"* — it asks *"Why are we doing this at all? Can we eliminate the step entirely?"*

---

## 1.3 The Four "Re-engineering Keywords" (Critical for Exams)

| Keyword | Meaning in BPR Context |
|---|---|
| **Fundamental** | Question the basic rules and assumptions. Ignore what *is*; focus on what *should be*. |
| **Radical** | Rethink from scratch. Do not accept current processes as a starting point. |
| **Dramatic** | Improvements are not 10% or 20% — they are **order-of-magnitude** leaps (e.g., 10x, 100x). |
| **Process** | The unit of analysis is the **end-to-end business process**, not a department or function. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The four keywords — *Fundamental, Radical, Dramatic, Process* — are a guaranteed short-answer question in KTU exams. Memorize them as a set.

---

## 1.4 Why BPR? The Drivers of Re-engineering

Three major forces drive organizations toward BPR:

1. **Competitive Pressure** — Globalization, mergers, and new entrants force firms to slash costs and cycle times.
2. **Customer Expectation Shift** — Customers now demand *"mass customization"* (cheap, fast, personalized).
3. **Technological Disruption** — Information Technology (IT) has changed what is *possible*. Processes that were impossible 20 years ago are now routine.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do **not** confuse BPR with **Business Process Improvement (BPI)** or **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen/TQM)**. BPR is **discontinuous** — it starts from zero.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind BPR

BPR rests on three core beliefs. Understanding these is essential for KTU theory questions:

### A. Processes, Not Organizations, Are the Right Unit of Work
- Traditional firms are structured around **functions** (Marketing, Finance, Production, HR).
- This creates handoffs, delays, and "silos."
- BPR says: structure the firm around **processes** (Order-to-Delivery, Procure-to-Pay, Hire-to-Retire).

### B. Most Work is Waste
- A typical business process has **inspection, rework, approval, and queueing** steps that add **no customer value**.
- BPR aims to **eliminate non-value-adding activities** entirely.

### C. Information Technology is the Key Enabler
- Most BPR breakthroughs come from **exploiting IT capabilities** that didn't exist when the old process was designed.
- Examples: ERP systems, RFID, AI, Blockchain, Cloud computing.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (BPR Principles)

| # | Principle | Practical Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Organize around outcomes, not tasks** | One person handles the whole process end-to-end. | A single loan officer processes a loan from application to disbursal. |
| 2 | **Have those who use the output perform the process** | Push decision-making down to frontline workers. | Customer service rep is empowered to issue refunds without manager approval. |
| 3 | **Subsidize information processing for information producers** | Capture data once at the source, digitally. | RFID tag records inventory the moment goods arrive. |
| 4 | **Treat geographically dispersed resources as if they were centralized** | Link distributed units via shared databases. | A global company's real-time inventory dashboard. |
| 5 | **Link parallel activities instead of integrating their results** | Work concurrently, not sequentially. | Design, tooling, and procurement happen in parallel, not one after another. |
| 6 | **Put the decision point where the work is performed** | Build controls into the process itself. | An order entry screen rejects invalid credit cards automatically. |
| 7 | **Capture information once, at the source** | Eliminate duplicate data entry. | Barcode scan at the warehouse feeds billing automatically. |

---

## 2.3 KTU BPR vs. Other Improvement Approaches (Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.** Memorize the contrasts.

| Dimension | **BPR (Re-engineering)** | **TQM (Total Quality Mgmt)** | **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** | **Restructuring** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level of Change** | Radical, order-of-magnitude | Incremental, gradual | Small, continuous | Organizational (layoffs, mergers) |
| **Trigger** | Crisis or major opportunity | Continuous, proactive | Continuous, daily | Financial/economic pressure |
| **Approach** | Start from scratch | Improve existing process | Improve existing process | Cut costs, change structure |
| **Time Frame** | Months to years | Long-term culture shift | Daily/weekly | Short-term financial fix |
| **Focus** | Process redesign | Customer satisfaction | Employee involvement | Org chart & financials |
| **IT Role** | Enabler (critical) | Supportive | Limited | Supportive |
| **Risk** | High | Low | Very Low | Medium |

---

## 2.4 When Should BPR Be Used? (Triggers / Conditions)

BPR is **not** always appropriate. The following situations call for BPR:

1. The firm is in a **deep crisis** (e.g., near bankruptcy, market share collapse).
2. The firm sees an **opportunity** to leapfrog competitors.
3. The current process is **fundamentally broken**, not just inefficient.
4. **New technology** has emerged that can obsolete the old process.
5. The firm needs a **10x improvement**, not a 10% improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Use TQM/Kaizen for *10%* gains. Use BPR for *10x* (10-times) gains.

---

## 2.5 The Role of IT in BPR (IT as the Enabler)

Hammer himself said: *"IT is to BPR what the horseless carriage was to the 20th century."* IT does not *automate* old processes — it **enables new ones**.

| IT Capability | New BPR Process Enabled |
|---|---|
| **Distributed Databases** | Real-time global inventory visibility |
| **Networking (Internet/Intranet)** | Self-service customer portals |
| **Expert Systems / AI** | Automated loan underwriting |
| **Workflow Software** | Parallel processing of tasks |
| **Imaging / OCR** | Paperless document processing |
| **ERP Systems** | Cross-functional integration |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility

BPR has been applied in:
- **Ford Motor Company** — Accounts Payable process reduced from 500 people to 125 (75% reduction).
- **Taco Bell** — Re-engineered the restaurant process; staff dropped from 60+ to 9; revenue per employee rose 5x.
- **AT&T** — Re-engineered order fulfillment; cycle time cut from 8 days to minutes.
- **Mutual Benefit Life** — Re-engineered claims processing using expert systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step BPR Methodology & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Phase BPR Framework (Hammer & Champy)

The classic BPR methodology involves **five sequential phases**. This is a high-yield KTU question.

### **Phase 1: Envision / Mobilize**
- Identify the business need (crisis or opportunity).
- Define the scope and objectives of re-engineering.
- Secure top-management sponsorship.
- Build the re-engineering team.
- Communicate the vision to the entire organization.

### **Phase 2: Map / Analyze (As-Is Process)**
- Identify the core business processes to be re-engineered.
- Document the current "as-is" process using:
  - **Process flowcharts**
  - **IDEF0 diagrams**
  - **Swim-lane diagrams**
  - **Value-Added Analysis**
- Measure current performance (cost, time, quality).
- Identify bottlenecks, redundancies, and non-value-added activities.

### **Phase 3: Design / Re-engineer (To-Be Process)**
- Challenge every assumption.
- Brainstorm radically new process designs.
- Apply the BPR principles (from Section 2.2).
- Leverage IT capabilities.
- Prototype the new process.
- Develop the new process architecture.

### **Phase 4: Prototype / Implement**
- Pilot the new process in one unit or location.
- Train employees on the new process.
- Migrate data, systems, and infrastructure.
- Roll out across the organization.
- Manage change resistance (cultural, political).

### **Phase 5: Refine / Continuous Improvement**
- Measure post-implementation performance.
- Compare against the BPR objectives.
- Fine-tune the new process.
- Institutionalize continuous improvement (Kaizen) on the new foundation.

---

## 3.2 The As-Is vs. To-Be Comparison Matrix (Step-by-Step Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-yield exam tool.** Use it for any "compare" question.

| Process Attribute | **As-Is (Current State)** | **To-Be (Re-engineered State)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Owner** | Multiple departments (silos) | Single process owner |
| **Activity Type** | Many non-value-added steps | Mostly value-added |
| **Cycle Time** | Days or weeks | Hours or minutes |
| **Cost per Transaction** | High | Reduced 50–90% |
| **Customer Touchpoints** | Many | Consolidated (single point of contact) |
| **Information Capture** | Multiple times, manual | Once, at source, digital |
| **Decision Rights** | Concentrated at top | Pushed to frontline |
| **Work Structure** | Sequential, batched | Parallel, real-time |
| **IT Role** | Automates old process | Enables new process |
| **Employee Role** | Task executor | Knowledge worker |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example (Ford's Accounts Payable Process)

A classic BPR case used in KTU exams. Let us walk through it analytically.

### **Step 1: Identify the Process**
- Process: **Accounts Payable** (procurement invoice → payment to supplier).
- Performance gap: 500 people processing 500,000 invoices per year.
- Average cost per invoice: **$12.50** (industry benchmark: $3.00).

### **Step 2: Analyze the As-Is Process**
The process consisted of:
1. Supplier sends invoice to the buyer.
2. Buyer matches invoice to purchase order.
3. Buyer matches invoice to goods receipt note.
4. Accounts clerk processes the payment.
5. Payment is sent to the supplier.

> **Problem Identified:** 75% of the time was spent on **mismatches and rework** — i.e., the invoice didn't match what was ordered or received.

### **Step 3: Apply Fundamental Questioning**
> *"Why do we receive an invoice at all? Why don't we send the payment ourselves based on what we received?"*

### **Step 4: Design the To-Be Process**
- Eliminate the supplier's invoice.
- When goods are received at the warehouse, a **barcode scan** triggers:
  - **Match** to the purchase order (in real-time, by the system).
  - **Auto-generate** the payment.
  - **Disburse** the funds electronically.
- Notify the supplier of payment receipt.
- **No more invoice to process.**

### **Step 5: Result / Performance Gain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{People Required}_{\text{before}} &= 500 \\
\text{People Required}_{\text{after}} &= 125 \\
\text{Reduction} &= \frac{500 - 125}{500} \times 100\% = 75\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per Invoice}_{\text{before}} &= \$12.50 \\
\text{Cost per Invoice}_{\text{after}} &= \$3.50 \\
\text{Savings per Invoice} &= 72\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 6: Identify the BPR Principles Applied**
- **Principle 1:** Process organized around outcome (procurement → payment end-to-end).
- **Principle 3:** Information captured once at source (barcode scan at receipt).
- **Principle 6:** Decision point at the work (system auto-approves if match is good).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The Ford case is **not** about installing new software on the old process. It is about **eliminating an entire process step** (the invoice) — a radical redesign, not automation.

---

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls in BPR Implementation

| Pitfall | Symptom | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Narrow process focus** | Only one department is re-engineered, missing upstream/downstream impacts. | Define end-to-end process scope. |
| **Treating BPR as a project** | Treated like a one-off IT project with a deadline. | Treat as an ongoing program. |
| **Underestimating change resistance** | Employees sabotage the new process. | Strong communication, training, and HR support. |
| **Over-relying on IT** | Buying expensive software without rethinking the process. | Rethink process first; then choose IT. |
| **Lack of top-management commitment** | Sponsorship wavers mid-project. | CEO-level champion with board mandate. |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End BPR Methodology Flow

> The following **Mermaid flowchart** represents the canonical five-phase BPR cycle. Each node is named using the alphanumeric-with-prefix rule to avoid Mermaid parser conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Business Crisis or Opportunity Identified])
    p1["Phase 1: Envision and Mobilize"]
    p2["Phase 2: Map and Analyze AS-IS Process"]
    p3["Phase 3: Design TO-BE Process"]
    p4["Phase 4: Prototype and Implement"]
    p5["Phase 5: Refine and Measure"]
    goal1([Dramatic Improvement Achieved])
    loop1([Continuous Improvement Loop])

    start1 --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> goal1
    p5 -.-> loop1
    loop1 -.-> p3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** show the linear progression of a BPR initiative.
- The **dashed arrow** entering `Phase 3` represents the *refine* phase feeding back into redesign — i.e., BPR is followed by continuous improvement on the new foundation.

---

## 4.2 BPR Transformation: As-Is → To-Be Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASIS["AS-IS Process (Sequential, Silos)"]
        a1[Customer] --> a2[Sales]
        a2 --> a3[Order Entry]
        a3 --> a4[Credit Check]
        a4 --> a5[Warehouse]
        a5 --> a6[Shipping]
        a6 --> a7[Billing]
    end

    subgraph TOBE["TO-BE Process (Integrated, IT-enabled)"]
        b1[Customer] --> b2[Integrated Order System]
        b2 --> b3[Real-time Database]
        b3 --> b4[Automated Warehouse and Ship]
        b4 --> b5[Digital Invoice and Payment]
    end

    ASIS -.Re-engineer.-> TOBE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left subgraph** shows a typical *silo-based* order fulfillment process: information passes through **six separate departments**, each with its own queue and delay.
- The **right subgraph** shows the re-engineered process: an **integrated IT system** collapses the same workflow into **three streamlined steps**, with one unified database eliminating the handoffs.

---

## 4.3 BPR Roles and Responsibilities Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    leader1[CEO / Senior Management Champion]
    steer1[Steering Committee]
    team1[Re-engineering Team Leader]
    core1[Core Re-engineering Team]
    ext1[External Consultants and IT Vendors]
    staff1[Affected Employees and Managers]
    pcs1[Process Owners and Supervisors]

    leader1 --> steer1
    steer1 --> team1
    team1 --> core1
    core1 --> ext1
    core1 --> staff1
    core1 --> pcs1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CEO** owns the strategic mandate.
- The **Steering Committee** sets priorities and removes obstacles.
- The **Re-engineering Team Leader** is the day-to-day driver.
- The **Core Team** does the actual analysis and design.
- **External Consultants** bring methodology expertise.
- **Affected Employees** provide ground-truth data and must be involved to ensure buy-in.

---

## 4.4 BPR vs. Other Improvement Approaches (Visual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root1{Improvement Approach}
    root1 --> a1[BPR - Radical Redesign]
    root1 --> a2[TQM - Incremental Quality]
    root1 --> a3[Kaizen - Continuous Small Steps]
    root1 --> a4[Restructuring - Org Change]

    a1 --> r1[Driven by IT and Vision]
    a2 --> r2[Driven by Customer Focus]
    a3 --> r3[Driven by Employee Involvement]
    a4 --> r4[Driven by Financial Crisis]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All four are *improvement approaches* but differ in **scope, driver, and magnitude of change**.
- BPR is the *most radical* of the four and is the only one that treats **IT as a strategic enabler** rather than a support tool.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define Business Process Re-engineering (BPR). State the four key characteristics of BPR.**

**Model Answer:**

Business Process Re-engineering (BPR) is the fundamental rethinking and radical redesign of business processes to achieve dramatic improvements in critical measures of performance such as cost, quality, service, and speed. *(Hammer & Champy, 1993)*

The **four key characteristics** are:

1. **Fundamental** — BPR questions the basic assumptions and rules on which the existing process is built. It asks *"Why do we do this at all?"* rather than *"How do we do this faster?"*
2. **Radical** — BPR ignores the current process entirely and redesigns from a clean slate, rather than modifying the existing process.
3. **Dramatic** — The improvements sought are *order-of-magnitude* (10x or more), not marginal (10%).
4. **Process** — The unit of analysis is the *end-to-end business process*, not a department, function, or organizational unit.

> **Valuator's Note:** A 3-mark answer must include the formal definition *plus* the four keywords. Skipping the keywords typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between Business Process Re-engineering (BPR) and Kaizen (Continuous Improvement).**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | **BPR (Re-engineering)** | **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature of Change** | Radical and discontinuous | Incremental and continuous |
| **Magnitude of Improvement** | Order-of-magnitude (10x or more) | Small, gradual (typically 5–30%) |
| **Time Horizon** | Months to a few years | Daily, ongoing |
| **Trigger** | Crisis or major opportunity | Continuous mindset |
| **Focus** | End-to-end process redesign | Small, employee-driven changes |
| **Risk** | High | Very low |
| **IT Role** | Strategic enabler | Supportive / limited |
| **Origin** | Hammer & Champy, 1993 | Toyota Production System, Japan |

In short, BPR *rebuilds* the process; Kaizen *refines* the existing process.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule (Module Internal Choice):** *Answer the following two questions. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each. Either full set OR with internal choice.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the five-phase methodology of Business Process Re-engineering proposed by Hammer and Champy. Mention the objective of each phase.

**(b) [7 marks)** With a suitable case example, illustrate how IT can act as a key enabler in a BPR initiative.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

The five-phase BPR methodology is as follows:

**Phase 1: Envision and Mobilize (Objective: Build commitment and define scope)**
- Identify the business opportunity or crisis that necessitates re-engineering.
- Obtain top-management sponsorship (a CEO-level champion is essential).
- Form the re-engineering team and define the boundaries of the process to be re-engineered.
- Communicate the BPR vision to the entire organization to reduce resistance.

**Phase 2: Map and Analyze the As-Is Process (Objective: Understand the current state)**
- Select the core business processes to be re-engineered (e.g., order-to-delivery, procure-to-pay).
- Document the current process using flowcharts, IDEF0 models, swim-lane diagrams, and value-added analysis.
- Measure baseline performance: cost per transaction, cycle time, defect rate, customer satisfaction.
- Identify bottlenecks, redundancies, handoffs, and non-value-added activities.

**Phase 3: Design the To-Be Process (Objective: Invent a fundamentally new process)**
- Challenge every existing rule and assumption.
- Brainstorm radically new process designs.
- Apply the BPR principles (organize around outcomes, push decisions to frontline, capture info once, etc.).
- Leverage IT capabilities to enable the new process.
- Develop prototypes and select the most viable design.

**Phase 4: Prototype and Implement (Objective: Roll out the new process)**
- Pilot the new process in one business unit or region.
- Train employees on the new workflow and IT systems.
- Migrate data, configure software, and update the infrastructure.
- Execute change management: address cultural, political, and emotional resistance.
- Roll out the new process to the entire organization.

**Phase 5: Refine and Measure (Objective: Lock in gains and sustain improvement)**
- Compare actual performance against the BPR objectives.
- Fine-tune the new process based on feedback.
- Institutionalize continuous improvement (Kaizen) on the new foundation to prevent regression.
- Document the new process and create standard operating procedures.

> **Valuator's Note:** Award 1 mark per phase, plus 2 marks for the *objective* statements. A diagram earns a bonus 1 mark if drawn.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**IT as the Key Enabler in BPR — Case: Ford Motor Company's Accounts Payable Process**

Ford's Accounts Payable (AP) department in North America employed 500 people to process 500,000 supplier invoices per year. The cost per invoice was about **$12.50** (vs. an industry benchmark of $3.00). Traditional improvement (Kaizen/automation) would have yielded marginal gains.

**BPR Approach (Fundamental Questioning):**
The team asked: *"Why do we receive an invoice at all? The buyer already knows what was ordered and what was received. The invoice is redundant."*

**To-Be Process (IT-enabled):**
- A **barcode-based goods receipt system** was installed at every warehouse.
- When a shipment arrives, the receiver **scans the barcode** on the goods.
- The system automatically **matches** the receipt to the *purchase order* and *goods receipt note* in real-time.
- If a match is found, the system **auto-generates the payment** and **disburses funds electronically** to the supplier.
- The supplier is **notified** of payment receipt — no invoice is sent or processed.

**Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Headcount Reduction} &= 500 \rightarrow 125 \text{ people (75% reduction)} \\
\text{Cost per Invoice} &= \$12.50 \rightarrow \$3.50 \text{ (72% reduction)} \\
\text{Error Rate} &= \text{Dropped significantly} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**IT Capabilities Exploited:**
- **Distributed database** (real-time PO and receipt data).
- **Barcode/RFID** (single data capture at source).
- **Workflow automation** (auto-matching and auto-payment).
- **Electronic funds transfer** (paperless disbursement).

**Conclusion:**
IT did not *automate* the old invoice-processing process. It *eliminated* the invoice itself. This is the essence of BPR: **IT as the enabler of a radically new process**, not the automation of an old one.

> **Valuator's Note:** The case is the differentiator. A generic answer without a named case and quantified results will lose 2–3 marks. Always quantify (numbers, percentages).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks)** Compare and contrast BPR with TQM (Total Quality Management). Highlight the situations in which each is most appropriate.

**(b) [7 marks)** List the seven principles of BPR. Explain any two in detail with industrial examples.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Comparison: BPR vs. TQM**

| Dimension | **BPR** | **TQM** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Radical redesign of processes for dramatic improvement. | Continuous improvement of processes for customer satisfaction. |
| **Type of Change** | Discontinuous, revolutionary | Incremental, evolutionary |
| **Improvement Magnitude** | 10x or more | 5% to 30% over time |
| **Trigger** | Crisis or breakthrough opportunity | Continuous, proactive philosophy |
| **Approach** | Start from scratch | Improve the existing process |
| **Time Frame** | Short, project-based | Long-term, cultural |
| **Risk** | High (50–70% failure rate reported) | Low |
| **Employee Involvement** | Limited to the re-engineering team | All employees (quality circles) |
| **IT Role** | Strategic enabler | Support tool |
| **Measurement** | Breakthrough metrics (cycle time, cost/transaction) | Statistical process control, defect rates |
| **Originated By** | Hammer & Champy (1993) | Deming, Juran, Crosby (1950s–80s) |

**When to Use Each:**

- **Use BPR** when:
  1. The firm is in *deep crisis* (e.g., near-bankruptcy).
  2. *New technology* can obsolete the existing process.
  3. The firm needs a *10x leap*, not a 10% improvement.
  4. *Competitors* are about to disrupt the industry.

- **Use TQM** when:
  1. The firm is *stable* and competitive.
  2. Continuous improvement of *existing* processes is needed.
  3. Building a *quality culture* over years is the goal.
  4. *Customer satisfaction* is the primary strategic driver.

> **Key Insight:** BPR and TQM are **not competitors** — they are **complementary**. Many firms use BPR for a *breakthrough* and then apply TQM/Kaizen to *sustain* the gain.

> **Valuator's Note:** A *table comparison* is the most efficient way to earn full marks. Adding 2–3 sentences of conclusion earns a bonus mark.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**The Seven Principles of BPR (Hammer & Champy):**

1. Organize around outcomes, not tasks.
2. Have those who use the output perform the process.
3. Subsidize information processing for information producers.
4. Treat geographically dispersed resources as if they were centralized.
5. Link parallel activities instead of integrating their results.
6. Put the decision point where the work is performed.
7. Capture information once, at the source.

**Detailed Explanation of Two Principles:**

### **Principle 1: Organize Around Outcomes, Not Tasks**

*Traditional Approach:* In a typical mortgage company, the application is processed by 10 different specialists — one fills the form, another verifies income, a third checks the property, a fourth approves the loan, etc. The customer is passed from one desk to another, leading to delays and frustration.

*Re-engineered Approach:* A **single "case worker"** is assigned to the customer and handles the *entire* mortgage process from application to disbursal. The case worker has access to all necessary information and tools.

*Industrial Example:* **Taco Bell** — re-engineered its restaurant operations so that each worker handles multiple tasks (taking orders, cooking, serving, cleaning) rather than one specialized task. This reduced staff from 60+ to about 9 per restaurant, while increasing service speed.

*Result:* Cycle time reduced dramatically; customer satisfaction increased; one person owns the outcome.

### **Principle 7: Capture Information Once, at the Source**

*Traditional Approach:* In a hospital, a patient's name, address, insurance, and medical history are recorded at the front desk, then re-entered by the nurse, then again by the lab technician, then again by the billing clerk. Each re-entry is a chance for error.

*Re-engineered Approach:* The patient is registered **once** at admission via a digital system. All downstream departments (lab, pharmacy, billing) access the **same central database** in real-time. No re-entry is needed.

*Industrial Example:* **Walmart's** RFID-based inventory system — when a pallet of goods is scanned at the warehouse, the data flows automatically to inventory, ordering, billing, and forecasting systems. No re-keying required.

*Result:* Data accuracy improved; administrative cost reduced; decision-making accelerated.

> **Valuator's Note:** Listing all seven principles earns 3 marks. Detailed explanation with *example* earns the remaining 4 marks. An *unlisted* custom example (not from a textbook) earns a bonus mark.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in BPR Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating BPR with Kaizen/TQM (1–2 mark loss).** Always clarify that BPR is *radical* and Kaizen is *incremental*. Use the words "radical" and "incremental" explicitly.
> 2. **Defining BPR as "IT implementation" (2–3 mark loss).** BPR is a *management philosophy*; IT is its enabler, not its definition.
> 3. **Forgetting the four keywords** (Fundamental, Radical, Dramatic, Process). These are 1.5 marks by themselves in a 3-mark question.
> 4. **Vague case examples** like "a company improved its sales" (1–2 mark loss). Always use **named, quantified** cases (Ford AP, Taco Bell, Walmart).
> 5. **No diagram in a 14-mark question (1 mark loss).** Even a simple process flowchart earns at least 1 extra mark.
> 6. **Confusing "re-engineering" with "restructuring."** Restructuring = organizational change (layoffs, mergers). Re-engineering = process change.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This)**

- ☐ **BPR definition** = Fundamental + Radical + Dramatic + Process redesign.
- ☐ **Originators:** Michael Hammer (1990) and Hammer & Champy (1993).
- ☐ **Unit of analysis:** End-to-end *process*, not function or department.
- ☐ **Four keywords to memorize:** *Fundamental, Radical, Dramatic, Process.*
- ☐ **BPR vs. TQM:** BPR = radical, project-based, high risk. TQM = incremental, culture-based, low risk.
- ☐ **BPR vs. Kaizen:** BPR = start from zero. Kaizen = improve the existing process step by step.
- ☐ **BPR vs. Restructuring:** BPR = process change. Restructuring = organization change (headcount, structure).
- ☐ **Five-phase methodology:** Envision → Map → Design → Implement → Refine.
- ☐ **Seven principles:** Organize around outcomes; user performs process; subsidize info producers; treat dispersed resources as centralized; link parallel activities; decision at work point; capture info once at source.
- ☐ **IT's role:** Enabler, not automator. The Ford AP case is the *canonical* example.
- ☐ **Triggers for BPR:** Crisis, breakthrough opportunity, new technology, 10x target.
- ☐ **Famous cases:** Ford AP (75% headcount cut), Taco Bell (60 → 9 staff), Mutual Benefit Life (claims automation).
- ☐ **Order-of-magnitude target:** BPR aims for *10x* improvement, not 10%.
- ☐ **Risk:** BPR has a 50–70% reported failure rate — change management is critical.
- ☐ **Complementary to Kaizen:** BPR creates the new foundation; Kaizen refines it continuously.
- ☐ **BPR is *not*:** A software project, an automation initiative, a cost-cutting exercise, or a re-organization.
- ☐ **BPR *is*:** A strategic, top-down, IT-enabled, end-to-end process redesign initiative.

> **Final Exam Tip:** *If a KTU question uses the words "dramatic" or "radical," the answer is BPR. If it uses the words "continuous" or "gradual," the answer is TQM or Kaizen. Use this heuristic as a tie-breaker.*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lean Manufacturing, Kaizen, and Toyota Production System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lean Manufacturing, Kaizen, and Toyota Production System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Lean Manufacturing
**Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic, holistic production philosophy that aims to create maximum value for the customer by **eliminating waste (Muda)** and optimizing flow across all operational processes. It was formally codified by **James P. Womack and Daniel T. Jones** in their landmark MIT-based research published as *"Lean Thinking"* (1996), building on earlier foundations by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Lean Manufacturing is a production practice that considers the expenditure of resources for any goal other than the creation of value for the end customer to be wasteful, and thus a target for elimination. Value, as defined by the customer, is the goal.*

- **Core Mantra:** *Create more value with less work.*
- **Origin Discipline:** Industrial Engineering + Operations Strategy.

#### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **water pipeline** with hidden leaks. Traditional manufacturing keeps pumping more water (raw material) to maintain flow, accepting leaks (waste). Lean Manufacturing says: **first plug the leaks**, *then* optimize pumping. Result: the same water (value) reaches the destination with far less wasted effort.

### 1.2 Kaizen
**Kaizen (改善)** is a Japanese compound word — *Kai* (改, "change") + *Zen* (善, "good") — meaning **"change for the better"** or **continuous improvement**. In an operations context, it is the philosophy of engaging **every employee — from the shop-floor worker to the CEO — in identifying and implementing small, incremental, low-cost improvements** to processes on a daily basis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kaizen vs. Innovation:** Kaizen = small, continuous, everyone-driven improvements (marginal gains). Innovation = large, discontinuous, R&D-driven breakthroughs (step changes). Both are required for operational excellence.

#### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **garden**. A gardener does not plant one day and then abandon the garden for a year. He **water, weed, prune, and tend it daily** — small acts that cumulatively produce a beautiful garden. Kaizen is the *daily tending* of business processes.

### 1.3 Toyota Production System (TPS)
The **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is the **origin and integrated framework** of all Lean thinking. Developed by **Taiichi Ohno, Eiji Toyoda, and Shigeo Shingo** at Toyota Motor Corporation (post-WWII, 1950s), it is a complete socio-technical system built on **two pillars**:

| Pillar | Japanese Term | English Translation | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pillar 1** | **JIT** (Just-in-Time) | *Jidoka* in some classifications | Produces and delivers *only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed.* |
| **Pillar 2** | **Jidoka** (自働化) | *Autonomation* / *Automation with a Human Touch* | The system **stops automatically** when abnormality or defect is detected, preventing defective output from moving downstream. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The foundational TPS document is *"Toyota Production System: Beyond Large-Scale Production"* by Taiichi Ohno (1978/1988). Do NOT attribute Lean Manufacturing to a non-existent textbook; reference Toyota's own publications.

#### Intuitive Analogy
Picture an **automatic escalator with a safety sensor**. If a person's hand gets stuck (defect/abnormality), the escalator **immediately stops** (Jidoka) rather than continuing to injure. Meanwhile, a **delivery truck** arrives at the escalator landing *only when passengers are waiting, and only in the quantity needed* (JIT). This is TPS in everyday life.

### 1.4 The Seven Wastes (Muda + Mura + Muri)
A central Lean tool is the classification of **seven classical wastes (TIMWOOD)**, plus the modern 8th waste:

| Symbol | Waste Type | Definition | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **T** | **Transport** | Unnecessary movement of materials/products | Moving parts 200m between distant workstations |
| **I** | **Inventory** | Excess raw materials, WIP, or finished goods | Piles of unsold stock in a warehouse |
| **M** | **Motion** | Unnecessary movement of *people* (bending, walking, reaching) | Worker walking 10m to fetch a tool repeatedly |
| **W** | **Waiting** | Idle time of people, machines, or materials | Machine waiting for operator |
| **O** | **Overproduction** | Making more than the customer orders, sooner than needed | Producing 1000 units when order is for 600 |
| **O** | **Over-processing** | Doing more work than the customer values | Polishing a surface not visible to the customer |
| **D** | **Defects** | Production of faulty products requiring rework/scrap | A printed circuit board with a missing resistor |
| **+T** | **Talent (8th waste)** | Underutilization of people's skills, creativity, or knowledge | Assigning a PhD to clerical data entry |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, candidates often forget the **8th Waste (Talent/Non-utilized skills)** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark trap question in Part A.

### 1.5 The Three Ms (Muda, Mura, Muri)
- **Muda (無駄)** = **Waste** — non-value-adding activity.
- **Mura (斑)** = **Unevenness** — variability in production (e.g., Monday 500 units, Friday 50 units).
- **Muri (無理)** = **Overburden** — overstressing people or equipment beyond natural limits.

TPS targets **all three simultaneously**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Principles of Lean (Womack & Jones Framework)

The Lean transformation follows **five sequential thinking principles**:

**Principle 1 — Specify Value (V)**
Define value from the **customer's perspective**, not the organization's. Value is any activity that the customer is **willing to pay for**.

**Principle 2 — Identify the Value Stream (VSM)**
Map **every step** (value-adding + non-value-adding) required to deliver the product, from raw material to customer. Eliminate non-value steps. The tool used is **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)**.

**Principle 3 — Make Value Flow (F)**
Reorganize the remaining steps so the product **flows continuously** without interruptions, stoppages, or backflows. This often requires reconfiguring cell layouts.

**Principle 4 — Establish Pull (P)**
The customer **pulls** the product from the producer; the producer never *pushes* finished goods. Implementation tool: **Kanban** (signal card).

**Principle 5 — Pursue Perfection (P)**
Repeat the cycle continuously. As waste is removed, new forms of waste become visible. This creates the **virtuous Lean cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for exams:** **V-V-F-P-P** → *Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection.*

### 2.2 The Four Rules of Toyota Production System
(According to Jeffrey Liker's *Toyota Way* synthesis — referenced generally, not as a textbook citation.)

1. **All work shall be highly specified as to content, sequence, timing, and outcome.**
2. **Every customer-supplier connection must be direct, unambiguous, and binary (send / receive).**
3. **The pathway for every product and service must be simple and direct.**
4. **Any improvement must be made in accordance with the scientific method, under the guidance of a teacher, at the lowest possible level in the organization.**

### 2.3 Kaizen: Mechanics of Continuous Improvement

Kaizen operates through the **PDCA (Deming) Cycle** and several structured tools:

$$ \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{(repeats)} $$

**Types of Kaizen:**
- **Kaizen Teian:** Bottom-up suggestion system (worker → management).
- **Kaizen Event / Blitz:** A focused 3–5 day rapid improvement workshop targeting one specific process.
- **Kaikaku:** Radical change (contrasts with Kaizen's incrementalism).

**5S Framework (Foundational to Kaizen):**

| Step | Japanese | English | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | *Seiri* | **Sort** | Remove unnecessary items |
| 2 | *Seiton* | **Set in Order** | Place everything in a designated location |
| 3 | *Seiso* | **Shine** | Clean the workplace |
| 4 | *Seiketsu* | **Standardize** | Establish norms and visual standards |
| 5 | *Shitsuke* | **Sustain** | Maintain discipline |

### 2.4 Key TPS Tools (High-Frequency KTU Exam Topics)

- **Kanban:** Visual signal card authorizing production/withdrawal of parts.
- **Poka-Yoke (ポカヨケ):** Mistake-proofing devices that prevent errors.
- **Heijunka:** Level scheduling of production to smooth demand.
- **Andon (行灯):** Visual signal/light board indicating workstation status.
- **Gemba (現場):** "The actual place" — managers go to the shop floor.
- **Jidoka:** Autonomous stop-on-defect.
- **Standardized Work:** Documented best-known method for a task.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Lean Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
