> The following formulas are **mathematically verifiable** and are routinely tested in KTU 2024 scheme Part B numerical/analytical questions. All variables are defined.

| # | Metric Name | Formula | Variables | Unit / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{T_a}{D}$ | $T_a$ = available production time per period; $D$ = customer demand per period | seconds/unit |
| 2 | **Cycle Time** | $T_c = \dfrac{T_v}{N}$ | $T_v$ = total value-adding time; $N$ = number of units produced | seconds/unit |
| 3 | **Lead Time** | $T_L = T_p + T_q + T_m$ | $T_p$ = processing time; $T_q$ = queue time; $T_m$ = move time | seconds |
| 4 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability; $P$ = Performance; $Q$ = Quality | decimal (0 to 1) |
| 5 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{good}$ = units passing without rework; $N_{total}$ = units started | decimal (0 to 1) |
| 6 | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $\text{VAR} = \dfrac{T_{VA}}{T_{TL}}$ | $T_{VA}$ = value-added time; $T_{TL}$ = total lead time | decimal (0 to 1) |
| 7 | **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** | $\text{PCE} = \dfrac{\sum T_{VA}}{T_{TL}}$ | Same as VAR (synonym in Lean) | decimal (0 to 1) |
| 8 | **Capacity Utilization (CU)** | $\text{CU} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_t} \times 100\%$ | $T_c$ = cycle time; $T_t$ = takt time | percentage |
| 9 | **Work-in-Progress (Little's Law)** | $\text{WIP} = \text{TH} \times T_L$ | TH = throughput (units/time); $T_L$ = lead time | units |
| 10 | **Kaizen Savings** | $S_K = (C_b - C_a) \times V$ | $C_b$ = cost before; $C_a$ = cost after; $V$ = annual volume | currency/year |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** If Cycle Time $>$ Takt Time, the line is **overloaded** (muri + muda). If Cycle Time $\ll$ Takt Time, there is **excess capacity** (overproduction risk).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
In KTU 2024 context, Lean metrics are essential for:
- **Industrial Engineering:** Line balancing, capacity planning, line design.
- **Production & Operations Management:** Bottleneck analysis.
- **Quality Management:** OEE-driven TPM (Total Productive Maintenance).
- **Supply Chain:** Lead-time compression and pull-based replenishment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Takt Time

Takt time is derived from German *Taktzeit*, where *Takt* = rhythm/beat (musical time signature), representing the **pulse of customer demand**.

$$
T_t = \frac{T_a}{D}
$$

**Derivation Logic (verbal):** If $D$ customers demand a product during a finite available production window $T_a$, then the producer must complete **one unit every $T_t$ seconds** to satisfy demand without delay and without overproduction.

**Worked Example (typical KTU 14-mark Part B setup):**

> A plant operates **two shifts of 8 hours each, 5 days/week**, with a total break time of **30 minutes per shift**. The weekly customer demand is **1,200 units**. Calculate the Takt Time in **seconds/unit**.

**Step 1 — Compute available production time per week ($T_a$):**

$$
T_a = \left(8 \times 60 \times 2\right)\ \text{min} - \left(30 \times 2\right)\ \text{min} = 960 - 60 = 900\ \text{min/week}
$$

Convert to seconds:

$$
T_a = 900 \times 60 = 54{,}000\ \text{seconds/week}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Takt Time formula:**

$$
T_t = \frac{54{,}000\ \text{s/week}}{1{,}200\ \text{units/week}} = 45\ \text{seconds/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The workstation must complete **one unit every 45 seconds** to meet demand exactly. If cycle time is greater than 45 s, demand cannot be met with current resources. If cycle time is less, the workstation has slack that risks overproduction.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

The OEE formula is **multiplicatively constructed** because each factor captures an **independent dimension of loss**. They are statistically independent, so total efficiency is the product.

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

**Step-by-step Expansion:**

$$
\text{OEE} = \underbrace{\frac{T_{\text{running}}}{T_{\text{planned}}}}_{\text{Availability}} \times \underbrace{\frac{(N_{\text{good}} + N_{\text{def}}) \times T_{\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{running}}}}_{\text{Performance}} \times \underbrace{\frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{good}} + N_{\text{def}}}}_{\text{Quality}}
$$

**Worked Example:**

> A CNC machine has **planned production time = 480 min/day**, **actual run time = 420 min/day**, **actual output = 950 units**, **defective units = 50 units**, and **ideal cycle time = 0.5 min/unit**.

**Step 1 — Availability ($A$):**

$$
A = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 \quad (87.5\%)
$$

**Step 2 — Performance ($P$):**

Theoretical max output = $\dfrac{420}{0.5} = 840$ units. Actual output = 950 units.

$$
P = \frac{950}{840} = 1.131
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Performance can exceed 1.0 (100%) when the machine runs faster than its ideal cycle. KTU examiners cap this at 1.0 unless stated otherwise. If capped:

$$
P_{\text{capped}} = 1.0
$$

**Step 3 — Quality ($Q$):**

$$
Q = \frac{950 - 50}{950} = \frac{900}{950} = 0.947 \quad (94.7\%)
$$

**Step 4 — OEE:**

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.875 \times 1.0 \times 0.947 = 0.829 \quad (82.9\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** **World-class OEE = 85% (5σ) per SEMI standards.** A value of 82.9% is close to best-in-class.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Lead Time Compression and Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)

The **Process Cycle Efficiency** is the heart of Lean — measuring the proportion of lead time that actually adds value.

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{\sum T_{VA}}{T_{TL}}
$$

Where:

- $T_{VA}$ = value-added time.
- $T_{TL}$ = total lead time = value-added + non-value-added.

**Worked Example (Kerala KTU 14-mark Part B classical problem):**

> A coffee table production process has the following task data: Cutting = 8 min, Sanding = 12 min, Assembly = 20 min, Finishing = 18 min, Inspection = 6 min, **Queue/Wait** = 4 hr (240 min) before Cutting, and **Storage/Transport** = 8 hr (480 min) between Sanding and Assembly.

**Step 1 — Identify value-added tasks (in Lean, **only** operations that change the product to a form the customer is willing to pay for):**

$$
T_{VA} = 8 + 12 + 20 + 18 = 58\ \text{minutes}
$$

(Note: **Inspection is non-value-added** in pure Lean terms, even though necessary — it is treated as a "necessary non-value-added" or "business value-add.")

**Step 2 — Compute total lead time:**

$$
T_{TL} = 58 + 240 + 480 = 778\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PCE:**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{58}{778} = 0.0746 \approx 7.46\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and Lean intervention:**

> Only **7.46%** of total lead time is value-adding. **92.54% is waste** (queue + transport). Lean would propose a **cell layout** to eliminate storage and queue time. After the change, lead time could drop from 778 min to ~58 min, raising PCE to ~100%.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Linking Concepts to the KTU 2024 Outcome

| KTU Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Linked Topic | Exam Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO4** — Apply Lean tools for waste elimination | Apply (L3) | Takt time, OEE, PCE calculations | 7–10 marks |
| **CO4** — Design improvement systems | Design (L6) | 5S, VSM, Kanban design | 4–5 marks |
| **CO5** — Evaluate continuous improvement | Evaluate (L5) | Kaizen analysis, PDCA critique | 2–3 marks |
| **CO4** — Explain Toyota's system | Understand (L2) | TPS House, 7 wastes, 5 principles | 3–5 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Toyota Production System (TPS) House

> [!NOTE]
> The **TPS House** is the canonical diagram for KTU Module 4. Memorize its structure: a **roof** (Goal), **two pillars** (JIT + Jidoka), and a **foundation** (Heijunka/Standardized Work), all sitting on **monozukuri/people-centered management**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    base1["Monozukuri Respect for People"]
    base2["Standardized Work"]
    base3["Heijunka Level Scheduling"]
    base4["Kaizen Continuous Improvement"]

    pillarL["JIT Just in Time"]
    pillarR["Jidoka Autonomation"]

    roof1["Highest Quality Lowest Cost Shortest Lead Time"]
    roof2["Just in Time Delivery"]

    base1 --> pillarL
    base2 --> pillarL
    base3 --> pillarL
    base1 --> pillarR
    base2 --> pillarR
    base4 --> pillarR

    pillarL --> roof1
    pillarR --> roof1
    pillarL --> roof2
    pillarR --> roof2

    style base1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style base2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style base3 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style base4 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800
    style pillarL fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#003366
    style pillarR fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#003366
    style roof1 fill:#d8f0d8,stroke:#006600
    style roof2 fill:#d8f0d8,stroke:#006600
```

---

### 4.2 The Five Principles of Lean (V-V-F-P-P Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Principle 1 Specify VALUE from customer view"]
    p2["Principle 2 Map the VALUE STREAM"]
    p3["Principle 3 Create VALUE FLOW"]
    p4["Principle 4 Establish PULL system"]
    p5["Principle 5 Pursue PERFECTION"]

    p1 ==> p2
    p2 ==> p3
    p3 ==> p4
    p4 ==> p5
    p5 -.continuous.-> p2

    style p1 fill:#fde2e2
    style p2 fill:#fde2e2
    style p3 fill:#fde2e2
    style p4 fill:#fde2e2
    style p5 fill:#fde2e2
```

---

### 4.3 Kaizen PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plan["PLAN identify problem set targets define method"]
    do["DO implement plan on small scale train people"]
    check["CHECK compare results against target"]
    act["ACT standardize if good revise if not repeat cycle"]

    plan ==> do
    do ==> check
    check ==> act
    act -.loop.-> plan

    style plan fill:#cce5ff
    style do fill:#cce5ff
    style check fill:#cce5ff
    style act fill:#cce5ff
```

---

### 4.4 The 8 Wastes (TIMWOOD + Talent) — Functional Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    waste["EIGHT WASTES TIMWOOD Plus Talent"]
    w1["T Transport"]
    w2["I Inventory"]
    w3["M Motion"]
    w4["W Waiting"]
    w5["O Overproduction"]
    w6["O Over Processing"]
    w7["D Defects"]
    w8["T Talent Underutilized"]

    waste --> w1
    waste --> w2
    waste --> w3
    waste --> w4
    waste --> w5
    waste --> w6
    waste --> w7
    waste --> w8

    w5 -.most critical.-> waste

    style waste fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style w5 fill:#ff9999
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In TPS theory, **Overproduction is considered the worst waste** because it *causes* all the other wastes (excess inventory, extra transport, motion to manage stock, waiting for slow-moving stock, defects hidden in piles).

---

### 4.5 Value Stream Mapping (VSM) — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Supplier"]
    a2["Receive Raw Materials"]
    a3["Process 1 VA"]
    a4["Buffer 1 NVA"]
    a5["Process 2 VA"]
    a6["Buffer 2 NVA"]
    a7["Process 3 VA"]
    a8["Finished Goods Storage"]
    a9["Customer Pull via Kanban"]

    a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4 --> a5 --> a6 --> a7 --> a8 --> a9

    a3 -. VA time.-> a3
    a4 -. NVA waste.-> a4
    a9 -. pull signal.-> a7

    style a3 fill:#d8f0d8
    style a5 fill:#d8f0d8
    style a7 fill:#d8f0d8
    style a4 fill:#fdd
    style a6 fill:#fdd
    style a8 fill:#ffe
```

> VA = Value-Added (green). NVA = Non-Value-Added waste (red). Lean = eliminate red blocks, merge green blocks into flow cells.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**

**Q1. Define Lean Manufacturing. List the five principles of Lean thinking.**

**Model Answer:**
Lean Manufacturing is a production philosophy focused on maximizing customer value while systematically eliminating waste (Muda), unevenness (Mura), and overburden (Muri). It was formalized by Womack & Jones (1996).

The **five principles** are:
1. Specify **Value** (from customer's view).
2. Identify the **Value Stream** (map all steps).
3. Make **Flow** (eliminate interruptions).
4. Establish **Pull** (produce only on demand).
5. Pursue **Perfection** (continuous improvement).

*Mnemonic:* **V-V-F-P-P.**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q2. What is Kaizen? Differentiate between Kaizen and Innovation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Kaizen** | **Innovation** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Scale | Small, incremental | Large, radical |
| Source | Every employee | R&D department |
| Frequency | Continuous, daily | Periodic, sporadic |
| Cost | Low, no major investment | High, capital-intensive |
| Risk | Low | High |
| Example | Reducing setup time from 15 min to 12 min | Replacing a manual line with a fully automated robotic cell |

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Model Paper 2024]**

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **EITHER**

**(a) Explain in detail the Toyota Production System (TPS). With the help of a block diagram, describe its two pillars and foundation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is the integrated production system developed by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota Motor Corporation, post-WWII, as a response to Japan's resource constraints. TPS was designed to deliver **the highest quality, at the lowest cost, with the shortest lead time** (the *roof* of the TPS House).

**1. The Two Pillars:**

**Pillar 1 — Just-in-Time (JIT):**
JIT means producing and delivering **only what is needed, only when it is needed, and only in the amount needed**. It is implemented via the **Kanban system** (visual signal cards), **Heijunka** (level production scheduling), and a **pull-based flow** that works backward from customer demand. JIT eliminates the waste of **overproduction**, the root waste in TPS.

**Pillar 2 — Jidoka (Autonomation):**
Jidoka = *automation with a human touch.* It empowers any worker or machine to **stop the production line immediately** upon detecting an abnormality or defect. This prevents defective products from moving downstream, where they would create rework, scrap, or warranty costs. The **Andon** (signal light) and **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) devices are Jidoka tools. Jidoka surfaces problems at the moment they occur, making them immediately solvable.

**2. The Foundation (House base):**
- **Heijunka** — level scheduling of production to smooth demand variability.
- **Standardized Work** — every task has a documented best-known method.
- **Kaizen** — continuous, employee-driven improvement.
- **Respect for People** — the philosophical bedrock: trust, challenge, and grow every worker.

**3. The Roof (Goal):**
The goal of TPS is to deliver **highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead time** through JIT and Jidoka.

**Block Diagram (Functional):**
```
                        ┌─────────────────────────────┐
                        │   GOAL: Highest Quality,    │
                        │   Lowest Cost, Shortest LT  │
                        └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                                       │
                       ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
                       │                               │
                ┌──────┴──────┐                ┌───────┴──────┐
                │     JIT     │                │   JIDOKA     │
                │   Pillar 1  │                │   Pillar 2   │
                └──────┬──────┘                └───────┬──────┘
                       │                               │
                       └───────────────┬───────────────┘
                                       │
              ┌────────────┬───────────┴────────┬────────────┐
              │ Heijunka   │ Standardized Work  │  Kaizen    │
              └────────────┴────────────────────┴────────────┘
                  FOUNDATION: Respect for People
```

---

**(b) What are the seven wastes (Muda) of Lean? Describe each with one engineering example. Explain why Overproduction is considered the most critical waste. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD)** are:

1. **Transport** — Unnecessary movement of materials between operations. *Example:* Forklifts carrying parts between distant warehouses.
2. **Inventory** — Excess raw materials, WIP, or finished goods tying up capital and space. *Example:* Three months of unsold finished goods stored in a factory yard.
3. **Motion** — Unnecessary human movement (bending, walking, searching). *Example:* A worker walking 20 m to fetch a tool on a poorly organized workstation.
4. **Waiting** — Idle time of workers, machines, or materials. *Example:* A CNC machine waiting 45 minutes for a tool change.
5. **Overproduction** — Producing more than the customer demands, or earlier than needed. *Example:* Producing 2,000 units when the order is for 1,200.
6. **Over-processing** — Doing more work than the customer values. *Example:* Painting a surface not visible after assembly.
7. **Defects** — Production of faulty items requiring rework/scrap. *Example:* A printed circuit with wrong resistor value.

**Why Overproduction is the most critical waste:**
- It **causes** all the other wastes: surplus inventory, extra transport, extra motion, defects hidden in piles, increased waiting at downstream stations.
- It is **hidden** — production is busy, KPIs look good, but real demand isn't met.
- It increases **lead time**, **storage cost**, and **working capital** requirements.
- It is **waste of waste** — once it occurs, downstream waste becomes inevitable.

In TPS doctrine, fixing Overproduction automatically exposes and resolves the other six wastes.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **OR** (Internal Choice)

**(a) A manufacturing unit operates for **2 shifts of 8 hours each, 6 days a week**. The break time per shift is **45 minutes**. The weekly customer demand is **2,500 units**. A particular machine produces at a cycle time of **48 seconds/unit**, with current defects of **4%**. Compute: (i) Takt Time, (ii) Cycle Time, (iii) Capacity Utilization, (iv) Good Units per Week (after quality), and (v) State whether the system is balanced, overloaded, or underutilized. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Available production time per week ($T_a$):**

$$
T_a = \left[(8 \times 60) \times 2 \times 6\right] - \left[45 \times 2 \times 6\right] = 5{,}760 - 540 = 5{,}220\ \text{min/week}
$$

Convert to seconds:

$$
T_a = 5{,}220 \times 60 = 313{,}200\ \text{seconds/week}
$$

**Step 2 — Takt Time ($T_t$):**

$$
T_t = \frac{T_a}{D} = \frac{313{,}200}{2{,}500} = 125.28\ \text{seconds/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle Time given = 48 seconds/unit.**

**Step 4 — Capacity Utilization (CU):**

$$
\text{CU} = \frac{T_c}{T_t} \times 100\% = \frac{48}{125.28} \times 100\% = 38.32\%
$$

**Step 5 — Good Units per Week:**

Total possible output in one week:

$$
N_{\text{out}} = \frac{T_a}{T_c} = \frac{313{,}200}{48} = 6{,}525\ \text{units/week (gross)}
$$

After 4% defects:

$$
N_{\text{good}} = 6{,}525 \times (1 - 0.04) = 6{,}264\ \text{good units/week}
$$

**Step 6 — System Status:**

Since $T_c = 48\ \text{s} \ll T_t = 125.28\ \text{s}$, the system is **underutilized** with substantial idle time at this single machine. However, the gross output (6,525 units) far exceeds weekly demand (2,500 units), risking **overproduction** waste. **Recommendation:** Apply Heijunka to schedule only 2,500 units, redistribute operator to other value streams, and analyze whether the machine cycle time can be increased to match takt time or whether the machine can be shared across products.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of Value Stream Mapping (VSM). With an example, show how it is used to identify and eliminate waste. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** is a **Lean tool** that visually represents every step (value-adding and non-value-adding) required to take a product from raw material to the customer's hands. It was pioneered as part of the *Lean Enterprise Institute* methodology building on Toyota's industrial engineering practices.

**Purpose of VSM:**
- Identify **value-added (VA)** and **non-value-added (NVA)** steps.
- Calculate **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)**.
- Design a **future state map** with reduced waste.

**Example:** A printed circuit board (PCB) assembly line:

| Step | Type | Time (min) | VA / NVA |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1. Receive components | Logistics | 720 | NVA |
| 2. Kitting | Logistics | 30 | NVA |
| 3. Solder paste application | Production | 3 | VA |
| 4. Pick-and-place | Production | 8 | VA |
| 5. Reflow oven | Production | 12 | VA |
| 6. **Buffer / Queue** | Inventory | 480 | NVA |
| 7. AOI inspection | Quality check | 4 | NVA (necessary) |
| 8. Final assembly | Production | 15 | VA |
| 9. **Storage warehouse** | Inventory | 1,440 | NVA |
| **Total VA time** |  | **38 min** | |
| **Total Lead Time** |  | **2,712 min** | |

**Process Cycle Efficiency:**

$$
\text{PCE} = \frac{38}{2{,}712} = 1.4\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **1.4%** of total lead time adds value. **98.6% is waste.**

**Future State VSM (Lean Proposal):**
- Eliminate Steps 1, 2, 6, 9 by introducing a **pull-based cell layout** with direct supplier-to-line delivery (JIT).
- Move AOI in-line (not as a separate queue).
- **New Lead Time ≈ 42 min**, new **PCE ≈ 90.5%**.

**Conclusion:** VSM transforms a process from a fragmented, wasteful, push-based system into a **continuous-flow, pull-based, value-stream-optimized** production line.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing 8th Waste (Talent):** Many students write only 7 wastes and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Conflating Mura and Muri with Muda:** All three are different — be precise in definitions.
> 3. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time:** Takt = demand-driven (target); Cycle = actual process time. They are not the same.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert minutes → seconds** in numerical problems (board will deduct full marks for unit inconsistency).
> 5. **Treating Inspection as Value-Added:** Strictly, in Lean, inspection is **necessary non-value-added**, not VA.
> 6. **Misattributing TPS origins:** Some students write that TPS was developed by "Henry Ford" — *Ford's system was the precursor (flow production), but TPS is uniquely Toyota's contribution.*
> 7. **In TPS House diagrams,** swapping the pillars (writing Jidoka in the JIT box) — a classic 1-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Lean = **maximize value, eliminate waste** (Muda, Mura, Muri).
- Lean Manufacturing origin: **Womack & Jones (1996), MIT**, building on **Taiichi Ohno's TPS**.
- **5 Lean Principles (V-V-F-P-P):** Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection.
- **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD):** Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects. **8th Waste = Talent.**
- **Overproduction** is the most critical waste because it causes all the others.
- **TPS Two Pillars:** JIT (Just-in-Time) and **Jidoka (Autonomation)**.
- **TPS House:** Foundation = Heijunka + Standardized Work + Kaizen + Respect for People. Roof = Quality + Low Cost + Short Lead Time.
- **Kaizen = Continuous Improvement.** PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act). 5S = Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- **Kaizen Teian** = bottom-up suggestions; **Kaizen Event** = 3–5 day blitz workshop.
- **Takt Time** $T_t = T_a / D$ (seconds/unit). **Cycle Time** = actual process time. **OEE** = A × P × Q. **PCE** = $T_{VA} / T_{TL}$.
- **Kanban** = visual signal card for pull. **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing. **Heijunka** = level scheduling. **Andon** = visual status signal. **Gemba** = the actual place (shop floor).
- **Value Stream Mapping (VSM):** tool to identify VA vs. NVA steps and design a future-state map.
- **World-class benchmark:** OEE = **85%**; PCE target = push toward **100%**.
- **PDCA** is also called the **Deming Cycle** (or Shewhart Cycle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Class Manufacturing Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** World Class Manufacturing Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of World Class Manufacturing (WCM)

**World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is a strategic, integrated, and holistic management philosophy that enables an organization to achieve world-class performance in quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and service by continuously improving its manufacturing operations through the adoption of best-in-class practices, advanced technologies, and people-centric systems.

According to the seminal definition by **Professor Yamashina (1995)** of Kyoto University, World Class Manufacturing refers to a manufacturing system that can deliver products and services of the **highest quality, lowest cost, shortest lead time, and maximum flexibility**, while maintaining **zero defects, zero accidents, and zero breakdowns**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBIET304 Module 4):**
> WCM is a set of management concepts, principles, tools, and techniques that, when implemented collectively, transform a traditional manufacturing enterprise into a globally competitive, customer-centric, and continuously improving organization operating at world-class benchmarks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Philosophy of WCM:**
> WCM is not a single tool or technique — it is an **integrated system** combining the best elements of Japanese manufacturing practices (TPS), Western management theory, and modern digital technologies. It is judged by **customer satisfaction metrics**, not by internal standards.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### The "Olympic Athlete" Analogy

Imagine two runners preparing for the Olympic 100m sprint:

- **Runner A (Traditional Manufacturer):** Runs fast enough to win local district meets. Trains using outdated methods, eats whatever is convenient, rests only when exhausted, and never measures his exact timing.
- **Runner B (World Class Manufacturer):** Trains under a scientifically designed regimen, eats precision-measured nutrition, uses video analytics to refine every micro-movement, recovers using data-driven physiotherapy, and benchmarks his split-second timing against the world record. He wins gold medals.

**World Class Manufacturing is exactly this transformation in the industrial context.** It is the discipline of benchmarking against the global best, eliminating every form of waste, empowering every employee, and engineering perfection into every process.

### The "Five-Star Hotel" Analogy

A five-star hotel delivers more than just a room. It offers:
- Personalized service
- Immaculate cleanliness
- On-time housekeeping
- Premium amenities
- Memorable experience

Similarly, a **world-class manufacturer** delivers more than just a product. It offers **on-time delivery, zero-defect quality, lifetime reliability, and continuous innovation**.

---

## 1.3 Origin and Evolution of WCM

| Era | Decade | Key Development |
|-----|--------|-----------------|
| Pre-WCM | 1950s–1970s | Mass production era (Ford, Taylor) |
| Birth of WCM Concept | **1980s** | Term coined during US study of Japanese manufacturing (Toyota Production System) |
| Maturity | 1990s | Yamashina's 20 criteria; WCM formally defined |
| Global Adoption | 2000s | Integration with ERP, Six Sigma, Lean Six Sigma |
| Industry 4.0 Era | 2010s–Present | WCM + IoT, AI, Big Data, Digital Twins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note for KTU Exams:**
> The term "World Class Manufacturing" was popularized after the **MIT Sloan School study (1986)** — *"The Machine That Changed the World"* — which analyzed why Japanese automakers had surpassed Western manufacturers in quality and productivity.

---

## 1.4 The WCM Performance Triad

Every WCM system targets three simultaneous performance vectors:

1. **Quality Excellence** — Zero Defects culture
2. **Cost Leadership** — Lowest Total Cost of Ownership
3. **Delivery Precision** — 100% On-Time Delivery (OTD)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Term:** The combination of Quality + Cost + Delivery is often abbreviated as **QCD** in WCM literature. Some authors add **Morale** (QCDM) to emphasize the human dimension.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10 Pillars of World Class Manufacturing

The **Yamashina Model (1995)** identifies **20 critical elements** of WCM grouped into **10 strategic pillars**. This is the most frequently tested framework in KTU examinations.

### Pillar 1: Quality (TQM & Quality Control)
- Defect prevention, Statistical Process Control (SPC), 5S, Poka-Yoke
- Target: **Zero Defects (ZD)**

### Pillar 2: Cost (Cost Management)
- Activity-Based Costing, Kaizen Costing, Target Costing
- Target: Continuous cost reduction

### Pillar 3: Delivery (Logistics & Lead Time)
- JIT delivery, optimized supply chain
- Target: **100% On-Time Delivery**

### Pillar 4: Safety, Health & Environment (SHE)
- Zero accidents, zero environmental incidents
- Target: **Zero Harm**

### Pillar 5: Maintenance (Total Productive Maintenance - TPM)
- Autonomous maintenance, preventive maintenance
- Target: **Zero Breakdowns (OEE > 85%)**

### Pillar 6: Work Place Organization (5S + 3S)
- Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain + Safety, Security, Satisfaction
- Target: Visual factory and zero waste

### Pillar 7: Logistics (Material Flow)
- Pull systems, Kanban, milk runs
- Target: Zero inventory waste

### Pillar 8: Product & Process Development (Concurrent Engineering)
- DFM, DFA, modular design, cross-functional teams
- Target: Reduced Time-to-Market

### Pillar 9: People (Human Resource Development)
- Skill matrix, multi-skill training, suggestion systems
- Target: Empowered, multi-skilled workforce

### Pillar 10: Management (Leadership & Strategy)
- Policy deployment (Hoshin Kanri), shop-floor management
- Target: Aligned vision and culture

---

## 2.2 The 20 Critical Elements (Yamashina's Detailed List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Question:** "List the 20 critical elements of World Class Manufacturing as per Yamashina."

| Sl. No. | Element | Sl. No. | Element |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Quality (TQM) | 11 | Maintenance (TPM) |
| 2 | Cost (Target Costing) | 12 | Material Handling |
| 3 | Delivery (JIT) | 13 | Layout (Cellular) |
| 4 | Safety | 14 | Software Systems (ERP) |
| 5 | Environment | 15 | Product Design |
| 6 | Process Design | 16 | Process Technology |
| 7 | Information Systems | 17 | Leadership |
| 8 | People (Training) | 18 | Management (Policy) |
| 9 | Work Organization | 19 | Supplier Management |
| 10 | Workplace (5S) | 20 | Customer Focus |

---

## 2.3 Core Principles of WCM

### Principle 1: Customer Focus
- Internal and external customers are the ultimate judges
- **VOC (Voice of Customer)** drives product and process design

### Principle 2: Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
- Small, incremental improvements made daily by every employee
- Opposite of *Kaikaku* (radical change)

### Principle 3: Elimination of Waste (Muda)
The **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)** identified by Toyota:
- **T**ransportation
- **I**nventory
- **M**otion
- **W**aiting
- **O**ver-production
- **O**ver-processing
- **D**efects

> [!NOTE]
> In the 8 Wastes model, **Skills (Non-utilized talent)** is added — often called **TIMWOODS**.

### Principle 4: Just-in-Time (JIT)
- The right items, in the right quantity, at the right time
- Pull-based production driven by real demand

### Principle 5: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
- Every operator maintains their own equipment
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is the key metric

### Principle 6: Employee Empowerment
- People are the most valuable asset
- Bottom-up innovation and suggestion schemes

### Principle 7: Process Approach
- Optimize the *system* over the *individual* (Deming's System Profound Knowledge)
- 85% of problems are system-related, 15% are people-related

### Principle 8: Supplier Partnership
- Long-term relationships with few, high-quality suppliers
- Single sourcing with mutual growth

### Principle 9: Benchmarking
- Learn from the best in the world
- **Types:** Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic

### Principle 10: Agility and Adaptability
- Rapid response to market changes
- Mass customization over mass production

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Purpose |
|------------------|----------|-------|---------|
| **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Percentage | Equipment productivity |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | % | Downtime loss |
| **Performance (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | % | Speed loss |
| **Quality (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$ | % | Defect loss |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | % | Process capability |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | % | Multi-step yield |
| **Takt Time** | $Takt = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | Sec/Unit | Pace of production |
| **Cycle Time** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Total Time}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | Sec/Unit | Actual time per unit |
| **Lead Time** | $LT = \text{Queue Time} + \text{Process Time} + \text{Move Time} + \text{Wait Time}$ | Hours/Days | Order-to-delivery |
| **Inventory Turns** | $IT = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Times/Year | Inventory efficiency |
| **Pareto (80/20 Rule)** | $Cumulative \% = \sum \text{Category \%}$ | % | Vital few vs trivial many |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | ppm | Six Sigma metric |
| **Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \dfrac{T - \mu}{S}$ | σ | Process capability |
| **Poka-Yoke Effectiveness** | $Poke = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Defects after Poka-Yoke}}{\text{Defects before Poka-Yoke}}$ | % | Mistake-proofing impact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the units of OEE as **%** and the world-class benchmark for OEE is **≥ 85%** (or "**World Class OEE**" is sometimes defined as ≥ 90%).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

WCM principles are not academic — they directly impact:

- **Automotive:** Toyota, Tesla, Maruti Suzuki
- **Electronics:** Foxconn, Samsung, Apple (via suppliers)
- **Steel:** POSCO, Tata Steel (Jamshedpur — winner of Deming Prize)
- **Aerospace:** Boeing, Airbus
- **Indian PSU:** BHEL, NTPC, Indian Oil (Kochi Refinery — first Indian plant to win the Deming Prize)

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Case Study for KTU:** Tata Steel's Jamshedpur plant adopted WCM in 2008 and became the **first Indian company to win the Deming Prize (2012)** for total quality management. Refer to this in any descriptive WCM answer for high marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison: WCM vs Traditional Manufacturing

| Dimension | Traditional Manufacturing | World Class Manufacturing |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Goal** | Acceptable quality, acceptable cost | Zero defects, lowest cost, fastest delivery |
| **Customer** | Sales-driven, product-out | Market-driven, design-in |
| **Quality** | Inspection-based, end-of-line | Prevention-based, in-process |
| **Inventory** | Large buffer, push system | Minimal, pull system (JIT) |
| **Workforce** | Specialized, do one task | Multi-skilled, empowered |
| **Suppliers** | Many, price-driven | Few, partnership-driven |
| **Maintenance** | Break-down, reactive | TPM, predictive, autonomous |
| **Management Style** | Top-down command | Bottom-up + top-down policy deployment |
| **Lead Time** | Weeks to months | Days to hours |
| **Batch Size** | Large | Single-piece flow (where possible) |
| **Improvement** | Crisis-driven, sporadic | Continuous, daily Kaizen |
| **Benchmarking** | Internal standards | Global best-in-class |
| **Information Flow** | Vertical, paper-based | Horizontal, real-time digital |
| **Waste** | Accepted as cost of doing business | Treated as a crime to be eliminated |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: OEE Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC machine is scheduled to run for 8 hours per shift (planned production time). During the shift:
- The machine broke down twice for a total of 45 minutes
- It was set up for changeover for 30 minutes
- It ran at 90% of its ideal speed
- Out of 400 units produced, 12 were rejected as defects

**Step 1: Calculate Planned Production Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Planned Production Time} &= 8 \text{ hours} \times 3600 \text{ sec/hour} \\
&= 28800 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic: 1 hour = 3600 seconds; hence 8 hours = 28800 seconds.*

**Step 2: Calculate Operating Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Time} &= \text{Planned Time} - \text{Downtime} \\
&= 28800 - (45 \times 60) - (30 \times 60) \\
&= 28800 - 2700 - 1800 \\
&= 24300 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic: Both downtime (45 min) and setup (30 min) are converted to seconds before subtraction to maintain unit consistency.*

**Step 3: Calculate Availability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100 \\
A &= \frac{24300}{28800} \times 100 \\
A &= 84.375\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation: The machine was available (running) 84.375% of the scheduled time. The remaining 15.625% is the Availability Loss (downtime + setup).*

**Step 4: Calculate Performance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

We need the ideal cycle time. Let us assume the ideal cycle time is **60 seconds per unit**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ideal Run Time} &= 60 \times 400 = 24000 \text{ seconds} \\
P &= \frac{24000}{24300} \times 100 \\
P &= 98.765\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation: The machine ran 1.235% slower than its theoretical maximum speed — the Performance Loss (minor speed losses, idling).*

**Step 5: Calculate Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} \times 100 \\
Q &= \frac{400 - 12}{400} \times 100 \\
Q &= \frac{388}{400} \times 100 \\
Q &= 97.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation: 97% of units passed the quality check. The 3% defect rate represents the Quality Loss.*

**Step 6: Calculate OEE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\
OEE &= 84.375\% \times 98.765\% \times 97.0\% \\
OEE &= 0.84375 \times 0.98765 \times 0.97 \\
OEE &= 0.8082 \text{ or } 80.82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** An OEE of 80.82% is **good** but not yet **world-class**. The world-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**, often cited as the "Discrete Manufacturing World Class" standard. To reach this, the manufacturer must address the **Availability Loss** (15.625%) which is the dominant contributor.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Takt Time

**Problem Statement:**
A factory operates **2 shifts of 8 hours each = 16 hours per day**. Each shift has **60 minutes of breaks + planned downtime**. The daily customer demand is **960 units**.

**Step 1: Calculate Available Production Time per Day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Scheduled Time} &= 2 \times 8 \times 60 = 960 \text{ min/day} \\
\text{Break/Downtime} &= 60 \text{ min/day} \\
\text{Available Time} &= 960 - 60 = 900 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to Seconds**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available Time} &= 900 \times 60 = 54000 \text{ sec/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Takt Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Takt &= \frac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}} \\
Takt &= \frac{54000}{960} \\
Takt &= 56.25 \text{ seconds per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation: To meet the daily demand, the production process must complete one unit every 56.25 seconds. This is the "heartbeat" of the production system. The cycle time of every process must be **≤ 56.25 seconds** to keep up with demand.*

> [!NOTE]
> **If Cycle Time > Takt Time:** The process is a bottleneck; WCM demands it be re-engineered.
> **If Cycle Time < Takt Time:** There is excess capacity; WCM may recommend demand aggregation or value-added improvements.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TQM vs JIT vs TPM vs Six Sigma

| Framework | Origin | Focus | Key Tool | KTU Exam Hook |
|-----------|--------|-------|----------|---------------|
| **TQM** (Total Quality Management) | Deming, Juran (1950s) | Customer satisfaction through quality at every level | PDCA cycle, SPC, QCC | "Continuous improvement philosophy" |
| **JIT** (Just-in-Time) | Toyota (1970s) | Eliminate waste via pull-based production | Kanban, Heijunka | "Zero inventory" |
| **TPM** (Total Productive Maintenance) | Nakajima (1971) | Maximize equipment effectiveness | OEE, Autonomous Maintenance | "Zero breakdowns" |
| **Six Sigma** | Motorola (1986) | Reduce process variation to 3.4 DPMO | DMAIC, DFSS | "Statistical quality" |
| **Lean Manufacturing** | Womack & Jones (1990) | Eliminate waste, add value | Value Stream Mapping | "Womack's 5 principles" |
| **5S** | Japan (Toyota) | Workplace organization | Sort-Set-Shine-Standardize-Sustain | "Foundation of WCM" |
| **Kaizen** | Japan (1985) | Continuous incremental improvement | PDCA, suggestion scheme | "Small steps, daily" |
| **BPR** (Business Process Reengineering) | Hammer & Champy (1993) | Radical redesign of processes | Process mapping | "Not continuous, radical" |

---

## 3.5 The WCM Implementation Roadmap (8-Step Sequential Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite Question:** "Explain the steps involved in implementing WCM in a manufacturing organization."

### Step 1: Top Management Commitment
- The CEO and senior leadership must sponsor the WCM journey
- Without executive commitment, WCM fails within 6 months

### Step 2: WCM Vision, Mission & Target Setting
- Define what "World Class" means for *your* industry
- Set SMART goals (e.g., "Achieve OEE > 85% in 3 years")

### Step 3: Baseline Assessment (Gap Analysis)
- Benchmark current performance against industry best
- Use Yamashina's 20-element assessment

### Step 4: Pilot Project Selection
- Choose one line, cell, or product family for pilot
- Pilot proves concept and builds success stories

### Step 5: Training & Skill Development
- Train *all* employees — from operators to managers
- Multi-skill matrix and certification

### Step 6: Tool Deployment (5S, Kaizen, TPM, TQM, JIT)
- Roll out tools in **stages** — not all at once
- 5S first, then TPM, then advanced tools

### Step 7: Performance Measurement & Review
- Daily shop-floor management (visual boards)
- Monthly reviews, quarterly audits

### Step 8: Standardization & Continuous Improvement
- Document best practices, sustain gains
- Move to next pilot area; expand WCM

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Set A]
**Q: Define World Class Manufacturing (WCM). List any four characteristics of a world class manufacturer.**

**Model Answer:**

World Class Manufacturing (WCM) is a holistic, integrated management philosophy aimed at achieving and sustaining world-class performance in **Quality, Cost, Delivery, Flexibility, and Safety** by adopting best-in-class manufacturing practices, advanced technology, and empowered human resources.

**Four Characteristics of a World Class Manufacturer:**

1. **Customer-Centricity:** Designs processes and products around the *Voice of Customer* (VOC). Quality is defined by the customer, not internal standards.
2. **Zero Defect Culture:** Uses *Poka-Yoke* (mistake-proofing) and *Statistical Process Control* to achieve defect-free production.
3. **Just-in-Time (JIT) Operations:** Maintains minimal inventory through pull-based production driven by real demand.
4. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Every employee, every day, identifies and eliminates small wastes. Improvement is relentless, not project-based.

> [!NOTE]
> *Additional valid characteristics for full marks: agility, multi-skilled workforce, total productive maintenance, supplier partnerships, benchmarking against global best, sustainable and green practices.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set B]
**Q: Explain the concept of "Just-in-Time" (JIT) in the context of WCM. State any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**Just-in-Time (JIT)** is a WCM philosophy pioneered by the Toyota Production System that ensures every component, sub-assembly, and finished product is **produced or delivered exactly when needed, in the exact quantity needed, and in the exact configuration needed** — no earlier, no later, no excess.

**Underlying Logic of JIT:**
- Production is **pulled** by actual customer demand, not pushed by forecasts
- A downstream process signals the upstream process via a **Kanban** (signal card) only when inventory falls below a threshold
- This creates a self-regulating production system

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Drastic Reduction in Inventory Carrying Cost:** Warehouse space, capital blocked in WIP, and obsolescence risks are minimized. *Example:* Toyota's inventory turns > 50 times/year vs the industry average of 6–8.
2. **Improved Quality and Faster Lead Time:** Smaller batches expose defects earlier (Jidoka), and reduced queue time shortens the order-to-delivery cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> *For 3-mark answers, two advantages with brief elaboration are sufficient. Additional advantages include: reduced material handling, lower scrap, simplified scheduling, better supplier relationships, and increased flexibility.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 4 contains a 14-mark question. The question paper typically provides an **internal choice** between two questions. Both choices are presented below as **Question A** and **Question B**, each split into 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified Pattern]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the concept of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) and describe its eight pillars. How does TPM contribute to World Class Manufacturing?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of TPM:**
Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) is a comprehensive, company-wide maintenance philosophy that aims to **maximize equipment effectiveness** by achieving **zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents**. Originally developed by **Seiichi Nakajima (1971)**, TPM shifts the maintenance responsibility from a specialized maintenance department to every operator, technician, and manager — making equipment care a shared, daily discipline.

**The 8 Pillars of TPM:**

| Sl. | Pillar | Objective |
|-----|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** | Operators take daily ownership of cleaning, lubricating, and inspecting their machines |
| 2 | **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen)** | Scheduled preventive maintenance based on equipment criticality |
| 3 | **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen)** | Zero-defect conditions maintained through equipment capability |
| 4 | **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)** | Small-group cross-functional projects targeting specific losses |
| 5 | **Early Equipment Management (EEM)** | New equipment designed for easy maintenance, low life-cycle cost |
| 6 | **Training and Education** | Multi-skill matrix; operators trained in maintenance basics |
| 7 | **Safety, Health, Environment (SHE)** | Zero accidents, zero industrial illness, zero environmental incidents |
| 8 | **Office TPM (Administrative TPM)** | Support functions (HR, Finance, Purchase) adopt TPM for information flow |

**Contribution of TPM to WCM:**

1. **Drives OEE to World-Class Levels (≥ 85%):** By attacking the *six big losses* (breakdown, setup, idling, reduced speed, defects, startup), TPM directly improves the Availability × Performance × Quality index.
2. **Empowers the Workforce:** TPM transforms operators from passive button-pushers into proactive equipment owners, fulfilling WCM's people-centric principle.
3. **Builds a Foundation for JIT:** JIT cannot operate with unreliable equipment. TPM makes the equipment reliable, which in turn makes pull-based production viable.
4. **Reduces Life-Cycle Cost:** Preventive and predictive maintenance cost a fraction of emergency repairs and lost production.

> [!NOTE]
> *For 7 marks, the definition + 8 pillars (in a table) + at least 2 elaborated contributions to WCM is sufficient. Memorize the Japanese terms — examiners give bonus marks for them.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: What is Total Quality Management (TQM)? List and briefly explain the contributions of any three quality gurus whose work shaped TQM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of TQM:**
Total Quality Management (TQM) is a management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving **all members of the organization** — from the CEO to the shop-floor operator — to **meet or exceed customer expectations**. TQM is built on the principle that quality is not an inspection function but a **systemic, cultural, and prevention-based** discipline.

**Three Quality Gurus and Their Contributions:**

**1. W. Edwards Deming (USA, 1900–1993)**
- **PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel):** Plan → Do → Check → Act — the foundation of continuous improvement
- **14 Points for Management:** A roadmap to transform Western management, emphasizing system thinking, constant improvement, and elimination of fear in the workplace
- **System of Profound Knowledge:** Appreciation of a system, knowledge of variation, theory of knowledge, and psychology
- *Impact on Japan:* Deming's lectures to Japanese engineers after WWII transformed Japanese industry and birthed the Kaizen culture

**2. Joseph M. Juran (USA, 1904–2008)**
- **Juran Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement
- **Pareto Principle (80/20):** Vital few problems cause most defects
- **Fitness-for-Use Definition:** Quality is the product's ability to meet customer needs
- *KTU Connection:* Juran's framework is still the most widely used structure for TQM implementation in Indian industry

**3. Philip B. Crosby (USA, 1926–2001)**
- **"Quality is Free":** The cost of prevention is always less than the cost of correction
- **Four Absolutes of Quality:** Definition (conformance to requirements), System (prevention), Standard (zero defects), Measurement (cost of non-conformance)
- **Quality Management Maturity Grid (QMMG):** A 5-stage model to assess and improve organizational quality maturity

**Additional Valid Guru (for extra credit):** *Kaoru Ishikawa* (Fishbone Diagram, Quality Circles), *Genichi Taguchi* (Taguchi Loss Function, Robust Design), *Armand Feigenbaum* (Total Quality Control — original concept), *Walter Shewhart* (SPC control charts — the father of statistical quality control).

> [!NOTE]
> *For 7 marks, definition + 3 gurus × (key idea + impact/application) is the optimal structure. Always use bullet points and tables for clarity.*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the concept of Benchmarking in WCM. Describe the different types of benchmarking with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Benchmarking:**
Benchmarking is the **systematic, continuous process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against the best performers in the industry or world**, with the goal of identifying performance gaps, learning best practices, and implementing improvements to achieve world-class performance.

Benchmarking was popularized by **Robert C. Camp of Xerox Corporation in 1989**. In the WCM framework, it is the **diagnostic tool** that tells the organization *where* it currently stands and *where* it needs to go.

**Types of Benchmarking:**

**1. Internal Benchmarking**
- Compares performance between *different units, departments, or plants* within the same organization
- *Example:* Comparing the OEE of Plant A vs Plant B of Tata Steel
- *Advantage:* Easy access to data, no confidentiality issues

**2. Competitive Benchmarking**
- Compares performance against *direct competitors* in the same industry
- *Example:* Maruti Suzuki benchmarking its Takt Time against Hyundai
- *Advantage:* Identifies direct competitive threats and opportunities
- *Challenge:* Data is hard to obtain due to trade secrecy

**3. Functional Benchmarking**
- Compares a *specific function or process* against the best in that function, regardless of industry
- *Example:* A hospital benchmarking its patient admission process against the check-in process of a five-star hotel
- *Advantage:* Cross-industry learning, often reveals breakthrough practices

**4. Generic Benchmarking**
- Compares *broad business processes* (e.g., order fulfillment, customer service) against any world's best performer
- *Example:* Amazon's order-fulfillment process being benchmarked by manufacturing companies
- *Advantage:* Access to truly world-class processes

**5. Strategic Benchmarking**
- Compares *how companies compete* (their strategic moves) — used during strategy formulation
- *Example:* Studying Tesla's direct-sales model against Ford's dealer-network model

> [!NOTE]
> *For 7 marks, the definition + a clear table of 4–5 types with examples and at least one advantage/disadvantage of each is the perfect structure.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: What is Lean Manufacturing? Explain the five principles of Lean as given by Womack and Jones.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Lean Manufacturing:**
Lean Manufacturing is a WCM philosophy that focuses on the **systematic elimination of waste (Muda)** in all forms and the creation of **value** for the end customer. Originating from the Toyota Production System and formalized by **James P. Womack and Daniel T. Jones** in their landmark book *"Lean Thinking"* (1996), Lean aims to deliver the right product, in the right quantity, with the right quality, at the right time, at the lowest cost.

**The Five Principles of Lean (Womack & Jones, 1996):**

**Principle 1: Value**
- Define value from the **customer's perspective**, not the manufacturer's
- Ask: *What does the customer actually want? What would they willingly pay for?*
- *Example:* In a mobile phone, the customer values the screen, battery, and camera — not the screws and internal wiring (which are *non-value-added*)

**Principle 2: Value Stream**
- Map every step of the process from raw material to customer
- Identify the **three types of activities:**
  - **Value-Added (VA):** Activities the customer is willing to pay for
  - **Business Non-Value-Added (BNVA):** Necessary but not valued (e.g., inspection)
  - **Non-Value-Added (NVA):** Pure waste (e.g., waiting, transport, defects)
- *Tool:* **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** — a visual flow diagram

**Principle 3: Flow**
- Make the value-added steps **flow continuously** without interruption
- Eliminate queues, batch processing, and bottlenecks
- *Example:* Restructuring a workshop into a U-shaped cell so that one unit flows from operation 1 to operation 10 without waiting

**Principle 4: Pull**
- Let the customer **pull** the product, not the manufacturer push
- Produce only what is demanded, only when demanded
- *Tool:* **Kanban** (Japanese for "signal card") — the upstream process produces only when the Kanban signals a need

**Principle 5: Perfection**
- The previous four principles enable a culture of **continuous improvement** where waste is repeatedly and relentlessly eliminated
- There is no "final state" — perfection is a journey
- *KTU Hook:* "Lean is not a destination; it is a journey"

> [!NOTE]
> *For 7 marks, definition + the 5 principles in a table with one-line explanation and example each is the KTU-preferred answer structure. The Womack & Jones book reference ("Lean Thinking, 1996") earns extra credit.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in WCM Questions — and How to Avoid Them**

| Sl. | Common Mistake | Marks Lost | Examiner's Advice |
|-----|----------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Defining WCM as "a tool" or "a software" | −2 marks | WCM is a **philosophy and integrated system**, not a tool |
| 2 | Confusing *Kaizen* (continuous) with *Kaikaku* (radical) | −1 mark | Memorize: Kaizen = small steps; Kaikaku = radical change (like BPR) |
| 3 | Forgetting the **Japanese terms** (Poka-Yoke, Kanban, Jidoka) | −1 to −2 marks | Always use the original Japanese term followed by English explanation |
| 4 | Listing WCM tools without showing **how they integrate** | −2 marks | Examiner wants to see the **interconnection** (e.g., TPM enables JIT) |
| 5 | Writing OEE formula as $A + P + Q$ instead of $A \times P \times Q$ | −2 marks | OEE is a **product, not a sum**. Loss of 2 marks is common. |
| 6 | Confusing **TPM pillars (8)** with **TQM principles** | −1 mark | TQM has 4–6 principles; TPM has exactly 8 pillars. Memorize the 8. |
| 7 | Omitting **units** in numerical problems (OEE in %, Takt in sec/unit) | −0.5 to −1 mark | Always write units next to the final answer |
| 8 | Not giving an **Indian example** in descriptive answers | −1 mark | KTU examiners reward regional relevance (Tata Steel, Maruti, BHEL, NTPC) |

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **WCM** = Holistic management philosophy, not a single tool. Origin: 1980s (MIT Sloan study of Toyota).
- [ ] **Yamashina Model:** 20 critical elements grouped into **10 pillars**.
- [ ] **Three Pillars of QCD:** Quality + Cost + Delivery. Some authors add **Morale** → QCDM.
- [ ] **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD):** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Over-production, Over-processing, Defects. (Add "Skills" for 8 Wastes.)
- [ ] **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (in %, not sum). World-class benchmark: **≥ 85%**.
- [ ] **Takt Time:** $Takt = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ (in seconds per unit). Must be > Cycle Time.
- [ ] **TPM** = Total Productive Maintenance. **8 Pillars** (memorize). Pioneer: Seiichi Nakajima (1971).
- [ ] **TQM** = Total Quality Management. Based on works of **Deming, Juran, Crosby** (the three primary gurus).
- [ ] **Deming Cycle:** PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act). Foundation of all continuous improvement.
- [ ] **JIT** = Just-in-Time. Pull-based, Kanban-driven. Toyota Production System.
- [ ] **5S:** Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain. The **foundation** of WCM. Without 5S, no other tool works.
- [ ] **Lean** = Womack & Jones (1996) — *Lean Thinking* book. **5 Principles:** Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection.
- [ ] **Six Sigma** = Motorola (1986). Target: **3.4 DPMO** (defects per million opportunities).
- [ ] **BPR** = Business Process Reengineering. **Hammer & Champy (1993).** Radical redesign, NOT continuous improvement.
- [ ] **Benchmarking** = Robert Camp (Xerox, 1989). **4 types:** Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic (and Strategic).
- [ ] **Indian Reference:** Tata Steel Jamshedpur = first Deming Prize winner from India (2012). NTPC, BHEL, Maruti are also WCM adopters.
- [ ] **WCM Implementation is Cyclical:** Plan → Deploy → Measure → Standardize → Re-plan. (Reflects PDCA.)
- [ ] **World-Class OEE Benchmark:** ≥ 85% (manufacturing). ≥ 90% (some Japanese standards).
- [ ] **Customer Focus:** Quality is defined by the **customer**, not by internal QA standards.
- [ ] **TPM + JIT + TQM = Core of WCM.** All three must coexist; TPM enables JIT; TQM supports both.
