The firm allows backorders. The desired service level is 95% ($z = 1.645$). Production rate is $P = 30{,}000$ units/year.

**(a) (7 marks)** Compute the optimal lot size, maximum inventory, and maximum backorder level for the **EPQ with backorders** model.

**(b) (7 marks)** Determine the **reorder point** and explain how the safety stock protects against lead-time uncertainty.

---

### Model Solution A (a) — EPQ with Backorders

**Step 1 — Apply the EPQ-with-backorders formula.**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DK}{h}\cdot\frac{P}{P-D}\cdot\frac{h+p}{p}}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250}{2}\cdot\frac{30{,}000}{30{,}000 - 12{,}000}\cdot\frac{2 + 8}{8}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each factor.**

$$\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250}{2} = 3{,}000{,}000$$

$$\frac{30{,}000}{30{,}000 - 12{,}000} = \frac{30{,}000}{18{,}000} = 1.6667$$

$$\frac{h+p}{p} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25$$

**Step 3 — Multiply and take the square root.**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{3{,}000{,}000 \times 1.6667 \times 1.25} = \sqrt{6{,}250{,}000} = 2500 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum inventory level.**

$$\hat I = Q^{*}\left(1 - \frac{D}{P}\right)\frac{p}{h+p} = 2500 \times (1 - 0.4) \times 0.8 = 2500 \times 0.48 = 1200 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum backorder level.**

$$\hat S = Q^{*}\cdot\frac{h}{h+p} = 2500 \times 0.2 = 500 \text{ units}$$

**Result (a):** $Q^{*} = 2500$ units, $\hat I = 1200$ units, $\hat S = 500$ units.

---

### Model Solution A (b) — Reorder Point & Safety Stock

**Step 1 — Mean lead-time demand.**

$$\mu_L = D \times L = 12{,}000 \times 0.04 = 480 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Safety stock.**

$$SS = z_\alpha \cdot \sigma_L = 1.645 \times 40 = 65.8 \approx 66 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Reorder point.**

$$R = \mu_L + SS = 480 + 66 = 546 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** When on-hand inventory falls to 546 units, place a replenishment order for 2500 units. The safety stock of 66 units absorbs normal lead-time variation, ensuring that the cycle service level stays at 95% — i.e., only 5% of cycles will experience a stock-out.

**Result (b):** $R = 546$ units; safety stock protects against the upper 5% tail of the lead-time demand distribution.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBIET304]`

**A pharmaceutical distributor faces seasonal demand for an anti-fever drug. The demand pattern over a 12-month year is approximated by $D(t) = 800 + 200\sin(2\pi t/12)$ units per month. Ordering cost $K = ₹500$ per order, holding cost $h = ₹3$ per unit per month. Lead time is 1 month and deterministic.**

**(a) (7 marks)** Apply the **piecewise variable-demand EOQ** technique by dividing the year into 4 quarters and computing the optimal lot size for each quarter.

**(b) (7 marks)** Compute the **reorder point** for each quarter and discuss why the reorder point varies across quarters.

---

### Model Solution B (a) — Piecewise Variable-Demand EOQ

**Step 1 — Compute average monthly demand in each quarter by integrating $D(t)$.**

For quarter $Q_j$ spanning months $t_1$ to $t_2$:

$$D_j = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2}\bigl[800 + 200\sin(2\pi t/12)\bigr]dt$$

**Step 2 — Quarter 1 (months 0–3):**

$$\int_0^3 800\,dt = 2400, \qquad \int_0^3 200\sin(\pi t/6)\,dt = 200 \cdot \left[-\frac{6}{\pi}\cos(\pi t/6)\right]_0^3$$

$$= -\frac{1200}{\pi}\bigl[\cos(\pi/2) - \cos(0)\bigr] = -\frac{1200}{\pi}(0 - 1) = \frac{1200}{\pi} \approx 381.97$$

$$D_1 = \frac{2400 + 381.97}{3} = \frac{2781.97}{3} \approx 927.32 \text{ units/month}$$

**Step 3 — Quarter 2 (months 3–6):**

By symmetry, $\int_3^6 \sin(\pi t/6)\,dt = \frac{1200}{\pi}[\cos(\pi/2) - \cos(\pi)] = \frac{1200}{\pi}(0 - (-1)) = \frac{1200}{\pi}$

Wait, recalculate: $\int_3^6 = -\frac{1200}{\pi}[\cos(\pi) - \cos(\pi/2)] = -\frac{1200}{\pi}(-1 - 0) = \frac{1200}{\pi} \approx 381.97$

$D_2 \approx 927.32$ units/month.

**Step 4 — Quarter 3 (months 6–9):**

$\int_6^9 \sin(\pi t/6)\,dt = -\frac{1200}{\pi}[\cos(3\pi/2) - \cos(\pi)] = -\frac{1200}{\pi}(0 - (-1)) = -\frac{1200}{\pi} \approx -381.97$

$D_3 = \frac{2400 - 381.97}{3} \approx 672.68$ units/month.

**Step 5 — Quarter 4 (months 9–12):**

$\int_9^{12} = -\frac{1200}{\pi}[\cos(2\pi) - \cos(3\pi/2)] = -\frac{1200}{\pi}(1 - 0) = -\frac{1200}{\pi}$

$D_4 \approx 672.68$ units/month.

**Step 6 — Quarter-by-quarter optimal lot size** $Q_j = \sqrt{2 D_j^{quarter} K / h}$, where $D_j^{quarter} = 3 D_j$ (total demand in the quarter).

- Q1: $D^{quarter} = 2781.97$; $Q_1 = \sqrt{2 \times 2781.97 \times 500 / 3} = \sqrt{927{,}323} \approx 963$ units
- Q2: $Q_2 \approx 963$ units
- Q3: $D^{quarter} = 2018.03$; $Q_3 = \sqrt{2 \times 2018.03 \times 500 / 3} = \sqrt{672{,}677} \approx 820$ units
- Q4: $Q_4 \approx 820$ units

**Result (a):** $Q_1 = Q_2 \approx 963$ units (peak season); $Q_3 = Q_4 \approx 820$ units (off-season). Notice the **17% reduction** in lot size off-season, which avoids overstocking.

---

### Model Solution B (b) — Variable Reorder Point

**Step 1 — Reorder point for a given period** is the demand expected over the lead time $L$:

$$R_j = D_j \times L$$

With $L = 1$ month, $R_j$ equals the average monthly demand in that quarter.

- $R_1 = R_2 \approx 927$ units
- $R_3 = R_4 \approx 673$ units

**Step 2 — Why the reorder point varies:** Demand is non-stationary; it rises in summer (fever season) and falls in winter. Holding a fixed $R$ would either over-stock in Q3–Q4 (tying up capital) or under-stock in Q1–Q2 (causing lost sales). Variable $R$ aligns inventory policy with demand pattern.

**Result (b):** $R_{peak} \approx 927$ units, $R_{off} \approx 673$ units. Variable reorder point is essential when demand is time-dependent.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — KTU 2024 valuation key observations:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks):** Mixing per-month $h$ with per-year $D$. Always normalize all parameters to the **same time unit** before plugging into the formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the production-rate factor in EPQ (–2 marks):** When the question states a finite production rate, the optimal lot size **must** include the $\sqrt{P/(P-D)}$ multiplier. Writing the bare Wilson formula is a common slip.
> 3. **Confusing cycle service level with fill rate (–1 mark):** If the question says "95% of cycles should not stock out," use $z = 1.645$. If it says "95% of demand should be met immediately," use the fill-rate formula — different $z$ value.
> 4. **Variable demand handled as constant (–3 marks):** A question with $D(t) = a + bt$ must be tackled by integrating over the lead time $R = \int_0^L D(t)\,dt$, not by plugging $D(0)$.
> 5. **No second-order check (–1 mark):** KTU examiners award a mark for confirming the second derivative is positive, proving that the critical point is a minimum.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 Project):**
> - **Wilson EOQ** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DK/h}$: assumptions are constant demand, infinite replenishment, zero lead time, no shortages.
> - **EOQ with backorders** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DK/h} \cdot \sqrt{(h+p)/p}$: maximum backorder $\hat S = Q^* \cdot h/(h+p)$.
> - **EPQ / EBQ** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DK/h \cdot P/(P-D)}$: peak inventory $\hat I = Q^*(1 - D/P)$; collapses to classical EOQ as $P \to \infty$.
> - **EPQ with backorders**: multiply the EPQ formula by $\sqrt{(h+p)/p}$.
> - **Reorder point with safety stock** $R = \bar d L + z_\alpha \sigma_L$; $z_{0.95} = 1.645$, $z_{0.975} = 1.96$, $z_{0.99} = 2.33$.
> - **Variable demand** $D(t)$: integrate over the lead time and use piecewise EOQ in sub-intervals.
> - **Total cost minimum** is always $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2DKh}$ (classical) or its equivalent in richer models — a quick sanity check.
> - **Second-order condition** $d^2(TC)/dQ^2 > 0$ must always be verified.
> - **Project workflow**: parametric input → analytical solution → numerical validation (e.g., grid search) → sensitivity analysis → executive summary with plots.
> - **Industry benchmarks**: typical service levels are 90–95%; typical $h$ is 20–30% of unit cost per year; $p$ is usually 2–5× $h$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Suggestion: Optimization of Inventory Costs with Quantity Discounts - Create an Optimization Algorithm Using Excel Solver, Python, or MATLAB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Suggestion: Optimization of Inventory Costs with Quantity Discounts - Create an Optimization Algorithm Using Excel Solver, Python, or MATLAB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Economic Order Quantity with Quantity Discounts (EOQ-QD)** is an extension of the classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model used in inventory management to determine the **optimal order quantity** that **minimizes the total annual inventory cost** when a supplier offers **tiered price reductions** (discounts) based on the volume of each individual order placed.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this is formally classified under **Independent Demand Inventory Models** and forms a high-weightage topic in Module 4 (Inventory Management) of PBIET304 - Operations Management.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Quantity discount is a pricing strategy where the per-unit purchase cost of an item decreases as the order quantity increases. The EOQ model with quantity discounts combines the classical EOQ formula with a step-wise price break schedule to determine the cost-minimizing order quantity.*

### 1.2 The Four Key Cost Components

In any inventory system, the **Total Annual Inventory Cost (TC)** is the sum of four distinct cost streams:

1. **Purchasing Cost (PC)** → Money paid to the supplier for the goods.
2. **Ordering Cost (OC)** → Fixed cost incurred per order (paperwork, transport setup, inspection).
3. **Holding (Carrying) Cost (HC)** → Cost of keeping inventory in storage (rent, insurance, capital tied up, spoilage).
4. **Shortage (Stock-out) Cost (SC)** → Penalty of running out of stock (lost sales, backorders).

For the EOQ with quantity discounts, the **Shortage Cost is assumed to be zero** (no stock-outs allowed), and the optimization focuses on minimizing the sum of the first three.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Quantity Discount Trade-off:**
> Ordering in **large quantities** reduces **purchasing cost** (cheaper per unit) and **ordering cost** (fewer orders per year) BUT **increases holding cost** (more average inventory in stock). The optimization algorithm finds the perfect balance point.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bulk Grocery Store" Intuition

Imagine you are buying rice for a small hostel mess. The local kirana shop offers:
- Up to 9 kg → ₹60/kg
- 10 to 49 kg → ₹55/kg
- 50 kg or more → ₹50/kg

If you buy only 1 kg at a time, you pay the full ₹60/kg and make 365 trips to the shop a year (lots of travel time and effort). If you buy the entire year's stock of 365 kg in one shot, you get the cheapest rate of ₹50/kg BUT you need a huge godown to store it (high rent and risk of spoilage).

**The optimization problem is asking: at what order quantity does the money you save on bulk discounts EXACTLY equal the extra money you spend on storage?**

This is the famous **economic trade-off** at the heart of operations management.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Real-World Insight:** Almost every industry — from auto-parts procurement by Maruti Suzuki, to pharma inventory at Apollo Pharmacy, to textile stocking at Myntra warehouses — uses some form of quantity-discount optimization. It is a **multi-crore rupee decision** in large supply chains.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Basic EOQ Model (Foundation)

The classical **EOQ (also called the Wilson Lot-Size Model)** is derived by minimizing the sum of **ordering cost** and **holding cost**, assuming the unit purchasing cost is **constant**.

Let:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $C$ = Unit purchasing cost (₹/unit)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units)
- $i$ = Holding cost as a fraction of unit cost (e.g., 0.20 = 20%)

**Average Inventory** = $\frac{Q}{2}$ (since inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$)

**Number of orders per year** = $\frac{D}{Q}$

The total annual cost is:

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{D \cdot C}_{\text{Purchase}} + \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding}}$$

To find the optimum, take the derivative with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

Solving for $Q$ gives the **EOQ formula**:

$$\boxed{EOQ = Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}}$$

At this optimal $Q^*$, the **minimum total variable cost** is:

$$TC_{min} = D \cdot C + \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot S \cdot H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $EOQ$ is called the "Economic" Order Quantity:**
> At $Q^*$, the **annual ordering cost EXACTLY equals the annual holding cost**.
> Proof: $\frac{D}{Q^*} \cdot S = \frac{Q^*}{2} \cdot H = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot S \cdot H}{2}}$
> This is a **favourite 2-mark KTU question.**

### 2.2 Extension: EOQ with Quantity Discounts

When a supplier offers **discounts**, the unit cost $C$ becomes a **step function** of $Q$. There are two common discount structures:

| Discount Type | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **All-Units Discount** | If order size ≥ threshold, the discount applies to **ALL** units in that order. | **Most common in KTU papers** |
| **Incremental Discount** | Discount applies **only to the units above** each threshold. | Less frequent, appears in Part B questions |

In the **all-units discount** case (which is the KTU standard), if the price schedule is $C_1 > C_2 > C_3$ for ranges $0$ to $Q_1$, $Q_1+1$ to $Q_2$, and $Q_2+1$ to $\infty$ respectively, then:

$$H_j = i \cdot C_j \quad \text{(holding cost changes with the discount level)}$$

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}$ | Classical EOQ (no discount) | units |
| 2 | $TC(Q) = D C + \frac{D}{Q} S + \frac{Q}{2} H$ | Total annual cost | ₹/year |
| 3 | $H = i \cdot C$ | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹/unit/year |
| 4 | $H_j = i \cdot C_j$ | Holding cost at price level $j$ | ₹/unit/year |
| 5 | $EOQ_j = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H_j}}$ | EOQ computed at price $C_j$ | units |
| 6 | $N = \frac{D}{Q}$ | Number of orders per year | orders/year |
| 7 | $T = \frac{Q}{D}$ | Cycle time (time between orders) | years |
| 8 | $Avg. Inventory = \frac{Q}{2}$ | Average stock level | units |
| 9 | $TCV = \frac{D}{Q} S + \frac{Q}{2} H$ | Total Variable Cost (excludes purchase cost) | ₹/year |
| 10 | $\Delta TC = TC_{\text{new}} - TC_{\text{old}}$ | Cost change between two candidates | ₹/year |

> [!CAUTION]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for KTU exams:**
> In the exam hall, **never** write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ inside a table. Use the form "$\mid x \mid$" or simply write the equation in sentence form to avoid scribal errors.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

This optimization framework is foundational in:

- **Supply Chain Management (SCM):** Procurement decisions in manufacturing firms (e.g., Tata Motors ordering steel).
- **ERP Systems:** SAP, Oracle, and Tally modules use these formulas for **automatic reorder point generation**.
- **E-Commerce Inventory:** Amazon, Flipkart use variants of this model for FBA (Fulfilment by Amazon) warehouse stocking.
- **Project Management:** Determining procurement batches for construction projects (cement, steel, tiles).
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Avoiding drug shortages while minimizing expiry-loss costs.
- **Production Planning:** Setting batch sizes in process industries (chemicals, paints, food processing).

The "Project Suggestion" in your KTU module typically asks students to **formulate this as a formal optimization problem** that can be solved by either:
- Spreadsheet Solver (Excel Solver / Google Sheets Solver) — the recommended approach for non-CS students.
- Mathematical software (used conceptually) — understanding the **objective function**, **decision variables**, and **constraints**.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024 Scheme, the project is assessed on the mathematical formulation and result interpretation — not on the actual coding.** A well-presented Excel sheet with formulas, decision cells, and solver output is fully sufficient.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Complete KTU Algorithm for EOQ with Quantity Discounts

This is the **canonical step-by-step methodology** (sometimes called the "Dijkstra-style price break algorithm") that the KTU examiner expects you to write out in full marks answers.

**Setup:** Given demand $D$, ordering cost $S$, holding cost rate $i$, and a price schedule with $n$ price breaks.

---

### **STEP 1 — List the price schedule in ascending order of quantity**

| Price Level $j$ | Range of $Q$ | Unit Cost $C_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0 \leq Q < Q_1$ | $C_1$ |
| 2 | $Q_1 \leq Q < Q_2$ | $C_2$ |
| 3 | $Q_2 \leq Q$ | $C_3$ |

Generally, $C_1 > C_2 > C_3$ and $Q_1 < Q_2$.

### **STEP 2 — For each price level, compute the holding cost**

$$H_j = i \cdot C_j$$

### **STEP 3 — Compute the EOQ at each price level**

$$EOQ_j = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H_j}}$$

### **STEP 4 — Feasibility Check (THE MOST IMPORTANT STEP)**

For each $j$, check whether the computed $EOQ_j$ falls within the feasible range of price level $j$.

- If **YES** (i.e., $Q_{j-1} \leq EOQ_j < Q_j$) → the $EOQ_j$ is a **valid candidate**.
- If **NO** (i.e., $EOQ_j$ is below the lower bound of that price break) → the $EOQ_j$ is **infeasible**. The candidate for that price level becomes $Q_j^{\text{cand}} = Q_{j-1}$ (the minimum quantity that qualifies for the discount).

### **STEP 5 — Compute Total Cost at all candidate quantities**

$$TC(Q) = D \cdot C_j + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H_j$$

### **STEP 6 — Select the candidate with the lowest $TC$**

That $Q^*$ is the **optimal order quantity**.

---

### 3.2 Fully Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Problem Statement:**
> A retail outlet purchases a product with annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. The ordering cost is $S = ₹200$ per order, and the holding cost is $i = 20\%$ of the unit cost per year. The supplier offers the following all-units quantity discount schedule:
>
> | Order Quantity $Q$ | Unit Cost $C$ |
> |---|---|
> | $0 \leq Q < 1000$ | ₹50 |
> | $1000 \leq Q < 2000$ | ₹48 |
> | $2000 \leq Q$ | ₹45 |
>
> Determine the optimal order quantity and the minimum total annual cost.

---

### **STEP 1: Price Schedule**

$$
C_1 = ₹50, \quad C_2 = ₹48, \quad C_3 = ₹45
$$

### **STEP 2: Holding Cost at Each Level**

$$
H_1 = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10 \text{ / unit / year}
$$
$$
H_2 = 0.20 \times 48 = ₹9.60 \text{ / unit / year}
$$
$$
H_3 = 0.20 \times 45 = ₹9.00 \text{ / unit / year}
$$

### **STEP 3: Compute EOQ at Each Level**

**At $C_1 = ₹50$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOQ_1 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 200}{10}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{10}} \\
&= \sqrt{400{,}000} \\
&\approx 632.46 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At $C_2 = ₹48$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOQ_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 200}{9.60}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{9.60}} \\
&= \sqrt{416{,}666.67} \\
&\approx 645.50 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At $C_3 = ₹45$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOQ_3 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 200}{9.00}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{9.00}} \\
&= \sqrt{444{,}444.44} \\
&\approx 666.67 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **STEP 4: Feasibility Check**

| Price Level | Feasible Range | Computed $EOQ$ | Feasible? | Candidate $Q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0 \leq Q < 1000$ | $632.46$ | ✅ YES | $632.46$ |
| 2 | $1000 \leq Q < 2000$ | $645.50$ | ❌ NO (below 1000) | $1000$ |
| 3 | $2000 \leq Q$ | $666.67$ | ❌ NO (below 2000) | $2000$ |

### **STEP 5: Compute Total Cost at Each Candidate**

The total cost formula is:

$$TC(Q) = D \cdot C_j + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H_j$$

**Candidate 1: $Q = 632.46$, $C_1 = ₹50$, $H_1 = ₹10$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_1 &= 10000 \times 50 + \frac{10000}{632.46} \times 200 + \frac{632.46}{2} \times 10 \\
&= 500000 + 3162.28 + 3162.28 \\
&\approx ₹506{,}324.56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Candidate 2: $Q = 1000$, $C_2 = ₹48$, $H_2 = ₹9.60$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_2 &= 10000 \times 48 + \frac{10000}{1000} \times 200 + \frac{1000}{2} \times 9.60 \\
&= 480000 + 2000 + 4800 \\
&= ₹486{,}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Candidate 3: $Q = 2000$, $C_3 = ₹45$, $H_3 = ₹9.00$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_3 &= 10000 \times 45 + \frac{10000}{2000} \times 200 + \frac{2000}{2} \times 9.00 \\
&= 450000 + 1000 + 9000 \\
&= ₹460{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### **STEP 6: Select the Minimum**

$$
TC_3 = ₹460{,}000 \quad \text{(Lowest)}
$$

### **3.3 FINAL ANSWER**

$$
\boxed{Q^* = 2000 \text{ units}, \quad TC_{min} = ₹4{,}60{,}000 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although the EOQ formula gives a smaller order size of ~633 units, the **bulk discount at 2000 units saves ₹50,000 in purchase cost** which more than offsets the higher holding cost. Hence, the company should order in batches of **2000 units**, placing $D/Q^* = 5$ orders per year (one every ~10 weeks).

---

### 3.4 Comparison Table (for Answer Writing)

| Candidate $Q$ | Purchase Cost | Ordering Cost | Holding Cost | **Total Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 632.46 | 5,00,000 | 3,162 | 3,162 | **5,06,324** |
| 1000 | 4,80,000 | 2,000 | 4,800 | **4,86,800** |
| 2000 | 4,50,000 | 1,000 | 9,000 | **4,60,000** ⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice how ordering cost + holding cost is always minimized near the EOQ, but the **purchase cost** (which is usually 90% of total cost) is the dominant factor. This is why **quantity discounts are so commercially powerful** — they can save a company lakhs of rupees annually.

---

### 3.5 Optimization Problem Formulation (For the Project Suggestion)

For a student building this as a formal optimization project, the standard mathematical formulation is:

**Decision Variable:** $Q$ (the order quantity in units)

**Objective Function (Minimize):**

$$
\text{Minimize } TC(Q) = D \cdot C(Q) + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H(Q)
$$

where $C(Q)$ and $H(Q) = i \cdot C(Q)$ are step-functions defined by the price schedule.

**Constraints:**

$$
Q \geq 0, \quad D > 0, \quad S > 0, \quad 0 < i < 1
$$

**Solution Method:** Exhaustive evaluation over each price break (as shown above), or use of a numerical solver where the price schedule is encoded as a lookup table.

> [!TIP]
> **In Excel Solver:** Set $Q$ as the changing cell, $TC(Q)$ as the objective cell (set to Min), and add a constraint $Q \geq 1$ (or $Q \geq$ first price break). Solver then finds the global minimum, automatically handling the price break discontinuities.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Decision Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define D, S, i and Price Schedule] --> B[Step 1: Sort price breaks in ascending order of Q]
    B --> C[Step 2: For each price level j, compute H sub j = i times C sub j]
    C --> D[Step 3: For each j, compute EOQ sub j using formula]
    D --> E[Step 4: Is EOQ sub j feasible in the range of price level j?]
    E -- Yes --> F[Use EOQ sub j as the candidate for level j]
    E -- No --> G[Set candidate equal to the minimum Q that qualifies for level j]
    F --> H[Step 5: Compute Total Cost TC at all candidates]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 6: Compare all TC values and pick the minimum]
    I --> J[Output: Optimal Q star and minimum Total Cost]
    J --> K[End]
```

### 4.2 Cost-vs-Quantity Schematic (Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the **shape of the Total Cost curve** with a quantity discount schedule. The curve has discontinuities (jumps downward) at each price break because the unit cost drops, reducing the total cost.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RegionA[Region 1: Small Orders, C=50]
        A1[Q from 0 to 1000]
        A1 --> A2[TC curve is U-shaped with minimum near EOQ=632]
    end
    subgraph RegionB[Region 2: Medium Orders, C=48]
        B1[Q from 1000 to 2000]
        B1 --> B2[TC curve has lower minimum, but at Q=1000 the curve has a downward jump from discount]
    end
    subgraph RegionC[Region 3: Large Orders, C=45]
        C1[Q from 2000 onwards]
        C1 --> C2[TC curve has lowest minimum at EOQ=666, but optimal lies at Q=2000 due to discount]
    end
    A2 -.Price Break.-> B2
    B2 -.Price Break.-> C2
    C2 --> D[Optimal Point Q star equals 2000 units, TC equals 460000]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of the Optimization Algorithm (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Input Module: D, S, i, Price Table] --> Pre[Preprocessor: Sort and Validate Price Breaks]
    Pre --> LoopIter[For each price level j in 1 to n]
    LoopIter --> HCalc[Compute H sub j = i times C sub j]
    HCalc --> EOQCalc[Compute EOQ sub j = sqrt of 2DS over H sub j]
    EOQCalc --> FeasCheck{Feasibility Check: EOQ sub j in range j?}
    FeasCheck -- Feasible --> CandAssign1[Assign Candidate Q sub j = EOQ sub j]
    FeasCheck -- Infeasible --> CandAssign2[Assign Candidate Q sub j = Lower Bound of Range j]
    CandAssign1 --> TCCalc
    CandAssign2 --> TCCalc[Compute TC at Candidate Q sub j]
    TCCalc --> Agg[Aggregator: Store all candidates and TC values]
    Agg --> Decision[Decision Engine: Select Q star with minimum TC]
    Decision --> Out[Output: Q star, TC min, Number of orders per year]
```

### 4.4 Cost Component Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PC[Purchase Cost D times C of Q] -- "Decreases as Q increases" --> TC[Total Cost]
    OC[Ordering Cost D times S over Q] -- "Decreases as Q increases" --> TC
    HC[Holding Cost Q times H over 2] -- "Increases as Q increases" --> TC
    SC[Shortage Cost - assumed zero] -.-> TC
    TC --> Min[Find minimum TC]
    Min --> Qstar[Optimal Q star]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram is exam-relevant:**
> This schematic directly answers a common KTU conceptual question: *"Why does total inventory cost first decrease and then increase as order quantity grows?"* The answer lies in the **opposing behaviour of ordering cost (decreasing) and holding cost (increasing)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). State the assumptions of the basic EOQ model.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) is the fixed order quantity that minimizes the **total annual inventory cost** (the sum of ordering, holding, and purchasing costs), assuming constant demand and fixed unit price.

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

**Assumptions (1.5 Marks):**
1. Demand $D$ is known, constant, and independent.
2. Lead time is known and constant.
3. Order is received instantaneously (infinite replenishment rate).
4. No quantity discounts — unit cost $C$ is fixed.
5. Ordering cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are constant.
6. Only ordering and holding costs are variable; purchase cost is constant.
7. No stock-outs are permitted.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between "All-Units Discount" and "Incremental Discount" in quantity discount models.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | All-Units Discount | Incremental Discount |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Discount applies to **all units** in the order once the threshold is crossed. | Discount applies **only to units exceeding** each threshold. |
| **Effective Unit Cost** | Drops to $C_j$ for the entire batch. | Weighted average across price tiers. |
| **Buyer Behaviour** | Encourages **bigger single orders** to maximize savings. | Allows **mixed-size orders** without over-stocking. |
| **Supplier Benefit** | Pushes large volume per transaction. | Maintains regular ordering patterns. |
| **KTU Difficulty** | Most common; standard EOQ-QD method applies. | Requires careful tier-wise cost calculation. |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]**

> A manufacturing firm uses **12,000 units** of a raw material annually. The cost of placing one order is **₹300**, and the holding cost is **25% of the unit cost per year**. The supplier offers the following all-units quantity discount:
>
> | Order Quantity (units) | Unit Price (₹) |
> |---|---|
> | Less than 500 | 40 |
> | 500 to 999 | 38 |
> | 1000 and above | 35 |
>
> **(a)** Determine the **EOQ** and the corresponding **total cost** if there were no quantity discount. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the **optimal order quantity with quantity discount** and the **minimum total annual inventory cost**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — No Discount (7 Marks)**

Using the lowest unit cost (since no discount case) — or more strictly, the unit cost is constant.

Let $C = ₹40$, $S = ₹300$, $D = 12000$, $i = 0.25$.

$$
H = 0.25 \times 40 = ₹10 \text{ per unit per year}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOQ &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 300}{10}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{7{,}200{,}000}{10}} \\
&= \sqrt{720{,}000} \\
&\approx 848.53 \text{ units (round to 849 units)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Cost at EOQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= D \cdot C + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H \\
&= 12000 \times 40 + \frac{12000}{848.53} \times 300 + \frac{848.53}{2} \times 10 \\
&= 480000 + 4242.64 + 4242.64 \\
&\approx ₹4{,}88{,}485.28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (a):** $EOQ \approx 849$ units, $TC \approx ₹4{,}88{,}485$ per year.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — With Quantity Discount (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute holding cost at each level**

$$
H_1 = 0.25 \times 40 = ₹10, \quad H_2 = 0.25 \times 38 = ₹9.50, \quad H_3 = 0.25 \times 35 = ₹8.75
$$

**Step 2: Compute EOQ at each level**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EOQ_1 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 300}{10}} = \sqrt{720000} \approx 848.53 \text{ units} \\
EOQ_2 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 300}{9.50}} = \sqrt{757894.74} \approx 870.57 \text{ units} \\
EOQ_3 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 300}{8.75}} = \sqrt{822857.14} \approx 907.12 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Feasibility Check**

| Level | Feasible Range | $EOQ_j$ | Feasible? | Candidate $Q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0 \leq Q < 500$ | $848.53$ | ❌ No | $500$ |
| 2 | $500 \leq Q < 1000$ | $870.57$ | ✅ Yes | $870.57$ |
| 3 | $Q \geq 1000$ | $907.12$ | ❌ No | $1000$ |

**Step 4: Compute Total Cost at each candidate**

**Candidate 1: $Q = 500$ at $C_1 = ₹40$:**

$$
TC_1 = 12000 \times 40 + \frac{12000}{500} \times 300 + \frac{500}{2} \times 10 = 480000 + 7200 + 2500 = ₹4{,}89{,}700
$$

**Candidate 2: $Q = 870.57$ at $C_2 = ₹38$:**

$$
TC_2 = 12000 \times 38 + \frac{12000}{870.57} \times 300 + \frac{870.57}{2} \times 9.50
$$
$$
= 456000 + 4135.90 + 4135.20 = ₹4{,}64{,}271.10
$$

**Candidate 3: $Q = 1000$ at $C_3 = ₹35$:**

$$
TC_3 = 12000 \times 35 + \frac{12000}{1000} \times 300 + \frac{1000}{2} \times 8.75
$$
$$
= 420000 + 3600 + 4375 = ₹4{,}27{,}975
$$

**Step 5: Select the minimum**

$TC_3 = ₹4{,}27{,}975$ is the lowest.

**Result (b):** $\boxed{Q^* = 1000 \text{ units}, \quad TC_{min} = ₹4{,}27{,}975 \text{ per year}}$

**Savings over no-discount case** $= 488485 - 427975 = ₹60{,}510$ per year.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper, Module 4]**

> A company has an annual demand of **6,000 units** of a particular item. The ordering cost is **₹150 per order** and the inventory carrying cost is **₹3 per unit per year**. The supplier gives the following quantity discounts:
>
> | Order Size (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
> |---|---|
> | $0$ to $499$ | $20$ |
> | $500$ to $999$ | $18$ |
> | $1000$ and above | $16$ |
>
> **(a)** Compute the **classical EOQ** and the corresponding **total annual cost**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using the quantity discount model, determine the **optimal order quantity** and the **minimum total cost**. Also calculate the **savings achieved** by using the quantity discount model. **(7 Marks)**

*(Self-practice — Solve using the same 6-step algorithm shown above. The candidate Q values will be approximately 774 units, 1000 units, and 6000/EOQ at $C_3$. Students should obtain a final answer of $Q^* = 1000$ units with $TC_{min} \approx ₹1{,}02{,}900$.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to update $H$ at each price level.** Many students use a single $H$ value for all price breaks. This is WRONG. Holding cost $H_j = i \cdot C_j$ changes with the unit cost. **[-2 marks]**
>
> 2. **Ignoring the feasibility check.** If $EOQ$ falls in a lower price range, you MUST adjust $Q$ upward to the minimum qualifying quantity. Using the infeasible $EOQ$ gives incorrect (and lower) cost, leading to wrong optimal selection. **[-3 marks]**
>
> 3. **Confusing Total Cost (TC) with Total Variable Cost (TCV).** TC includes the purchase cost $D \cdot C$; TCV excludes it. KTU problems always ask for TC, not TCV. **[-2 marks]**
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always state "₹ per year" for $TC$ and "units" for $Q$. **[-1 mark]**
>
> 5. **Using the wrong discount structure.** If the problem says "incremental discount," the all-units method is incorrect. Read the problem carefully. **[-2 to -4 marks]**
>
> 6. **Skipping the comparison table.** Examiners give **1–2 extra marks** for a clean candidate-comparison table. Always include it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core formula (no discount):** $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ — derived by equating $dTC/dQ = 0$.
- **Key insight:** At EOQ, **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** (a classic viva question).
- **Quantity discount adds complexity:** $H$ becomes price-dependent: $H_j = i \cdot C_j$.
- **Six-step KTU algorithm:** Sort → Compute $H$ → Compute $EOQ$ → Feasibility Check → Compute $TC$ → Pick minimum.
- **Feasibility check is mandatory:** If $EOQ$ falls below a price break, snap to the lower bound of that price break.
- **Purchase cost is dominant:** It usually accounts for 85–95% of total cost — so bulk discounts save substantially.
- **Common KTU numerical ranges:** $D$ in thousands of units, $S$ in hundreds of rupees, $i$ between 10% and 30%, price breaks typically 2 or 3 levels.
- **Project submission tip:** Present the problem as a clear optimization formulation with **decision variable**, **objective function**, and **constraints**. Even a hand-solved table is acceptable; a Solver-backed Excel sheet is the gold standard.
- **For incremental discounts:** Break each candidate $Q$ into tier-segments and compute a **weighted average unit cost** for that batch.
- **Sensitivity analysis bonus marks:** Show how the optimal $Q^*$ changes if $D$ increases by 10% or $S$ doubles — this impresses examiners.
- **KTU CO mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO4** of PBIET304 — *"Apply inventory models to derive optimal order quantities under various cost conditions."*
- **Bloom's Taxonomy level:** **Apply (Level 3)** and **Analyse (Level 4)** — students must execute the algorithm and interpret results.

> **END OF NOTES — PBIET304 MODULE 4 | EOQ with Quantity Discounts**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Suggestion: MPS Optimization for a Manufacturing Firm - Design an Optimized Master Production Schedule Using Linear Decision Rules and Software Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Suggestion: MPS Optimization for a Manufacturing Firm - Design an Optimized Master Production Schedule Using Linear Decision Rules and Software Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a detailed time-phased plan that specifies *what finished products* are to be produced, *in what quantities*, and *when* (by due date / period) to satisfy forecasted demand while respecting capacity, inventory, and workforce constraints. The MPS serves as the **bridge between aggregate planning and shop-floor execution (MRP / Shop Floor Control)**.

**Linear Decision Rules (LDR)** refer to a class of mathematical optimization techniques — originally formalized by **Holt, Modigliani, Muth, and Simon (HMMM, 1960)** — that produce a *linear* decision function relating optimal production and workforce levels to demand forecasts and current inventory state. The objective is to **minimize a quadratic aggregate cost function** comprising production, holding, hiring/firing, and overtime/idle-time costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** "LDR assumes a quadratic cost structure and yields optimal workforce and production decisions as *linear functions* of demand forecasts and inventory state — a closed-form, deterministic alternative to simulation-based aggregate planning."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bakery That Plans Tomorrow's Bread

Imagine you own a bakery. Each morning you must decide **how many workers to call in (W)** and **how much bread to bake (P)**. If you bake too little, you lose sales (penalty). If you bake too much, unsold bread goes stale (holding cost). If you hire/fire people every day, there's a morale and training cost. Your *forecasts* of next week's festival demand, school orders, and weather matter.

**LDR** is like the bakery owner's *smart rule-of-thumb notebook* — it says, *"Look at the demand forecast and current stock, then apply a fixed linear formula to set today's workers and oven cycles such that the long-run total cost is minimum."* You don't have to recompute every day; the **rule is fixed**, only the *inputs* (forecasts, current inventory) change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping for KTU Project Work:**
> - **W_t (Workforce decision)** → Number of production-line workers assigned in week *t*
> - **P_t (Production decision)** → Units of finished good to produce in week *t*
> - **I_t (Inventory state)** → Closing stock at the end of week *t*
> - **F_t (Forecast)** → Market demand forecast for week *t*

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

| Metric | Standard Value/Notation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **MPS Frozen Horizon** | **4 – 6 weeks** | Period during which MPS cannot be altered without cost penalty |
| **Planning Horizon (T)** | **6 – 18 months** | Typical LDR aggregate planning window |
| **MPS Time Fence** | **2 – 4 weeks** | Boundary between firm and flexible orders |
| **LDR Optimality Condition** | **Quadratic cost function** | Guarantees a closed-form linear solution |
| **Search Decision Rules (SDR)** | **Linear cost function** | Counterpart; LDR is preferred for quadratic costs |
| **Common Software Tools** | **SAP PP, Oracle SCM, MS Dynamics, Arena, ProModel** | Industrial-grade MPS execution |

### 1.4 Project Goal Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Aim:** *Design an Optimized Master Production Schedule for a hypothetical/sampled manufacturing firm using Linear Decision Rules (LDR), validate the schedule through software simulation (Arena / Excel Solver), and benchmark it against a Chase and Level strategy baseline.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Production Schedule — Architectural Role

The MPS occupies the critical middle layer of the **Production Planning Hierarchy**:

1. **Aggregate Production Plan (APP)** — high-level, product-family, 6–18 months
2. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — discrete end-items, weekly buckets ← *(our focus)*
3. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** — components and raw materials
4. **Shop Floor Scheduling (SFC)** — operations on machines

The MPS must satisfy three simultaneous constraints:
- **Demand satisfaction** (customer orders + forecast)
- **Capacity feasibility** (labor hours, machine hours)
- **Inventory policy** (safety stock, lot-sizing rules)

### 2.2 The LDR Cost Structure (Why Quadratic?)

LDR assumes a **quadratic cost function** because the second derivative of cost (the *marginal* cost) is *linear* in the decision variable — implying the firm experiences **diminishing returns** to scaling workforce or production. The total cost minimized over the horizon $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$ is:

$$
C = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ c_t P_t + h_t I_t + f_t (W_t - W_{t-1})^2 + g_t (P_t - P_{t-1})^2 \right]
$$

where each term has a clear operational meaning (see Section 2.3).

### 2.3 Term-by-Term Logic

- **$c_t P_t$** — Direct production cost. Linear in units produced; represents raw material + variable conversion cost.
- **$h_t I_t$** — Inventory holding cost. Charged on closing inventory $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ (where $D_t$ is actual demand). Captures warehousing, insurance, capital tied up, and obsolescence.
- **$f_t (W_t - W_{t-1})^2$** — Workforce adjustment cost. **Quadratic** because the *first worker hired* is cheaper than the *hundredth* (training, disruption, union friction grow disproportionately). The same logic applies to layoffs.
- **$g_t (P_t - P_{t-1})^2$** — Production-rate change cost. Captures setup changes, machine warm-up, and subcontracting surges/drops.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Square the Changes?** KTU examiners frequently test this. Quadratic cost models the *real-world asymmetry* that **adjusting the workforce or production rate is increasingly expensive at the margin** — the foundation of the LDR's mathematical tractability.

### 2.4 The LDR Optimality Theorem (HMMM Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HMMM Theorem (1960):** *If the cost function is quadratic and the demand is known, the optimal decisions $P_t$ and $W_t$ are **linear functions** of the demand forecasts and the current inventory state. Specifically, the optimal workforce follows a "rate-of-production" rule and a "production-smoothing" rule.*

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Symbol | Name | Formula / Definition | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_t$ | Production in period $t$ | Decision variable | units | MPS output |
| $W_t$ | Workforce in period $t$ | Decision variable | workers | Workforce plan |
| $I_t$ | Closing inventory period $t$ | $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$ | units | Inventory balance |
| $D_t$ | Actual / forecast demand | External input | units | Demand schedule |
| $F_t$ | Forecast for period $t$ | Exogenous | units | LDR input |
| $c$ | Unit production cost | Given | ₹ / unit | Cost term |
| $h$ | Holding cost per unit | Given | ₹ / unit-period | Cost term |
| $f$ | Workforce adjustment cost coeff. | Given | ₹ / worker² | Cost term |
| $g$ | Production rate change coeff. | Given | ₹ / unit² | Cost term |
| $k$ | Production / workforce ratio | $k = P / W$ | units / worker | Productivity |
| $\Delta W_t$ | Change in workforce | $W_t - W_{t-1}$ | workers | LDR output |
| $\alpha$ | LDR workforce coefficient | Function of $f, h, k$ | dimensionless | Decision rule |
| $\beta$ | LDR production coefficient | Function of $g, h, k$ | dimensionless | Decision rule |
| $TC$ | Total cost over horizon | $\sum (\text{cost terms})$ | ₹ | Objective |
| $\vert x \vert$ | Absolute value (use $\vert$ in text) | Linear penalty form | — | SDR variant |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of LDR-Based MPS

- **Real-time recalculation:** Once coefficients are derived, the *rule* is fixed — a manager simply plugs in updated forecasts. No solver runs needed at the shop floor.
- **Closed-form transparency:** Unlike Linear Programming (LP), the rule is *interpretable* — operations teams can explain *why* workforce went up by 3, not 5.
- **Integration with ERP:** The decision rule maps cleanly to **SAP PP, Oracle SCM, and Microsoft Dynamics 365 Supply Chain Management** planning functions.
- **Benchmark against heuristics:** LDR-MPS can be compared to *Chase Strategy* (W = D/k) and *Level Strategy* (P = constant) to demonstrate cost savings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LDR Workforce Decision Rule

**Step 1 — Set up the inventory balance equation (system dynamics):**

$$
I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t
$$

This says: closing stock = opening stock + what was made − what was demanded. This is a *conservation law* in operations.

**Step 2 — Express production as a workforce multiple (productivity link):**

$$
P_t = k \cdot W_t
$$

where $k$ is the productivity (units per worker per period), assumed constant. This links the two decision variables.

**Step 3 — Substitute into inventory equation:**

$$
I_t = I_{t-1} + k W_t - D_t
$$

**Step 4 — Write the per-period cost function:**

$$
C_t = h \cdot I_t + f \cdot (W_t - W_{t-1})^2
$$

(We hold $c \cdot P_t$ aside because $P_t = k W_t$ makes it a *constant* once workforce is set.)

**Step 5 — Express the cumulative inventory $I_t$ in terms of workforce history:**

Iterating the balance equation forward:

$$
I_t = I_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{t} (k W_j - D_j)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and form the Lagrangian minimization problem:**

We minimize over the horizon $T$:

$$
\min_{W_1, \dots, W_T} \; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left[ h \left( I_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{t}(kW_j - D_j) \right) + f(W_t - W_{t-1})^2 \right]
$$

**Step 7 — Take the first-order condition $\partial C / \partial W_s = 0$ for any interior period $s$:**

$$
2f(W_s - W_{s-1}) - 2f(W_{s+1} - W_s) + h k (T - s + 1) = 0
$$

**Step 8 — Solve the resulting second-order difference equation:**

The general solution has a particular solution (linear ramp) and a homogeneous solution (exponential decay). After applying boundary conditions at the terminal period $T$, we obtain the famous HMMM **rate-of-production rule**:

$$
W_t = \lambda \sum_{j=t}^{T} (T - j + 1) D_j - \mu \cdot I_{t-1}
$$

where the optimal coefficients $\lambda$ and $\mu$ depend only on the cost parameters $f, h, k$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{hk}{2f} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{(boundary factor)}}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{hk}{2f} \cdot \text{(boundary factor 2)}
$$

For the standard infinite-horizon HMMM solution, the closed forms simplify to:

$$
\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{hk^2}{2f(1+r)^2}} \quad \text{and} \quad \mu = (1 + r) - 1 = r
$$

where $r = \sqrt{hk^2 / 2f}$ is the *HMMM damping factor*.

**Step 9 — The Final Linear Decision Rule (Kernal of the LDR-MPS):**

$$
\boxed{ \; W_t = \lambda \sum_{j=t}^{T} \phi_j \, D_j \;-\; \mu \cdot I_{t-1} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{ \; P_t = k \cdot W_t = k\lambda \sum_{j=t}^{T} \phi_j \, D_j \;-\; k\mu \cdot I_{t-1} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *operational heart* of the project. The rule is **linear** in (a) future demand forecasts and (b) current inventory. Hence the name **Linear Decision Rule**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Project-Style)

**Problem Setup:** A firm has 8 periods of demand forecasts (in 000 units): $D_1=20, D_2=24, D_3=22, D_4=26, D_5=28, D_6=30, D_7=24, D_8=22$. Productivity $k = 1.0$ unit/worker/period. Holding cost $h = 1$ ₹/unit. Workforce adjustment cost $f = 4$ ₹/worker². Initial inventory $I_0 = 10$ (000 units). Initial workforce $W_0 = 22$.

**Step A — Compute the HMMM coefficients:**

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{hk^2}{2f}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 \cdot 1}{8}} = \sqrt{0.125} \approx 0.3536
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{2f} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + r} \cdot hk = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{1}{1.3536} \cdot 1 \approx 0.0924
$$

$$
\mu = r \cdot hk = 0.3536 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 0.3536
$$

**Step B — Apply the rule for $t = 1$:**

$$
W_1 = \lambda \cdot [1 \cdot D_1 + 2 \cdot D_2 + \dots + 8 \cdot D_8] - \mu \cdot I_0
$$

Compute the weighted forecast sum:

$$
S_1 = (1)(20) + (2)(24) + (3)(22) + (4)(26) + (5)(28) + (6)(30) + (7)(24) + (8)(22)
$$

$$
S_1 = 20 + 48 + 66 + 104 + 140 + 180 + 168 + 176 = 902
$$

Therefore:

$$
W_1 = 0.0924 \cdot 902 - 0.3536 \cdot 10 = 83.34 - 3.54 = 79.80 \text{ workers}
$$

Round to $W_1 = 80$ workers. Then $P_1 = k \cdot W_1 = 80$ units. Closing inventory $I_1 = I_0 + P_1 - D_1 = 10 + 80 - 20 = 70$.

**Step C — Apply the rule for $t = 2$:**

$$
S_2 = (1)(24) + (2)(22) + (3)(26) + (4)(28) + (5)(30) + (6)(24) + (7)(22)
$$

$$
S_2 = 24 + 44 + 78 + 112 + 150 + 144 + 154 = 706
$$

$$
W_2 = 0.0924 \cdot 706 - 0.3536 \cdot 70 = 65.23 - 24.75 = 40.48 \approx 40 \text{ workers}
$$

This large drop signals the model is *front-loading* — a known LDR characteristic requiring damping or a planning horizon shorter than the back-log window.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error in KTU Project Valuation:** Treating $\lambda$ and $\mu$ as universal constants. **They depend on $f$, $h$, and $k$** — recompute for every parameter change. Examiners will deduct 2–3 marks for hard-coding the coefficients.

**Step D — Total Cost Computation for Validation:**

For $t = 1$:

$$
C_1 = h I_1 + f(W_1 - W_0)^2 = (1)(70) + (4)(80 - 22)^2 = 70 + 4 \cdot 3364 = 70 + 13456 = 13526 \; \text{₹ (in 000s)}
$$

Repeat for all $t$ and sum. Then compare $TC_{LDR}$ with $TC_{Chase}$ and $TC_{Level}$ to demonstrate optimization.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Aggregate Planning Strategies

| Criterion | Chase Strategy | Level Strategy | **LDR Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Workforce variability | Very High | None | Moderate (smoothed) |
| Inventory variability | Very Low | High | Moderate (forecast-aware) |
| Cost structure fit | Linear | Linear | **Quadratic (optimal)** |
| Computational complexity | Trivial | Trivial | Closed-form algebraic |
| Demand adaptability | Excellent (W tracks D) | Poor (P fixed) | **Good (P = f(F, I))** |
| Reaction to forecast error | Oscillates | Slow | **Exponentially damped** |
| Industry use | Contract / project Mfg | Process industries | **Discrete batch mfg.** |

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU project report, the **analytical matrix** above (with cost-comparison numbers) earns the highest marks under CO4 (*Design Thinking*) and CO5 (*Engineering Tools*).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LDR-MPS Project Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step A: Industry and Product Selection] --> A1[Step B: Data Collection]
    A1 --> A2[Step C: Demand Forecast F-t]
    A2 --> A3[Step D: Cost Parameter Estimation]
    A3 --> A4[Step E: Derive LDR Coefficients]
    A4 --> A5[Step F: Apply Linear Decision Rule]
    A5 --> A6[Step G: Generate MPS Table]
    A6 --> A7[Step H: Simulate in Software]
    A7 --> A8[Step I: Compute Total Cost]
    A8 --> A9[Step J: Compare with Chase and Level]
    A9 --> A10[Step K: Validate and Report]

    subgraph SOFTWARE[Software Tool Stack]
        S1[Excel Solver for LDR Coeff]
        S2[Arena or ProModel for Simulation]
        S3[SAP PP or Oracle SCM for MPS]
    end

    A4 --> S1
    A6 --> S3
    A7 --> S2
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Closed-Loop LDR-MPS Control Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Forecast Module F-t] --> B1[Sum Weighted Future Demand]
    B1 --> B2[Compute Lambda times Sum]
    I0[Current Inventory I-t-1] --> B3[Compute Mu times Inventory]
    B3 --> B4[Subtract Term from B2]
    B2 --> B4
    B4 --> B5[Optimal Workforce W-t]
    B5 --> B6[Optimal Production P-t equals k times W-t]
    B6 --> B7[Update Inventory I-t]
    B7 --> B0
    B7 --> I0
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Project Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Choose Planning Strategy] --> C1{Is Cost Quadratic?}
    C1 -- Yes --> C2[Apply LDR Method]
    C1 -- No --> C3{Is Cost Linear?}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4[Apply Search Decision Rules]
    C3 -- No --> C5[Apply Simulation Optimisation]
    C2 --> C6[Derive Closed Form Rule]
    C4 --> C7[Heuristic Local Search]
    C5 --> C8[Arena or AnyLogic Run]
    C6 --> C9[Generate Final MPS]
    C7 --> C9
    C8 --> C9
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Software Tool Stack Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D0[Project Layer] --> D1[Data Input Layer]
    D1 --> D2[Optimization Layer]
    D2 --> D3[Simulation Layer]
    D3 --> D4[Validation Layer]
    D1 --> D11[Excel Demand Sheet]
    D1 --> D12[Cost Database]
    D2 --> D21[Excel Solver LDR]
    D2 --> D22[LINGO or AMPL]
    D3 --> D31[Arena Simulation]
    D3 --> D32[ProModel Runtime]
    D4 --> D41[SAP PP Visualisation]
    D4 --> D42[Power BI Dashboard]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** and explain its position in the production planning hierarchy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a time-phased, product-level plan specifying the *quantities* of finished products to be produced in each time period to meet forecasted demand and confirmed customer orders. (1 mark)

It occupies the **second tier** of the production planning hierarchy, sitting *below* the **Aggregate Production Plan (APP)** (which deals with product families and total volumes) and *above* the **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** (which explodes the MPS into components and raw materials). (1 mark)

Its primary purposes are: (i) to drive MRP, (ii) to serve as a baseline for capacity planning, and (iii) to provide customer-order promising dates via the **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** logic. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]** What is a **Linear Decision Rule (LDR)** in aggregate production planning? Why is it called *linear*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **LDR** is a mathematical rule, derived by Holt, Modigliani, Muth, and Simon (HMMM, 1960), that prescribes the *optimal workforce* and *optimal production* for each period as a function of the demand forecasts and the current inventory. (1.5 marks)

It is called *linear* because, under a **quadratic cost function** (covering production, holding, hiring/firing, and production-rate change costs), the optimal decisions are **linear combinations** of the input variables — i.e., of the form $W_t = a \cdot (\text{forecast sum}) + b \cdot I_{t-1}$. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]** *(a)* Explain the **HMMM quadratic cost model** for aggregate planning. List and interpret each cost term. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Derive the **LDR workforce decision rule** from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Quadratic Cost Model (7 marks):**

The HMMM model minimizes the total cost over the planning horizon $T$:

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \Big[ c_t P_t + h_t I_t + f_t (W_t - W_{t-1})^2 + g_t (P_t - P_{t-1})^2 \Big]
$$

*Cost term interpretations:* (1 mark each)

- **$c_t P_t$** — Linear direct production cost. Represents raw material, energy, and variable labour per unit produced.
- **$h_t I_t$** — Holding cost on closing inventory. Captures warehousing, insurance, capital cost, and obsolescence.
- **$f_t (W_t - W_{t-1})^2$** — **Workforce adjustment cost** (hiring + firing). Quadratic because the marginal cost of *each additional worker* hired or laid off rises as the firm moves further from its steady-state workforce. Reflects training, morale, severance, and union constraints.
- **$g_t (P_t - P_{t-1})^2$** — **Production-rate change cost.** Models machine setup, subcontracting changes, and the disruption of frequent ramp-up/ramp-down.

The **quadratic** specification is the *key enabler* of the closed-form linear solution — the resulting first-order conditions yield a linear rule. (1 mark for conceptual link)

**Part (b) — Derivation of the LDR Workforce Rule (7 marks):**

*Assumption set:* (2 marks)
1. Quadratic cost function as above.
2. Single product (or aggregated product family) with productivity $k$ such that $P_t = k W_t$.
3. Deterministic demand forecast $\{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_T\}$.
4. Inventory balance: $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$.

*Derivation steps:* (5 marks)

**Step 1:** Substitute $P_t = k W_t$ into inventory balance:

$$
I_t = I_{t-1} + k W_t - D_t
$$

**Step 2:** Recursive unrolling:

$$
I_t = I_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{t} (k W_j - D_j)
$$

**Step 3:** Form the per-period cost:

$$
C_t = h \cdot \left[ I_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{t}(kW_j - D_j) \right] + f(W_t - W_{t-1})^2
$$

**Step 4:** Total cost is the sum over $t$. Differentiate with respect to $W_s$ and set to zero for first-order optimality:

$$
2f(W_s - W_{s-1}) - 2f(W_{s+1} - W_s) + hk(T - s + 1) = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Solve the second-order linear difference equation. The general solution is the sum of a *homogeneous* part (exponentially damped) and a *particular* part (linear ramp on the cumulative forecast).

**Step 6:** Apply boundary conditions at $t = 0$ and $t = T$. The HMMM closed-form result is:

$$
\boxed{ \; W_t = \lambda \sum_{j=t}^{T} \phi_j \, D_j \;-\; \mu \cdot I_{t-1} \;}
$$

where $\lambda = \sqrt{h / 2f} \cdot k$ and $\mu = \sqrt{2fh} / k$ (within the infinite-horizon limit). (1 mark for the final boxed form)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. **Skipping assumptions** — Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for listing *productivity* ($P = kW$), *deterministic demand*, and *quadratic cost*. Omitting any one costs a mark.
> 2. **Forgetting inventory balance** — The LDR *only* makes sense if you include the inventory equation. A derivation without it is incomplete and loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing the rule without the damping coefficient** — The final answer must include both $\lambda$ and $\mu$ as parameters, not as numerics. The general form is what examiners want.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]** *(a)* Describe the step-by-step **methodology for designing an LDR-based Master Production Schedule** for a manufacturing firm. List the software tools that can be used. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A firm has demand forecasts for 4 periods: $D_1=100, D_2=120, D_3=110, D_4=130$ (units). Productivity $k = 1$ unit/worker, $h = 2$ ₹/unit, $f = 5$ ₹/worker², initial workforce $W_0 = 110$, initial inventory $I_0 = 20$. Compute the optimal workforce and production for $t = 1$ using the LDR rule. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Methodology (7 marks):**

The project is executed in **11 sequential steps**: (≈0.5 mark each, with full credit at step 7 = 7 marks)

1. **Industry and Product Selection** — Choose a discrete-batch manufacturing firm (e.g., FMCG, automotive components, electronics assembly).
2. **Data Collection** — Gather 2 years of monthly/weekly demand data, workforce levels, inventory, and cost parameters.
3. **Demand Forecasting** — Apply **moving average, exponential smoothing, or ARIMA** to obtain $\{D_1, \dots, D_T\}$.
4. **Cost Parameter Estimation** — Estimate $c, h, f, g$ from the firm's accounting records.
5. **HMMM Coefficient Computation** — Calculate $\lambda = \sqrt{h / 2f} \cdot k$ and $\mu = \sqrt{2fh} / k$.
6. **Apply LDR Rule** — Use the closed-form formula to compute $W_t$ and $P_t$ for each $t$.
7. **MPS Table Generation** — Tabulate beginning inventory, production, demand, ending inventory per period.

*Software tools:* (1 mark bonus for tool mapping)

- **Microsoft Excel Solver** — for coefficient computation and rule application.
- **LINGO / AMPL** — for algebraic model formalization.
- **Arena / ProModel / AnyLogic** — for stochastic simulation of the MPS in dynamic demand.
- **SAP PP / Oracle SCM / Microsoft Dynamics 365** — for enterprise-grade MPS execution and ATP logic.
- **Power BI / Tableau** — for dashboard visualization of the MPS performance vs. benchmark strategies.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute HMMM coefficients (1.5 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \sqrt{\frac{h}{2f}} \cdot k = \sqrt{\frac{2}{10}} \cdot 1 = \sqrt{0.2} = 0.4472
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{\sqrt{2fh}}{k} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 5 \cdot 2} = \sqrt{20} = 4.4721
$$

**Step 2: Compute the weighted forecast sum for $t = 1$ (2 marks):**

$$
S_1 = (1)(100) + (2)(120) + (3)(110) + (4)(130)
$$

$$
S_1 = 100 + 240 + 330 + 520 = 1190
$$

**Step 3: Apply the LDR workforce rule (2 marks):**

$$
W_1 = \lambda \cdot S_1 - \mu \cdot I_0
$$

$$
W_1 = 0.4472 \times 1190 - 4.4721 \times 20
$$

$$
W_1 = 532.17 - 89.44 = 442.73 \approx 443 \text{ workers}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the LDR production rule and update inventory (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_1 = k \cdot W_1 = 1 \times 443 = 443 \text{ units}
$$

$$
I_1 = I_0 + P_1 - D_1 = 20 + 443 - 100 = 363 \text{ units}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):** $W_1 = 443$ workers, $P_1 = 443$ units, $I_1 = 363$ units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit confusion** — Productivity $k$ is in *units per worker*. If $k = 0.5$, then $P_1 = 221.5$, not 443. Always check.
> 2. **Inventory term sign error** — The LDR *subtracts* $\mu I_{t-1}$. A common error is adding it, which inflates the workforce spuriously.
> 3. **Forecast weighting direction** — The weights $\phi_j = (T - j + 1)$ increase *as the period goes further away* (more distant demand gets *more* weight in the current decision because it influences the cumulative production ramp). Forgetting this loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — Project Suggestion: LDR-Based MPS Optimization — Quick-Recall Sheet**

- **MPS** = time-phased, product-level production plan, *not* shop-floor schedule.
- **MPS Hierarchy position:** APP → **MPS** → MRP → SFC.
- **Frozen Horizon** ≈ 4–6 weeks; **Planning Horizon** ≈ 6–18 months.
- **HMMM (1960)** = originators of the LDR model.
- **Quadratic cost function** has 4 terms: production, holding, workforce-adjustment, production-rate-change.
- **Why quadratic?** Captures *diminishing returns* to scaling workforce/production.
- **Inventory balance equation:** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t - D_t$.
- **Productivity link:** $P_t = k W_t$ (assumes constant output per worker).
- **HMMM coefficients:** $\lambda = \sqrt{h / 2f} \cdot k$ and $\mu = \sqrt{2fh} / k$.
- **LDR Workforce Rule:** $W_t = \lambda \sum_{j=t}^{T} \phi_j D_j - \mu I_{t-1}$.
- **LDR Production Rule:** $P_t = k W_t$.
- **LDR vs. SDR:** LDR optimal under *quadratic* cost; SDR optimal under *linear* cost.
- **Chase Strategy:** Workforce tracks demand ($W_t = D_t / k$).
- **Level Strategy:** Production is constant ($P_t = \bar{D}$).
- **Software stack:** Excel Solver (algebra), LINGO/AMPL (formulation), Arena/ProModel (simulation), SAP PP / Oracle SCM (execution), Power BI (visualization).
- **Project report structure:** Introduction → Data → Forecasting → Cost Estimation → LDR Derivation → MPS Table → Simulation → Comparison vs. Chase & Level → Validation → Conclusion.
- **Common pitfall #1:** Treating $\lambda, \mu$ as universal constants — they are *parameter-dependent*.
- **Common pitfall #2:** Forgetting the inventory balance in the derivation.
- **Common pitfall #3:** Sign error in the inventory term of the LDR formula.
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** CO4 — *Design/Develop solutions for MPS problems*; CO5 — *Use modern engineering tools* (software simulation); CO3 — *Apply quantitative methods* (LDR optimization).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Suggestion: BOM Structuring and MRP System Development - Develop a Comprehensive BOM and Implement MRP Using MS Project or Specialized Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Suggestion: BOM Structuring and MRP System Development - Develop a Comprehensive BOM and Implement MRP Using MS Project or Specialized Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bill of Materials (BOM)?

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a comprehensive, structured inventory of all raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, and quantities required to manufacture one unit of a finished product. It serves as the master recipe that translates the product design into a manufacturing plan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):** A BOM is a hierarchical document listing all parts, assemblies, sub-assemblies, and raw materials along with the quantity of each required to produce one unit of the end item, organized by levels of manufacture.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a **Black Forest Cake** for 10 people. Your BOM is your recipe card which says:
- Level 0 (Final Product): Black Forest Cake × 1
- Level 1 (Sub-assemblies): Sponge Cake × 1, Whipped Cream × 500 g, Chocolate Shavings × 100 g
- Level 2 (Raw Materials): Flour × 200 g, Sugar × 150 g, Eggs × 4, Cocoa Powder × 50 g...

If a customer orders 3 cakes, you instantly know you need 3 sponges, 1500 g cream, 600 g flour, etc. **This is exactly what a BOM does in a factory** — it explodes a finished product into its constituent parts so that the planning system knows what to buy, when to buy, and how much to buy.

> [!NOTE]
> **BOM Precision Metric:** A well-structured BOM is measured by its **BOM Accuracy (in %)**. Industry benchmark = **BOM Accuracy ≥ 98%** for stable, mature products.

---

## 1.2 What is Material Requirements Planning (MRP)?

**Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** is a computer-based production planning and inventory control system used to manage dependent demand inventory. It calculates the quantity and timing of material orders needed to meet the Master Production Schedule (MPS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):** MRP is a time-phased, lot-sizing, and order-release system that translates the Master Production Schedule into planned order releases for raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies using BOM and inventory data.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of MRP as a **smart grocery list generator** for a restaurant. The chef has a reservation list (MPS) for the next 7 days. Instead of guessing ingredients, MRP automatically:
1. Looks at what dishes are planned (MPS),
2. Checks the recipe book (BOM),
3. Checks what's already in the fridge (Inventory Status),
4. **Then tells the chef exactly** what to buy, how much, and on which day.

It eliminates the panic of running out of ingredients mid-service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dependent vs Independent Demand:** Finished products have *independent* demand (driven by customers). Components in a BOM have *dependent* demand (driven by demand for the parent). MRP manages **dependent demand only**.

---

## 1.3 The Project Scope: What Students Must Deliver

For the KTU 2024 project suggestion, the deliverables are:

| Deliverable | Description |
|---|---|
| **BOM Document** | Multi-level structured BOM (Excel / MS Project) |
| **MRP Logic File** | Time-phased MRP table with all 8 columns |
| **Lot-Sizing Analysis** | Comparison of L4L, EOQ, POQ techniques |
| **Software Implementation** | Screenshot/export from MS Project or specialized ERP |
| **Report** | Word/PDF report with methodology and results |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often submit a "list of parts" and call it a BOM. A BOM is a *hierarchical, level-coded, quantity-specified* document. Submitting a flat parts list will attract **heavy mark deductions** (typically 4–5 marks out of 14).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BOM Structure — Hierarchical Levels

A BOM is built in a **tree structure** (also called the product structure tree). Each level represents a stage of manufacture.

### Level Coding (Universal Standard)

- **Level 0** — Finished / End Product (e.g., "Bicycle")
- **Level 1** — Major Sub-assemblies (e.g., "Wheel Assembly")
- **Level 2** — Sub-sub-assemblies (e.g., "Hub")
- **Level 3** — Purchased / Raw parts (e.g., "Ball Bearing")

### Types of BOM (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **Single-Level BOM** — Lists only the immediate children of the parent. Flat structure.
2. **Multi-Level (Indented) BOM** — Lists all levels indented under each parent. Most common in practice.
3. **Modular BOM** — A planning BOM used for forecasting families of products before final configuration.
4. **Planning BOM** — Used in assemble-to-order environments.
5. **Phantom BOM** — Sub-assemblies that are not stocked; built and consumed immediately in production.

### BOM Record Fields (Must Include)

| Field | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Item Number | Unique identifier | `BOM-1001` |
| Description | Item name | "Brake Lever" |
| Quantity per Assembly | Qty of this part in 1 parent | 2 |
| Unit of Measure (UOM) | Standard unit | "pcs", "kg", "m" |
| Reference Designator | Where used in parent | "BL-01" |
| Effectivity Date | Valid from date | "01-Jan-2025" |
| Phantom Flag | Y / N | "N" |
| Scrap % | Expected process loss | 3% |

---

## 2.2 MRP System — The Three Inputs

MRP is a black box with three mandatory inputs:

$$\text{MRP} = f(\text{MPS}, \text{BOM}, \text{Inventory Status File})$$

### Input 1: Master Production Schedule (MPS)
A time-phased plan stating **what finished products** to produce, **in what quantities**, and **by what due dates**.

### Input 2: Bill of Materials (BOM)
The recipe file giving parent-to-child relationships and quantities.

### Input 3: Inventory Status File
Real-time data on:
- **On-hand inventory** (OH)
- **Scheduled receipts (SR)** — already-ordered items due to arrive
- **Allocated stock** (reserved for other orders)
- **Safety stock (SS)** — buffer for variability
- **Lead time (LT)** — time from order placement to receipt

---

## 2.3 MRP Processing Logic — Step-by-Step

MRP executes four logical steps (the KTU board loves asking this as a 7-marker):

### Step 1: **Netting (Gross-to-Net Explosion)**
For each time bucket, calculate **Net Requirement**:

$$\text{Net Requirement} = \text{Gross Requirement} + \text{Safety Stock} - \text{On-Hand Inventory} - \text{Scheduled Receipts}$$

If Net Requirement ≤ 0, no order is needed in that period.

### Step 2: **Lot Sizing (Quantity Decision)**
Determine the **planned order quantity** using a lot-sizing rule:
- **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**
- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**
- **Period Order Quantity (POQ)**
- **Fixed Period Requirements (FPR)**

### Step 3: **Backscheduling (Offset by Lead Time)**
Determine the **planned order release date** by offsetting the planned order receipt backward by the item's lead time:

$$\text{Planned Order Release} = \text{Planned Order Receipt} - \text{Lead Time}$$

### Step 4: **Explosion (BOM Cascade)**
The planned order release of a parent is converted into **gross requirements** of its children (using BOM quantities) in the next MRP pass.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your exam-day survival card for MRP calculations. Memorize every row.**

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Net Requirement | $\text{NR} = \text{GR} + \text{SS} - \text{OH} - \text{SR}$ | All in **units of the item** |
| Projected On-Hand (POH) | $\text{POH}_t = \text{POH}_{t-1} + \text{SR}_t + \text{PR}_t - \text{GR}_t$ | Computed period by period |
| Planned Order Receipt | $\text{PR} = \text{Net Requirement}$ (under L4L) | Placed in period of need |
| Planned Order Release | $\text{PRel} = \text{PR} - \text{LT}$ | Offset by lead time |
| Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = order cost, $H$ = holding cost |
| Period Order Quantity (POQ) | $\text{Number of periods} = \dfrac{\text{EOQ}}{\text{Average period usage}}$ | Round up to next integer |
| Scrap Adjustment | $\text{Adjusted Qty} = \dfrac{\text{Net Req}}{1 - \text{Scrap \%}}$ | When BOM scrap factor is given |
| Low-Level Coding | Lowest level at which item appears | Drives MRP sequence |
| BOM Accuracy | $\dfrac{\text{Error-free BOM lines}}{\text{Total BOM lines}} \times 100\%$ | Target ≥ 98% |
| MRP Regeneration Time | $\text{Time to recompute full plan}$ | Measure of system efficiency |

### Real-World Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A single car has ~30,000 parts. MRP plans millions of orders per week.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Used in PCB assembly to plan component purchases across global supply chains.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Validates regulatory BOM accuracy (FDA 21 CFR Part 11 compliance).
- **Aerospace:** MRP-II (Manufacturing Resource Planning) extends this to capacity and shop floor scheduling.

---

## 2.5 Lot Sizing — When to Use Which Rule

| Rule | Best For | Drawback |
|---|---|---|
| **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** | Make-to-order, low inventory carrying cost | Many small orders → high ordering cost |
| **EOQ** | Stable, independent demand | Ignores period-by-period timing |
| **POQ** | Lumpy demand, fewer orders | Slightly higher holding cost than EOQ |
| **FPR** | Covering N periods exactly | Requires known forward demand |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For **dependent-demand items** (which is the entire scope of MRP), the **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** rule is mathematically optimal because it minimizes average inventory. This is frequently asked in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Constructing a Multi-Level BOM

**Problem Statement:**
A company manufactures a **Pedestal Fan**. The structure is given below:

- **Fan (End Product)** = 1 Base + 1 Motor + 1 Blade Assembly
- **Base** = 1 Stand + 4 Rubber Feet
- **Motor** = 1 Coil + 1 Shaft + 2 Bearings
- **Blade Assembly** = 3 Blades + 1 Hub

**Step 1 — Identify Levels:**

| Level | Item | Type |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Pedestal Fan | End Product |
| 1 | Base, Motor, Blade Assembly | Sub-assemblies |
| 2 | Stand, Rubber Feet, Coil, Shaft, Bearings, Blades, Hub | Purchased / Detail parts |

**Step 2 — Build the Indented BOM Table:**

| Item Code | Description | Level | Qty per Parent | UOM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FAN-001 | Pedestal Fan | 0 | 1 | nos |
| BASE-01 | Base | 1 | 1 | nos |
| MTR-02 | Motor | 1 | 1 | nos |
| BLADE-03 | Blade Assembly | 1 | 1 | nos |
| STD-04 | Stand | 2 | 1 | nos |
| RBF-05 | Rubber Feet | 2 | 4 | nos |
| COIL-06 | Coil | 2 | 1 | nos |
| SHAFT-07 | Shaft | 2 | 1 | nos |
| BRG-08 | Bearing | 2 | 2 | nos |
| BLD-09 | Blade | 2 | 3 | nos |
| HUB-10 | Hub | 2 | 1 | nos |

**Step 3 — Compute Gross Requirements for 50 Fans (under L4L):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Requirement of Base} &= 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ units} \\
\text{Requirement of Motor} &= 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ units} \\
\text{Requirement of Stand} &= 1 \times 50 = 50 \text{ units} \\
\text{Requirement of Rubber Feet} &= 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ units} \\
\text{Requirement of Bearings} &= 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ units} \\
\text{Requirement of Blades} &= 3 \times 50 = 150 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** The gross requirement of a child = (qty per parent) × (gross requirement of parent). This is called **BOM explosion** and is the foundation of MRP cascading.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Full MRP Table (L4L Method)

**Given Data for Item X:**

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req. | 30 | 20 | 40 | 10 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 40 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- **On-Hand Inventory (start of period 1):** 35 units
- **Safety Stock:** 10 units
- **Lead Time:** 2 periods
- **Lot Sizing Rule:** Lot-for-Lot (L4L)

**Step 1 — Initialize:**

$$\text{POH at end of Period 0} = \text{On-Hand} = 35 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Period 1 calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available before GR} &= 35 + 0 \text{ (SR)} = 35 \\
\text{Net Req. in Period 1} &= 30 + 10 \text{ (SS)} - 35 = 5 \text{ units} \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt} &= 5 \text{ (L4L)} \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{Period 1} - 2 \text{ (LT)} = \text{Period } -1
\end{aligned}
$$

*Negative period release indicates the order should have been placed before the start of the MPS horizon. Log as a **Past Due** exception.*

**Step 3 — Period 2 calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Projected On-Hand at end of P1} &= 35 - 30 = 5 \text{ units} \\
\text{Net Req. in Period 2} &= 20 + 10 - 5 - 40 \text{ (SR)} = -15
\end{aligned}
$$

*Net requirement is negative → **no order needed**. POH carries forward.*

**Step 4 — Period 3 calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POH at end of P2} &= 5 + 40 - 20 = 25 \text{ units} \\
\text{Net Req. in P3} &= 40 + 10 - 25 - 0 = 25 \text{ units} \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt in P3} &= 25 \text{ (L4L)} \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{P3} - 2 = \text{Period 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Period 4 calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POH at end of P3} &= 25 + 25 - 40 = 10 \text{ units} \\
\text{Net Req. in P4} &= 10 + 10 - 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ units} \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt in P4} &= 10 \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{P4} - 2 = \text{Period 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Period 5 calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POH at end of P4} &= 10 + 10 - 10 = 10 \text{ units} \\
\text{Net Req. in P5} &= 50 + 10 - 10 - 0 = 50 \text{ units} \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt in P5} &= 50 \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{P5} - 2 = \text{Period 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Period 6 calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POH at end of P5} &= 10 + 50 - 50 = 10 \text{ units} \\
\text{Net Req. in P6} &= 20 + 10 - 10 - 0 = 20 \text{ units} \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt in P6} &= 20 \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{P6} - 2 = \text{Period 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Period 7 & 8 calculation (similar logic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POH at end of P6} &= 10 + 20 - 20 = 10 \text{ units} \\
\text{Net Req. in P7} &= 30 + 10 - 10 - 0 = 30 \text{ units} \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt in P7} &= 30 \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{P7} - 2 = \text{Period 5} \\
\\
\text{POH at end of P7} &= 10 + 30 - 30 = 10 \text{ units} \\
\text{Net Req. in P8} &= 40 + 10 - 10 - 0 = 40 \text{ units} \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt in P8} &= 40 \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{P8} - 2 = \text{Period 6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Compile Final MRP Table:**

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 30 | 20 | 40 | 10 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 40 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand | 5 | 25 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| Net Requirements | 5 | 0 | 25 | 10 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| Planned Order Receipts | 5 | 0 | 25 | 10 | 50 | 20 | 30 | 40 |
| Planned Order Releases | — | — | 5 | 25 | 10 | 50 | 20 | 30 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Check:** At the end of every period, Projected On-Hand should be ≥ Safety Stock (10). In the above table, POH = 10 in all periods ≥ Period 3. ✓ Valid MRP.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: EOQ & POQ Calculation

**Given:** Annual demand $D$ = 12,000 units, ordering cost $S$ = ₹200/order, holding cost $H$ = ₹4/unit/year.

**EOQ Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EOQ} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times D \times S}{H}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12000 \times 200}{4}} \\
