&= \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} \\
&= 1095.45 \text{ units} \\
&\approx 1095 \text{ units (rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of orders per year:**

$$
\text{Number of orders} = \frac{D}{\text{EOQ}} = \frac{12000}{1095} \approx 10.96 \approx 11 \text{ orders}
$$

**POQ Calculation (assuming 12 periods per year, so 1 period = 1 month):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average period usage} &= \frac{12000}{12} = 1000 \text{ units/month} \\
\text{Number of periods covered} &= \frac{\text{EOQ}}{\text{Average usage}} = \frac{1095}{1000} \approx 1.095
\end{aligned}
$$

Round up to next integer → **POQ = 2 periods**.

**Therefore, order every 2 months, with quantity = 2 × 1000 = 2000 units per order.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Lot-Sizing Comparison

| Metric | L4L | EOQ | POQ (n=2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Order Qty | Net Req. each period | 1095 units | 2000 units |
| No. of Orders/Year | 12 (monthly) | 11 | 6 |
| Avg Inventory | Very Low | ~547 units | ~1000 units |
| Ordering Cost/Year | ₹2400 | ₹2200 | ₹1200 |
| Holding Cost/Year | Low | ~₹2190 | ~₹4000 |
| **Total Cost** | **Lowest** | Moderate | Higher holding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to choose, justify L4L for **dependent demand items** in MRP because demand is lumpy and time-phased — fixed order intervals don't help.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Reading the tree:** Reading top-down gives **disassembly (explosion)**. Reading bottom-up gives **assembly (implosion)**. MRP uses the explosion direction.

## 4.2 MRP System Architecture — Functional Flow

    I1["MPS Master Production Schedule"]

    I3["ISF Inventory Status File"]

    O1["Planned Order Schedule"]

    O3["MRP Reports Order Release"]

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P4
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

## 4.3 MS Project Implementation — Functional Block Architecture

## 4.4 Lot Sizing Decision Flowchart

    S1 -- Steady high volume --> S3

    S4 -- Cover fixed N periods --> S5
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Bill of Materials. List any four fields of a BOM record.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a structured, multi-level document listing all raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, and the quantity of each required to manufacture one unit of a finished product.

**Four essential fields of a BOM record:**

1. **Item Number** — Unique identifier for the component (e.g., `BRG-08`).
2. **Quantity per Assembly** — Number of units of this part required in one parent (e.g., 2 bearings per motor).
3. **Unit of Measure (UOM)** — Standard unit such as pieces, kg, metres.
4. **Reference Designator** — Locator code showing where the part is used in the parent (e.g., `BL-01` for brake lever).

*Supplementary fields: Effectivity date, scrap %, phantom flag, planner code.*

**[Marks distribution: 2 marks definition + 1 mark for listing fields correctly]**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Independent and Dependent Demand with one example each.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Independent Demand | Dependent Demand |
|---|---|---|
| Driver | Customer orders / market forecast | Demand for the parent item |
| Forecasting | Required (time-series methods) | Not required (derived from parent) |
| Management Technique | EOQ, ROP, Safety Stock | MRP |
| Example | Demand for finished cars | Demand for car tyres (derived from car demand) |
| Variability | High | Low (deterministic once parent is fixed) |

**Key Insight:** MRP is designed **only for dependent demand** items. The finished product itself has independent demand and is planned via the MPS.

**[Marks distribution: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark example contrast]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Construct a Multi-Level BOM and Run MRP

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for Module 4]**

A company manufactures a **Study Table** with the following structure:

- **Study Table** = 1 Top + 4 Legs + 2 Side Panels
- **Top** = 1 Plywood Sheet + 2 Lamination Sheets
- **Side Panel** = 1 Plywood + 1 Lamination

The MPS shows a demand of **100 tables in week 6**. Lead time for Plywood is 1 week, for Lamination is 2 weeks, for Legs is 1 week. Initial inventory: Plywood = 50, Lamination = 30, Legs = 0. **Use Lot-for-Lot (L4L).**

#### Part (a) — Construct the Multi-Level BOM (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Levels:**

| Level | Item |
|---|---|
| 0 | Study Table |
| 1 | Top, Legs, Side Panel |
| 2 | Plywood, Lamination |

**Step 2 — Indented BOM Table:**

| Item Code | Description | Level | Qty per Parent | UOM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TBL-01 | Study Table | 0 | 1 | nos |
| TOP-02 | Table Top | 1 | 1 | nos |
| LEG-03 | Table Leg | 1 | 4 | nos |
| SIDE-04 | Side Panel | 1 | 2 | nos |
| PLY-05 | Plywood | 2 | 1 (in Top) + 1 (in Side) = 2 | nos |
| LAM-06 | Lamination Sheet | 2 | 2 (in Top) + 1 (in Side) = 3 | nos |

**Step 3 — Gross Requirements for 100 Tables:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top} &= 1 \times 100 = 100 \\
\text{Legs} &= 4 \times 100 = 400 \\
\text{Side Panels} &= 2 \times 100 = 200 \\
\text{Plywood} &= 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ (1 per Top + 1 per Side Panel)} \\
\text{Lamination} &= 3 \times 100 = 300 \text{ (2 per Top + 1 per Side Panel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **BOM Marks Awarded:** 2 marks for level identification + 3 marks for the BOM table + 2 marks for gross requirement calculation.

---

#### Part (b) — Compute MRP for Plywood and Lamination (7 Marks)

**Given:** Safety Stock = 0 for simplicity. Lead times: Plywood = 1 week, Lamination = 2 weeks. L4L lot-sizing.

**MRP for PLYWOOD (LT = 1, OH = 50):**

| Period | Week 4 | Week 5 | Week 6 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 0 | 200 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand | 50 | 50 | 50 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 0 | 150 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 0 | 150 |
| Planned Order Release | 0 | 150 (Week 5) | 0 |

**Calculation Check for Week 6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Req.} &= 200 + 0 - 50 - 0 = 150 \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt} &= 150 \text{ (L4L)} \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= 150 \text{ in Week } (6 - 1) = \text{Week 5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MRP for LAMINATION (LT = 2, OH = 30):**

| Period | Week 3 | Week 4 | Week 5 | Week 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 0 | 0 | 0 | 300 |
| Scheduled Receipts | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Projected On-Hand | 30 | 30 | 30 | 30 |
| Net Requirements | 0 | 0 | 0 | 270 |
| Planned Order Receipt | 0 | 0 | 0 | 270 |
| Planned Order Release | 0 | 0 | 270 (Week 4) | 0 |

**Calculation Check for Week 6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Req.} &= 300 + 0 - 30 - 0 = 270 \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= 270 \text{ in Week } (6 - 2) = \text{Week 4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **MRP Marks Awarded:** 2 marks for setting up tables + 3 marks for correct netting + 2 marks for backscheduling.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): Lot-Sizing and MRP Comparison

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]**

A product **X** has the following gross requirements and current inventory status:

- On-hand inventory = **40 units**, Safety Stock = **20 units**
- Lead time = **3 weeks**
- Annual Demand $D$ = 2400 units, Order cost $S$ = ₹300, Holding cost $H$ = ₹6/unit/year

| Week | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Requirements | 50 | 30 | 60 | 20 | 70 | 30 | 50 | 40 |

#### Part (a) — Compute the MRP Plan using Lot-for-Lot (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Initial state:** POH = 40, SS = 20.

**Step 2 — Compute period by period (showing only critical periods):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Week 1:} \quad \text{NR} &= 50 + 20 - 40 - 0 = 30 \\
\text{Planned Order Receipt} &= 30 \text{ (L4L)} \\
\text{Planned Order Release} &= \text{Week 1} - 3 = \text{Week } -2 \text{ (Past Due)} \\
\text{POH end W1} &= 40 - 50 = -10 \rightarrow 0 + 30 = 30 \\
\\
\text{Week 2:} \quad \text{NR} &= 30 + 20 - 30 - 0 = 20 \\
\text{PR} &= 20, \text{PRel} = -1 \text{ (Past Due)} \\
\text{POH end W2} &= 0 + 20 - 30 + 20 = 10 \\
\\
\text{Week 3:} \quad \text{NR} &= 60 + 20 - 10 = 70 \\
\text{PR} &= 70, \text{PRel} = \text{Week 0 (Past Due)} \\
\text{POH end W3} &= 10 + 70 - 60 = 20 \\
\\
\text{Week 4:} \quad \text{NR} &= 20 + 20 - 20 = 20 \\
\text{PR} &= 20, \text{PRel} = \text{Week 1} \\
\text{POH end W4} &= 20 + 20 - 20 = 20 \\
\\
\text{Week 5:} \quad \text{NR} &= 70 + 20 - 20 = 70 \\
\text{PR} &= 70, \text{PRel} = \text{Week 2} \\
\text{POH end W5} &= 20 + 70 - 70 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final MRP Table (L4L):**

| Period | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gross Req. | 50 | 30 | 60 | 20 | 70 | 30 | 50 | 40 |
| Net Req. | 30 | 20 | 70 | 20 | 70 | 30 | 50 | 40 |
| POH | 30 | 20 | 30 | 30 | 30 | 20 | 20 | 20 |
| Planned Receipt | 30 | 20 | 70 | 20 | 70 | 30 | 50 | 40 |
| Planned Release | — | — | — | 30 | 20 | 70 | 20 | 70 |

---

#### Part (b) — Calculate EOQ and POQ, then Compare Total Cost (7 Marks)

**EOQ Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EOQ} &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2400 \times 300}{6}} = \sqrt{240000} \\
&\approx 489.9 \approx 490 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of orders/year** = $2400 / 490 \approx 4.9 \approx 5$ orders/year.

**Total Cost under EOQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ordering Cost} &= 5 \times 300 = ₹1500 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= \frac{490}{2} \times 6 = ₹1470 \\
\text{Total Cost (EOQ)} &= ₹1500 + ₹1470 = ₹2970
\end{aligned}
$$

**POQ Calculation** (assume 12 periods/year → 1 period = 1 month):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average weekly demand} &= 2400 / 52 \approx 46.15 \text{ units} \\
\text{Number of periods (weeks) covered} &= \frac{490}{46.15} \approx 10.6 \rightarrow 11 \text{ weeks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Cost under POQ (11 weeks coverage):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Order Quantity} &= 11 \times 46.15 = 507.65 \text{ units (round to 510)} \\
\text{No. of orders/year} &= 2400 / 510 \approx 4.7 \rightarrow 5 \\
\text{Ordering Cost} &= 5 \times 300 = ₹1500 \\
\text{Holding Cost} &= \frac{510}{2} \times 6 = ₹1530 \\
\text{Total Cost (POQ)} &= ₹1500 + ₹1530 = ₹3030
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Summary:**

| Method | Order Qty | Orders/Year | Total Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| L4L | Variable (Net Req.) | 8+ | Lowest for dependent demand |
| EOQ | 490 units | 5 | ₹2970 |
| POQ (11 wks) | 510 units | 5 | ₹3030 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For this *dependent-demand* scenario, L4L is operationally superior because it responds exactly to the MPS signal. EOQ/POQ are static and ignore period-by-period variations. **[Full 7 marks awarded with proper justification]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Exams (Operations Management):**
>
> 1. **BOM without Levels:** A flat list of parts is **NOT** a BOM. Always show Level 0, 1, 2 — expect **2-mark penalty** per level missing.
> 2. **Forgetting Safety Stock:** MRP Net Requirement formula must include SS. Omitting it loses 2 marks in Part B calculations.
> 3. **Wrong Backscheduling:** Students often offset by lead time in the wrong direction. The rule is: **Planned Order Release = Planned Order Receipt − Lead Time** (not the other way).
> 4. **POQ vs EOQ confusion:** POQ orders a *fixed number of periods* of demand; EOQ orders a *fixed quantity* based on cost trade-off. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Missing Software Documentation:** If the question asks to "implement using MS Project," submitting only a manual MRP table will lose **3–4 marks** for not demonstrating software use. Include screenshots of the Gantt chart and Resource Sheet.
> 6. **Mixing Independent and Dependent Demand logic:** EOQ is for independent demand. Applying EOQ inside an MRP table (for dependent items) is a conceptual error. Deduct 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: BOM and MRP**

- **BOM (Bill of Materials):** A hierarchical recipe listing all components and quantities to make one unit of a finished product. Levels are numbered from 0 (top) downward.
- **MRP (Material Requirements Planning):** A computer-based dependent-demand planning system. Three inputs = **MPS + BOM + Inventory Status File**.
- **MRP Logic Order:** Netting → Lot Sizing → Backscheduling → Explosion.
- **Net Requirement Formula:** $\text{NR} = \text{GR} + \text{SS} - \text{OH} - \text{SR}$
- **Backscheduling Rule:** $\text{PRel} = \text{PR} - \text{LT}$
- **EOQ Formula:** $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Used for **independent** demand items.
- **L4L Rule:** Order exactly the net requirement. Mathematically optimal for **dependent** demand in MRP.
- **POQ Rule:** Cover a fixed *number of periods* whose cumulative demand equals EOQ.
- **MS Project Mapping:** Use the **Resource Sheet → Material Resources** tab to map BOM items, then assign to tasks. The "Level Resources" feature acts as a basic netting engine.
- **Specialized Software Options:** SAP PP, Oracle NetSuite, Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central, Tally.ERP9 (for SMEs), and open-source tools like OpenBoxes or xTuple.
- **BOM Accuracy Benchmark:** **≥ 98%** is the industry standard for stable, mature products.
- **Safety Stock Application:** In MRP, SS is **added to gross requirements** (not subtracted from on-hand).
- **Common Output Reports of MRP:** Planned Order Schedule, Order Release Notice, Exception Reports (past-due, quantity variance), Inventory Status Report.
- **MRP-II Extension:** Manufacturing Resource Planning extends MRP to include capacity planning, shop floor control, and financial integration.
- **Exam Day Rule:** Always label BOM levels clearly, and always show the **Gross → Net → POH → Planned Receipt → Planned Release** sequence in the MRP table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Suggestion: Case Study on Real-World ERP Implementation - Analyze Challenges, Solutions, and Outcomes in a Large Enterprise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET304 - OPERATIONS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project Suggestion: Case Study on Real-World ERP Implementation - Analyze Challenges, Solutions, and Outcomes in a Large Enterprise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** is defined as a comprehensive, integrated software system that consolidates an enterprise's core operational functions — including finance, human resources, supply chain, manufacturing, sales, and customer relationship management — into a single unified database and application architecture to facilitate real-time information flow, data consistency, and informed decision-making across the organization (Slack, Chambers & Johnston, *Operations Management*, 9th ed., KTU recommended reference framework).

**ERP Implementation Case Study** (as a project assignment per KTU Module 4 guidelines) is a structured analytical exercise in which a student investigates, documents, and critically evaluates a real-world deployment of an ERP system in a large enterprise — covering the pre-implementation phase, the go-live challenges, the post-implementation outcomes, and the strategic lessons learned.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ERP as the "Central Nervous System" of a company.**

Imagine a large hospital with 30 different departments — cardiology, pharmacy, billing, emergency, radiology, kitchen, administration. Without a central nervous system, the cardiology department would have no idea what drugs the pharmacy has dispensed to a patient, and the billing department would send duplicate or wrong invoices. This is exactly what happens in a large company **without ERP** — every department uses its own isolated software, leading to data silos, conflicting reports, and chaos.

**Now, install a "central brain" (the ERP system)** that connects all 30 departments. The moment a doctor prescribes medicine, the pharmacy stock updates, the billing system generates a charge, and the insurance desk gets the claim — **all in real time, from a single source of truth**. This is what ERP does for a Fortune-500 company. However, installing this "central brain" is like performing **open-heart surgery on a running organism** — it is risky, painful, and sometimes fatal (e.g., the famous FoxMeyer Drugs collapse).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Operations Management

| Aspect | Relevance to Operations Management |
|---|---|
| **Process Integration** | ERP is the ultimate expression of *process integration* in operations |
| **Supply Chain Visibility** | Enables real-time *supply chain* coordination (Module 3 link) |
| **Lean & Agile Operations** | Modern ERPs embed *lean principles* and *agility metrics* |
| **Strategic Decision-Making** | ERP data feeds *forecasting, capacity planning, and inventory* models |
| **Change Management** | ERP failures teach valuable *human resource* and *cultural* lessons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metrics used in ERP Case Studies (Bold Highlights):**
> - **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — typically **1–3% of annual revenue** for large enterprises
> - **Implementation Time** — industry average: **14–36 months**
> - **Failure Rate** — historically **60–75%** of ERP projects fail to meet original objectives (Standish Group CHAOS Report)
> - **Average ROI** — measurable benefits accrue in **2–4 years** post go-live
> - **User Adoption Rate** — target benchmark: **>80%** within 6 months of go-live

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Any ERP Case Study Analysis

A well-structured KTU project case study on ERP must dissect the implementation into **three analytical pillars**:

### Pillar 1: CHALLENGES (The "What Went Wrong" Diagnosis)

> [!NOTE]
> ERP implementation challenges are categorized into **Six Critical Dimensions**. Every real-world failure or success story can be mapped to one or more of these.

1. **Strategic Misalignment** — Top management chooses ERP for "trend" rather than for solving a specific operational problem
2. **Scope Creep** — Trying to implement too many modules at once
3. **Data Migration Catastrophe** — Legacy data is dirty, duplicated, and incompatible
4. **Organizational Resistance** — Employees fear job loss and resist new workflows
5. **Customization Overload** — Modifying the ERP beyond its best-practice framework
6. **Underestimation of Change Management** — Training and communication budgets are slashed

### Pillar 2: SOLUTIONS (The "What Was Done" Recovery Plan)

| Challenge | Solution Framework | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| Strategic Misalignment | **Business Process Re-engineering (BPR)** before selection | Nestlé's SAP rollout |
| Scope Creep | **Phased Rollout (Big-Bang vs. Phased)** | Nike phased by region |
| Data Migration | **Data Cleansing + Master Data Management (MDM)** | Hershey's pre-go-live cleanup |
| Organizational Resistance | **Change Management (Kotter's 8-Step Model)** | Cisco's "Champions Network" |
| Customization Overload | **"Vanilla" Implementation** + minimal fit-gap analysis | Procter & Gamble's SAP |
| Training Gaps | **Role-based Learning + Sandbox environments** | Infosys internal ERP training |

### Pillar 3: OUTCOMES (The "What Was Achieved" Measurement)

Outcomes are measured across **Four Performance Vectors**:

- **Operational Efficiency** — cycle time reduction, inventory turnover, order fulfillment speed
- **Financial Performance** — cost savings, working capital release, revenue growth enablement
- **Strategic Agility** — time-to-market for new products, M&A integration speed
- **Human Capital** — employee satisfaction, decision-making empowerment, skill upgrades

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quantifiable ERP Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although ERP is a qualitative project, KTU expects students to apply **quantitative metrics** to evaluate outcomes. The following table is your **exam-ready cheat sheet**.

| Metric | Formula | Unit / Standard | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ROI of ERP** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefits} - \text{TCO}}{\text{TCO}} \times 100\%$ | % (Target: > 15% in 5 yrs) | Justifies the capital expenditure |
| **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{TCO}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Benefit}}$ | Years (Target: < 4 yrs) | Measures recovery speed |
| **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** | $\text{TCO} = C_{\text{lic}} + C_{\text{impl}} + C_{\text{training}} + C_{\text{maint}}$ | Currency (₹ / $) | Lifetime cost projection |
| **Schedule Variance (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \text{Earned Value (EV)} - \text{Actual Cost (AC)}$ | Currency / Hours | Earned Value Management |
| **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Earned Value (EV)}}{\text{Actual Cost (AC)}}$ | Ratio (Target: $\geq 1.0$) | Budget control metric |
| **User Adoption Rate** | $\text{UAR} = \dfrac{\text{Active Users}}{\text{Target Users}} \times 100\%$ | % (Target: > 80%) | Measures change success |
| **Defect Density** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Bugs Reported}}{\text{Function Points}}$ | Bugs per FP | Software quality |
| **Downtime Cost** | $\text{DC} = \text{Go-Live Hours} \times \text{Hourly Revenue}$ | Currency per hour | Risk exposure assessment |
| **Break-Even Volume** | $\text{BEV} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed ERP Cost}}{\text{Price per Transaction} - \text{Variable Cost}}$ | Number of transactions | Cloud vs. on-prem decision |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Marking Note:** When you write a formula in the answer sheet, **always state the unit** after the value. Examiners in KTU deduct marks for unitless numerical answers.

## 2.3 Real-World ERP Case Studies — The "Big Four" Reference Set

For your KTU project, you may select **any one** of the following classic cases, all of which are widely cited in operations management literature:

| # | Company | Industry | ERP System | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hershey Company** | FMCG / Confectionery | SAP R/3 (1996–1999) | **Failed** go-live → $151M revenue drop |
| 2 | **Nike** | Sportswear / Retail | SAP (2000) | **Failed** → $100M+ loss, supply chain collapse |
| 3 | **FoxMeyer Drugs** | Pharmaceutical Distribution | SAP R/3 (1994–1996) | **Bankrupt** — the "ERP Graveyard" case |
| 4 | **Procter & Gamble** | FMCG | SAP (1997–2001) | **Successful** — $200M+ annual savings |
| 5 | **Cisco Systems** | Networking Hardware | Oracle ERP (1990s) | **Successful** — $1.8B annual savings |
| 6 | **Tata Steel** | Steel Manufacturing | SAP S/4HANA | **Successful** — Indian reference case |
| 7 | **Reliance Jio** | Telecom | Custom ERP + SAP | **Successful** — Greenfield ERP launch |

> [!TIP]
> For a KTU project submission, **Hershey** or **FoxMeyer** are the most analysed cases in academic literature. For an Indian context, **Tata Steel** or **Infosys** are recommended.

## 2.4 The Real-World Utility in Engineering & Operations Fields

ERP systems underpin **every modern engineering enterprise**:
- **Manufacturing Engineers** use ERP-integrated **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** to track shop-floor output
- **Civil Engineers** use ERP modules for **project billing, BOQ tracking, and contractor management**
- **Mechanical Engineers** use ERP-linked **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management Systems)** for predictive maintenance
- **Electrical Engineers** use ERP **asset management modules** for grid and substation lifecycle tracking

> [!NOTE]
> **Linkage to KTU Module 3 (Supply Chain Management):** ERP is the digital backbone that enables *end-to-end supply chain visibility* — from raw material supplier to end customer. Without ERP, modern SCM concepts like VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory) and CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment) cannot function at scale.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework for Case Study Project

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this topic is **project-based and analytical** (not formula-heavy), Section 3 provides a **rigorous step-by-step methodology** for executing the case study. This is the **KTU-expected analytical framework** that examiners will use to grade your project report.

## 3.1 The 10-Step KTU ERP Case Study Project Methodology

### Step 1: Define the Scope and Boundaries

```
Scope = {Company, Time Period, ERP System, Functional Modules Covered}
```

> Example: *"This case study analyses the SAP S/4HANA implementation at Tata Steel's Jamshedpur plant between 2018 and 2022, covering modules FI/CO, MM, PM, and PP."*

### Step 2: Conduct Background Research

Gather data from:
- Annual reports (10-K filings for US companies, Annual Reports for Indian firms)
- Industry analyst reports (**Gartner Magic Quadrant, Forrester Wave**)
- Press releases and case study white papers from **SAP, Oracle, Microsoft** official sites
- Academic journal articles (search Google Scholar with keywords: *"{Company name}" + "ERP implementation" + "case study"*)

### Step 3: Map the Pre-Implementation Challenges

Construct a **Challenges Matrix** (this is your primary diagnostic tool):

| Dimension | Specific Challenge | Evidence from Source | Severity (H/M/L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic | Lack of executive sponsorship | Interview with CIO, 2019 | **H** |
| Operational | Inefficient legacy mainframe | Pre-ERP audit report | **H** |
| Financial | Budget overrun | News article, *The Economist*, 2020 | **M** |
| Human | 60% employee resistance | Internal HR survey | **H** |
| Technical | Data quality issues | Master Data Audit | **M** |

### Step 4: Apply an Analytical Framework

Choose **one** of the following established academic frameworks (KTU examiners prefer **Kotter + Nolan Norton**):

- **Kotter's 8-Step Change Model** — for the *human/change* dimension
- **Nolan Norton ERP Implementation Phases** — for the *technical* dimension
- **TOE (Technology-Organization-Environment) Framework** — for the *strategic* dimension
- **Critical Success Factors (CSF) Model by Holland & Light** — for *success* evaluation
- **Risks in ERP Implementation Model by Sumner** — for *failure* evaluation

### Step 5: Document the Solutions Implemented

For each challenge in Step 3, document the **specific solution** the company deployed. Use a **Solution Traceability Matrix**:

| Challenge ID | Challenge Description | Solution Deployed | Investment (₹ / $) | Timeline |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C-01 | Legacy system fragmentation | Greenfield SAP implementation | $50M | 24 months |
| C-02 | Employee resistance | 18-month change program + champions network | $5M | 18 months |
| C-03 | Data quality | MDM tool + 6-month cleansing | $3M | 6 months |

### Step 6: Quantify the Outcomes

Use the **formula sheet from Section 2.2** to calculate quantitative outcomes.

**Worked Example — ROI Calculation for Hypothetical Case:**

Let us assume:
- Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) = $80 million
- Annual Net Cash Benefits = $25 million
- Project Life = 7 years

The **Total Net Benefits over 7 years**:

$$
\text{Total Net Benefits} = 25 \times 7 = 175 \text{ million USD}
$$

The **Net Profit (after deducting TCO)**:

$$
\text{Net Profit} = 175 - 80 = 95 \text{ million USD}
$$

The **Return on Investment (ROI)**:

$$
\text{ROI} = \dfrac{95}{80} \times 100\% = 118.75\%
$$

The **Payback Period**:

$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{80}{25} = 3.2 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An ROI of **118.75%** over 7 years with a payback of **3.2 years** is considered **excellent** by industry benchmarks (target ROI > 100% in 5 years for ERP).

### Step 7: Identify Critical Success Factors (CSFs) and Critical Failure Factors (CFFs)

**CSFs (Why it worked)**:
1. **Executive Sponsorship** — CEO personally chaired steering committee
2. **Phased Rollout** — pilot plant first, then full scale
3. **Robust Training** — 40 hours per employee average

**CFFs (Why some elements failed)**:
1. **Vendor Lock-in** — too dependent on a single consulting partner
2. **KPI Mismatch** — measured wrong metrics post go-live
3. **Integration Gaps** — non-ERP systems not integrated

### Step 8: Conduct a SWOT Analysis of the Implementation

> This is a **mandatory section** in any KTU project report.

| Category | Findings |
|---|---|
| **Strengths** | Strong IT team, clear vision, sufficient budget |
| **Weaknesses** | Limited internal SAP skills, over-reliance on consultants |
| **Opportunities** | AI/ML integration, IoT-enabled shop floor, predictive analytics |
| **Threats** | Cybersecurity risks, rapid technology obsolescence, vendor pricing changes |

### Step 9: Derive Generic Lessons Learned (Transferable Knowledge)

This is the **most heavily weighted section** in KTU grading. Convert specific findings into **generic principles**:

1. **Lesson 1:** "Top-down executive sponsorship is non-negotiable for ERP success"
2. **Lesson 2:** "Phased implementation reduces risk compared to Big-Bang approach"
3. **Lesson 3:** "Data cleansing must consume at least 20% of total project budget"
4. **Lesson 4:** "Change management is 50% of the project, not 10%"
5. **Lesson 5:** "Customization beyond 20% of vanilla scope invites failure"

### Step 10: Conclude with Future Recommendations

Suggest 2–3 **forward-looking improvements** such as:
- Migration to **Cloud ERP (S/4HANA Cloud, Oracle Fusion)**
- Integration of **AI/ML for predictive maintenance**
- Adoption of **Digital Twin** of the supply chain
- **Blockchain integration** for supplier transparency

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Three Landmark ERP Cases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **side-by-side matrix** is a high-yield template KTU examiners love to see in project reports and viva voce.

| Dimension | **Hershey (Failure)** | **FoxMeyer (Failure)** | **Procter & Gamble (Success)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year | 1996 | 1994 | 1997 |
| ERP System | SAP R/3 | SAP R/3 | SAP R/3 |
| Implementation Approach | Big-Bang (over a weekend) | Big-Bang | Phased, multi-year |
| Training Investment | Low (cut to save cost) | Minimal | High ($50M+) |
| Data Preparation | Rushed | Rushed | 18-month cleansing |
| Customization | Excessive (50%+) | Excessive | Minimal (vanilla) |
| Outcome | $151M revenue drop, lawsuits | Bankruptcy filing | $200M+ annual savings |
| Root Cause of Failure | Inadequate testing + go-live timing (peak season) | Over-customization + loss of operational capability | N/A (success case) |
| Key Lesson | **Never go live during peak season** | **Never let ERP shut down warehouse operations** | **Patience and process discipline pay off** |

## 3.3 The Critical Success Factor (CSF) Hierarchy

Based on extensive academic literature, the **ranked CSF hierarchy** for ERP implementation is:

$$
\text{CSF}_{\text{ranked}} = \{\text{Executive Sponsorship},\ \text{Project Management},\ \text{Change Management},\ \text{Data Quality},\ \text{Vendor Selection},\ \text{User Training},\ \text{Scope Control},\ \text{Testing Rigor}\}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this hierarchy for the **Part A (3-mark)** questions in the KTU exam. It is one of the most frequently asked questions.
