# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Q: Define the term "Value Stream Mapping". List any four standardized icons used in a VSM and briefly state their purpose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Value Stream Mapping (VSM) is a lean-management tool that visually represents every step in a process — both material flow and information flow — from raw material to the end customer, to identify value-added and non-value-added activities. It uses a standardized set of icons (developed by Rother & Shook) for universal readability.

**Four icons (0.5 marks each):**
1. **Process Box (Rectangle)** — Represents a single manufacturing or service process step; displays cycle time, changeover time, operators, and yield.
2. **Inventory Triangle (▲)** — Represents stock of raw material, WIP, or finished goods between two processes; shows quantity and waiting time.
3. **Material Movement Arrow (→)** — Indicates the physical flow of materials from one process to the next; pull arrows go from customer to supplier, push arrows go the other way.
4. **Information Box / Dashed Line** — A dashed line at the top of the map indicating electronic or verbal information flow such as production schedules, kanban signals, or forecasts.

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, students may also mention the **Customer/Supplier icon** (factory silhouette), **Shipment truck icon**, and the **Production Control Box**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Distinguish between "Value-Added" and "Non-Value-Added" activities with two examples each. Why is eliminating non-value-added activities central to lean?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Value-Added (VA) | Non-Value-Added (NVA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | An activity that transforms the product/service in a way the customer is willing to pay for. | An activity that consumes resources (time, material, cost) but does not increase value as perceived by the customer. |
| **Three Criteria** | (i) Customer cares, (ii) Physically changes the product, (iii) Done right the first time | Fails any one of the three VA criteria |
| **Example 1** | Drilling a hole in a metal sheet | Moving the metal sheet from one building to another |
| **Example 2** | Welding two metal parts | Storing the parts in a queue for 3 days before welding |

**Why central to lean (1 mark):** The Toyota Production System, the philosophical root of lean, holds that *all non-value-added activities are waste (Muda)*. Eliminating them shortens lead time, reduces cost, frees capacity, and improves quality — which are the very outcomes lean promises. In a typical factory, only **< 5% of total lead time is value-added**, so the potential improvement is enormous.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Module 4 Choice A]

> **Q (14 marks): You have been appointed as a Lean Manufacturing consultant for an SME that produces 200 aluminium die-cast components per day in a 9-hour single shift. The production manager reports high WIP, missed delivery dates, and rising scrap (currently 6%). Using VSM and waste-reduction techniques, develop a comprehensive Lean Implementation Plan.**

#### Part (a) — Diagnose the As-Is Situation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Define the Project Charter (2 marks)**
- **Product family:** Aluminium die-cast components (e.g., motor housings).
- **Goal:** Reduce lead time by 50%, reduce scrap by 60%, and reduce WIP by 70% within 6 months.
- **Scope:** End-to-end from aluminium ingot receipt to finished, packed goods.
- **Team:** Cross-functional — 1 production manager, 2 supervisors, 1 quality engineer, 1 materials handler, 1 maintenance technician.

**Step 2: Walk the Gemba and Collect Data (2 marks)**

| Process | Cycle Time | Changeover | Downtime | Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Melting & Die-Casting | 110 sec | 25 min | 12% | 96% |
| Trimming & Degating | 35 sec | 8 min | 5% | 98% |
| Shot Blasting | 50 sec | 10 min | 6% | 99% |
| CNC Machining | 140 sec | 30 min | 10% | 94% |
| Leak Testing | 45 sec | 5 min | 4% | 100% |
| Packing & Despatch | 30 sec | 5 min | 3% | 100% |

**Step 3: Calculate Takt Time (1 mark)**
$$
TT = \frac{9 \times 3600}{200} = 162 \text{ sec/unit}
$$

**Step 4: Draw the Current State Map (1 mark)**
Map every process box, every inventory triangle, material flow arrows, and the information-flow dashed line. Total WIP observed = 1,500 units; total lead time = 14 days; value-added time = $110+35+50+140+45+30 = 410$ sec = 6.83 min.

**Step 5: Identify Wastes (1 mark)**
- **Overproduction:** Melting more molten metal than needed → excess solid ingots.
- **Waiting:** Operators idle during die-cooling (90 sec/cycle).
- **Inventory:** 1,500 WIP units between processes.
- **Defects:** 6% scrap due to porosity and dimensional variation.
- **Motion:** Operators walk 20 ft to fetch trimming tools.

#### Part (b) — Design the Future State & Implementation Plan (7 marks)

**Step 1: Quantify Waste — Calculate Process Efficiency (1 mark)**
$$
PE = \frac{410 \text{ sec}}{14 \times 9 \times 3600 \text{ sec}} \times 100\% = \frac{410}{453600} \times 100\% \approx 0.090\%
$$

**Step 2: Apply Targeted Lean Tools (3 marks)**

| Waste Identified | Lean Tool to Apply | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Overproduction of molten metal | **Heijunka + Level Scheduling** | Melt only what the next station needs; cut energy by 20% |
| Long die-cooling wait | **SMED** — convert cooling to external activity via pre-heated dies | Reduce cooling from 90s → 40s |
| 1,500 WIP units | **Kanban pull system** between cells | WIP ≤ 150 units (90% reduction) |
| 6% scrap | **Poka-Yoke** — auto-reject parts with wrong dimensions using a go/no-go gauge | Scrap ≤ 2% |
| Operators walking 20 ft | **5S + Cellular layout** redesign | Walking ≤ 5 ft; floor space −30% |
| Variability in cycle time | **Standard Work** documentation | CT variation ±10% |

**Step 3: Draw the Future State Map (1 mark)**
The FSM shows three U-shaped cells: (i) Casting & Trimming, (ii) Shot Blast & CNC, (iii) Leak Test & Pack. Each cell is connected by a single-card Kanban. Information flow is now a daily *Heijunka box* and electronic Kanban signal, replacing weekly email schedules.

**Step 4: Phased Rollout Plan (1 mark)**

| Phase | Duration | Activities | Success Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Foundation** | Weeks 1–4 | Top-management buy-in, 5S training, charter sign-off | 100% workforce trained |
| **VSM Current State** | Weeks 5–7 | Gemba walk, data, CSM validated | CSM signed off |
| **Future State + Cell Pilot** | Weeks 8–12 | Build Cell 1, install SMED, launch Kanban | Cell 1 OEE ≥ 80% |
| **Scale-out** | Weeks 13–24 | Replicate to Cells 2 & 3; Poka-Yoke rollout | All 3 cells operational |
| **Sustain** | Week 25+ | Daily Lean board, monthly Kaizen events | Sustained 50% lead-time reduction |

**Step 5: Expected ROI (1 mark)**
- Lead time: 14 days → 5 days (↓ 64%).
- WIP: 1,500 → 200 (↓ 87%).
- Scrap: 6% → 2% (↓ 67%) — annual saving ≈ ₹8 lakh (assumed ₹40 scrap cost/unit × 200/day × 250 days × 0.04 reduction).
- OEE: 55% → 78% (+23 points).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Losing Marks):** Students often describe *activities* (e.g., "we will do 5S") without specifying **which waste each tool targets, who is responsible, and what the measurable target is**. Always tie every tool to a specific waste, an owner, a deadline, and a KPI.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Module 4 Choice B, Alternative]

> **Q (14 marks): A garment-manufacturing unit producing 500 shirts/day faces the following problems: (i) excessive fabric inventory, (ii) high re-stitching rate (8%), (iii) long changeovers between styles. As a Lean consultant, design a Value Stream Map for the current state and a detailed action plan using waste-reduction techniques.**

#### Part (a) — Current State VSM and Waste Diagnosis (7 marks)

**Step 1: Process Identification (1.5 marks)**
The garment value stream has six core processes:
1. Fabric Receiving & Inspection
2. Spreading & Cutting
3. Stitching (Assembly line with 12 operators)
4. Ironing & Pressing
5. Quality Check & Patching
6. Packing & Despatch

**Step 2: Data Collection & Current State Map (3 marks)**

| Process | CT (sec) | C/O (min) | Yield (%) | WIP Before (units) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fabric Receiving | — | — | 100 | 1,200 |
| Spreading & Cutting | 30 | 20 | 99 | 400 |
| Stitching | 240 | 15 | 92 (8% rework) | 600 |
| Ironing & Pressing | 50 | 5 | 99 | 250 |
| QC & Patching | 40 | — | 95 | 150 |
| Packing | 35 | 10 | 100 | 200 |

Takt time = $9 \times 3600 / 500 = 64.8$ sec/shirt. Lead time = 10 days. Value-added time = $30+240+50+40+35 = 395$ sec = 6.58 min.

**Step 3: Identify the 8 Wastes Present (2.5 marks)**
- **Inventory:** 1,200 fabric rolls sitting in store → I-Waste.
- **Waiting:** Stitching operators idle while bundle of cut pieces moves from cutting to stitching.
- **Defects:** 8% re-stitching due to needle break, uneven stitch length.
- **Transportation:** Fabric rolls physically moved 3 times before reaching cutting table.
- **Overproduction:** Cutting department cuts 550 pieces even though demand is 500.
- **Motion:** Operator walks to fetch thread spools from a central rack.
- **Extra Processing:** Hand-pressing of cuffs that could be done by machine.
- **Non-Utilized Talent:** Skilled tailors assigned to simple button-stitching.

#### Part (b) — Future State Design and Action Plan (7 marks)

**Step 1: Set Improvement Targets (1 mark)**

| Metric | Current | Target | Reduction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lead Time | 10 days | 4 days | 60% |
| WIP | 2,800 units | 400 units | 86% |
| Rework | 8% | 2% | 75% |
| Fabric Inventory | 1,200 rolls | 200 rolls | 83% |
| Changeover (Spreading) | 20 min | ≤ 8 min | 60% |

**Step 2: Apply Lean Tools with Specific Actions (3 marks)**

1. **5S in Spreading Department** — Sort (remove unused fabric rolls), Set in order (dedicated roll racks by colour/size), Shine (clean floor daily), Standardize (SOPs), Sustain (daily audit). *Target: cut fabric-roll search time from 10 min to 1 min.*

2. **SMED on Spreading & Cutting** — Convert internal C/O activities (e.g., laying new marker paper) to external by *preparing the next marker while the current one is being cut*. *Target: 20 min → 8 min.*

3. **Kanban between Cutting and Stitching** — Each stitching operator carries a Kanban card. The card signals upstream to cut only when the operator is ready to take the next bundle. *Target: WIP drops from 600 to 50 between Cut and Stitch.*

4. **Poka-Yoke at Stitching Stations** — Install a stitch-length sensor that stops the sewing machine if the stitch is outside tolerance. *Target: rework from 8% → 2%.*

5. **Jidoka — Andon Cord** — Each operator pulls a cord if quality is suspect; line leader resolves within 60 sec. *Target: defect escape to QC drops 70%.*

6. **Heijunka Box** — Level the production mix across the week instead of batching by style. *Target: smoother workflow, no Friday overload.*

7. **Quick Changeover on Ironing Press** — Use colour-coded temperature presets to avoid manual adjustment. *Target: 5 min → 1 min.*

**Step 3: Future State Map Highlights (1 mark)**
- Three **U-shaped cells**: Cell A (Cut + Stitch), Cell B (Iron + QC), Cell C (Pack + Despatch).
- Material flow is **single-piece pull** for high-value shirts, batch-of-five for low-value.
- Information flow: **daily Heijunka box** + electronic Kanban signal to the cutting table.

**Step 4: Implementation Timeline (1 mark)**

| Phase | Weeks | Activities |
|---|---|---|
| **Prep** | 1–3 | Training, 5S, baseline VSM |
| **Quick Wins** | 4–6 | 5S audit, Andon cord, Poka-Yoke sensors |
| **Pull System** | 7–12 | Kanban pilot, cell redesign |
| **SMED** | 13–16 | Spreading C/O reduction |
| **Scale & Sustain** | 17+ | Roll to other product families, daily Kaizen |

**Step 5: Cultural & Sustainability Elements (1 mark)**
- **Suggestion system** (1 idea per operator per month).
- **Monthly Gemba walks** by senior management.
- **Visual management board** showing OEE, scrap %, on-time delivery, daily.
- **Recognition programme** for the best Kaizen of the month.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Losing Marks):**
> 1. Writing only a *list of lean tools* without linking each to a *specific waste, owner, KPI, and deadline* = lose up to 4 marks.
> 2. Drawing VSM as a generic flowchart with non-standard icons = lose 1–2 marks. Always use the *Rother & Shook* standardized VSM icon set.
> 3. Ignoring the **cultural / people aspect** of lean (Kaizen, respect for people, suggestion systems) = lose 1 mark. Lean is a *socio-technical* system, not just a toolbox.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize These for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- ✅ Lean = *maximize customer value, eliminate waste*; originally from **Toyota Production System (TPS)**.
- ✅ Three Ms to attack: **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden).
- ✅ Eight wastes: **DOWNTIME** — Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing.
- ✅ Five Lean principles: **Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection** (Womack & Jones).
- ✅ VSM has **two** maps: **Current State Map (CSM)** + **Future State Map (FSM)**.
- ✅ Standardized VSM icons (Rother & Shook): process box, inventory triangle, material arrow, customer/supplier icon, info flow dashed line, production control box, shipment truck.
- ✅ Takt Time formula: $TT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ (sec/unit).
- ✅ Process Efficiency: $PE = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ Rolled Throughput Yield: $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ across all stations.
- ✅ OEE $= A \times P \times Q$; world-class threshold ≥ **85%**.
- ✅ Lead Time ≠ Cycle Time — Lead Time is the *elapsed time from order to delivery*; Cycle Time is *one operation at one station*.
- ✅ Core Lean tools to memorize: **5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke, Jidoka, SMED, Heijunka, Andon, Kaizen, A3, Gemba, Standard Work**.
- ✅ **5S** = *Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke* (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain).
- ✅ **SMED** = Single-Minute Exchange of Die; target changeover < 10 min; convert *internal* to *external* activities.
- ✅ **Kanban** = visual *pull* signal that triggers upstream production; replaces *push* (forecast-based) systems.
- ✅ **Jidoka** = automation with a human touch — stop the line on abnormality (*autonomation*).
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing device (e.g., USB-only-fits-one-way, car-ignition-while-brake-pressed).
- ✅ **Heijunka** = level scheduling; smooths production to avoid Mura.
- ✅ Implementation follows **5 phases**: Foundation → CSM → FSM + Tool selection → Pilot → Sustain & Scale.
- ✅ Cultural pillars: **Respect for People, Continuous Improvement (Kaizen), Gemba Walks, Standard Work, Visual Management**.
- ✅ Always quantify improvements in **₹ saved, % reduction, days saved** — examiners reward metrics.
- ✅ Lean is **not a cost-cutting programme** — it is a *value-creation* philosophy; never say "lean = job cuts" in viva.
